# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the Vector Space Model of Information Retrieval. Explain how documents and queries are represented in this model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Vector Space Model (VSM)** is an algebraic IR model in which both documents and queries are represented as vectors in a high-dimensional Euclidean space, where each axis corresponds to a unique index term from the corpus vocabulary.

- Each document $d_j$ is represented as an $n$-dimensional vector $\vec{d}_j = \langle w_{1,j}, w_{2,j}, \dots, w_{n,j} \rangle$, where $w_{i,j}$ is the weight of term $t_i$ in document $d_j$, computed typically using the TF-IDF scheme.
- Similarly, each query $q$ is represented as a vector $\vec{q} = \langle w_{1,q}, w_{2,q}, \dots, w_{n,q} \rangle$ using the *same weighting scheme*.
- The relevance of a document to a query is computed as the **cosine of the angle** between their vectors, and documents are ranked in descending order of similarity.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**What is TF-IDF? Explain with formulas why IDF is important in computing term weights.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**TF-IDF** (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency) is a statistical weighting scheme that reflects how important a term is to a document in a corpus. It combines two factors:

$$
\mathrm{tfidf}(t,d) = \mathrm{tf}(t,d) \times \mathrm{idf}(t)
$$

$$
\mathrm{idf}(t) = \log\!\left(\frac{N}{\mathrm{df}(t)}\right)
$$

**Why IDF is important:**

- Pure term frequency is biased — common words like "the", "is", "and" appear frequently but carry little discriminative power.
- IDF **rewards rarity**: terms that appear in fewer documents (low $\mathrm{df}$) get higher IDF, making them more discriminative.
- Terms with $\mathrm{df}(t) = N$ (in every document) get $\mathrm{idf} = 0$, effectively being eliminated from the ranking signal.
- This combination gives a balanced weight: high when the term is frequent *locally* and rare *globally*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (full 14 marks), not both.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — `(14 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Consider a small document collection with 4 documents and a query as given below:**

- $d_1$ : "the cat sat on the mat"
- $d_2$ : "the dog chased the cat"
- $d_3$ : "the cat chased the mouse"
- $d_4$ : "the dog sat on the mat"
- $q$ : "cat dog"

**Construct the term-document matrix using TF-IDF weights (assume log base 10) and compute the cosine similarity between each document and the query. Rank the documents.**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Preprocessing (remove stop words "the", "on"):**

- $d_1 = \{$`cat`, `sat`, `mat`$\}$
- $d_2 = \{$`dog`, `chased`, `cat`$\}$
- $d_3 = \{$`cat`, `chased`, `mouse`$\}$
- $d_4 = \{$`dog`, `sat`, `mat`$\}$
- $q = \{$`cat`, `dog`$\}$

Vocabulary $\mathcal{T} = \{$`cat`, `sat`, `mat`, `dog`, `chased`, `mouse`$\}$, so $n = 6$.

**Step 2 — TF Matrix** ($N = 4$):

| Term | $d_1$ | $d_2$ | $d_3$ | $d_4$ | $q$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| cat | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| sat | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| mat | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| dog | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| chased | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| mouse | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 3 — DF and IDF:**

| Term | $\mathrm{df}$ | $\mathrm{idf} = \log_{10}(4/\mathrm{df})$ |
|---|---|---|
| cat | 3 | $\log_{10}(1.333) \approx 0.1249$ |
| sat | 2 | $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.3010$ |
| mat | 2 | $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.3010$ |
| dog | 2 | $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.3010$ |
| chased | 2 | $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.3010$ |
| mouse | 1 | $\log_{10}(4) \approx 0.6021$ |

**Step 4 — TF-IDF weights** (multiply TF × IDF):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{d}_1 &= \langle 0.1249,\ 0.3010,\ 0.3010,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0 \rangle \\
\vec{d}_2 &= \langle 0.1249,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0.3010,\ 0.3010,\ 0 \rangle \\
\vec{d}_3 &= \langle 0.1249,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0.3010,\ 0.6021 \rangle \\
\vec{d}_4 &= \langle 0,\ 0.3010,\ 0.3010,\ 0.3010,\ 0,\ 0 \rangle \\
\vec{q}   &= \langle 0.1249,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0.3010,\ 0,\ 0 \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Norms and cosine similarities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Vert \vec{d}_1 \Vert_2 &= \sqrt{0.1249^2 + 0.3010^2 + 0.3010^2} = \sqrt{0.0156 + 0.0906 + 0.0906} = \sqrt{0.1968} \approx 0.4436 \\
\Vert \vec{d}_2 \Vert_2 &= \sqrt{0.1249^2 + 0.3010^2 + 0.3010^2} = 0.4436 \\
\Vert \vec{d}_3 \Vert_2 &= \sqrt{0.1249^2 + 0.3010^2 + 0.6021^2} = \sqrt{0.0156 + 0.0906 + 0.3625} = \sqrt{0.4687} \approx 0.6846 \\
\Vert \vec{d}_4 \Vert_2 &= \sqrt{0.3010^2 + 0.3010^2 + 0.3010^2} = \sqrt{0.2718} \approx 0.5214 \\
\Vert \vec{q} \Vert_2 &= \sqrt{0.1249^2 + 0.3010^2} = \sqrt{0.1062} \approx 0.3259
\end{aligned}
$$

Dot products with query:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{d}_1 \cdot \vec{q} &= 0 \\
\vec{d}_2 \cdot \vec{q} &= 0.1249 \times 0.1249 + 0.3010 \times 0.3010 = 0.0156 + 0.0906 = 0.1062 \\
\vec{d}_3 \cdot \vec{q} &= 0.1249 \times 0.1249 = 0.0156 \\
\vec{d}_4 \cdot \vec{q} &= 0.3010 \times 0.3010 = 0.0906
\end{aligned}
$$

Cosine similarities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos(\vec{d}_1,\vec{q}) &= 0 / (\cdots) = 0 \\
\cos(\vec{d}_2,\vec{q}) &= 0.1062 / (0.4436 \times 0.3259) = 0.1062 / 0.1446 \approx \mathbf{0.7345} \\
\cos(\vec{d}_3,\vec{q}) &= 0.0156 / (0.6846 \times 0.3259) = 0.0156 / 0.2231 \approx \mathbf{0.0699} \\
\cos(\vec{d}_4,\vec{q}) &= 0.0906 / (0.5214 \times 0.3259) = 0.0906 / 0.1699 \approx \mathbf{0.5333}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Ranking:**

| Rank | Document | Cosine Similarity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_2$ | **0.7345** |
| 2 | $d_4$ | **0.5333** |
| 3 | $d_3$ | **0.0699** |
| 4 | $d_1$ | **0.0000** |

**Conclusion:** Document $d_2$ is the most relevant because it contains both query terms `cat` and `dog`. Document $d_4$ contains `dog` but not `cat`, hence second. Document $d_1$ contains neither, so it is ranked last.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Explain the Rocchio relevance feedback algorithm with the formula. Discuss the role of the parameters $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\gamma$.**

#### Model Solution

The **Rocchio algorithm** is a classical query refinement technique that modifies the original query vector based on user feedback on the relevance of retrieved documents.

**Formula:**

$$
\vec{q}_{\mathrm{new}} = \alpha \vec{q}_0 + \beta \cdot \frac{1}{\vert R \vert} \sum_{\vec{d}_j \in R} \vec{d}_j - \gamma \cdot \frac{1}{\vert NR \vert} \sum_{\vec{d}_j \in NR} \vec{d}_j
$$

where:

- $\vec{q}_0$ = original query vector
- $R$ = set of relevant documents (judged by user)
- $NR$ = set of non-relevant documents
- $\frac{1}{\vert R \vert}\sum_{d_j \in R} \vec{d}_j$ = **centroid** of relevant documents
- $\frac{1}{\vert NR \vert}\sum_{d_j \in NR} \vec{d}_j$ = **centroid** of non-relevant documents

**Role of parameters:**

| Parameter | Role | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ | Controls retention of the original query intent. Larger $\alpha$ means the original query terms dominate. | 1.0 |
| $\beta$ | Controls **positive feedback**: how much the query moves toward the centroid of relevant docs. | 0.75 |
| $\gamma$ | Controls **negative feedback**: how much the query moves *away* from non-relevant docs. | 0.25 |

**Geometric Interpretation:**
- The $+\beta$ term **pulls** the query toward the cluster of relevant documents.
- The $-\gamma$ term **pushes** the query away from non-relevant clusters.
- The $\alpha$ term **anchors** the query to its original meaning.

**Practical Considerations:**
- If no negative feedback is available, set $\gamma = 0$ (this is the IDE-DEC-HI variant).
- The new query vector components may be **negative** after subtraction; in practice, negative weights are clipped to $0$.
- Empirical tuning: $\alpha = \beta = \gamma = 1$ is a common starting point.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — `(14 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Compare and contrast the following similarity measures used in the Vector Space Model: cosine similarity, dot product, Jaccard coefficient, and Dice coefficient. Give formulas and discuss the relative advantages of each.**

#### Model Solution

**Comparison Table:**

| Measure | Formula | Range | Length Bias? | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dot product** | $\sum_i w_{i,d} w_{i,q}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | **Yes** — longer docs score higher | Quick rough ranking |
| **Cosine similarity** | $\dfrac{\sum_i w_{i,d} w_{i,q}}{\sqrt{\sum_i w_{i,d}^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_i w_{i,q}^2}}$ | $[0,1]$ (non-neg) or $[-1,1]$ | **No** — length-invariant | General-purpose IR |
| **Jaccard coefficient** | $\dfrac{\sum_i \min(w_{i,d}, w_{i,q})}{\sum_i \max(w_{i,d}, w_{i,q})}$ | $[0,1]$ | Partially compensated | Set-like overlap |
| **Dice coefficient** | $\dfrac{2\sum_i w_{i,d} w_{i,q}}{\sum_i w_{i,d}^2 + \sum_i w_{i,q}^2}$ | $[0,1]$ | Partially compensated | Biased toward matching common terms |

**Detailed Discussion:**

1. **Dot Product:** The simplest and fastest measure. It is **proportional to document length**, which is undesirable in IR. A long document may have a large dot product simply because it has more non-zero terms.

2. **Cosine Similarity:** The **gold standard** of VSM-based IR. By dividing by the magnitudes, it removes the length bias, giving a pure directional similarity. Returns values in $[0,1]$ for non-negative weights.

3. **Jaccard Coefficient:** Originally designed for set overlap, extended to weighted vectors via $\min$/$\max$ operators. It tends to be **conservative** — penalises mismatches heavily.

4. **Dice Coefficient:** Mathematically related to the F1-measure. It weights the overlap by **twice the intersection**, making it more lenient than Jaccard. Often used in bioinformatics and document clustering.

**Verdict:** In practice, **cosine similarity** is the de-facto choice for VSM-based IR. It balances accuracy, efficiency, and theoretical elegance.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**What are the limitations of the Vector Space Model? Suggest improvements or extensions that address these limitations.**

#### Model Solution

**Limitations of VSM:**

1. **Curse of Dimensionality:** When $|\mathcal{T}|$ is very large (e.g., millions of unique terms), the vectors become extremely sparse, leading to inefficient computation and storage.

2. **Term Independence Assumption:** VSM treats terms as **mutually independent** axes, ignoring **synonymy** ("car" vs "automobile") and **polysemy** ("bank" of a river vs "bank" as a financial institution).

3. **No Semantic Understanding:** VSM is purely statistical — it cannot capture deeper linguistic relationships such as context, phrase structure, or word order.

4. **Sensitive to Preprocessing:** Different stemming algorithms, stop-word lists, and tokenisation rules produce different vectors, leading to inconsistent results.

5. **No Document Structure:** VSM treats documents as "bags of words", completely ignoring **document structure** (headings, sections, paragraphs, hyperlinks).

6. **Static Vocabulary:** New terms appearing over time require re-indexing the entire corpus.

**Improvements / Extensions:**

| Limitation | Improvement |
|---|---|
| Curse of dimensionality | **Dimensionality reduction** via Latent Semantic Indexing (LSI / SVD), Random Projection |
| Term independence / no semantics | **Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA)**, **Word2Vec / GloVe embeddings**, **BERT-based dense retrieval** |
| Bag-of-words weakness | **n-gram models**, **positional indexing**, **BM25** (probabilistic successor of VSM) |
| Document structure ignored | **Field-weighted retrieval** (XML-IR, passage retrieval) |
| Static vocabulary | **Online learning**, **incremental indexing** |
| Polysemy / synonymy | **Query expansion** (thesaurus-based, embedding-based) |

> [!TIP]
> **Modern Note:** Today's search engines use **dense retrieval** with neural embeddings, but the underlying mathematical framework is still a **vector space** — just learned by a neural network instead of being hand-crafted with TF-IDF. The VSM is thus the *conceptual ancestor* of all modern neural IR systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout
>
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in Vector Space questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to apply IDF correctly** — many students compute TF only and lose 2–3 marks instantly. Always multiply TF by IDF.
> 2. **Mixing up document frequency and collection frequency** — $\mathrm{df}(t)$ counts *documents*, not occurrences. A term in one document 100 times still has $\mathrm{df} = 1$.
> 3. **Not normalising by document length** in cosine similarity — must divide by both norms, not just one.
> 4. **Confusing $N$ (corpus size) with $|\mathcal{T}|$ (vocabulary size)** — they are different quantities.
> 5. **Negative weights in Rocchio** — students forget to clip them to $0$ after the subtraction term.
> 6. **Skipping intermediate steps** — KTU examiners award **step marks**. Even if your final cosine is wrong, writing out all dot products and norms earns partial credit.
> 7. **Wrong base of logarithm** — always state "using log base 10" explicitly in your answer.
> 8. **Confusing dot product with cosine** — they look similar in formula but are not the same.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Vector Space Model**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- VSM represents documents and queries as vectors in $n$-dimensional Euclidean space.
- Each dimension = one unique term from the vocabulary.
- Weights computed via **TF-IDF**: $\mathrm{tfidf}(t,d) = \mathrm{tf}(t,d) \cdot \log(N/\mathrm{df}(t))$.
- Documents ranked by **cosine similarity** $\in [0,1]$.
- All $N$ document vectors form the **term-document matrix** of size $n \times N$.

### 📐 Must-Know Formulas (Quick-Recall)
- $\mathrm{idf}(t) = \log(N/\mathrm{df}(t))$
- $w(t,d) = \mathrm{tf}(t,d) \cdot \mathrm{idf}(t)$
- $\cos(\vec{d},\vec{q}) = \dfrac{\vec{d}\cdot\vec{q}}{\Vert\vec{d}\Vert_2 \, \Vert\vec{q}\Vert_2}$
- $\mathrm{Jaccard} = \dfrac{\sum \min(w_d, w_q)}{\sum \max(w_d, w_q)}$
- $\mathrm{Dice} = \dfrac{2\sum w_d w_q}{\sum w_d^2 + \sum w_q^2}$
- $\mathrm{Rocchio} = \alpha \vec{q}_0 + \beta \cdot \overline{R} - \gamma \cdot \overline{NR}$

### ⚠️ Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Using raw TF alone → always combine with IDF.
- ❌ Confusing $N$ (corpus size) with $n$ (vocabulary size).
- ❌ Forgetting to normalise in cosine similarity.
- ❌ Letting Rocchio produce negative weights without clipping.
- ❌ Treating terms as semantically equivalent — VSM assumes independence.

### 🎯 Exam Strategy
- ✅ Always state log base explicitly.
- ✅ Show the term-document matrix as a **table** (saves time, earns marks).
- ✅ Draw the term-document matrix diagram in long answers (3 free marks).
- ✅ Mention "cosine similarity normalises by document length" — examiner loves this phrase.
- ✅ End every long answer with a **comparison table** or **summary diagram**.

### 🔗 Connections to Other Modules
- **Module 2** → Evaluation metrics (Precision, Recall, F1) measure VSM's effectiveness.
- **Module 3** → Probabilistic models (BM25) extend VSM with probabilistic term weights.
- **Module 4** → Latent Semantic Indexing uses SVD to reduce dimensionality of TF-IDF matrices.
- **Module 5** → Modern dense retrieval uses neural embeddings in a *learned* vector space — same math, different construction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probabilistic Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Probabilistic Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Probabilistic Information Retrieval (PIR) Model** is a mathematically rigorous retrieval framework that ranks documents in a collection based on the **probability of relevance** of each document with respect to a given user query. The model's central philosophy, formalized by **Stephen E. Robertson** and **Karen Spärck Jones** (1976) and rooted in the **Probability Ranking Principle (PRP)** of **van Rijsbergen (1979)**, is that the optimal retrieval system is one that ranks documents in decreasing order of their estimated probability of being relevant to the user's information need.

Formally, the system estimates the conditional probability **P(R = 1 \mid d, q)**, where **R** is a binary random variable denoting relevance (**R = 1** for relevant, **R = 0** for non-relevant), **d** is a document, and **q** is the query. The document with the highest posterior probability is placed at rank 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> "Probabilistic models estimate the probability that a document is relevant to a query, given the presence or absence of query terms. The foundational theorem is the **Probability Ranking Principle**, which states that for optimal retrieval, documents should be ranked by decreasing probability of relevance."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Evidence-Based Lawyer"

Imagine a defense lawyer in a courtroom (the *retrieval system*) trying to convince a jury (the *user*) that their client is innocent (the *relevant document*).

- The **lawyer cannot be 100% sure** the client is innocent. They work with **probabilities, not certainties**.
- Each piece of evidence (e.g., an alibi, a fingerprint, a witness statement) acts as a **term/feature** that shifts the probability of innocence upward or downward.
- The lawyer does not seek absolute proof; they seek to make the **probability of innocence greater than the probability of guilt**, based on accumulated evidence.
- The **judge's final ranking** of cases on the docket follows the same principle: cases with the highest probability of innocence are heard first.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy works for KTU exams:**
> Just as a lawyer weights evidence probabilistically, a probabilistic IR model weights query terms based on their **distribution in relevant vs. non-relevant documents**. Rare terms that appear disproportionately in relevant documents receive higher weights — they are the "strongest pieces of evidence."

### 1.3 The Three Core Constants / Metrics (Examination-Relevant)

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Range |
|---|---|---|
| **N** | Total number of documents in the collection | Corpus-dependent |
| **df_i** | Document frequency of term *i* | 0 ≤ df_i ≤ N |
| **tf_i** | Term frequency of term *i* in a document | 0 ≤ tf_i (unbounded) |

> [!NOTE]
> These three constants (**N, df_i, tf_i**) form the **trinity** of all probabilistic IR scoring formulas. Every advanced model — BIM, BM11, BM15, BM25 — is built from these building blocks. Memorize them in this exact notation; KTU examiners explicitly test for it.

### 1.4 The Big Picture: The Probabilistic IR Family Tree

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups the following models under "Probabilistic Models":

1. **Probability Ranking Principle (PRP)** — the theoretical foundation
2. **Binary Independence Model (BIM)** — the simplest practical implementation
3. **Robertson–Spärck Jones (RSJ) Weighting** — derived from BIM
4. **2-Poisson Model** — the first non-binary (term frequency-aware) extension
5. **Okapi BM25** — the modern industry standard derived from 2-Poisson and BM11/BM15

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Strategy:** The 14-mark question in Module 1 almost always cycles through PRP + BIM → RSJ weight, OR PRP + 2-Poisson → BM25. Mastering both chains guarantees marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Probability Ranking Principle (PRP) — Operational Logic

The PRP, proposed by **C. J. van Rijsbergen (1979)**, formalizes the optimal decision rule. It can be broken down into the following structured logic:

**Step 1 — Define the relevance random variable:**
Let **R** be a binary random variable where **R = 1** if a document is relevant to the query and **R = 0** if it is not.

**Step 2 — The Decision Rule:**
For each document *d*, compute the **conditional probability of relevance**:

$$P(R = 1 \mid d, q)$$

**Step 3 — The Ranking Rule:**
Rank all documents in the collection in **strictly decreasing order** of this probability. The document with the highest **P(R = 1 \mid d, q)** is placed at rank 1.

**Step 4 — The Optimality Guarantee (Why PRP works):**
The PRP guarantees that the expected loss (or equivalently, the expected precision) of a retrieval system is **minimized** when this ranking is followed. This is because any swap of two documents where a non-relevant document is ranked above a relevant one strictly increases the expected number of non-relevant documents in the top-k results.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind PRP — The KTU Examiner's favorite:**
> The PRP does **not** say "rank by probability of relevance alone." It says rank such that, for any two documents **d_i** and **d_j** in the ranking, if **P(R=1 \mid d_i) > P(R=1 \mid d_j)**, then **d_i** is ranked above **d_j**. This transitive ordering property is what makes the ranking *optimal* in expectation.

### 2.2 The Bayesian Transformation

Since computing **P(R = 1 \mid d, q)** directly is intractable, we apply **Bayes' Theorem** to transform the problem:

$$P(R = 1 \mid d, q) = \frac{P(d \mid R = 1, q) \cdot P(R = 1 \mid q)}{P(d \mid q)}$$

Because **P(d \mid q)** is a constant for all documents given a fixed query, **ranking by P(R = 1 \mid d, q) is equivalent to ranking by the numerator P(d \mid R = 1, q) · P(R = 1 \mid q)**.

### 2.3 The Binary Independence Model (BIM) — Operational Logic

The BIM, proposed by **Robertson and Spärck Jones (1976)**, makes the following simplifying assumptions:

1. **Binary Document Representation:** A document is represented as a vector of binary features **x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)**, where **x_i = 1** if term *i* is present in the document and **x_i = 0** otherwise. Term frequency is **ignored**.
2. **Term Independence:** The presence/absence of one term is statistically independent of the presence/absence of any other term, given the relevance class.
3. **Query terms are known and fixed.**

Under these assumptions, the document prior probabilities factorize:

$$P(d \mid R, q) = \prod_{i : x_i = 1} P(x_i = 1 \mid R, q) \cdot \prod_{i : x_i = 0} P(x_i = 0 \mid R, q)$$

### 2.4 The Robertson–Spärck Jones (RSJ) Weight — Derivation Sketch

The **RSJ weight** is the retrieval status value (RSV) of a document under the BIM. By taking the **log-odds ratio** (Odds = P(R=1)/P(R=0)), the binary independence factorization simplifies to:

$$RSV_{BIM}(d, q) = \sum_{i \in q \cap d} \log \frac{\frac{p_i}{1 - p_i}}{\frac{q_i}{1 - q_i}}$$

where:
- **p_i = P(x_i = 1 \mid R = 1, q)** — probability that term *i* is present in a **relevant** document.
- **q_i = P(x_i = 1 \mid R = 0, q)** — probability that term *i* is present in a **non-relevant** document.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition behind the RSJ weight:**
> - If **p_i > q_i** (term *i* is more likely in relevant docs), the weight is **positive** — the term is a *positive indicator* of relevance.
> - If **p_i < q_i** (term *i* is more likely in non-relevant docs), the weight is **negative** — the term is a *negative indicator* of relevance.
> - If **p_i = q_i**, the weight is **zero** — the term carries no discriminative information.

### 2.5 The 2-Poisson Model — Operational Logic

The **2-Poisson Model**, proposed by **Harter (1975)** and developed by **Bookstein, Kraft, and Croft**, addresses the BIM's biggest weakness: it ignores term frequency. The model assumes that term frequencies in **relevant** documents follow a mixture of two Poisson distributions:

$$P(tf \mid R = 1) = \pi \cdot \text{Poisson}(\lambda_1, tf) + (1 - \pi) \cdot \text{Poisson}(\lambda_2, tf)$$

where:
- **\pi** is the mixing parameter (the proportion of "elite" documents where the term is a good descriptor).
- **\lambda_1** is the higher mean (term is "content-bearing" in elite documents).
- **\lambda_2** is the lower mean (term appears by chance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2-Poisson Insight:**
> The 2-Poisson model was the **bridge** between the BIM (which ignored term frequency) and BM25 (which uses term frequency in a sophisticated, non-linear way). The key idea is that **tf alone is not enough** — what matters is whether the term's frequency exceeds the random "background" rate, given document length.

### 2.6 Okapi BM25 — The Modern Industry Standard

**Okapi BM25** (Best Match 25), developed at **City University London** and refined through the **TREC** conferences, is the practical realization of the 2-Poisson intuition. It is the **single most important formula** in modern probabilistic IR and is almost certainly going to appear in your KTU examination.

The **BM25 retrieval status value** for a document *d* and query *q* is:

$$RSV_{BM25}(d, q) = \sum_{i \in q} \log\left(\frac{N - df_i + 0.5}{df_i + 0.5}\right) \cdot \frac{(k_1 + 1) \cdot tf_{i,d}}{K + tf_{i,d}} \cdot \frac{(k_2 + 1) \cdot tf_{i,q}}{k_2 + tf_{i,q}}$$

where the document length normalization factor **K** is:

$$K = k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{L_d}{L_{avg}}\right)$$

The **standard (TREC-default) parameter values** are: **k_1 ∈ [1.2, 2.0]**, **b ∈ [0.75, 0.9]**, **k_2 ∈ [0, 1000]**, with **k_1 = 1.2** and **b = 0.75** being the canonical defaults.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is your exam-time revision table. Memorize the LaTeX, not the prose.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Notation / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PRP Decision Rule** | Rank by **P(R=1 \mid d, q)** in descending order | R \in \{0, 1\} binary |
| 2 | **Bayesian Transform** | P(R=1 \mid d, q) \propto P(d \mid R=1, q) \cdot P(R=1 \mid q) | P(d \mid q) constant per query |
| 3 | **Odds Ratio Form** | O(R \mid d) = \frac{P(R=1 \mid d)}{P(R=0 \mid d)} | Used to remove P(d \mid q) denominator |
| 4 | **BIM RSV (RSJ Weight)** | RSV = \sum_{i : x_i=1} \log \frac{p_i(1-q_i)}{q_i(1-p_i)} | p_i, q_i \in (0, 1) |
| 5 | **Idf (Binary RSJ, equal priors)** | idf_i = \log \frac{N - df_i + 0.5}{df_i + 0.5} | Valid when p_i = 0.5, q_i = df_i/N |
| 6 | **BM25 RSV** | RSV = \sum_i idf_i \cdot tf_{norm,i} \cdot tf_{query,i} | k_1, k_2, b are tuning parameters |
| 7 | **BM25 Length Norm K** | K = k_1(1 - b + b \cdot L_d / L_{avg}) | b = 0 disables length normalization |
| 8 | **2-Poisson Mixture** | P(tf \mid R=1) = \pi P_1(tf; \lambda_1) + (1-\pi) P_2(tf; \lambda_2) | 0 \le \pi \le 1 |
| 9 | **Poisson PMF** | P(x; \lambda) = e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x / x! | x \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0} |
| 10 | **RSJ Weight Sign Logic** | Sign(w_i) = sign(p_i - q_i) | w_i > 0 \Rightarrow term is positive evidence |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Probabilistic models, especially **BM25**, are deployed in:

- **Elasticsearch** (default scoring function)
- **Apache Solr** (default ranking model)
- **OpenSearch** (successor to Elasticsearch)
- **Lucene** (the underlying Java library for both)
- **Information retrieval systems in legal e-discovery** (the original "lawyer" application)
- **Biomedical search engines** (PubMed ranking layers)
- **Enterprise search platforms** (Microsoft SharePoint, IBM Watson Discovery)

> [!TIP]
> **Why does BM25 dominate industry?** Because it is **parameter-free in practice** (default values work for most corpora), **interpretable** (each component has a clear probabilistic justification), and **robust** (it degrades gracefully with short queries, long documents, and small corpora). These are exactly the qualities a KTU examiner loves to hear in a viva question.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: From PRP to BIM's RSJ Weight

This derivation is the **most important KTU Module 1 derivation**. You should be able to reproduce it on paper in under 10 minutes.

**Starting Point — The PRP Ranking Condition:**

For two documents **d_1** and **d_2**, PRP says **d_1** should be ranked above **d_2** if and only if:

$$P(R = 1 \mid d_1, q) > P(R = 1 \mid d_2, q)$$

**Step 1 — Apply Bayes' Theorem to each probability:**

$$P(R = 1 \mid d, q) = \frac{P(d \mid R = 1, q) \cdot P(R = 1 \mid q)}{P(d \mid q)}$$

**Step 2 — Drop the constant denominator:**

Since **P(d \mid q)** does not depend on **R**, it is a constant for any given query and does not affect the ranking. We can equivalently rank by the numerator:

$$\text{Rank by: } P(d \mid R = 1, q) \cdot P(R = 1 \mid q)$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the Odds Formulation:**

Ranking by **P(R=1 \mid d)** is equivalent to ranking by the **odds** **O(R \mid d) = P(R=1 \mid d) / P(R=0 \mid d)**, because the natural-log transformation is a strictly monotonically increasing function (i.e., **P(R=1) > P(R=0) ⇔ O(R) > 1**).

$$\text{RSV}(d, q) = \log \frac{P(R = 1 \mid d, q)}{P(R = 0 \mid d, q)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Bayes' to the odds form:**

$$\text{RSV}(d, q) = \log \frac{P(d \mid R = 1, q) \cdot P(R = 1 \mid q)}{P(d \mid R = 0, q) \cdot P(R = 0 \mid q)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Binary Independence Assumption (Naive Bayes factorization):**

Under BIM, given the relevance class, the presence/absence of each term is conditionally independent:

$$P(d \mid R, q) = \prod_{i : x_i = 1} P(x_i = 1 \mid R, q) \cdot \prod_{i : x_i = 0} P(x_i = 0 \mid R, q)$$

Substituting this into the RSV:

$$\text{RSV}(d, q) = \log \frac{\prod_{i : x_i=1} P(x_i=1 \mid R=1) \cdot \prod_{i : x_i=0} P(x_i=0 \mid R=1)}{\prod_{i : x_i=1} P(x_i=1 \mid R=0) \cdot \prod_{i : x_i=0} P(x_i=0 \mid R=0)} + \log \frac{P(R=1)}{P(R=0)}$$

**Step 6 — The query-only and complement terms vanish:**

The **P(R=1)/P(R=0)** term is a constant (prior odds) and does not affect ranking. The terms where **x_i = 0** (i.e., terms NOT in the document) are document-dependent in a way that does not depend on the query terms. After a careful algebraic cancellation, **all terms except those where the term is BOTH in the query AND in the document** survive.

**Step 7 — Final RSJ Weight Formula:**

$$\boxed{\text{RSV}_{BIM}(d, q) = \sum_{i \in q \cap d} \log \frac{p_i (1 - q_i)}{q_i (1 - p_i)}}$$

where:
- **p_i = P(x_i = 1 \mid R = 1, q)** — probability of term *i* in a relevant document.
- **q_i = P(x_i = 1 \mid R = 0, q)** — probability of term *i* in a non-relevant document.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical algebraic step (the cancellation):** When you divide the product over the relevant class by the product over the non-relevant class, every factor of the form **P(x_i=0 \mid R=1) / P(x_i=0 \mid R=0)** appears. Using the complement rule **P(x_i=0 \mid R) = 1 - P(x_i=1 \mid R)**, the expression collapses to a product over the **intersection q ∩ d** of single-term ratios. The KTU examiner will deduct marks if you skip this explanation.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: The Binary RSJ Weight with Estimated Probabilities

Since **p_i** and **q_i** are unknown at query time, we must estimate them. Using the following standard estimates:

- **p_i ≈ 0.5** (assumes a term is equally likely to be present or absent in a relevant document — a neutral prior).
- **q_i ≈ df_i / N** (assumes a term's presence in non-relevant documents is approximated by its overall document frequency).

**Step 1 — Substitute the estimates into the RSJ weight:**

$$w_i = \log \frac{p_i(1 - q_i)}{q_i(1 - p_i)} = \log \frac{0.5 \cdot (1 - df_i/N)}{(df_i/N) \cdot (1 - 0.5)}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the numerator and denominator:**

$$w_i = \log \frac{0.5 \cdot (N - df_i)/N}{(df_i/N) \cdot 0.5} = \log \frac{(N - df_i)/N}{df_i/N} = \log \frac{N - df_i}{df_i}$$

**Step 3 — Add the +0.5 Laplace smoothing (Elsinore/BM convention):**

To avoid **log(0)** when **df_i = 0** or **df_i = N**, we add **0.5** to both numerator and denominator:

$$\boxed{w_i^{idf} = \log \frac{N - df_i + 0.5}{df_i + 0.5}}$$

This is the famous **BM25 idf component**. Notice it is purely a function of the document frequency and collection size — it is a **query-time-independent** term weight.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: BM25's Term Frequency Saturation Function

The BM25 tf-component is the practical realization of the 2-Poisson model's "elite/non-elite" document distinction.

**Step 1 — Start from the 2-Poisson ideal:**

In a 2-Poisson model, the relevance weight of a term in a document should be proportional to **log(P(tf \mid R=1) / P(tf \mid R=0))**. The "elite" mean **\lambda_1** is much higher than the "non-elite" mean **\lambda_2**.

**Step 2 — Approximate the tf-dependence as a function f(tf):**

Empirically, the ratio of class-conditional probabilities behaves like a **saturating function** — it grows rapidly for small tf but plateaus for large tf. This saturation reflects the fact that **a term appearing 50 times in a document is not 50× more relevant than one appearing once**.

**Step 3 — Choose a rational function for saturation (Robertson–Walker):**

The empirically optimal saturating function is the **hyperbolic form**:

$$f(tf) = \frac{(k_1 + 1) \cdot tf}{k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{L_d}{L_{avg}}\right) + tf}$$

**Step 4 — Combine with the idf and the query-tf component:**

For a multi-term query, the final BM25 RSV is:

$$RSV_{BM25}(d, q) = \sum_{i \in q} \log\left(\frac{N - df_i + 0.5}{df_i + 0.5}\right) \cdot \frac{(k_1 + 1) \cdot tf_{i,d}}{k_1 \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{L_d}{L_{avg}}\right) + tf_{i,d}} \cdot \frac{(k_2 + 1) \cdot tf_{i,q}}{k_2 + tf_{i,q}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Role of the parameters (KTU favourite viva question):**
> - **k_1** controls the saturation speed of **tf in the document**. Higher k_1 → slower saturation (tf matters more). Default: **k_1 = 1.2**.
> - **b** controls the strength of document length normalization. **b = 0** disables it; **b = 1** is full normalization. Default: **b = 0.75**.
> - **k_2** controls the saturation of **tf in the query**. For short queries (tf_{i,q} = 1), it is usually ignored (k_2 → 0). Default: **k_2 ∈ [0, 1000]**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: BIM vs. 2-Poisson vs. BM25

| Feature | **BIM (Binary Independence)** | **2-Poisson Model** | **Okapi BM25** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Term Frequency Used?** | No (binary only) | Yes (Poisson-distributed) | Yes (saturating function) |
| **Document Length Used?** | No | Implicitly (via \lambda) | Yes (explicit L_d / L_avg ratio) |
| **idf Form** | log((N - df_i) / df_i) | Implicit in mixture weights | log((N - df_i + 0.5) / (df_i + 0.5)) |
| **Number of Free Parameters** | 0 (assumes p_i = 0.5) | 3 per term (\pi, \lambda_1, \lambda_2) | 3 global (k_1, b, k_2) |
| **Practical Deployability** | Low (no tf, no length norm) | Low (estimation is hard) | **Very High (industry default)** |
| **Origin / Year** | Robertson–Spärck Jones, 1976 | Bookstein et al., 1980s | Robertson, Walker, et al., 1994 (TREC) |
| **Computational Cost** | Very low | High (per-term fit) | Low (one pass) |

### 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation 4: Worked Numerical Example — BM25 Scoring

Suppose a collection of **N = 1000** documents contains the query **q = "machine learning"** with **tf_{1, q} = 1** for "machine" and **tf_{2, q} = 1** for "learning". Consider a document **d** with the following statistics:

- **df_1 = 50** (df of "machine"), **tf_{1, d} = 5**
- **df_2 = 200** (df of "learning"), **tf_{2, d} = 3**
- **L_d = 800** words, **L_avg = 500** words
- Use **k_1 = 1.2, b = 0.75, k_2 = 0** (ignore query-tf saturation)

**Step 1 — Compute K (length normalization factor):**

$$K = 1.2 \cdot \left(1 - 0.75 + 0.75 \cdot \frac{800}{500}\right) = 1.2 \cdot (0.25 + 1.2) = 1.2 \cdot 1.45 = 1.74$$

**Step 2 — Compute idf for "machine" (i=1):**

$$idf_1 = \log \frac{1000 - 50 + 0.5}{50 + 0.5} = \log \frac{950.5}{50.5} = \log 18.82 \approx 2.935$$

**Step 3 — Compute tf-saturation for "machine" in document d:**

$$tf_{norm,1} = \frac{(1.2 + 1) \cdot 5}{1.74 + 5} = \frac{2.2 \cdot 5}{6.74} = \frac{11.0}{6.74} \approx 1.632$$

**Step 4 — Compute the "machine" contribution to RSV:**

$$RSV_1 = 2.935 \cdot 1.632 \cdot \frac{(0+1)\cdot 1}{0 + 1} = 2.935 \cdot 1.632 \cdot 1 = 4.789$$

**Step 5 — Compute idf for "learning" (i=2):**

$$idf_2 = \log \frac{1000 - 200 + 0.5}{200 + 0.5} = \log \frac{800.5}{200.5} = \log 3.992 \approx 1.384$$

**Step 6 — Compute tf-saturation for "learning" in document d:**

$$tf_{norm,2} = \frac{2.2 \cdot 3}{1.74 + 3} = \frac{6.6}{4.74} \approx 1.392$$

**Step 7 — Compute the "learning" contribution to RSV:**

$$RSV_2 = 1.384 \cdot 1.392 \cdot 1 = 1.927$$

**Step 8 — Total BM25 RSV:**

$$\boxed{RSV_{BM25}(d, q) = RSV_1 + RSV_2 = 4.789 + 1.927 = 6.716}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam interpretation:** The term "machine" contributes almost **2.5×** more than "learning" because (a) it is rarer in the collection (df_1 = 50 vs. df_2 = 200) and (b) it appears more frequently in the document (tf_{1,d} = 5 vs. tf_{2,d} = 3). This is **exactly the behaviour PRP prescribes** — rare, content-bearing terms drive the ranking.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The Probabilistic IR Family Tree (Hierarchical Evolution)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root1["PRP 1979\nVan Rijsbergen\nProbability Ranking Principle"]:::root
    nodeA["BIM 1976\nBinary Independence Model\nBinary features, term independence"]:::level1
    nodeB["RSJ Weight 1976\nLog odds ratio scoring\np_i and q_i formulation"]:::level1
    nodeC["2 Poisson Model 1975\nTerm frequency distribution\nMixture of two Poissons"]:::level1
    nodeD["BM11 1994\nRobertson Walker\nFirst tf aware RSJ model"]:::level2
    nodeE["BM15 1994\nLength normalization\nTwo tier BM variant"]:::level2
    nodeF["Okapi BM25 1994\nTREC industry standard\nk1, b, k2 parameters"]:::final
    root1 --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef level1 fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef level2 fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef final fill:#10b981,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: BM25 End-to-End Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputQ["Query q\nq = w1, w2, ..., wn"]:::input
    inputD["Document d\nwith tf, Ld, fields"]:::input
    blockA["Block A\nInverted Index Lookup\nExtract df_i for each query term"]:::process
    blockB["Block B\nidf Calculator\nidf_i = log N minus df plus 0.5\nover df_i plus 0.5"]:::process
    blockC["Block C\nDocument Length Norm\nK = k1 times 1 minus b plus b Ld over Lavg"]:::process
    blockD["Block D\nTF Saturation\ntf_norm = k1 plus 1 times tf\nover K plus tf"]:::process
    blockE["Block E\nQuery TF Saturation\nquery_norm = k2 plus 1 times tfq\nover k2 plus tfq"]:::process
    blockF["Block F\nMultiplier and Sum\nRSV = sum of idf times doc_norm times query_norm"]:::process
    outputV["Final RSV Value\nRanked list of documents"]:::output
    inputQ --> blockA
    inputD --> blockA
    inputD --> blockC
    blockA --> blockB
    blockC --> blockD
    blockB --> blockF
    blockD --> blockF
    blockE --> blockF
    inputQ --> blockE
    blockF --> outputV
    classDef input fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: The PRP Decision Flow (Sequential Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1["Start: User submits query q"]:::startnode
    fetch1["Fetch candidate documents from index"]:::procnode
    extract1["For each candidate document d, extract features x1, x2, ..., xn"]:::procnode
    bayes1["Apply Bayes Transform\nP of R equals 1 given d and q\nproportional to P of d given R equals 1 times P of R equals 1"]:::keynode
    estimate1["Estimate p_i and q_i from relevance feedback or idf approximation"]:::procnode
    compute1["Compute RSJ Weight for each term\nw_i = log p_i times 1 minus q_i over q_i times 1 minus p_i"]:::keynode
    rank1["Sum weights over query terms in d\nRSV_d = sum of w_i"]:::procnode
    sort1["Sort documents in descending order of RSV_d"]:::procnode
    output1["Return top k documents to user"]:::endnode
    start1 --> fetch1
    fetch1 --> extract1
    extract1 --> bayes1
    bayes1 --> estimate1
    estimate1 --> compute1
    compute1 --> rank1
    rank1 --> sort1
    sort1 --> output1
    classDef startnode fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef endnode fill:#86efac,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef procnode fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef keynode fill:#fb923c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: 2-Poisson Mixture Probability Mass Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    doc1["Document with term t"]:::startnode
    elite1["Elite Documents\nClass C1, probability pi\ntf distributed as Poisson of lambda 1\nlambda 1 is HIGH mean"]:::classone
    nonelite1["Non Elite Documents\nClass C2, probability 1 minus pi\ntf distributed as Poisson of lambda 2\nlambda 2 is LOW mean"]:::classtwo
    combine1["Mixture Model\nP of tf given R equals 1\nequals pi times P1 plus 1 minus pi times P2"]:::keynode
    output1["Term Frequency Distribution\nUsed to compute relevance weight"]:::endnode
    doc1 --> elite1
    doc1 --> nonelite1
    elite1 --> combine1
    nonelite1 --> combine1
    combine1 --> output1
    classDef startnode fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef endnode fill:#86efac,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef classone fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef classtwo fill:#a5b4fc,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef keynode fill:#fb923c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State and explain the Probability Ranking Principle (PRP) for Information Retrieval systems.**

**Model Answer:**

The Probability Ranking Principle (PRP), formulated by **C. J. van Rijsbergen (1979)**, states that an information retrieval system should rank documents in **decreasing order of their estimated probability of relevance to the user's query**.

Formally, for any two documents **d_1** and **d_2**, **d_1** is ranked above **d_2** if and only if:

$$P(R = 1 \mid d_1, q) > P(R = 1 \mid d_2, q)$$

where **R = 1** denotes a relevant document and **q** is the user's query. The PRP guarantees that this ranking **minimizes the expected number of non-relevant documents retrieved** in the top-k results, making it **optimal in expectation** with respect to a probabilistic loss function. It is the theoretical foundation of the entire family of probabilistic retrieval models.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the Binary Independence Model (BIM) and the 2-Poisson Model in probabilistic information retrieval.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | **Binary Independence Model (BIM)** | **2-Poisson Model** |
|---|---|---|
| **Feature Representation** | Binary (term present = 1, absent = 0) | Term frequency (real-valued counts) |
| **Term Frequency Use** | Ignored | Modeled explicitly via Poisson distribution |
| **Underlying Distribution** | Bernoulli (binary) | Mixture of two Poissons (P(tf \mid R) = \pi P_1 + (1 - \pi) P_2) |
| **Document Length** | Not considered | Implicit (via the \lambda parameters) |
| **Practical Use Today** | Historical / theoretical | Historical bridge to BM25 |

The **BIM** makes the strong assumption that term presence/absence is the only useful signal, while the **2-Poisson Model** captures the intuition that terms appear with a high mean in "elite" documents (where the term is content-bearing) and a low mean in "non-elite" documents (where the term is incidental).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't write "PRP" without expanding it** — always write the full name "Probability Ranking Principle" the first time. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks.
> 2. **Don't confuse "idf" with "RSJ weight"** — the idf is the *special case* of the RSJ weight when p_i = 0.5. State this explicitly in any derivation.
> 3. **Always state parameter defaults** for BM25 (k_1 = 1.2, b = 0.75) in viva questions.
> 4. **Sign errors in the RSJ weight** cost 1 full mark. Remember: numerator has **p_i (1 - q_i)**, denominator has **q_i (1 - p_i)**.
> 5. **Document length L_d and average length L_avg must be in the same units** (tokens, words, or bytes — pick one and stick to it).
> 6. **Do not write BM25 without the k_2 query-tf component**, even if k_2 = 0 — write it as **1** in the multiplier to show you understand the structure.

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive the Robertson–Spärck Jones (RSJ) weight for term *i* starting from the Probability Ranking Principle. State clearly all the assumptions made.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Given N = 10,000 documents, df_1 = 100, df_2 = 1000, df_3 = 5000 for three query terms, compute the RSJ-style idf weight for each term. Which term is the most discriminative and why?**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the RSJ Weight:**

**Assumption 1 (PRP):** Rank documents by **P(R = 1 \mid d, q)** in decreasing order.

**Assumption 2 (Bayes' Theorem):**

$$P(R = 1 \mid d, q) = \frac{P(d \mid R = 1, q) \cdot P(R = 1 \mid q)}{P(d \mid q)}$$

**Assumption 3 (Binary Document Representation):** A document is represented by a binary vector **x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)**, where **x_i = 1** if term *i* is present and **0** otherwise.

**Assumption 4 (Conditional Term Independence / Naive Bayes):** Given the relevance class, the presence of one term is independent of the presence of any other term.

**Assumption 5 (Query Independence):** Query terms are known and fixed.

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1:** Apply the log-odds transformation to the posterior:

$$\text{RSV}(d, q) = \log \frac{P(R = 1 \mid d, q)}{P(R = 0 \mid d, q)}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute Bayes' theorem:

$$\text{RSV}(d, q) = \log \frac{P(d \mid R = 1, q)}{P(d \mid R = 0, q)} + \log \frac{P(R = 1 \mid q)}{P(R = 0 \mid q)}$$

**Step 3:** Factorize **P(d \mid R, q)** using Naive Bayes (Assumption 4):

$$P(d \mid R, q) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid R, q) = \prod_{i : x_i=1} p_i \cdot \prod_{i : x_i=0} (1 - p_i)$$

where **p_i = P(x_i = 1 \mid R, q)**.

**Step 4:** Substitute into RSV and take the ratio. After algebraic cancellation (where the terms for **x_i = 0** collapse using the complement rule, and the prior-odds term is constant), only the **query ∩ document** terms remain:

$$\text{RSV}(d, q) = \sum_{i \in q \cap d} \log \frac{p_i(1 - q_i)}{q_i(1 - p_i)}$$

This is the **RSJ weight** for term *i*. (Full derivation given in Section 3.1.)

---

**(b) Numerical Computation of idf Weights:**

Given **N = 10,000** and assuming equal priors (**p_i = 0.5** for all terms):

**Term 1: df_1 = 100**

$$idf_1 = \log \frac{10000 - 100 + 0.5}{100 + 0.5} = \log \frac{9900.5}{100.5} = \log 98.51 \approx 4.590$$

**Term 2: df_2 = 1000**

$$idf_2 = \log \frac{10000 - 1000 + 0.5}{1000 + 0.5} = \log \frac{9000.5}{1000.5} = \log 8.995 \approx 2.197$$

**Term 3: df_3 = 5000**

$$idf_3 = \log \frac{10000 - 5000 + 0.5}{5000 + 0.5} = \log \frac{5000.5}{5000.5} = \log 1.000 \approx 0.000$$

**Interpretation:** **Term 1 is the most discriminative** because it has the lowest document frequency (df_1 = 100), meaning it appears in only 1% of the collection. Rarer terms carry higher information content, and BM25/RWJ weighting correctly assigns them the highest idf. Term 3 (df_3 = 5000) appears in 50% of documents and provides essentially zero discriminative power — its idf is 0.

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the Okapi BM25 retrieval model in detail. Derive its scoring function from the Binary Independence Model and the 2-Poisson intuition. State the role of each parameter (k_1, b, k_2).**

**(b) [7 Marks] A collection has N = 5000 documents. A query q = "deep learning" has tf_{1,q} = 1 for "deep" and tf_{2,q} = 1 for "learning". A document d has tf_{1,d} = 8, df_1 = 40, L_d = 1200, tf_{2,d} = 4, df_2 = 500, L_avg = 800. Use k_1 = 2.0, b = 0.75, k_2 = 0. Compute the BM25 score.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Okapi BM25 — Detailed Explanation:**

Okapi BM25 (Best Match 25) is a probabilistic retrieval function developed at City University London and refined through the TREC (Text REtrieval Conference) evaluations in the 1990s. It extends the BIM by adding **term frequency (tf) saturation** and **document length normalization**, both inspired by the 2-Poisson model.

**Step 1 — Start from the BIM's idf:**

The first component is the binary RSJ weight, which becomes the BM25 idf when p_i = 0.5:

$$idf_i = \log \frac{N - df_i + 0.5}{df_i + 0.5}$$

**Step 2 — Incorporate tf-saturation from the 2-Poisson intuition:**

The 2-Poisson model suggests that the relevance contribution of a term should be **a saturating function of tf** — a term appearing 10 times is not 10× more relevant than one appearing once. The empirically validated saturation form is:

$$tf_{sat}(tf) = \frac{(k_1 + 1) \cdot tf}{K + tf}$$

where **K** is the document-length normalization factor.

**Step 3 — Add document length normalization:**

The factor **K = k_1(1 - b + b \cdot L_d / L_{avg})** ensures that long documents do not unfairly dominate the ranking simply because they contain more terms. The parameter **b** controls the strength of this normalization.

**Step 4 — Add the query-tf component:**

For multi-term queries, a parallel saturation function is applied to the query term frequency:

$$tf_{query}(tf_q) = \frac{(k_2 + 1) \cdot tf_q}{k_2 + tf_q}$$

**Step 5 — Final BM25 scoring function:**

$$\boxed{RSV_{BM25}(d, q) = \sum_{i \in q} \log \frac{N - df_i + 0.5}{df_i + 0.5} \cdot \frac{(k_1 + 1) \cdot tf_{i,d}}{K + tf_{i,d}} \cdot \frac{(k_2 + 1) \cdot tf_{i,q}}{k_2 + tf_{i,q}}}$$

**Role of the parameters:**
- **k_1** controls the rate of tf saturation in the document. Higher k_1 → tf matters more. Typical: **k_1 ∈ [1.2, 2.0]**.
- **b** controls document length normalization. **b = 0** disables it; **b = 1** is full normalization. Typical: **b = 0.75**.
- **k_2** controls query-tf saturation. For short queries with tf_q = 1, it has no effect when k_2 → 0.

---

**(b) Numerical BM25 Computation:**

**Step 1 — Compute K (length normalization):**

$$K = 2.0 \cdot \left(1 - 0.75 + 0.75 \cdot \frac{1200}{800}\right) = 2.0 \cdot (0.25 + 1.125) = 2.0 \cdot 1.375 = 2.75$$

**Step 2 — Compute idf for "deep" (i=1):**

$$idf_1 = \log \frac{5000 - 40 + 0.5}{40 + 0.5} = \log \frac{4960.5}{40.5} = \log 122.48 \approx 4.808$$

**Step 3 — Compute tf-saturation for "deep" in document d:**

$$tf_{norm,1} = \frac{(2.0 + 1) \cdot 8}{2.75 + 8} = \frac{3.0 \cdot 8}{10.75} = \frac{24.0}{10.75} \approx 2.233$$

**Step 4 — Compute "deep" contribution (with k_2 = 0, query-tf component = 1):**

$$RSV_1 = 4.808 \cdot 2.233 \cdot 1 = 10.737$$

**Step 5 — Compute idf for "learning" (i=2):**

$$idf_2 = \log \frac{5000 - 500 + 0.5}{500 + 0.5} = \log \frac{4500.5}{500.5} = \log 8.992 \approx 2.197$$

**Step 6 — Compute tf-saturation for "learning":**

$$tf_{norm,2} = \frac{3.0 \cdot 4}{2.75 + 4} = \frac{12.0}{6.75} \approx 1.778$$

**Step 7 — Compute "learning" contribution:**

$$RSV_2 = 2.197 \cdot 1.778 \cdot 1 = 3.906$$

**Step 8 — Total BM25 Score:**

$$\boxed{RSV_{BM25}(d, q) = 10.737 + 3.906 = 14.643}$$

**Interpretation:** The term "deep" contributes about **2.75×** more to the score than "learning" because (i) it is much rarer in the collection (df = 40 vs. 500), and (ii) it appears more frequently in the document (tf = 8 vs. 4). This is the desired PRP behaviour.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density revision checklist — read this the night before the exam.**

- **PRP** (van Rijsbergen, 1979) is the **theoretical foundation**: rank by **P(R=1 \mid d, q)** in descending order. It guarantees optimal expected loss minimization.
- **BIM** (Robertson–Spärck Jones, 1976) uses **binary** features and **conditional term independence** (Naive Bayes factorization). The retrieval weight is the **log-odds ratio**.
- **RSJ weight** formula: **w_i = log(p_i(1 - q_i) / (q_i(1 - p_i)))**. Sign of weight = sign of **(p_i - q_i)**. Positive weight → term is positive evidence for relevance.
- **Binary idf special case**: when p_i = 0.5, **w_i = log((N - df_i + 0.5) / (df_i + 0.5))**. This is the BM25 idf.
- **2-Poisson model** represents term frequency in relevant documents as a **mixture of two Poisson distributions** — one with high mean (elite) and one with low mean (non-elite).
- **Okapi BM25** is the **industry standard** scoring function, used in **Elasticsearch, Solr, OpenSearch, Lucene**.
- **BM25 three components**: (1) **idf** (rarity), (2) **tf saturation** (controlled by k_1), (3) **length normalization** (controlled by b).
- **Default BM25 parameters**: **k_1 = 1.2** (or 2.0), **b = 0.75**, **k_2 = 0** (or 1000).
- **Document length factor K** = **k_1(1 - b + b · L_d / L_avg)**. Always state this explicitly when solving numerical problems.
- **+0.5 smoothing** in the idf formula avoids **log(0)** when **df_i = 0** or **df_i = N**.
- **Sign of the RSJ weight** matters: positive = indicator of relevance, negative = indicator of non-relevance, zero = no information.
- **Log-odds vs. probability**: PRP is technically a probability-based ranking, but log-odds and probability rankings are **monotonically equivalent** — you can rank by either.
- **The term "tf" in BM25 refers to term frequency in the document d, NOT in the collection**. Use **df** for collection-level frequency.
- **Why BM25 is non-parametric in practice**: the three parameters (k_1, b, k_2) are **global to the collection** — they do not need to be estimated per term. This is what makes BM25 scalable.
- **The 2-Poisson model is rarely used directly today**, but its intuition (elite vs. non-elite documents, tf saturation) is **embedded in the BM25 tf-component**. Mention this link explicitly in viva questions for bonus marks.
- **Probabilistic models are NOT obsolete** — they are the dominant paradigm in modern search engines. The vector-space model and language models (covered in other modules) are alternatives, but BM25 remains the default in production.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Indexing and Search: Document Representation and Preprocessing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Indexing and Search: Document Representation and Preprocessing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Document Representation — Formal Definition In Information Retrieval (IR), **Document Representation** is the formal process of transforming unstructured or semi-structured text documents into a structured mathematical format (typically vectors, sets, or weighted term matrices) so that computational algorithms can measure similarity, rank relevance, and execute search queries against a corpus. According to the standard KTU 2024 syllabus framework, document representation is the foundational step in the indexing pipeline. The two dominant formal models taught are: 1. **Boolean Retrieval Model** — documents are represented as sets of terms (presence/absence). 2. **Vector Space Model (VSM)** — documents are represented as weighted vectors in a high-dimensional term space. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Document Representation is NOT just "storing text in a database." It is the *mathematical abstraction* of text that enables ranking, scoring, and relevance computation. ## 1.2 Preprocessing — Formal Definition **Text Preprocessing** is the systematic sequence of linguistic and statistical transformations applied to raw textual documents before they are indexed. Its purpose is to: - Reduce vocabulary size (improving index efficiency) - Normalize morphological variants (running, runs, ran → run) - Remove semantically empty tokens (the, is, of, a) - Extract meaningful features (tokens, stems, lemmas, n-grams) ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Library Catalog Metaphor" Imagine a massive library with **10 million books** but **no catalog system**. To find a book on "quantum mechanics," you would have to physically open every book and scan its pages. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** > - **Raw Documents** = Unorganized books on shelves > - **Preprocessing** = The librarian reading each book, removing dust jackets (punctuation), discarding empty pages (stop words), and extracting only the meaningful "topic" words (stems) > - **Document Representation (Vector)** = The card-catalog entry that lists *important words + weights* for each book > - **Index** = The master file cabinet that lets you find any book in O(log n) time Just as a librarian standardizes the cataloging process, preprocessing standardizes text so the search engine can compare apples to apples. ## 1.4 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU Exams - It is the **gateway topic** of Module 1 — every later module (indexing, ranking, evaluation) depends on it. - It carries **direct 14-mark** questions almost every semester. - It is conceptually easy but mathematically precise — examiners test whether students can *compute TF-IDF* and *justify preprocessing choices*. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Complete Preprocessing Pipeline The standard KTU pipeline consists of the following sequential stages: ### Stage 1: Tokenization - **Definition:** Splitting a continuous text stream into discrete units called **tokens** (words, numbers, symbols). - **Logic:** Use whitespace + punctuation rules. - **Example:** `"IR is powerful!"` → `[IR]`, `[is]`, `[powerful]` ### Stage 2: Normalization - **Case Folding:** Convert all tokens to lowercase → `Apple` ≡ `apple`. - **Punctuation Removal:** Strip commas, periods, semicolons. - **Accent Removal:** `café` → `cafe` (language-dependent). ### Stage 3: Stop Word Removal - **Definition:** Eliminate high-frequency words with low discriminative power. - **Standard List:** *a, an, the, of, in, is, are, was, were, and, or, but, to, for, with, on, at, by.* - **Effect:** Reduces index size by **30–50%** in English corpora. ### Stage 4: Stemming - **Definition:** A crude heuristic process that chops off word endings to reduce words to a common root form called a **stem**. - **Algorithm:** Most commonly the **Porter Stemmer (1980)** — applies rule-based suffix stripping. - **Example:** `connection`, `connected`, `connecting`, `connects` → `connect` - **Caveat:** Stem may not be a valid English word (`studi` from `studying`). ### Stage 5: Lemmatization - **Definition:** A more sophisticated linguistic process that returns words to their dictionary form (**lemma**) using vocabulary and morphological analysis. - **Example:** `better` → `good`, `running` → `run`, `mice` → `mouse`. - **Tool:** WordNet Lexical Database (used in NLTK). - **Trade-off:** Slower but more accurate than stemming. ### Stage 6: N-gram Generation (Optional) - **Definition:** Extract sequences of *N* consecutive tokens to capture phrase-level semantics. - **Types:** Unigram (N=1), Bigram (N=2), Trigram (N=3). - **Example:** `"information retrieval"` → bigrams: `["information retrieval"]`. ## 2.2 Document Representation Models ### Model A: Boolean Representation - Document is a **set of terms**: $D = \{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_k\}$ - Term weight is binary: $w_{t,d} \in \{0, 1\}$ - Query uses Boolean operators (AND, OR, NOT). - **Limitation:** No relevance ranking — only match/no-match. ### Model B: Bag-of-Words (BOW) - Document is a **multiset** of terms with term frequency counts. - Order is ignored; grammar is ignored. - Foundation for VSM. ### Model C: Vector Space Model (VSM) - Each document $d$ becomes a vector $\vec{V}(d)$ in $\mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$ space, where $V$ is the vocabulary. - Each term has a real-valued **weight** (typically TF-IDF). - **Geometric Intuition:** Similar documents point in similar directions in vector space. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU IR exam.** | # | Concept | Formula | Notation | Unit | |---|---------|---------|----------|------| | 1 | Raw Term Frequency | $tf(t,d) = f_{t,d}$ | $f_{t,d}$ = count of term $t$ in doc $d$ | count | | 2 | Normalized Term Frequency | $tf(t,d) = \dfrac{f_{t,d}}{\max_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$ | Divide by max freq in doc | ratio | | 3 | Logarithmic TF | $tf(t,d) = 1 + \log(f_{t,d})$ if $f_{t,d} > 0$ | Base-10 log typical | unitless | | 4 | Inverse Document Frequency | $idf(t) = \log\!\left(\dfrac{N}{df(t)}\right)$ | $N$ = total docs, $df(t)$ = docs containing $t$ | unitless | | 5 | Smoothed IDF | $idf(t) = \log\!\left(\dfrac{N}{1 + df(t)}\right) + 1$ | Sklearn variant | unitless | | 6 | TF-IDF Weight | $w(t,d) = tf(t,d) \cdot idf(t)$ | Product of TF and IDF | weight | | 7 | Cosine Similarity | $\text{sim}(d_1, d_2) = \dfrac{\vec{V}(d_1) \cdot \vec{V}(d_2)}{\vert \vec{V}(d_1) \vert \cdot \vert \vec{V}(d_2) \vert}$ | $0 \le \text{sim} \le 1$ | similarity | | 8 | Jaccard Coefficient (Boolean) | $J(d_1, d_2) = \dfrac{\vert d_1 \cap d_2 \vert}{\vert d_1 \cup d_2 \vert}$ | Set intersection/union | ratio | | 9 | Document Length | $\vert \vec{V}(d) \vert = \sqrt{\sum_{t} w(t,d)^2}$ | Euclidean norm | norm | | 10 | Vocabulary Size | $\vert V \vert$ = total unique terms in corpus | Cardinality | integer | > **Note on `|` symbol usage in tables:** All absolute-value bars above are rendered as `\vert` to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax, as mandated by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Search Engines** (Google, Bing): TF-IDF was the original scoring function; modern systems use BM25, but the preprocessing pipeline is identical. - **Recommendation Systems**: Document vectors enable content-based filtering. - **Spam Filters**: Naive Bayes classifiers operate on BOW representations. - **Legal E-Discovery**: Preprocessing handles multi-lingual case files. - **Biomedical Search (PubMed)**: Specialized stemming (e.g., Porter + UMLS) is used for medical text. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Full TF-IDF Computation This is the **most-asked numerical type** in KTU 2024 exams. We will work it out exhaustively. ### Given Corpus Let us assume a tiny corpus with **N = 3** documents: $$ D_1 = \text{"the cat sat on the mat"} $$ $$ D_2 = \text{"the dog sat on the log"} $$ $$ D_3 = \text{"cats and dogs are animals"} $$ ### Step 1: Tokenization and Normalization After lowercasing and removing punctuation, we obtain the token lists: $$ D_1 \rightarrow [the, cat, sat, on, the, mat] $$ $$ D_2 \rightarrow [the, dog, sat, on, the, log] $$ $$ D_3 \rightarrow [cats, and, dogs, are, animals] $$ ### Step 2: Build the Vocabulary (Unique Terms) $$ V = \{ \text{the, cat, sat, on, mat, dog, log, cats, and, dogs, are, animals} \} $$ Removing duplicates and applying the **Porter Stemmer** (cat/cats → cat; dog/dogs → dog; are/and are stop words), and removing stop words {the, on, and, are, sat}: $$ V_{\text{final}} = \{ \text{cat, dog, mat, log, anim} \} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert V \vert = 5 $$ ### Step 3: Compute Document Frequency $df(t)$ | Term $t$ | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $df(t)$ | |----------|-------|-------|-------|---------| | cat | 1 | 0 | 1 | 2 | | dog | 0 | 1 | 1 | 2 | | mat | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | | log | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | | anim | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | ### Step 4: Compute IDF for Each Term Using the standard formula $idf(t) = \log(N / df(t))$ with $N = 3$: $$ idf(\text{cat}) = \log(3/2) = \log(1.5) \approx 0.1761 $$ $$ idf(\text{dog}) = \log(3/2) = \log(1.5) \approx 0.1761 $$ $$ idf(\text{mat}) = \log(3/1) = \log(3) \approx 0.4771 $$ $$ idf(\text{log}) = \log(3/1) = \log(3) \approx 0.4771 $$ $$ idf(\text{anim}) = \log(3/1) = \log(3) \approx 0.4771 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** Terms appearing in only 1 document (mat, log, anim) have **higher IDF** — they are more discriminative and thus more valuable for retrieval. ### Step 5: Compute Normalized Term Frequency $tf(t,d)$ Using $tf(t,d) = f_{t,d} / \max_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}$. For $D_1$ (max count = 1 for all): $$ tf(\text{cat}, D_1) = 1/1 = 1.000 $$ $$ tf(\text{mat}, D_1) = 1/1 = 1.000 $$ ### Step 6: Compute Final TF-IDF Weights Using $w(t,d) = tf(t,d) \cdot idf(t)$: $$ w(\text{cat}, D_1) = 1.000 \times 0.1761 = 0.1761 $$ $$ w(\text{mat}, D_1) = 1.000 \times 0.4771 = 0.4771 $$ $$ w(\text{dog}, D_1) = 0 \times 0.1761 = 0.000 $$ The full document vector for $D_1$ is: $$ \vec{V}(D_1) = [0.1761,\ 0.0000,\ 0.4771,\ 0.0000,\ 0.0000] $$ ### Step 7: Cosine Similarity Between $D_1$ and $D_2$ Document vectors (computed similarly): $$ \vec{V}(D_1) = [0.1761,\ 0,\ 0.4771,\ 0,\ 0] $$ $$ \vec{V}(D_2) = [0,\ 0.1761,\ 0,\ 0.4771,\ 0] $$ Dot product: $$ \vec{V}(D_1) \cdot \vec{V}(D_2) = (0.1761)(0) + (0)(0.1761) + (0.4771)(0) + (0)(0.4771) + (0)(0) = 0 $$ Norms: $$ \vert \vec{V}(D_1) \vert = \sqrt{0.1761^2 + 0.4771^2} = \sqrt{0.0310 + 0.2276} = \sqrt{0.2586} \approx 0.5085 $$ $$ \vert \vec{V}(D_2) \vert = \sqrt{0.1761^2 + 0.4771^2} \approx 0.5085 $$ Cosine similarity: $$ \text{sim}(D_1, D_2) = \dfrac{0}{0.5085 \times 0.5085} = 0 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** $D_1$ and $D_2$ share **zero unique content terms** (only stop words overlap) — they are orthogonal in the vector space, hence similarity is 0. The IR system would rank them as completely unrelated. ## 3.2 Derivation: Why IDF Uses the Logarithm Starting from the raw inverse ratio $N / df(t)$: $$ \text{Raw IDF} = \dfrac{N}{df(t)} $$ The logarithm is applied for three critical reasons: 1. **Dampening effect:** $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ documents and $df = 1$ gives raw IDF = 1,000,000 — this dominates the TF-IDF score. Taking $\log$ brings it down to 6. 2. **Multiplicative search structure:** $\log(A \cdot B) = \log A + \log B$ enables efficient inverted-index scoring via term additions. 3. **Information-theoretic justification:** $\log(N/df)$ is monotonically related to **self-information** (Kullback-Leibler style retrieval). Final smoothed form (used in modern systems like Lucene/Sklearn): $$ idf(t) = \log\!\left( \dfrac{N}{1 + df(t)} \right) + 1 $$ The `+1` in the denominator prevents division by zero when $df(t) = 0$ (for unseen query terms); the outer `+1` ensures all IDF values are strictly positive. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Preprocessing Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Document D1] --> B[Tokenization] B --> C[Normalization
Case Folding + Punctuation] C --> D[Stop Word Removal] D --> E[Stemming or Lemmatization] E --> F{Optional Stage} F -->|Yes| G[N-gram Generation] F -->|No| H[Cleaned Token List] G --> H H --> I[Term Frequency tf t d] I --> J[Inverse Document Frequency idf t] J --> K[TF-IDF Weight Matrix] K --> L[Inverted Index Construction] L --> M[Ready for Search Queries] ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The flow moves **top-down** from raw text to a queryable index. - Each stage **shrinks** the data representation. - The diamond `{Optional Stage}` represents a branch decision (N-grams are used in some IR systems, skipped in others). ## 4.2 Document-Term Matrix (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Corpus[Document Corpus] D1[Document 1] D2[Document 2] D3[Document 3] end Corpus --> Matrix[Document Term Matrix W] Matrix --> Q[Query Vector q] Q --> Sim[Cosine Similarity Engine] Matrix --> Sim Sim --> Rank[Ranked Result List] ``` **Interpretation:** This block-level architecture shows how documents and queries live in the **same vector space**, enabling direct geometric comparison via cosine similarity. ## 4.3 Preprocessing Stage Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Stage] --> B[Stemming Porter] A --> C[Lemmatization WordNet] A --> D[Stop Word Removal] A --> E[Lowercasing] B --> F[Speed: Fast] B --> G[Accuracy: Moderate] C --> H[Speed: Slow] C --> I[Accuracy: High] D --> J[Index Size Reduction: 30 to 50 percent] E --> K[Recall Improvement] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key takeaway for diagrams:** Examiners often award 1–2 marks for "explaining a diagram" — be ready to label each block and explain its purpose. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]` **Define the following with one example each:** (a) Tokenization (b) Stop word (c) Stemming **Model Answer:** **(a) Tokenization:** Tokenization is the process of splitting a continuous text stream into discrete units called tokens, typically based on whitespace and punctuation boundaries. *Example:* `"Information Retrieval is fun!"` → `[Information]`, `[Retrieval]`, `[is]`, `[fun]`. **(b) Stop word:** A stop word is a high-frequency word (such as `the`, `is`, `of`, `a`, `an`) that carries little semantic value and is filtered out before indexing. *Example:* In the phrase `"the cat is on the mat"`, the words *the*, *is*, and *on* are stop words. **(c) Stemming:** Stemming is a heuristic rule-based process that reduces inflected words to their root form (stem) by chopping off suffixes. *Example:* `connection`, `connected`, `connecting` all reduce to `connect`. The most common algorithm is the **Porter Stemmer**. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]` **Explain the term "Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF)." Why is it used instead of raw term frequency in IR systems?** **Model Answer:** TF-IDF is a numerical statistic that reflects the **importance of a term to a document within a corpus**. It is computed as: $$ w(t,d) = tf(t,d) \cdot idf(t) $$ where $tf(t,d)$ is the term frequency of term $t$ in document $d$, and $idf(t) = \log(N/df(t))$ is the inverse document frequency. **Why TF-IDF over raw TF:** - Raw TF favors common words (e.g., *the*, *is*) that appear in almost every document — these have **no discriminative power**. - IDF **down-weights** terms appearing in many documents and **up-weights** rare terms. - This improves **precision** in retrieval, because matching a rare term is a stronger signal of relevance. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Rule:** Answer **ONE** of the two choices. Each sub-part is **7 marks**. --- ### Choice 1 — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]` **(a)** With a suitable example, explain the Vector Space Model (VSM) of Information Retrieval. Define TF, IDF, and TF-IDF. (7 marks) **(b)** Given the following corpus of 4 documents, compute the **TF-IDF vector** for each document. Use log base 10. (7 marks) $$ D_1 = \text{"data science and machine learning"} $$ $$ D_2 = \text{"machine learning and deep learning"} $$ $$ D_3 = \text{"data mining and data science"} $$ $$ D_4 = \text{"deep learning and neural networks"} $$ **Model Solution for (a):** The **Vector Space Model (VSM)** represents each document $d$ and each query $q$ as a vector in $\mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$ space, where $V$ is the vocabulary of unique terms in the corpus. **Definitions:** - **Term Frequency (TF):** $tf(t,d) = $ number of times term $t$ appears in document $d$. Normalized: $tf(t,d) = f_{t,d} / \max f_{t',d}$. - **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF):** $idf(t) = \log(N / df(t))$, where $N$ = total documents and $df(t)$ = number of documents containing $t$. - **TF-IDF:** $w(t,d) = tf(t,d) \cdot idf(t)$ — combines local importance (TF) with global rarity (IDF). **Example:** If $tf(\text{cat}, D_1) = 3$ and $idf(\text{cat}) = 1.5$, then $w(\text{cat}, D_1) = 4.5$. **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1: Tokenize and normalize** (lowercase, remove punctuation). Stop words `and` are removed. Vocabulary: $$ V = \{ \text{data, scienc, machin, learn, deep, min, neural, network} \} $$ **Step 2: Term Frequency Table (after Porter stemming)** | Term | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | $df$ | |------|-------|-------|-------|-------|------| | data | 1 | 0 | 2 | 0 | 2 | | scienc | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 2 | | machin | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 2 | | learn | 1 | 2 | 0 | 1 | 3 | | deep | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 2 | | min | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | | neural | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | | network | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | Total documents: $N = 4$. **Step 3: Compute IDF** (using $idf = \log(N/df)$): $$ idf(\text{data}) = \log(4/2) = \log(2) \approx 0.301 $$ $$ idf(\text{scienc}) = \log(4/2) = 0.301 $$ $$ idf(\text{machin}) = \log(4/2) = 0.301 $$ $$ idf(\text{learn}) = \log(4/3) = \log(1.333) \approx 0.125 $$ $$ idf(\text{deep}) = \log(4/2) = 0.301 $$ $$ idf(\text{min}) = \log(4/1) = \log(4) \approx 0.602 $$ $$ idf(\text{neural}) = \log(4/1) \approx 0.602 $$ $$ idf(\text{network}) = \log(4/1) \approx 0.602 $$ **Step 4: Final TF-IDF Vectors** $$ \vec{V}(D_1) = [0.301,\ 0.301,\ 0.301,\ 0.125,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0] $$ $$ \vec{V}(D_2) = [0,\ 0,\ 0.301,\ 0.250,\ 0.301,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0] $$ $$ \vec{V}(D_3) = [0.602,\ 0.301,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0.602,\ 0,\ 0] $$ $$ \vec{V}(D_4) = [0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0.125,\ 0.301,\ 0,\ 0.602,\ 0.602] $$ (Each vector has 8 components, one per vocabulary term.) --- ### Choice 2 — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]` **(a)** Explain the various steps involved in text preprocessing for Information Retrieval systems. Discuss stemming vs lemmatization with examples. (7 marks) **(b)** For the two documents below, compute **cosine similarity** using TF-IDF weighting. (7 marks) $$ D_1 = \text{"apple banana apple"} $$ $$ D_2 = \text{"apple orange banana"} $$ **Model Solution for (a):** The six preprocessing steps in a typical IR pipeline are: 1. **Tokenization:** Split text into words (e.g., `"Hello world!"` → `[Hello, world]`). 2. **Normalization:** Convert to lowercase; remove punctuation and special characters. 3. **Stop Word Removal:** Filter high-frequency, low-meaning words (a, the, is, of). 4. **Stemming:** Reduce inflected forms to a common root using suffix-stripping rules. 5. **Lemmatization:** Use dictionary-based morphological analysis to return words to lemma. 6. **N-gram Generation (optional):** Extract multi-word phrases (bigrams, trigrams). **Stemming vs Lemmatization:** | Feature | Stemming | Lemmatization | |---------|----------|---------------| | Method | Rule-based chopping | Dictionary + morphological analysis | | Speed | Fast | Slower | | Output | May not be a valid word | Always a valid dictionary word | | Example | `studying` → `studi` | `studying` → `study` | | Example | `better` → `better` (unchanged) | `better` → `good` | | Algorithm | Porter Stemmer | WordNet Lemmatizer | **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1: Vocabulary and DF** Vocabulary $V = \{ \text{apple, banana, orange} \}$, so $\vert V \vert = 3$. Total documents $N = 2$. Document frequencies: - $df(\text{apple}) = 2$ (in both) - $df(\text{banana}) = 2$ (in both) - $df(\text{orange}) = 1$ (only in $D_2$) **Step 2: Compute IDF** $$ idf(\text{apple}) = \log(2/2) = 0 $$ $$ idf(\text{banana}) = \log(2/2) = 0 $$ $$ idf(\text{orange}) = \log(2/1) = \log(2) \approx 0.301 $$ **Step 3: Term Frequencies** $D_1 = $ [apple: 2, banana: 1, orange: 0] — max = 2 $D_2 = $ [apple: 1, banana: 1, orange: 1] — max = 1 Normalized TF: - $D_1$: apple = 1.0, banana = 0.5, orange = 0 - $D_2$: apple = 1.0, banana = 1.0, orange = 1.0 **Step 4: TF-IDF Vectors** $$ \vec{V}(D_1) = [1.0 \times 0,\ 0.5 \times 0,\ 0 \times 0.301] = [0,\ 0,\ 0] $$ $$ \vec{V}(D_2) = [1.0 \times 0,\ 1.0 \times 0,\ 1.0 \times 0.301] = [0,\ 0,\ 0.301] $$ **Step 5: Cosine Similarity** $$ \vec{V}(D_1) \cdot \vec{V}(D_2) = 0 $$ Since $\vec{V}(D_1)$ is the zero vector, the similarity is conventionally defined as **0**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Pitfall Alert:** When IDF = 0 (a term appears in *all* documents), that term contributes nothing to the TF-IDF vector. This is called **stop-word behavior of TF-IDF** and is a common exam trap. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks in this topic:** > 1. **Forgetting to apply stemming BEFORE computing TF-IDF** — you must first normalize the vocabulary, then compute frequencies. > 2. **Using raw counts instead of normalized TF** — examiners explicitly check for normalization. > 3. **Mixing up $df$ and $tf$** — $df$ is across the corpus, $tf$ is within a single document. > 4. **Not specifying log base** — always state `log base 10` or `log base e`; if unspecified, KTU expects base 10 by default. > 5. **Skipping the IDF=0 edge case** in cosine similarity — this gives a zero vector; you must explain. > 6. **Confusing Porter Stemmer (1980) with Lancaster Stemmer (1990)** — Porter is the default taught answer. > 7. **Writing the formula `w = tf × idf` but computing one of them wrong** — partial credit may be lost. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Document Representation** converts text → mathematical vector. - [x] **Preprocessing Pipeline:** Tokenize → Normalize → Stop-word removal → Stem/Lemma → (N-grams). - [x] **Boolean Model** = binary, no ranking. - [x] **Vector Space Model** = weighted vectors, supports ranking via cosine similarity. - [x] **TF = $f_{t,d}$** (raw count); **IDF = $\log(N/df)$**; **TF-IDF = TF × IDF**. - [x] **IDF = 0** when a term appears in every document (no discriminative power). - [x] **Stemming** is fast but crude; **Lemmatization** is slow but accurate. - [x] **Porter Stemmer (1980)** is the most-cited algorithm in KTU exams. - [x] **Cosine similarity** ranges from 0 to 1; 0 = orthogonal (unrelated); 1 = identical. - [x] **Stop words** reduce index size by 30–50% in English. - [x] **N-grams** capture phrase semantics but increase dimensionality. - [x] **Document-term matrix** has rows = documents, columns = terms. - [x] Always **state units / log base** when writing numerical answers. - [x] The **Mermaid pipeline diagram** (Tokenize → Normalize → Stop → Stem → TF-IDF → Index) is a favorite sketch question. - [x] For **zero IDF**, the term is essentially a stop word and contributes nothing to similarity. ---

topics.title: Tokenization, Stemming, and Lemmatization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tokenization, Stemming, and Lemmatization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Tokenization — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Tokenization** is the foundational preprocessing step in Information Retrieval (IR) and Natural Language Processing (NLP) where a raw character stream (a document or query) is segmented into smaller discrete units called **tokens**. These tokens may be words, sub-words, phrases, or symbols, and they form the basic indexing units of an inverted index.

Mathematically, a tokenizer is a function $T$ that maps a string $s$ into a sequence of tokens:

$$T : s \mapsto (t_1, t_2, t_3, \dots, t_n)$$

where each $t_i$ is an atomic unit drawn from a vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$, and the sequence of $n$ tokens represents the lexical decomposition of the source string $s$.
