**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a chef preparing a recipe. Before the ingredients (meat, vegetables, spices) can be cooked, they must first be separated, cleaned, and chopped into usable pieces. **Tokenization is the "chopping block" of text processing** — it slices a continuous stream of characters (the raw document) into manageable linguistic units (tokens) that downstream algorithms can actually digest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The quality of tokenization directly influences **recall and precision** of the entire IR system. Poor tokenization (e.g., splitting "U.S.A." into "U" and "S" and "A") causes the indexer to miss valid retrieval matches.

---

### 1.2 Stemming — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Stemming** is a crude, rule-based heuristic process that reduces inflected (or sometimes derived) words to their **word stem** (root form) by chopping off suffixes and prefixes, often without regard to the linguistic validity of the resulting stem.

The stemming function can be expressed as:

$$S : w \mapsto \sigma$$

where $w$ is the original surface word and $\sigma$ is the stem. The output $\sigma$ need **not** be a valid dictionary word; it is simply a canonical morphological root.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of stemming as a gardener using hedge clippers — it indiscriminately chops off the ends of words to make them shorter. "Running" becomes "runn", "connection" becomes "connect", and "studies" becomes "studi". The result is rough, sometimes ungrammatical, but useful for matching morphological variants of the same concept.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The most widely used algorithm is the **Porter Stemmer (1980)**, which applies a set of suffix-stripping rules in **5 sequential phases**.

---

### 1.3 Lemmatization — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Lemmatization** is a sophisticated, vocabulary-and-morphology-aware process that reduces a word to its **lemma** (canonical dictionary form) by considering the word's context, part-of-speech (POS), and morphological structure. The output is always a valid word found in a dictionary.

Formally, the lemmatization function considers the lexical category:

$$L : (w, \text{POS}) \mapsto \ell$$

where $\ell$ is the lemma (e.g., "better" with POS=adjective $\to$ "good"; "running" with POS=verb $\to$ "run").

**Intuitive Analogy:** Lemmatization is like a **linguistics professor** who, when given the word "better", does not just chop the suffix — instead consults a dictionary, recognizes it as the irregular comparative form of "good", and returns the proper dictionary form. It is the "intelligent" version of stemming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Lemmatization typically requires a **lexical resource** (such as **WordNet** for English) and **morphological analysis** to disambiguate the word's role in context.

---

### 1.4 Why These Three Steps Matter in IR

These three operations form the **text normalization pipeline** that bridges the gap between how humans write and how machines index/search:

| Stage | Input | Output | Purpose |
|-------|-------|--------|---------|
| Tokenization | Raw character stream | List of tokens | Establish indexing units |
| Stopword Removal | Token list | Filtered token list | Remove noise |
| Stemming / Lemmatization | Surface words | Root forms | Group morphological variants |

> [!NOTE]
> Without these normalization steps, a query for "**fishing**" would fail to match a document containing "**fished**", "**fisher**", or "**fishes**" — a critical failure in any search engine.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Tokenization Techniques

#### 2.1.1 Whitespace Tokenization
The simplest possible tokenizer. It splits the input string on any whitespace character (spaces, tabs, newlines, carriage returns).

- **Rule:** $T(s) = \text{split}(s, \text{regex} = \backslash s+)$
- **Advantages:** Extremely fast, O(n) time complexity, zero memory overhead.
- **Disadvantages:** Fails on punctuation (`"hello,world!"` becomes `["hello,world!"]`), contractions (`"don't"` stays as one token), and multi-word expressions.

#### 2.1.2 Regular Expression (Regex) Tokenization
Uses pattern-matching rules to handle complex linguistic boundaries. The NLTK `RegexpTokenizer` and spaCy's tokenizer are prime examples.

- **Rule:** Tokens are matches of the form `[A-Za-z]+` for words, with optional handling of digits, hyphenated terms, and abbreviations.
- **Advantages:** Highly customizable; can preserve email addresses, URLs, or hyphenated words (`"state-of-the-art"`).
- **Disadvantages:** Requires expert-crafted rules; brittle to new linguistic phenomena.

#### 2.1.3 Sentence Tokenization (Sentence Segmentation)
Breaks a document into sentences before word-level tokenization. Uses punctuation marks (`.`, `?`, `!`) combined with capitalization heuristics.

- **Algorithm:** Maximum entropy classifier or rule-based (e.g., NLTK's `PunktSentenceTokenizer`).
- **Challenge:** Disambiguating periods in abbreviations like `"Dr."`, `"U.S.A."`, `"etc."`.

#### 2.1.4 Subword Tokenization
Modern NLP uses subword units to handle the **out-of-vocabulary (OOV)** problem. Key algorithms include:

- **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)** — Sennrich et al. (2016): Iteratively merges the most frequent character pairs.
- **WordPiece** — Used in BERT: Greedy longest-match-first against a learned vocabulary.
- **Unigram Language Model** — Kudo (2018): Probabilistic subword segmentation.

> [!NOTE]
> Subword tokenization is the backbone of modern transformer-based IR systems (e.g., ColBERT, SPLADE) and is critical for handling multilingual and rare-term vocabularies.

---

### 2.2 The Porter Stemming Algorithm (1980)

The Porter Stemmer is the canonical reference algorithm. It operates in **5 phases** of word-length-dependent suffix removal rules, applied sequentially.

#### Phase Structure
Each rule has the form:
**(condition) suffix $\to$ replacement**

The condition is typically of the form: `(m > 0)` where $m$ is the **measure** of the word stem — the number of consonant-vowel (VC) sequences.

**Measure Formula:**

$$m(w) = \text{count of } (VC) \text{ pairs in stem of } w$$

Examples: `TR(EE) = 0`, `TR(OUBLE) = 1`, `TR(EES) = 1`, `TR(OUBLES) = 2`, `TR(OUBLY) = 2`.

#### The Five Phases

1. **Phase 1a — Plural suffixes:** Handles `-sses`, `-ies`, `-ss`, `-s`.
2. **Phase 1b — Past tense / gerund:** Handles `-eed`, `-ed`, `-ing`, `-edly`, `-ingly`.
3. **Phase 1c — Y to I:** Replaces trailing `-y` with `-i` if preceded by a non-vowel.
4. **Phase 2 — Suffix removal (m > 0):** Strips suffixes like `-ational`, `-tional`, `-enci`, `-anci`, `-izer`, `-ization`, etc.
5. **Phase 3 — Suffix removal (m > 0):** Strips suffixes like `-icate`, `-ative`, `-alize`, `-iciti`, `-ical`, `-ful`, `-ness`.
6. **Phase 4 — Long suffix removal (m > 1):** Removes suffixes like `-al`, `-ance`, `-ence`, `-er`, `-ic`, etc.
7. **Phase 5a — Final 'e' removal (m > 1).**
8. **Phase 5b — Double-letter simplification (m > 1 and ends in double letter).**

---

### 2.3 Lemmatization Methodology

Lemmatization requires two key resources:

1. **Lexicon (Dictionary):** A mapping from surface forms to lemmas. The most famous is **WordNet** (Princeton), containing ~155,000 words indexed by synset.
2. **Morphological Analyzer:** A function that produces the lemma given a surface form and POS tag.

**Algorithm Flow:**

$$w \xrightarrow{\text{POS tagger}} (w, \text{POS}) \xrightarrow{\text{morphological lookup}} \ell$$

**Example Lemmatization Output (using spaCy / WordNet):**

| Surface Form | POS | Lemma |
|:-------------|:----|:------|
| running | verb (VBG) | run |
| ran | verb (VBD) | run |
| better | adjective (JJR) | good |
| best | adjective (JJS) | good |
| mice | noun (NNS) | mouse |
| studies | noun (NNS) | study |
| studying | verb (VBG) | study |

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Formula | Engineering Use |
|:--------|:--------------------|:---------------|
| Tokenization | $T(s) = (t_1, \dots, t_n)$ | Index construction, query parsing |
| Vocabulary | $\mathcal{V} = \{t \mid t \text{ appears in corpus}\}$ | Defines search space |
| Type / Token Ratio (TTR) | $\text{TTR} = \dfrac{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert}{\sum_{d} \vert d \vert}$ | Lexical richness measure |
| Porter Measure | $m = \vert \{VC \text{ pairs in stem}\} \vert$ | Rule condition in stemming |
| Stemming | $S(w) = \sigma$ (may be non-word) | Fast, aggressive normalization |
| Lemmatization | $L(w, \text{POS}) = \ell$ (always a word) | Linguistically faithful normalization |
| Inverted Index Key | $k = \sigma$ or $k = \ell$ (after normalization) | Retrieval matching |
| Zipf's Law | $f \cdot r \approx C$ (constant) | Vocabulary distribution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exams, always state the **difference** between stemming and lemmatization explicitly. Examiners award marks for: (a) linguistic accuracy claim, (b) example demonstration, (c) computational cost comparison, (d) impact on IR recall vs. precision.

---

### 2.5 Engineering Trade-off Analysis

| Dimension | Tokenization | Stemming | Lemmatization |
|:----------|:-------------|:---------|:--------------|
| **Speed** | O(n) | O(n) | O(n log n) due to lookup |
| **Accuracy** | Medium | Low (crude) | High (linguistic) |
| **Output validity** | Always valid tokens | Often non-words | Always valid words |
| **POS awareness** | No | No | Yes |
| **Resource needs** | Minimal | Rule table | Lexicon + tagger |
| **IR impact** | Defines index units | Increases recall | Increases precision |
| **OOV handling** | Subword = good | n/a | Requires lexicon entry |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Porter Stemming of "CONNECTIONS"

We will apply the Porter Stemmer step-by-step to the word **"connections"** (11 letters).

**Initial Word:** `CONNECTIONS`

The Porter algorithm uses a representation like:

$$[C](VC)^m[V]$$

where `[C]` is an optional initial consonant sequence, `(VC)^m` is $m$ repetitions of vowel-consonant pairs, and `[V]` is an optional final vowel sequence.

**Step 1 — Compute the stem of the word:**
- Strip affixes: stem of "connections" is "connect".
- Stem of "connect" is `CVCVC` (Co-n-ne-ct).
- Measure $m$ of "connect": we have `(V-C)(V-C)` = $m = 2$.

**Step 2 — Apply Phase 1a rules:**

The word "connections" ends in `-ions`. Looking at Phase 1a rules:
- Rule: `(m>0) SSES -> SS` — does not match.
- Rule: `(m>0) IES -> I` — does not match.
- Rule: `(m>0) SS -> SS` — does not match.
- Rule: `(m>0) S ->` — matches! "connections" ends in `-s`, and the stem "connection" has $m > 0$ (specifically $m=2$).

**After Phase 1a:** `CONNECTION`

**Step 3 — Apply Phase 1b rules:**
- No match for `-eed`, `-ed`, `-ing`, or their variants.
- No change.

**After Phase 1b:** `CONNECTION`

**Step 4 — Apply Phase 1c rules:**
- No trailing `-y`. No change.

**Step 5 — Apply Phase 2 rules:**

Looking at suffix `-ION` with measure check. The rule `(m>0) ATIONAL -> ATE` does not apply; we have `CONNECTION` ending in `-ION`. Rule `(m>0) IZATION -> IZE` does not apply. Rule `(m>0) ATION -> ATE` does not match. The rule `(m>0) TION -> TION` is one of the Porter rules — it specifically preserves `-tion`.

**After Phase 2:** `CONNECTION` (unchanged)

**Step 6 — Apply Phase 3 rules:**

Rule `(m>0) ICAL -> IC` does not apply. Rule `(m>0) FUL -> ` does not apply.

**After Phase 3:** `CONNECTION`

**Step 7 — Apply Phase 4 rules:**

The stem is `CONNECT`, $m = 2 > 1$. Suffix `-ION` matches rule `(m>1) ION -> ` (one of the long-suffix removal rules). Specifically, the rule `(m>1) ION -> ` removes the `-ion` suffix when $m > 1$.

**After Phase 4:** `CONNECT`

**Step 8 — Apply Phase 5a:**

Rule `(m>1) E -> ` does not apply since the word is now `CONNECT` with no trailing `E`.

**Final Stem: `CONNECT`**

> [!NOTE]
> The Porter stemmer correctly groups **connect, connects, connected, connecting, connection, connections** all to the same stem `CONNECT`, dramatically improving IR recall.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Lemmatization of "STUDIES" and "BETTER"

#### Sub-example A: "STUDIES"

**Step 1 — POS Tagging:** The context is needed. Assume the sentence *"The studies show that..."* — "studies" is a plural noun.

**Step 2 — Morphological Rule Lookup:** The WordNet lemmatizer looks up `"studies"` with POS = `n` (noun).

**Step 3 — Rule Application:** The rule `NNS (plural noun) -> SS or singular` applies. Stripping `-ies` and adding `-y`:

$$\text{studies} \xrightarrow{\text{NNS rule}} \text{study}$$

**Final Lemma: `study`**

#### Sub-example B: "BETTER"

**Step 1 — POS Tagging:** In *"This is a better solution"*, "better" is a comparative adjective (JJR).

**Step 2 — Morphological Rule Lookup:** The lemmatizer searches WordNet for `"better"`.

**Step 3 — Disambiguation:** WordNet's morphological exception dictionary flags `"better"` as the irregular comparative of `"good"`.

**Step 4 — Rule Application:**

$$\text{better} \xrightarrow{\text{JJR irregular mapping}} \text{good}$$

**Final Lemma: `good`**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **stemmer** would produce `"better"` → `"better"` (no change) or `"bett"` (crude chop), failing to recognize the irregular morphology. A **lemmatizer** correctly returns `"good"`, which is the *true* canonical form for IR matching.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Tokenization of a Complex Sentence

**Input Text:** `"Dr. Smith's state-of-the-art AI system, developed in the U.S.A., processes 1,000+ queries/sec!"`

#### Naive Whitespace Tokenizer Output (BROKEN):

$$T_{\text{ws}}(s) = [\text{"Dr."}, \text{"Smith's"}, \text{"state-of-the-art"}, \text{"AI"}, \text{"system,"}, \dots]$$

Problem: `"Smith's"` retains the apostrophe; `"system,"` retains the comma; `"1,000+"` is one non-standard token.

#### Regex Tokenizer Output (NLTK-style):

$$T_{\text{regex}}(s) = [\text{"Dr"}, \text{"."}, \text{"Smith"}, \text{"'s"}, \text{"state"}, \text{"-"}, \text{"of"}, \text{"-"}, \text{"the"}, \text{"-"}, \text{"art"}, \text{"AI"}, \text{"system"}, \text{","}, \text{"developed"}, \dots]$$

#### Sentence Tokenizer Output (Punkt):

$$S(s) = [\text{"Dr. Smith's state-of-the-art AI system, developed in the U.S.A., processes 1,000+ queries/sec!"}]$$

Only one sentence detected — Punkt correctly identifies `"Dr."` and `"U.S.A."` as abbreviations and does not split.

#### Recommended Pipeline (spaCy-style):

$$T_{\text{spaCy}}(s) = [\text{"Dr."}, \text{"Smith's"}, \text{"state-of-the-art"}, \text{"AI"}, \text{"system"}, \text{","}, \text{"developed"}, \text{"in"}, \text{"the"}, \text{"U.S.A."}, \text{","}, \text{"processes"}, \text{"1,000"}, \text{"+"}, \text{"queries"}, \text{"/"}, \text{"sec"}, \text{"!"}]$$

This tokenizer:
- Preserves multi-word entities (`"state-of-the-art"`, `"U.S.A."`).
- Splits off punctuation as separate tokens.
- Detects the numeric `"1,000"` and the operator `"+"`.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Stemming vs. Lemmatization

| Word | Stemmer (Porter) Output | Lemmatizer Output | Observation |
|:-----|:------------------------|:------------------|:------------|
| studies | studi | study | Both correct |
| studying | studi | study | Both correct |
| connection | connect | connection | Stemmer strips noun `-ion` |
| better | better | good | Lemmatizer handles irregular form |
| ran | ran | run | Lemmatizer handles irregular past tense |
| mice | mice | mouse | Lemmatizer handles irregular plural |
| easily | easili | easily | Stemmer introduces non-word |
| argument | argument | argument | Both preserve |
| universal | univers | universal | Stemmer too aggressive |
| fishing | fish | fishing | Stemmer strips `-ing` (loses tense) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above matrix is a **favorite KTU 14-mark question** pattern. Students are expected to compute outputs for both stemmer and lemmatizer and explain the *qualitative difference in IR behavior*.

---

### 3.5 Quantitative Impact on IR

Let $D$ be a document collection and $Q$ a query set. Define the **vocabulary compression ratio** as:

$$\rho_{\text{compression}} = \frac{\vert \mathcal{V}_{\text{original}} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{V}_{\text{normalized}} \vert}$$

- For a typical English news corpus (e.g., **Reuters-21578** with 10,000 unique types), Porter stemming yields $\rho \approx 1.4$, while lemmatization yields $\rho \approx 1.3$.
- This $\approx 30\%$ reduction in vocabulary size directly reduces inverted index storage by the same factor.
- However, **recall** improves more with aggressive stemming (over-grouping of related forms), while **precision** improves more with lemmatization (preserves word boundaries).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The IR Text Normalization Pipeline (Top-Level Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawDoc[Raw Document Stream] --> t1[Tokenization Module]
    t1 --> t2[Stopword Removal Filter]
    t2 --> t3[Normalization Stage]
    t3 --> t4[Porter Stemmer Engine]
    t3 --> t5[Lemmatization Engine]
    t4 --> idx[Inverted Index Builder]
    t5 --> idx
    idx --> out[Searchable Index]

    classDef proc fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class t1,t2,t3,t4,t5,idx proc
    class rawDoc,out io
```

### 4.2 Tokenization Sub-Pipeline (Detailed View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputChar[Input Character Stream] --> sentSeg[Sentence Segmenter]
    sentSeg --> regexT[Regex Word Tokenizer]
    regexT --> normT[Normalization Layer]
    normT --> subwT[Subword Tokenizer BPE or WordPiece]
    subwT --> finalTok[Final Token Stream]
    normT --> oovCheck{OOV Term Detected}
    oovCheck -->|Yes| subwordSplit[Subword Decomposition]
    oovCheck -->|No| finalTok

    classDef stage fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef check fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    class inputChar,sentSeg,regexT,normT,subwT,finalTok,subwordSplit stage
    class oovCheck check
```

### 4.3 Stemming vs. Lemmatization Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Input Word w] --> posCheck{POS Tag Available?}
    posCheck -->|No| stemPath[Apply Porter Stemmer]
    posCheck -->|Yes| lemmaPath[Consult WordNet + Morphology]
    stemPath --> stemOut[Output Stem sigma]
    lemmaPath --> lemmaOut[Output Lemma ell]
    stemOut --> irAppStem[Used When Speed Critical]
    lemmaOut --> irAppLemma[Used When Precision Critical]

    classDef io fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f9a825,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class start,stemOut,lemmaOut,irAppStem,irAppLemma io
    class posCheck decision
    class stemPath,lemmaPath process
```

### 4.4 Porter Stemmer Phase Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1a[Phase 1a: Plural Suffixes] --> p1b[Phase 1b: Past Tense and Gerund]
    p1b --> p1c[Phase 1c: Y to I Conversion]
    p1c --> p2[Phase 2: Suffix Removal m greater than 0]
    p2 --> p3[Phase 3: Suffix Removal m greater than 0]
    p3 --> p4[Phase 4: Long Suffix Removal m greater than 1]
    p4 --> p5a[Phase 5a: Final E Removal]
    p5a --> p5b[Phase 5b: Double Letter Simplification]
    p5b --> final[Final Stem Output]

    classDef phase fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef finalNode fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class p1a,p1b,p1c,p2,p3,p4,p5a,p5b phase
    class final finalNode
```

### 4.5 Architecture: Indexing Pipeline with Stemming/Lemmatization Module

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph docPrep[Document Preparation]
        corpus[Text Corpus] --> clean[Text Cleaning]
        clean --> tok[Tokenizer]
        tok --> filt[Stopword Filter]
    end
    subgraph norm[Normalization Layer]
        filt --> normChoice{Normalization Strategy}
        normChoice -->|Speed Priority| porter[Porter Stemmer]
        normChoice -->|Linguistic Quality| lemma[Lemmatizer with WordNet]
        normChoice -->|Hybrid| both[Porter plus Lemmatizer]
    end
    subgraph idxBuild[Index Construction]
        porter --> invIdx[Inverted Index]
        lemma --> invIdx
        both --> invIdx
    end
    invIdx --> storage[Disk Storage]

    classDef subgrp fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef stage fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff176,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    class docPrep,norm,idxBuild subgrp
    class corpus,clean,tok,filt,porter,lemma,both,invIdx,storage stage
    class normChoice decision
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams form a complete schematic of the **Module 1 IR preprocessing stack**. In KTU exams, students may be asked to draw or describe any one of these flows — practice labeling every block precisely.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between Stemming and Lemmatization with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Stemming | Lemmatization |
|:-------|:---------|:--------------|
| **Definition** | Crude heuristic suffix removal | Vocabulary + morphology-based reduction |
| **Output** | May be a non-word (e.g., *"troubl"*) | Always a valid word (e.g., *"trouble"*) |
| **POS awareness** | No | Yes |
| **Examples** | `studies` → `studi` | `studies` (noun) → `study` |
| | `better` → `better` (unchanged) | `better` (adjective) → `good` |
| **Speed** | Faster | Slower |
| **IR impact** | Higher recall, lower precision | Higher precision, slightly lower recall |

> **Allocation:** 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for example difference.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Sample]**
**Explain the need for tokenization in Information Retrieval. List two issues faced during tokenization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Tokenization is the first step in building an inverted index. It converts raw text into discrete units (tokens) that the IR system can index, match, and rank.

**Two major issues:**
1. **Punctuation handling** — How to split `"U.S.A."` (one entity) vs `"end. Start"` (sentence boundary)?
2. **Hyphenated words** — Should `"state-of-the-art"` be one token, three tokens, or kept as-is?
3. **Contractions** — `"don't"` → `"don"` + `"t"` or `"do"` + `"not"`?
4. **Multi-word expressions** — `"New York"`, `"machine learning"` as single vs. multiple tokens?

> **Allocation:** 1 mark for need, 1 mark each for two issues (2 marks).

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the various steps in tokenization with examples. Discuss the challenges in tokenizing multilingual text. (7 marks)**

#### OR

#### **Q3(b). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Describe the Porter Stemming Algorithm in detail. Apply the Porter Stemmer to the words "CONNECTIONALLY", "STUDIES", and "FISHING", showing each phase. (7 marks)**

---

### 🔹 Model Solution for Q3(a) — Tokenization (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define Tokenization (1 mark):**

Tokenization is the process of splitting a character stream into a sequence of tokens. It is formally defined as:

$$T : s \rightarrow (t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n)$$

**Step 2 — Steps in Tokenization (3 marks):**

1. **Sentence Segmentation:** Split the document at sentence boundaries (`.`, `?`, `!`) using rules to avoid splitting abbreviations.
2. **Word Tokenization:** Within each sentence, split on whitespace and punctuation.
3. **Normalization:** Lowercasing, removing diacritics, expanding contractions.
4. **Subword Splitting (optional):** For OOV handling using BPE/WordPiece.

**Example:**

$$\text{"The dogs are running."} \rightarrow [\text{"the"}, \text{"dogs"}, \text{"are"}, \text{"running"}, \text{"."}]$$

**Step 3 — Multilingual Challenges (3 marks):**

- **Script variation** — Arabic, Devanagari, CJK have no whitespace; tokenization requires language-specific models (e.g., **Jieba** for Chinese).
- **Agglutinative languages** — Turkish, Finnish: a single word encodes a full sentence; tokenization is non-trivial.
- **Code-mixed text** — Indian English mixing Hindi/Tamil: tokenizer must handle multiple scripts in one sentence.
- **Diacritics and tone marks** — Vietnamese has tone marks that change word meaning.
- **Right-to-left scripts** — Arabic/Hebrew need bidirectional processing.

> **Allocation:** 1 mark definition, 3 marks steps, 3 marks challenges.

---

### 🔹 Model Solution for Q3(b) — Porter Stemmer Worked Example (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Algorithm Description (2 marks):**

The Porter Stemmer uses suffix-stripping rules applied in **5 phases**, each with a measure condition $m > k$ where $m$ counts VC pairs in the stem.

**Step 2 — Stem "CONNECTIONALLY" (2 marks):**

- Phase 1a: No change.
- Phase 1b: No change.
- Phase 1c: No trailing `-y`.
- Phase 2: `(m>0) ATIONALLY -> ATE` does not match. Rule `(m>0) TIONAL -> TION` does not match. No change.
- Phase 3: `(m>0) IONALLY -> ` matches (m=3 > 0). Stem becomes `CONNECTION`.
- Phase 4: `(m>1) ION -> ` matches (m=2 > 1). Stem becomes `CONNECT`.
- Phase 5: No change.
- **Final: `CONNECT`**

**Step 3 — Stem "STUDIES" (1.5 marks):**

- Phase 1a: `(m>0) IES -> I` matches. Word becomes `STUDI`.
- Subsequent phases: no applicable rules.
- **Final: `STUDI`**

**Step 4 — Stem "FISHING" (1.5 marks):**

- Phase 1a: No change.
- Phase 1b: `(m>0) ING -> ` matches. Word becomes `FISH`.
- Subsequent phases: no change.
- **Final: `FISH`**

> **Allocation:** 2 marks algorithm + 5 marks worked examples (proportional).

---

#### **Q4(a). [KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Sample]**
**Explain Lemmatization in detail. Compare it with Stemming in terms of accuracy, speed, and IR effectiveness. (7 marks)**

#### OR

#### **Q4(b). [KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Sample]**
**Describe the complete text preprocessing pipeline for an Information Retrieval system. Include tokenization, stopword removal, and normalization stages with suitable diagrams. (7 marks)**

---

### 🔹 Model Solution for Q4(a) — Lemmatization vs. Stemming (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define Lemmatization (2 marks):**

Lemmatization reduces a word to its dictionary form (**lemma**) using a lexicon (e.g., WordNet) and morphological analysis with the word's part-of-speech.

**Step 2 — Working Example (2 marks):**

| Word | POS | Lemma |
|:-----|:----|:------|
| better | adjective | good |
| mice | noun | mouse |
| ran | verb | run |
| studies | noun | study |

**Step 3 — Comparative Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Stemming | Lemmatization |
|:----------|:---------|:--------------|
| Accuracy | Lower (non-words possible) | Higher (always valid) |
| Speed | Fast (rule-based) | Slower (lookup + POS) |
| POS awareness | None | Required |
| Resource needs | Rule table | WordNet + tagger |
| IR recall | Higher (over-grouping) | Moderate |
| IR precision | Lower | Higher |
| Use case | Large-scale web IR | Question answering, semantic search |

---

### 🔹 Model Solution for Q4(b) — Full Preprocessing Pipeline (7 marks)

**The pipeline in 5 stages:**

1. **Text Cleaning** — Remove HTML tags, special characters, encoding fixes.
2. **Tokenization** — Split into words/sentences using regex/sentence segmenter.
3. **Stopword Removal** — Filter common words (the, is, at, which) using a stopword list.
4. **Normalization** — Lowercasing, stemming or lemmatization.
5. **Index Construction** — Build the inverted index from normalized tokens.

**(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram for the visual representation.)**

> **Allocation:** 1 mark per stage (5 marks) + 2 marks for diagram/description.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
> 1. **Confusing stemming with lemmatization outputs** — Many students write `"better" → "better"` for both, losing 2-3 marks. Always show the lemmatizer's irregular-form handling.
> 2. **Skipping the Porter phases** — Listing Porter's 5 phases without showing *applied steps* on a sample word costs heavy deductions. Always show the *transformation*, not just the rule names.
> 3. **Forgetting POS context in lemmatization** — `"running"` as a noun (e.g., *The running of the bulls*) does **not** become `"run"`; only as a verb does. Mention POS-dependence explicitly.
> 4. **Missing the IR impact angle** — KTU 2024 scheme expects **outcome-based answers**. Always tie your answer to *recall/precision/index-size effects* on the IR system.
> 5. **Spelling "lemmatization" as "lemmatisation" or "lematization"** — Examiners deduct marks for spelling in 2-mark definition questions.
> 6. **Not mentioning WordNet** — When discussing lemmatization, always cite WordNet as the underlying lexical resource.
> 7. **Confusing tokenization with parsing** — Tokenization is *lexical*; parsing is *syntactic*. KTU questions sometimes intentionally test this.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical things to remember for KTU 2024 exam:**

- ✅ **Tokenization** = splitting text into tokens; first step of IR indexing; output is a sequence of tokens $(t_1, \dots, t_n)$.
- ✅ **Stemming** = rule-based suffix chopping; output may be non-words; fast; classic algorithm = **Porter Stemmer (5 phases)**.
- ✅ **Lemmatization** = dictionary-based reduction; output always valid words; requires **POS tag + WordNet**.
- ✅ Porter Stemmer has **5 phases** with measure condition $m > k$.
- ✅ Porter Stemmer measure $m$ = number of VC pairs in the stem.
- ✅ Stemming improves **recall**; lemmatization improves **precision**.
- ✅ Stemmer is **faster**; lemmatizer is **more accurate**.
- ✅ Example: `studies` → Porter: `studi`; Lemmatizer: `study`.
- ✅ Example: `better` → Porter: `better`; Lemmatizer: `good` (irregular form).
- ✅ Subword tokenization (BPE, WordPiece) handles OOV in modern IR/NLP.
- ✅ Multilingual tokenization faces script, morphology, and bidirectional challenges.
- ✅ Always mention **WordNet** when describing lemmatization.
- ✅ Vocabulary compression ratio $\rho = \dfrac{\vert \mathcal{V}_{\text{original}} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{V}_{\text{normalized}} \vert}$ quantifies normalization effect.
- ✅ In KTU answers, always conclude with a **recall/precision statement** linking the technique to IR performance.
- ✅ Use diagrams (Section 4) wherever a 7-mark question asks for "describe the pipeline".

> **Module 1 Mastery Tip:** KTU examiners frequently combine these three concepts in a single 14-mark question. Practice writing integrated answers that flow: *Document → Tokenize → Normalize (Stem or Lemmatize) → Index → Search*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverted Index and Boolean Queries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inverted Index and Boolean Queries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Inverted Index – Formal Definition

An **Inverted Index** is the central data structure used by most Information Retrieval (IR) systems. It is a mapping from **index terms (vocabulary words)** to the **set of documents** in which they appear, along with auxiliary information such as term frequency and positional data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> An inverted index is a word-oriented mechanism for indexing a text collection to speed up the search task. For each term $t$ in the vocabulary, a **postings list** $P(t)$ is maintained that records the documents in which $t$ occurs, optionally enriched with term frequencies and term positions.

Formally, for a corpus $C$ containing documents $\{d_1, d_2, \dots, d_N\}$:

$$
\text{InvertedIndex}(t) \;\mapsto\; P(t) = \{ (d_i, f_{t,i}), (d_j, f_{t,j}), \dots \}
$$

where $f_{t,i}$ is the frequency of term $t$ in document $d_i$.

### Conceptual Analogy – The Library Card Catalogue

Imagine walking into a large public library containing **millions of books** (our *document collection*). You want every book mentioning the word *"Retrieval"*. A *forward index* would be each book listing its own contents — useless, because you'd still have to crack open every book. Instead, the librarian hands you the **Card Catalogue**: a drawer organized alphabetically by term, and each term-card lists *every book containing that term* along with shelf numbers. **That card catalogue IS the inverted index.** The vocabulary (term list) is the *alphabetical drawer*, and the postings list is the *list of book-shelf numbers on each card*.

## 1.2 Boolean Queries – Formal Definition

A **Boolean Query** is a query expression composed of terms connected by the logical operators **AND ($\land$)**, **OR ($\lor$)**, and **NOT ($\lnot$)**. The system retrieves all documents that satisfy the truth value of the expression.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Boolean Operators (KTU 2024 Module 1):**
> - **AND** – retrieves documents containing *all* specified terms (intersection).
> - **OR** – retrieves documents containing *any* of the specified terms (union).
> - **NOT** – retrieves documents containing the first term but excluding the second (set difference).
> - **Precedence**: NOT $>$ AND $>$ OR, with parentheses overriding.

### Real-World Analogy – A Filter Cocktail

Think of a Boolean query as a coffee filter. A query like `coffee AND sugar AND NOT milk` returns only the cups that have **coffee, sugar, but no milk** — each clause is a sieve, and the documents must pass through *all* of them. The query engine, in turn, evaluates these by **set operations on postings lists** (intersection for AND, union for OR, difference for NOT).

## 1.3 Why Inverted Index + Boolean Queries Matter (Engineering Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Significance:**
> Inverted indexes are the **backbone of modern search engines** — Google, Bing, Elasticsearch, Apache Solr, Lucene — and are the standard answer to sub-linear retrieval over billions of documents. Boolean retrieval, although the *simplest* model, remains the foundation upon which **ranked retrieval (TF-IDF, BM25)** and **semantic search (vector indexes)** are built.

**Standard performance metrics in Boolean IR systems:**

- **Time complexity** for query evaluation: $O(\sum |P(t_i)|)$ in the worst case, but optimized to $O(\min(|P(t_1)|, |P(t_2)|))$ using merge-based intersection.
- **Space complexity** of postings: $O(\text{total term occurrences})$ ≈ **inverted index size is roughly 10–30% of the raw collection size** for typical English corpora.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Inverted Index

An inverted index is composed of two cooperating components:

1. **The Vocabulary (Dictionary):** A sorted list of all unique index terms $T = \{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_M\}$ extracted from the corpus.
2. **The Postings List:** For each $t_i \in T$, a list $P(t_i)$ of document IDs (and optionally positions) where $t_i$ occurs.

### 2.1.1 The Two Storage Variants

| Variant | Description | Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Inverted Index** | Stores only document IDs where the term appears. | Memory-efficient; supports Boolean retrieval and basic ranking. |
| **Full (Positional) Inverted Index** | Stores `<docID, term-frequency, [positions]>` tuples. | Required for **phrase queries** and **proximity queries**. |

### 2.1.2 Index Term Granularity Choices

- **Word-level** indexing (most common; default choice for KTU problems).
- **N-gram** indexing (handles morphological variants and misspelt queries).
- **Stem/lemma** indexing (reduces vocabulary via stemming, e.g., Porter's algorithm).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Inverted Index Construction

The construction pipeline in any production-grade IR system follows these stages:

1. **Document Acquisition** → Gather all $N$ documents $\{d_1, d_2, \dots, d_N\}$.
2. **Tokenization** → Split each document into a stream of words (tokens).
3. **Linguistic Pre-processing** → Lowercasing, stop-word removal, stemming/lemmatization.
4. **Term Assignment** → Decide the indexable terms (the **vocabulary** $T$).
5. **Inversion** → For each term $t$, collect and sort all `(docID, frequency[, positions])` tuples into a postings list.
6. **Storage Optimization** → Apply variable-byte, gamma, or **gap-encoding** compression to postings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (Why 'Inverted'?):**
> The name arises because it **inverts the relationship** between documents and terms. A *forward index* maps *document → terms it contains*, while an *inverted index* maps *term → documents containing it*. The latter is more efficient for retrieval because the user begins with a *term* (the query) and wants the *documents*.

## 2.3 Boolean Query Processing Logic

A Boolean query $Q$ is evaluated against an inverted index using the following set operations on postings lists:

### Boolean Operator Set Operations

For two postings lists $P(t_1)$ and $P(t_2)$:

- **AND**: $P(t_1) \land P(t_2) = P(t_1) \cap P(t_2)$
- **OR**: $P(t_1) \lor P(t_2) = P(t_1) \cup P(t_2)$
- **AND-NOT**: $P(t_1) \land \lnot P(t_2) = P(t_1) \setminus P(t_2)$

### Query Optimization Heuristic (Crucial for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process postings lists in order of increasing length.** A query `t1 AND t2 AND t3` should be evaluated as `((P(t1) ∩ P(t_shortest)) ∩ P(t3))` — this minimizes the intermediate set sizes and hence the total work, achieving near-optimal cost $O(\min_i \mid P(t_i)\mid)$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Units / Type |
|---|---|---|
| $f_{t,d}$ | Frequency of term $t$ in document $d$ | Integer count |
| $df_t$ | Document frequency of $t$ = $\mid\{d : f_{t,d} > 0\}\mid$ | Integer count |
| $tf_t$ | Collection frequency = total occurrences of $t$ in corpus | Integer count |
| $N$ | Total number of documents in collection | Integer |
| $\mid P(t)\mid$ | Length of postings list of term $t$ = $df_t$ | Integer |
| $idf_t = \log_2 \dfrac{N}{df_t}$ | Inverse document frequency (for ranked extension) | Real, non-negative |
| $w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \cdot idf_t$ | TF-IDF weight of term $t$ in $d$ | Real |
| $S_{\text{inv}} \approx 0.10 \text{ to } 0.30 \cdot S_{\text{raw}}$ | Empirical size of inverted index as fraction of raw corpus | Fraction |
| $T_{\text{AND}} = O\!\left(\min_i \mid P(t_i)\mid\right)$ | Optimal AND query time (after sorting by postings length) | Asymptotic |
| $T_{\text{naive-AND}} = O\!\left(\sum_i \mid P(t_i)\mid\right)$ | Naive AND query time | Asymptotic |
| $C_{\text{gap}} = \sum_{i=2}^{k} (d_i - d_{i-1})$ | Total size after gap-encoding a postings list | Integer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used to size the **RAM/CPU budget** of a search engine, decide **index sharding** strategies, and tune **query latency** SLAs. For example, Elasticsearch's `refresh_interval` and Lucene's `IndexWriter` memory consumption are derived from the gap-encoded postings formulas above.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example – Building an Inverted Index from Scratch

Consider the following toy document collection (after tokenization, lowercasing, and stop-word removal):

| DocID | Document Text (processed) |
|---|---|
| $d_1$ | `retrieval model boolean` |
| $d_2$ | `boolean retrieval query` |
| $d_3$ | `vector model retrieval` |
| $d_4$ | `boolean query model` |

### Step 1 — Vocabulary Construction

Collect all unique terms and sort them lexicographically:

$$
T = \{ \text{boolean}, \text{model}, \text{query}, \text{retrieval}, \text{vector} \}
$$

So $M = \vert T \vert = 5$ unique terms.

### Step 2 — Term Frequency Table $f_{t,d}$

| Term $\backslash$ Doc | $d_1$ | $d_2$ | $d_3$ | $d_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **boolean** | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| **model**   | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| **query**   | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| **retrieval** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| **vector**  | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |

### Step 3 — Document Frequency $df_t$

Sum each row of the table:

$$
\begin{aligned}
df_{\text{boolean}}   &= 1 + 1 + 0 + 1 = 3 \\
df_{\text{model}}     &= 1 + 0 + 1 + 1 = 3 \\
df_{\text{query}}     &= 0 + 1 + 0 + 1 = 2 \\
df_{\text{retrieval}} &= 1 + 1 + 1 + 0 = 3 \\
df_{\text{vector}}    &= 0 + 0 + 1 + 0 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Build the Postings Lists

Sort each list in ascending document-ID order (already sorted here):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{boolean})   &= \{d_1, d_2, d_4\} \quad\;\; (df=3) \\
P(\text{model})     &= \{d_1, d_3, d_4\} \quad\;\; (df=3) \\
P(\text{query})     &= \{d_2, d_4\} \quad\;\;\; (df=2) \\
P(\text{retrieval}) &= \{d_1, d_2, d_3\} \quad\;\;(df=3) \\
P(\text{vector})    &= \{d_3\} \quad\;\;\;\; (df=1)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Positional (Full) Inverted Index (for phrase queries)

Recording positions in each document:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{boolean})   &= \{ (d_1, 3), (d_2, 1), (d_4, 1) \} \\
P(\text{model})     &= \{ (d_1, 2), (d_3, 2), (d_4, 3) \} \\
P(\text{query})     &= \{ (d_2, 3), (d_4, 2) \} \\
P(\text{retrieval}) &= \{ (d_1, 1), (d_2, 2), (d_3, 1) \} \\
P(\text{vector})    &= \{ (d_3, 1) \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation Note:** The position count is the 1-based offset of the term in the *processed* token stream, NOT in the raw text. This is critical for phrase query matching (see §3.3).

## 3.2 Boolean Query Evaluation – Worked Walkthrough

**Query:** `retrieval AND boolean AND NOT vector`

### Step A — Retrieve the Three Postings Lists

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{retrieval}) &= \{d_1, d_2, d_3\} \\
P(\text{boolean})   &= \{d_1, d_2, d_4\} \\
P(\text{vector})    &= \{d_3\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step B — Optimize by Processing Shortest First

> [!NOTE]
> Apply the **shortest-first** heuristic: start with the list having the smallest cardinality.

$$
|P(\text{vector})| = 1 < |P(\text{query})|=2 < |P(\text{boolean})|=|P(\text{retrieval})|=3
$$

Process: `retrieval AND boolean` first, then subtract `vector`.

### Step C — Compute Intersection of `retrieval AND boolean`

Sort both lists ascending; traverse with two pointers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{retrieval}) \cap P(\text{boolean}) &= \{d_1, d_2, d_3\} \cap \{d_1, d_2, d_4\} \\
&= \{d_1, d_2\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Detailed pointer walk-through:

| Step | $p_1$ (retrieval) | $p_2$ (boolean) | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_1$ | $d_1$ | $p_1 = p_2$ → add $d_1$ | $\{d_1\}$ |
| 2 | $d_2$ | $d_2$ | $p_1 = p_2$ → add $d_2$ | $\{d_1, d_2\}$ |
| 3 | $d_3$ | $d_4$ | $p_1 < p_2$ → advance $p_1$ | $\{d_1, d_2\}$ |
| 4 | end | — | stop | $\{d_1, d_2\}$ |

### Step D — Apply NOT vector (Set Difference)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{d_1, d_2\} \setminus \{d_3\} &= \{d_1, d_2\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step E — Final Result

$$
\boxed{\; \text{retrieval AND boolean AND NOT vector} \;\mapsto\; \{d_1, d_2\}\;}
$$

## 3.3 Phrase Query Evaluation (Using Positional Index)

**Query:** `"boolean retrieval"` (exact phrase, terms adjacent)

The algorithm requires the two terms to occur at positions $p$ and $p+1$ in the same document. Generalizing, for a phrase of $k$ terms, we need $k-1$ consecutive positional matches.

### Step 1 — Get positional postings for both terms

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{boolean})   &= \{ (d_1, 3), (d_2, 1), (d_4, 1) \} \\
P(\text{retrieval}) &= \{ (d_1, 1), (d_2, 2), (d_3, 1) \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — For each common document, check positional adjacency

- **$d_1$:** boolean at 3, retrieval at 1. Need 3 = 1+1? **No (3 ≠ 2)**. No match.
- **$d_2$:** boolean at 1, retrieval at 2. Need 1 = 2−1? **Yes (1 = 1)**. ✓ Match.
- **$d_4$:** retrieval does not appear in $d_4$. Skip.

### Step 3 — Final result

$$
\boxed{\; \text{"boolean retrieval"} \;\mapsto\; \{d_2\}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalized Phrase Query Complexity:**
> For a phrase of $k$ terms, the worst-case cost is $O(\text{TT}_k)$ where $\text{TT}_k$ is the total number of term-positions across all involved postings lists. This is why **positional indexes are 3–5× larger** than non-positional ones — a KTU-favorite trade-off question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Inverted Index System

The diagram below shows the full pipeline — from raw documents to query evaluation — and the role of Boolean query processing within the retrieval engine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Document Collection D1 to DN] --> B[Tokenizer and Linguistic Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Vocabulary Builder Unique Terms]
    C --> D[Postings List Builder DocID Freq Positions]
    D --> E[Inverted Index Storage Gap Encoded]
    E --> Q[User Boolean Query]
    Q --> R[Query Parser AND OR NOT Tree]
    R --> S[Query Optimizer Shortest Postings First]
    S --> T[Postings Traversal Engine Intersection and Union and Difference]
    T --> U[Result Set of Matching DocIDs]
    E --> T
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Boolean Query Evaluation via Postings Traversal

This diagram illustrates the *shortest-first* query optimization strategy for an `AND` query of three terms.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Pt1["P of t1 length 5000"] --> Opt[Query Optimizer Sort Ascending]
    Pt2["P of t2 length 80"] --> Opt
    Pt3["P of t3 length 1200"] --> Opt
    Opt --> Step1["Step 1 Intersect P of t2 and P of t3 length 70"]
    Step1 --> Step2["Step 2 Intersect result with P of t1 length 60"]
    Step2 --> Result[Final DocID Set for AND Query]
```

## 4.3 Architecture Flow — Inverted Index Construction Pipeline

A more granular view emphasizing the in-memory data structures used during construction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[Documents] --> Tok[Tokenize]
    Tok --> Norm[Normalize Case Stopwords Stem]
    Norm --> Stream[Token Stream]
    Stream --> Sort1[Local Sort by Term then DocID]
    Sort1 --> Merge[Global Merge Across All Documents]
    Merge --> Dict[Dictionary Term to Offset Map]
    Merge --> Post[Postings File Gaps and Frequencies]
    Dict --> Disk[Disk Resident Inverted Index]
    Post --> Disk
    Disk --> Engine[Query Engine]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Notes (for KTU Exam Drawing Questions):**
> 1. The **dictionary** is small and is usually kept fully in RAM (for binary-search lookups in $O(\log M)$).
> 2. The **postings** are large and reside on disk; only the active lists during a query are paged in.
> 3. The **query optimizer** consults the dictionary's $df_t$ values (already stored alongside each term) to decide the **shortest-first** traversal order — this is a **frequently asked KTU question**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define an **inverted index**. List its two main components and explain the role of each in a Boolean retrieval system.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** An inverted index is a data structure that maps each unique term in a document collection to a list (postings list) of documents containing that term.
- **Component 1 – Vocabulary / Dictionary (1 mark):** A sorted list of all unique index terms; enables $O(\log M)$ lookup of a term.
- **Component 2 – Postings List (1 mark):** For each term, a list of `(docID, term-frequency[, positions])` entries; used to evaluate Boolean set operations (AND/OR/NOT) at query time.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** With an example, explain how a **NOT** operator is processed using an inverted index when evaluating a Boolean query.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The NOT operator is evaluated as a **set difference** between postings lists.
- Example: Query `retrieval AND NOT vector` over the worked corpus.
- $P(\text{retrieval}) = \{d_1, d_2, d_3\}$, $P(\text{vector}) = \{d_3\}$.
- Result: $\{d_1, d_2, d_3\} \setminus \{d_3\} = \{d_1, d_2\}$.
- (1 mark) Definition of NOT as set difference; (1 mark) example; (1 mark) final set computation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Construct the **inverted index** for the following four documents. Show the vocabulary, term frequency matrix, document frequencies, and the final postings lists. Assume stop-words and case have already been normalized. **(7 marks)**

| DocID | Document Text |
|---|---|
| $d_1$ | `information retrieval system` |
| $d_2$ | `retrieval of information` |
| $d_3$ | `system and information` |
| $d_4$ | `database information retrieval` |

**(b)** Using the index built in part (a), evaluate the following Boolean queries. Show the postings traversal for each: **(7 marks)**

1. `information AND retrieval`
2. `retrieval AND NOT system`
3. `database OR system`

---

#### Model Solution – Question A

##### Part (a) — Inverted Index Construction (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Vocabulary (1 mark):**

$$
T = \{ \text{and}, \text{database}, \text{information}, \text{of}, \text{retrieval}, \text{system} \}
$$

**Step 2 — Term Frequency Matrix (2 marks):**

| Term $\backslash$ Doc | $d_1$ | $d_2$ | $d_3$ | $d_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **and** | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| **database** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| **information** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| **of** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| **retrieval** | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| **system** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |

**Step 3 — Document Frequencies (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
df_{\text{and}} &= 1, \quad df_{\text{database}} = 1, \quad df_{\text{information}} = 4, \\
df_{\text{of}} &= 1, \quad df_{\text{retrieval}} = 3, \quad df_{\text{system}} = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Postings Lists (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{and})        &= \{d_3\} \\
P(\text{database})   &= \{d_4\} \\
P(\text{information})&= \{d_1, d_2, d_3, d_4\} \\
P(\text{of})         &= \{d_2\} \\
P(\text{retrieval})  &= \{d_1, d_2, d_4\} \\
P(\text{system})     &= \{d_1, d_3\}
\end{aligned}
$$

##### Part (b) — Boolean Query Evaluation (7 marks)

**Query 1:** `information AND retrieval` **(2 marks)**

$$
P(\text{information}) \cap P(\text{retrieval}) = \{d_1, d_2, d_3, d_4\} \cap \{d_1, d_2, d_4\} = \{d_1, d_2, d_4\}
$$

**Query 2:** `retrieval AND NOT system` **(3 marks)**

- Apply shortest-first: $|P(\text{system})|=2 < |P(\text{retrieval})|=3$.
- Step 1 — $P(\text{retrieval}) \setminus P(\text{system}) = \{d_1, d_2, d_4\} \setminus \{d_1, d_3\}$.
- Pointer walk:

| Step | $p$ (retrieval) | $q$ (system) | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_1$ | $d_1$ | equal → discard $d_1$ | $\{\}$ |
| 2 | $d_2$ | $d_3$ | $p<q$ → keep $d_2$, advance $p$ | $\{d_2\}$ |
| 3 | $d_4$ | $d_3$ | $p>q$ → advance $q$ | $\{d_2\}$ |
| 4 | $d_4$ | end | keep $d_4$ | $\{d_2, d_4\}$ |

- Result: $\boxed{\{d_2, d_4\}}$

**Query 3:** `database OR system` **(2 marks)**

$$
P(\text{database}) \cup P(\text{system}) = \{d_4\} \cup \{d_1, d_3\} = \{d_1, d_3, d_4\}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> 1. **Forgetting to sort** postings lists ascending by `docID` → full 1-mark deduction per list. *(Most common mistake.)*
> 2. Confusing **AND** (intersection) with **OR** (union) — the *set operation symbol itself* must be written; examiners award 1 mark for explicit $\cap$ / $\cup$ notation.
> 3. Skipping the **shortest-first** justification in part (b) → loses the "optimization" mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**(a)** Differentiate between a **forward index** and an **inverted index** with a neat diagram. Why does an IR system prefer the inverted index for query processing? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given a positional inverted index where $P(\text{world}) = \{(d_5, 4), (d_8, 1), (d_8, 6)\}$ and $P(\text{cup}) = \{(d_5, 2), (d_5, 5), (d_8, 2)\}$, evaluate the phrase query **"world cup"** and list the matching documents. Show the positional adjacency check. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution – Question B

##### Part (a) — Forward vs Inverted Index (7 marks)

**Forward Index (2 marks):** Maps each document → list of terms it contains.

| Document | Terms |
|---|---|
| $d_1$ | information, retrieval, system |
| $d_2$ | retrieval, of, information |
| $d_3$ | system, and, information |
| $d_4$ | database, information, retrieval |

**Inverted Index (2 marks):** Maps each term → list of documents containing it (see postings lists from Question A above).

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Forward Index | Inverted Index |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Document → Terms | Term → Documents |
| Lookup | $O(\vert d_i \vert)$ scan | $O(\log M)$ dictionary + $O(df_t)$ scan |
| Best for | Document similarity, classification | Query-driven retrieval |
| Storage | $O(\sum \vert d_i \vert)$ | $O(\text{total term occurrences})$ |

**Why Inverted Index is Preferred (2 marks):**
1. Query processing starts with a **term**, not a document — so the term-to-documents mapping is the natural access path.
2. Avoids the cost of scanning all $N$ documents at query time.
3. Enables efficient **set operations** for Boolean queries (intersection, union, difference).
4. Supports **sub-linear retrieval** by exploiting posting-list length statistics.

##### Part (b) — Phrase Query `"world cup"` (7 marks)

**Positional data:**

- $P(\text{world}) = \{ (d_5, 4),\; (d_8, 1),\; (d_8, 6) \}$
- $P(\text{cup})   = \{ (d_5, 2),\; (d_5, 5),\; (d_8, 2) \}$

**Algorithm:** For a phrase of two terms, term2 must appear at position $p_2 = p_1 + 1$ where $p_1$ is a position of term1 in the same document.

**Check $d_5$ (3 marks):**

| world positions in $d_5$ | cup positions in $d_5$ | Is $p_{\text{cup}} = p_{\text{world}} + 1$? |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | 2, 5 | $4+1 = 5$ — YES (cup at 5) ✓ |

Match: $d_5$ contains `"world cup"` at positions 4–5.

**Check $d_8$ (3 marks):**

| world positions in $d_8$ | cup positions in $d_8$ | Is $p_{\text{cup}} = p_{\text{world}} + 1$? |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | $1+1 = 2$ — YES (cup at 2) ✓ |
| 6 | 2 | $6+1 = 7$ — NO ✗ |

Match: $d_8$ contains `"world cup"` at positions 1–2.

**Final Result (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{\; \text{"world cup"} \;\mapsto\; \{d_5, d_8\}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> 1. **Off-by-one error** in positional adjacency — examiners are strict; you must write "term2 at position $p_1+1$" explicitly, not just "next to".
> 2. Forgetting that a document may match the phrase **multiple times** (e.g., $d_8$ has two `"world"`s but only one valid adjacency with `"cup"`) — list all *distinct* matching documents.
> 3. **Not stating the time complexity** of phrase query evaluation ($O(TT_2)$) — a frequently tested 1-mark addition.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Inverted Index & Boolean Queries**

- ✅ **Inverted Index = Vocabulary + Postings Lists.** Vocabulary stores unique terms; postings store `(docID, freq[, positions])`.
- ✅ **Inverted index size** is approximately **10–30%** of the raw collection size.
- ✅ **Boolean operators** map to **set operations**: AND → ∩, OR → ∪, NOT → ∖.
- ✅ **Query optimization** rule: **Process postings in order of increasing length** (shortest-first). Complexity drops from $O(\sum \vert P(t_i)\vert)$ to $O(\min_i \vert P(t_i)\vert)$.
- ✅ **Document frequency** $df_t$ = number of documents containing $t$. Stored alongside each term in the dictionary.
- ✅ **Collection frequency** $cf_t$ = total number of occurrences of $t$ (sum of $f_{t,d}$).
- ✅ **Positional (full) inverted index** is required for **phrase queries** and **proximity queries**; size is **3–5× larger** than non-positional.
- ✅ **Phrase query matching:** term2 must be at position $p_1+1$ in the same document as term1.
- ✅ **Gap encoding** reduces postings storage by storing differences $d_i - d_{i-1}$ instead of raw docIDs.
- ✅ **Precedence** in Boolean queries: **NOT** $>$ **AND** $>$ **OR**; parentheses override.
- ✅ **Standard performance metric:** $T_{\text{intersect}} = O(m+n)$ for two lists of sizes $m$ and $n$ via two-pointer merge (lists must be sorted).
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** confusing **AND-NOT** (difference) with **NOT-AND** (which by De Morgan's law equals the union of complements — never use this form in IR; always rewrite as $A \setminus B$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Term Frequency and Document Frequency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Term Frequency and Document Frequency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Term Frequency (TF)

**Formal Definition:** Term Frequency (tf) of a term `t` in a document `d` is defined as the number of times the term `t` occurs in the document `d`. It quantifies how *important* a term is *within a specific document*.

$$tf(t, d) = f_{t,d}$$

where $f_{t,d}$ is the raw frequency count of term `t` in document `d`.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A high `tf` value indicates that the term `t` is locally frequent in document `d`. However, raw frequency is biased toward longer documents, which is why **normalization schemes** (Section 2) are mandatory in KTU numerical problems.

### 1.2 Document Frequency (DF)

**Formal Definition:** Document Frequency (df) of a term `t` is the number of documents in the collection `D` that contain the term `t` at least once.

$$df(t) = \vert \{ d \in D : t \in d \} \vert$$

where $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes the cardinality (count) of the set of documents.

### 1.3 Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)

**Formal Definition:** Inverse Document Frequency (idf) measures how *rare* or *informative* a term is across the **entire collection** `D`. It is the inverse of document frequency, typically with a logarithmic dampener.

$$idf(t, D) = \log \frac{N}{df(t)}$$

where `N` is the total number of documents in the corpus `D`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why IDF Exists:** Common words like *"the"*, *"is"*, *"a"* appear in almost every document and have **zero discriminative power**. IDF automatically down-weights these stopwords and amplifies rare, content-bearing terms.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **library of 1,000 books** where a librarian wants to recommend the best book on *"Quantum Computing"*.

- **Term Frequency (TF)** = How many times the phrase *"Quantum Computing"* appears *in a specific book*. A book that mentions it 50 times is probably more relevant than one that mentions it once.
- **Document Frequency (DF)** = How many *out of the 1,000 books* even mention *"Quantum Computing"*. If only 5 books mention it, the term is **rare and highly distinctive**.
- **IDF** = The librarian's intuition: *"Rare terms are more interesting than common terms."* The word *"the"* appears in all 1,000 books (DF=1000), so its IDF is near zero. But *"Quantum Computing"* with DF=5 has a very high IDF — it pinpoints the right shelf.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** *TF* = local relevance (within document); *IDF* = global rarity (across collection).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic Breakdown

The TF-IDF weighting scheme operates on a **two-stage philosophy**:

**Stage 1 — Local Relevance (Term Frequency):**
- Step 1: Tokenize the document into terms.
- Step 2: Count occurrences of each term `t` in document `d`.
- Step 3: Apply one of the normalization schemes (raw, log, augmented, double-normal) to remove length bias.

**Stage 2 — Global Rarity (Inverse Document Frequency):**
- Step 1: Iterate over the entire corpus `D` of `N` documents.
- Step 2: For each term `t`, count `df(t)` = number of documents containing `t`.
- Step 3: Compute $idf(t) = \log(N / df(t))$ to penalize ubiquitous terms.

**Stage 3 — Combined Weight (TF-IDF):**
- Multiply Stage 1 and Stage 2: $w(t,d) = tf(t,d) \times idf(t)$.
- The resulting weight is high only when a term is **frequent in a document** AND **rare across the corpus**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula Name** | **Equation** | **Use Case / When to Apply** |
|---|---|---|
| Raw Term Frequency | $tf(t,d) = f_{t,d}$ | Simplest count; biased toward long documents |
| Binary Frequency | $tf(t,d) = 1$ if $t \in d$, else $0$ | Boolean IR models |
| Log Normalization | $tf(t,d) = 1 + \log(f_{t,d})$ | Dampens high frequencies |
| Augmented Frequency | $tf(t,d) = 0.5 + 0.5 \cdot \frac{f_{t,d}}{\max_w f_{w,d}}$ | Preferred in KTU problems; bounds between 0.5 and 1.0 |
| Inverse Document Frequency | $idf(t) = \log \frac{N}{df(t)}$ | Standard textbook formulation |
| Smoothed IDF | $idf(t) = \log \frac{N}{1 + df(t)}$ | Prevents division by zero |
| TF-IDF Weight | $w(t,d) = tf(t,d) \times idf(t)$ | Final ranking score |
| Collection Frequency | $cf(t) = \sum_{d \in D} f_{t,d}$ | Total count in entire collection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always specify **which base of logarithm** is being used. The default is **base 2** in classical IR textbooks (Manning, Raghavan, Schütze) and **base e (natural log)** in modern ML literature. Default to **log base 10** unless the problem states otherwise.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** Use TF-IDF (and its successors like BM25) as foundational ranking signals.
- **Document Clustering & Classification:** TF-IDF vectors form the feature space for clustering algorithms like k-means.
- **Recommender Systems:** TF-IDF similarity (cosine of TF-IDF vectors) powers content-based recommenders.
- **Spam Filters:** Terms with abnormally high TF in spam corpora get high IDF-weighted scores as spam markers.
- **Plagiarism Detection:** Sentence-level TF-IDF cosine similarity flags paraphrased content.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical KTU Numerical Example

**Given Corpus (3 documents, tokenized):**

- **D1:** `cat dog cat mouse dog`
- **D2:** `dog mouse horse horse`
- **D3:** `cat mouse horse horse`

Total documents $N = 3$. Vocabulary $V = \{$ `cat`, `dog`, `mouse`, `horse` $\}$.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step TF Calculation (Raw Counts)

| Term $t$ | $tf(t, D1)$ | $tf(t, D2)$ | $tf(t, D3)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| `cat` | 2 | 0 | 1 |
| `dog` | 2 | 1 | 0 |
| `mouse` | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| `horse` | 0 | 2 | 2 |

**Derivation for `cat` in D1:**
The token list for D1 is [`cat`, `dog`, `cat`, `mouse`, `dog`]. Counting occurrences of `cat`: position 1 and position 3. Therefore:

$$tf(\text{cat}, D1) = f_{\text{cat}, D1} = 2$$

### 3.3 Step-by-Step DF & IDF Calculation

**Step 1 — Document Frequency:** Count documents containing each term.

- $df(\text{cat}) = 2$ (appears in D1 and D3)
- $df(\text{dog}) = 2$ (appears in D1 and D2)
- $df(\text{mouse}) = 3$ (appears in D1, D2, D3)
- $df(\text{horse}) = 2$ (appears in D2 and D3)

**Step 2 — Apply the IDF Formula** (using $\log_{10}$):

$$idf(t) = \log_{10}\left( \frac{N}{df(t)} \right)$$

**For `cat`:**

$$idf(\text{cat}) = \log_{10}\left( \frac{3}{2} \right) = \log_{10}(1.5)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(1.5) &= \log_{10}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \\
&= \log_{10}(3) - \log_{10}(2) \\
&= 0.4771 - 0.3010 \\
&= 0.1761
\end{aligned}
$$

**For `mouse`:**

$$idf(\text{mouse}) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{3}{3}\right) = \log_{10}(1) = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The term `mouse` appears in **all 3 documents**, so its IDF is **zero**. It carries zero discriminative power — a classic stopword-like behavior.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step TF-IDF Calculation

$$w(t, d) = tf(t, d) \times idf(t)$$

**For `cat` in D1:**

$$w(\text{cat}, D1) = tf(\text{cat}, D1) \times idf(\text{cat}) = 2 \times 0.1761 = 0.3522$$

**For `cat` in D3:**

$$w(\text{cat}, D3) = 1 \times 0.1761 = 0.1761$$

**For `horse` in D2:**

$$idf(\text{horse}) = \log_{10}\left( \frac{3}{2} \right) = 0.1761$$

$$w(\text{horse}, D2) = 2 \times 0.1761 = 0.3522$$

### 3.5 Complete TF-IDF Weight Matrix

| Term $t$ | $w(t, D1)$ | $w(t, D2)$ | $w(t, D3)$ | $idf(t)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `cat` | 0.3522 | 0.0000 | 0.1761 | 0.1761 |
| `dog` | 0.3522 | 0.1761 | 0.0000 | 0.1761 |
| `mouse` | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 |
| `horse` | 0.0000 | 0.3522 | 0.3522 | 0.1761 |

### 3.6 Analytical Interpretation of Results

- The term **`mouse`** receives **zero weight everywhere** because $idf(\text{mouse}) = 0$. It is a corpus-wide common term.
- The terms **`cat`, `dog`, `horse`** carry identical IDF scores because they each appear in exactly 2 of 3 documents. Their ranking within a document depends solely on local TF.
- In **D1**, the term `cat` has the highest TF (count=2) among discriminating terms, so it dominates the document's profile vector.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TF-IDF Computational Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Corpus D with N documents] --> B[Tokenization Stage]
    B --> C[Remove Stopwords and Punctuation]
    C --> D[Build Vocabulary V]
    D --> E[Stage 1: Compute tf t,d for each term]
    D --> F[Stage 2: Compute df t across corpus]
    F --> G[Compute idf t using log N by df t]
    E --> H[Multiply tf t,d with idf t]
    G --> H
    H --> I[TF-IDF Weight Matrix W]
    I --> J[Output: Ranked Document Scores or Similarity Vectors]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Relationship Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Document d] --> B[Term Frequency tf]
    C[Full Corpus D] --> D[Document Frequency df]
    D --> E[Inverse Document Frequency idf]
    B --> F[TF-IDF Weight w t,d]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Document Vector Space]
    G --> H[Ranking / Clustering / Classification]
```

### 4.3 Frequency Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Frequency Concepts] --> B[Collection Frequency cf t]
    A --> C[Document Frequency df t]
    A --> D[Term Frequency tf t,d]
    B --> B1[Total occurrences in entire corpus]
    C --> C1[Number of documents containing t]
    D --> D1[Occurrences of t in single document d]
    C --> E[Inverse Document Frequency idf]
    E --> E1[Log dampening of df]
    D --> F[TF-IDF Final Weight]
    E1 --> F
    F --> G[Ranking Signal for IR System]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The arrows indicate data flow direction. In the KTU examination, you may be asked to *draw* or *describe* such a pipeline. Always label each block with the input it consumes and the output it produces.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

Define Term Frequency and Document Frequency. How does Inverse Document Frequency differ from Document Frequency?

**Model Answer:**

- **Term Frequency (tf):** The number of times a term `t` occurs in a document `d`. It measures *local* importance within a document.
- **Document Frequency (df):** The number of documents in the collection `D` that contain the term `t`. It measures *corpus-level presence*.
- **Inverse Document Frequency (idf):** The logarithmic inverse of df, computed as $\log(N / df(t))$. Unlike df, which *increases* with commonness, idf *decreases* for common terms and *increases* for rare terms, making it suitable as a weighting factor.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

Why is raw term frequency often normalized? List any two normalization schemes.

**Model Answer:**

Raw term frequency is normalized to remove **document length bias**, since longer documents naturally contain higher term counts.

**Two normalization schemes:**

1. **Log Normalization:** $tf(t,d) = 1 + \log(f_{t,d})$ — dampens large counts.
2. **Augmented Frequency:** $tf(t,d) = 0.5 + 0.5 \cdot \frac{f_{t,d}}{\max_w f_{w,d}}$ — bounds values between 0.5 and 1.0 using the max term count in the document as the divisor.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to apply the **logarithm** when computing IDF. Students often write $\frac{N}{df(t)}$ without the $\log$, losing 2 marks immediately.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **df(t)** (number of *documents*) with **cf(t)** (total *occurrences* in corpus). Always re-read the question carefully.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not specifying the **base of logarithm**. KTU expects explicit mention (e.g., $\log_{10}$).
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Mixing up tf and TF-IDF in final answer tables. Always label columns with the correct formula.

---

#### **Question A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Given the following corpus of 3 documents:**

- **D1:** `information retrieval system`
- **D2:** `database information system`
- **D3:** `retrieval database system`

**(a)** Compute the Document Frequency (df) and Inverse Document Frequency (idf) for each unique term. Use $\log_{10}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Construct the complete TF-IDF weight matrix for all terms across all documents. Identify the term with the highest discriminative power. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Vocabulary Extraction:**
$V = \{$ `information`, `retrieval`, `system`, `database` $\}$, $N = 3$.

**Step 2 — Document Frequency Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
df(\text{information}) &= \vert\{D1, D2\}\vert = 2 \\
df(\text{retrieval}) &= \vert\{D1, D3\}\vert = 2 \\
df(\text{system}) &= \vert\{D1, D2, D3\}\vert = 3 \\
df(\text{database}) &= \vert\{D2, D3\}\vert = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — IDF Computation using $idf(t) = \log_{10}(N / df(t))$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
idf(\text{information}) &= \log_{10}(3/2) = 0.1761 \\
idf(\text{retrieval}) &= \log_{10}(3/2) = 0.1761 \\
idf(\text{system}) &= \log_{10}(3/3) = \log_{10}(1) = 0 \\
idf(\text{database}) &= \log_{10}(3/2) = 0.1761
\end{aligned}
$$

| Term | $df(t)$ | $idf(t)$ |
|---|---|---|
| `information` | 2 | 0.1761 |
| `retrieval` | 2 | 0.1761 |
| `system` | 3 | 0.0000 |
| `database` | 2 | 0.1761 |

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Raw TF Matrix:**

| Term | $tf(t,D1)$ | $tf(t,D2)$ | $tf(t,D3)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| `information` | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| `retrieval` | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| `system` | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| `database` | 0 | 1 | 1 |

**Step 2 — Multiply by IDF column-wise to get TF-IDF weights:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(\text{information}, D1) &= 1 \times 0.1761 = 0.1761 \\
w(\text{information}, D2) &= 1 \times 0.1761 = 0.1761 \\
w(\text{information}, D3) &= 0 \times 0.1761 = 0.0000 \\
w(\text{retrieval}, D1) &= 1 \times 0.1761 = 0.1761 \\
w(\text{retrieval}, D3) &= 1 \times 0.1761 = 0.1761 \\
w(\text{system}, \cdot) &= 1 \times 0 = 0 \text{ everywhere} \\
w(\text{database}, D2) &= 1 \times 0.1761 = 0.1761 \\
w(\text{database}, D3) &= 1 \times 0.1761 = 0.1761
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final TF-IDF Weight Matrix:**

| Term | $w(t,D1)$ | $w(t,D2)$ | $w(t,D3)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| `information` | 0.1761 | 0.1761 | 0.0000 |
| `retrieval` | 0.1761 | 0.0000 | 0.1761 |
| `system` | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 |
| `database` | 0.0000 | 0.1761 | 0.1761 |

**Conclusion:** The term `system` has **zero discriminative power** (idf = 0). The terms `information`, `retrieval`, and `database` carry **equal discriminative power** (idf = 0.1761), so any of them could be considered the highest-discrimination term among the non-zero-idf vocabulary.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice) — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Consider the corpus:**

- **D1:** `machine learning model`
- **D2:** `deep learning neural model`
- **D3:** `machine deep neural network`

**(a)** Compute the Augmented Term Frequency for each term in each document. Use the formula $tf_{aug}(t,d) = 0.5 + 0.5 \cdot \frac{f_{t,d}}{\max_w f_{w,d}}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the TF-IDF weight matrix using the augmented TF values and $\log_{10}$-based IDF. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Raw TF and Maximum Term Count per Document:**

| Document | Tokens | Max Raw $tf$ |
|---|---|---|
| D1 | `machine`, `learning`, `model` | 1 (all unique) |
| D2 | `deep`, `learning`, `neural`, `model` | 1 (all unique) |
| D3 | `machine`, `deep`, `neural`, `network` | 1 (all unique) |

Since every term appears at most once in each document, $f_{t,d} \in \{0, 1\}$.

**Step 2 — Apply Augmented Formula:**
For terms present in a document:

$$tf_{aug}(t,d) = 0.5 + 0.5 \cdot \frac{1}{1} = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0$$

For terms absent:

$$tf_{aug}(t,d) = 0.5 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 0.5$$

**Augmented TF Matrix:**

| Term | $tf_{aug}(t,D1)$ | $tf_{aug}(t,D2)$ | $tf_{aug}(t,D3)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| `machine` | 1.0 | 0.5 | 1.0 |
| `learning` | 1.0 | 1.0 | 0.5 |
| `model` | 1.0 | 1.0 | 0.5 |
| `deep` | 0.5 | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| `neural` | 0.5 | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| `network` | 0.5 | 0.5 | 1.0 |

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Vocabulary and Document Frequencies:**
$V = \{$ `machine`, `learning`, `model`, `deep`, `neural`, `network$ \}$, $N = 3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
df(\text{machine}) &= 2 \quad (D1, D3) \\
df(\text{learning}) &= 2 \quad (D1, D2) \\
df(\text{model}) &= 2 \quad (D1, D2) \\
df(\text{deep}) &= 2 \quad (D2, D3) \\
df(\text{neural}) &= 2 \quad (D2, D3) \\
df(\text{network}) &= 1 \quad (D3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — IDF Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
idf(\text{machine}) = idf(\text{learning}) = idf(\text{model}) = idf(\text{deep}) = idf(\text{neural}) &= \log_{10}(3/2) = 0.1761 \\
idf(\text{network}) &= \log_{10}(3/1) = \log_{10}(3) = 0.4771
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — TF-IDF Weights via $w(t,d) = tf_{aug}(t,d) \times idf(t)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(\text{network}, D3) &= 1.0 \times 0.4771 = 0.4771 \\
w(\text{machine}, D1) &= 1.0 \times 0.1761 = 0.1761 \\
w(\text{learning}, D1) &= 1.0 \times 0.1761 = 0.1761 \\
w(\text{deep}, D3) &= 1.0 \times 0.1761 = 0.1761
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final TF-IDF Matrix (excerpt showing highest weights):**

| Term | $w(t,D1)$ | $w(t,D2)$ | $w(t,D3)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| `network` | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | **0.4771** |
| `machine` | 0.1761 | 0.0000 | 0.1761 |
| `learning` | 0.1761 | 0.1761 | 0.0000 |
| `deep` | 0.0000 | 0.1761 | 0.1761 |
| `neural` | 0.0000 | 0.1761 | 0.1761 |
| `model` | 0.1761 | 0.1761 | 0.0000 |

**Conclusion:** The term `network` has the **highest discriminative power** because it appears in only 1 out of 3 documents, yielding the highest IDF score of 0.4771.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TF = Local Frequency.** Counts term occurrences *within* a single document.
- **DF = Global Presence.** Counts *how many documents* contain the term.
- **IDF = Inverse of DF with Log Dampening.** $idf(t) = \log(N / df(t))$; higher IDF ⇒ rarer term ⇒ higher discriminative value.
- **TF-IDF = TF × IDF.** Combines local frequency with global rarity for ranking.
- **Stopwords** like `the`, `is`, `a` naturally get **zero or near-zero TF-IDF weights** because $df \to N$.
- **Normalization is mandatory** for TF when document lengths vary. Common schemes: **log**, **augmented**, **double-normal**.
- **Always declare the log base** (typically $\log_{10}$ in classical KTU IR problems).
- **DF ≠ CF.** Document frequency counts *documents*; collection frequency counts *total occurrences across corpus*.
- **Smoothed IDF formula** $\log(N / (1 + df(t)))$ prevents division-by-zero errors when $df(t) = 0$.
- **TF-IDF vectors** form the basis of **vector space models** for ranking, clustering, and similarity (e.g., cosine similarity).
- **KTU Exam Tip:** When a numerical problem is asked, present your work in *three labeled tables* — TF matrix, DF/IDF table, and final TF-IDF matrix — for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relevance Feedback and Query Expansion: Query Reformation Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Relevance Feedback and Query Expansion: Query Reformation Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Relevance Feedback (RF)** is an *interactive* information retrieval technique in which the user explicitly (or the system implicitly) judges the relevance of initially retrieved documents, and this judgment is then used to automatically reformulate the query so that the second-pass retrieval moves closer to the user's true information need in vector space.

**Query Expansion (QE)** is the broader process of augmenting the original query terms with *additional* terms — drawn from thesauri, pseudo-relevant documents, or co-occurrence statistics — to overcome **vocabulary mismatch** between the user's query language and the document indexing vocabulary.

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus, both techniques are grouped under **Query Reformation Techniques**, the core intent being: the *original query* $Q_0$ is *never* assumed to be the final query. The system iteratively refines $Q_0 \rightarrow Q_1 \rightarrow Q_2 \dots$ until precision/recall converge.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Helpful Librarian"

Imagine you walk into a massive library and ask the librarian:
> *"I want books on **how animals survive winter**."*

The librarian brings you **5 books**. You skim and say:
- 🟢 *Two* of them are exactly what I need (one talks about *hibernation*, the other about *migratory patterns*).
- 🔴 *Three* are useless (they are about animal *hunting*, not survival).

A *smart librarian* does **two things**:
1. **Relevance Feedback**: Notices your "yes" books are about *hibernation* and *migration*, so she searches for more books whose themes **overlap** with those two books.
2. **Query Expansion**: Realises that the words you used ("survive winter") don't literally appear in the best books, so she **adds** new terms like "**hibernation**", "**torpor**", "**migration**", "**insulation**" to the new search.

This is *exactly* what a search engine does behind the scenes after you click or skip results.

## 1.3 Why These Techniques Exist — The Core Problem

| Problem | Symptom in IR | Solution Family |
|---|---|---|
| **Vocabulary Mismatch** | User types *"car"*, document uses *"automobile"* | Query Expansion |
| **Query Ambiguity** | Query *"jaguar"* could mean animal or car | Relevance Feedback (disambiguates) |
| **Short Queries** | Average web query ≈ 2–3 words → too sparse | Both QE & RF |
| **User's Inability to Articulate Need** | User doesn't know domain jargon | Query Expansion via thesaurus |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric for Evaluation (Module 1 anchor):** Improvement in retrieval is measured using **Precision $\mathcal{P}$**, **Recall $\mathcal{R}$**, and the **F-measure $\mathcal{F}_\beta$**, where:
> $$\mathcal{F}_\beta = \frac{(1+\beta^2)\,\mathcal{P}\,\mathcal{R}}{\beta^2\,\mathcal{P}+\mathcal{R}}$$
> For symmetric weighting, $\beta = 1$, giving the **F1-score**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Step Feedback Loop

The relevance feedback procedure is fundamentally a **closed-loop control system**:

**Step 1 — Initial Retrieval**
The user submits the original query $Q_0$. The system ranks documents using a similarity function (e.g., cosine similarity in the **Vector Space Model**).

**Step 2 — User Judgment**
