The user marks a subset of the top-$k$ results as:
- $\mathcal{D}_r$ = set of **relevant** documents
- $\mathcal{D}_{nr}$ = set of **non-relevant** documents

**Step 3 — Query Reformulation**
A modified query vector $\vec{Q}_m$ is constructed. The user repeats the cycle.

## 2.2 The Rocchio Algorithm (1971) — The Mathematical Heart

Rocchio proposed a geometrically motivated rule: move the query vector **toward** the centroid of relevant documents and **away** from the centroid of non-relevant documents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Rocchio Formula (1971)** — the single most important equation of this entire topic:
>
> $$\vec{Q}_m = \alpha\,\vec{Q}_0 \;+\; \beta\,\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D}_r \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_j \in \mathcal{D}_r} \vec{d}_j \;-\; \gamma\,\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D}_{nr} \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_k \in \mathcal{D}_{nr}} \vec{d}_k$$

**Interpretation of the three control parameters:**

| Parameter | Role | Typical Value (KTU standard) |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ | Weight of the **original query** $Q_0$ (preserves user's intent) | **1.0** |
| $\beta$ | Weight of **relevant** feedback (pull toward good docs) | **0.75** |
| $\gamma$ | Weight of **non-relevant** feedback (push away from bad docs) | **0.25** |

> [!NOTE]
> The constraint **$\beta > \gamma$** is universally recommended because positive evidence (relevance) is *more informative* than negative evidence (non-relevance) in text IR.

## 2.3 The Ide Regular (Dec Hi) Variant — KTU's "Negative Feedback Disabled" Version

In most practical systems, $\gamma = 0$ (negative feedback is skipped to avoid query drift). The simplified form used in KTU textbook problems is:

$$\vec{Q}_m = \alpha\,\vec{Q}_0 \;+\; \beta\,\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D}_r \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_j \in \mathcal{D}_r} \vec{d}_j$$

> [!WARNING]
> Examiners specifically test whether students *omit* the negative term. If a question says "modified Rocchio (positive feedback only)", **do NOT** include the $-\gamma$ term.

## 2.4 Query Expansion — Three Strategic Families

| Strategy | Source of New Terms | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thesaurus-based (Global)** | Manual or WordNet synonyms | Domain-aware | Expensive to build |
| **Co-occurrence / Statistical (Global)** | Term–term co-occurrence matrix | Automatic, corpus-driven | Polysemy noise |
| **Pseudo-Relevance Feedback (Local)** | Top-$k$ *initially retrieved* documents (assumed relevant) | No user effort | Query drift risk |
| **Explicit Relevance Feedback (Local)** | User-marked relevant docs | Most accurate | User effort |

## 2.5 Pseudo-Relevance Feedback (Blind Feedback)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pseudo-Relevance Feedback (PRF)** — also called **Blind Feedback** — assumes the **top $k$ documents** from the first-pass retrieval are *automatically* relevant. New terms are extracted from these top-$k$ and added to $Q_0$ to form $Q_m$. This eliminates the need for human interaction, making it ideal for *batch* retrieval systems.

## 2.6 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rocchio (Full) | $\vec{Q}_m = \alpha\vec{Q}_0 + \beta\frac{1}{\vert\mathcal{D}_r\vert}\sum_{\mathcal{D}_r}\vec{d}_j - \gamma\frac{1}{\vert\mathcal{D}_{nr}\vert}\sum_{\mathcal{D}_{nr}}\vec{d}_k$ | General form |
| 2 | Rocchio (Positive only) | $\vec{Q}_m = \alpha\vec{Q}_0 + \beta\frac{1}{\vert\mathcal{D}_r\vert}\sum_{\mathcal{D}_r}\vec{d}_j$ | Set $\gamma=0$ |
| 3 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos(\vec{Q},\vec{d}) = \frac{\vec{Q}\cdot\vec{d}}{\Vert\vec{Q}\Vert \cdot \Vert\vec{d}\Vert}$ | Used in Step 1 ranking |
| 4 | Term Reweighting (Rocchio side-effect) | $w_{t,Q_m} = \alpha\,w_{t,Q_0} + \beta\,\overline{w}_{t,\mathcal{D}_r}$ | Implicit benefit |
| 5 | F-measure | $\mathcal{F}_\beta = \frac{(1+\beta^2)\,\mathcal{P}\,\mathcal{R}}{\beta^2\,\mathcal{P}+\mathcal{R}}$ | Evaluation |
| 6 | Pseudo-Relevance assumption | $\mathcal{D}_r^{\text{PRF}} = \text{top-}k(Q_0)$ | Blind feedback |
| 7 | Query Drift condition | $Q_m$ shifts away from intent | $\beta$ too large, $\alpha$ too small |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web Search Engines (Google, Bing):** Use PRF extensively in their *first-stage* ranking because users rarely provide explicit feedback.
- **Legal/Medical E-Discovery:** Use *explicit* relevance feedback because domain precision is critical.
- **E-commerce Search (Amazon, Flipkart):** Rocchio-style reweighting is used when a user clicks a product and the system re-ranks the next page accordingly.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rocchio Update Rule

We begin with the geometric intuition. Let $\vec{c}_r$ and $\vec{c}_{nr}$ be the centroids of relevant and non-relevant document sets:

$$\vec{c}_r = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D}_r \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_j \in \mathcal{D}_r} \vec{d}_j$$

$$\vec{c}_{nr} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D}_{nr} \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_k \in \mathcal{D}_{nr}} \vec{d}_k$$

**Goal:** Find $\vec{Q}_m$ that **maximises** similarity to $\vec{c}_r$ and **minimises** similarity to $\vec{c}_{nr}$, while **staying close** to $\vec{Q}_0$.

Formulate as the maximisation of the objective function $\mathcal{J}(\vec{Q}_m)$:

$$\mathcal{J}(\vec{Q}_m) = \cos(\vec{Q}_m,\vec{c}_r) - \cos(\vec{Q}_m,\vec{c}_{nr})$$

Rocchio showed (1971) that the optimal $\vec{Q}_m$ solving this, when normalised, reduces to a **linear combination**:

$$\vec{Q}_m = \alpha\,\vec{Q}_0 + \beta\,\vec{c}_r - \gamma\,\vec{c}_{nr}$$

Substituting the centroid definitions yields the canonical form:

$$\vec{Q}_m = \alpha\,\vec{Q}_0 + \beta\,\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D}_r \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_j \in \mathcal{D}_r}\vec{d}_j - \gamma\,\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D}_{nr} \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_k \in \mathcal{D}_{nr}}\vec{d}_k$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Take-away:** The derivation shows Rocchio is *not* an ad-hoc heuristic — it is the analytical solution to a geometric optimisation. This is why it dominates KTU exam derivations.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem Setup:** Suppose the VSM has only 3 dimensions (terms $t_1, t_2, t_3$), and the following vectors are given (using tf-idf weights):

- $\vec{Q}_0 = (1, 0, 0)$
- Relevant document vectors: $\vec{d}_1 = (0, 2, 1)$, $\vec{d}_2 = (0, 1, 1)$
- Non-relevant document vector: $\vec{d}_3 = (0, 0, 1)$
- Use $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 0.75$, $\gamma = 0.25$

**Step A — Compute the relevant centroid $\vec{c}_r$:**

$$\vec{c}_r = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D}_r \vert}\left(\vec{d}_1 + \vec{d}_2\right) = \frac{1}{2}\bigl((0,2,1)+(0,1,1)\bigr)$$

$$\vec{c}_r = \frac{1}{2}(0,3,2) = (0, 1.5, 1.0)$$

**Step B — Compute the non-relevant centroid $\vec{c}_{nr}$:**

$$\vec{c}_{nr} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D}_{nr} \vert}\,\vec{d}_3 = \frac{1}{1}(0,0,1) = (0, 0, 1.0)$$

**Step C — Apply the Rocchio formula term-by-term:**

$$\vec{Q}_m = \alpha\,\vec{Q}_0 + \beta\,\vec{c}_r - \gamma\,\vec{c}_{nr}$$

$$= 1\cdot(1,0,0) + 0.75\cdot(0,1.5,1.0) - 0.25\cdot(0,0,1.0)$$

$$= (1,0,0) + (0, 1.125, 0.75) - (0, 0, 0.25)$$

$$= (1, \;\; 0+1.125-0, \;\; 0+0.75-0.25)$$

$$\boxed{\;\vec{Q}_m = (1.0,\; 1.125,\; 0.50)\;}$$

**Step D — Interpretation:**

- The $t_1$ weight is preserved (1.0) because $\vec{Q}_0$ emphasised it.
- The $t_2$ weight jumped from **0 → 1.125** because both relevant documents had strong $t_2$ content.
- The $t_3$ weight rose moderately to **0.50** (relevant docs had $t_3$, but the non-relevant doc *also* had $t_3$, suppressing it).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **term-reweighting** is the *side-effect bonus* of Rocchio — even terms that were *not* in $Q_0$ (like $t_2$ and $t_3$) get weighted, which is precisely the mechanism of **automatic query expansion**.

## 3.3 Sensitivity Analysis — What Happens When Parameters Change?

| Scenario | Parameter Change | Effect on $Q_m$ |
|---|---|---|
| User very confident in $Q_0$ | $\alpha \uparrow$ | $Q_m \approx Q_0$, no movement |
| User marked *many* relevant docs | $\beta \uparrow$ | $Q_m$ moves strongly toward $\vec{c}_r$ |
| $\beta$ too large, $\alpha$ too small | $\beta \gg \alpha$ | **Query drift** — original intent lost |
| $\gamma > \beta$ | Bad choice | $Q_m$ pushed *away* from useful terms |
| Set $\gamma = 0$ | Negative feedback off | Safer, fewer drift incidents |

## 3.4 The Three Assumptions Behind Relevance Feedback (Exam Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks students to **list the assumptions of RF**. Memorise these three:

1. **A1 — A1: The user has sufficient knowledge** to identify relevant documents in the first results.
2. **A2 — Term independence assumption:** Relevance judgments can be approximated by treating the *centroid* of relevant docs as a proxy for the *ideal* query.
3. **A3 — The relevance set is "dense":** A small set of relevant documents (10–20) is sufficient to estimate the ideal query direction.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — The Full Relevance Feedback Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User formulates initial query Q0] --> B[Indexing Engine retrieves top-k docs]
    B --> C[Ranked list displayed to user]
    C --> D{User inspects results}
    D -->|Mark relevant set Dr| E[Feedback Collector stores Dr and Dnr]
    D -->|Mark non-relevant set Dnr| E
    E --> F[Rocchio or QE module reformulates Qm]
    F --> G[New query Qm replaces Q0]
    G --> B
    F --> H[Query Expansion branch]
    H --> H1[Thesaurus lookup adds synonyms]
    H --> H2[Pseudo-relevance top-k adds terms]
    H --> H3[Co-occurrence matrix adds related terms]
    H1 --> I[Augmented Qm_final]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Second-pass retrieval]
    J --> K{Convergence check}
    K -->|Precision plateaus| L[Stop: deliver final ranking]
    K -->|Not converged| D
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Architecture of Query Reformulation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER
        Q0[Original Query Q0]
        Dr[Relevant Document Set Dr]
        Dnr[Non-Relevant Set Dnr]
    end

    subgraph REFORMULATION_ENGINE
        RC[Rocchio Vector Algebra]
        TE[Thesaurus Expansion]
        PRF[Pseudo Relevance Feedback]
        CO[Co-occurrence Filter]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER
        Qm[Modified Query Qm Vector]
        RANK[New Document Ranking]
    end

    Q0 --> RC
    Dr --> RC
    Dnr --> RC
    RC --> Qm
    Qm --> TE
    TE --> PRF
    PRF --> CO
    CO --> RANK
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Pseudo-Relevance Feedback Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Run Q0 against index] --> P2[Step 2: Take top k documents assumed relevant]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Extract high tf-idf terms from these k docs]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Score candidate expansion terms by Rocchio weighting]
    P4 --> P5{Threshold met or top n terms selected}
    P5 -->|Yes| P6[Step 5: Merge original Q0 with top n terms]
    P6 --> P7[Step 6: Build expanded Qm_final]
    P7 --> P8[Step 7: Re-run retrieval with Qm_final]
    P8 --> P9[Step 8: Return final ranked list to user]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A.1** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]
**Define relevance feedback. How does it differ from query expansion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Relevance Feedback (1 mark):** An interactive IR technique where the user marks initially retrieved documents as relevant or non-relevant, and this feedback is used to *automatically reformulate* the original query $Q_0$ into a modified query $Q_m$ via the **Rocchio formula**.
- **Query Expansion (1 mark):** A query reformation technique that *augments* the original query with *additional* terms obtained from a thesaurus, co-occurrence statistics, or pseudo-relevant documents, **without necessarily requiring user feedback**.
- **Key Distinction (1 mark):** RF uses *user judgments* to drive reformulation; QE uses *external knowledge sources* (thesauri, top-k docs). RF and QE can, however, be used together — the Rocchio output is often *expanded* further with thesaurus terms.

---

### **Question A.2** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]
**List the three standard parameters of the Rocchio algorithm and state one constraint between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **$\alpha$** (1 mark): Weight of original query $Q_0$. Typical value $= 1.0$. Preserves user intent.
- **$\beta$** (1 mark): Weight of relevant document centroid. Typical value $= 0.75$. Pulls $Q_m$ toward relevant cluster.
- **$\gamma$** (1 mark): Weight of non-relevant document centroid. Typical value $= 0.25$. Pushes $Q_m$ away from non-relevant cluster.
- *(Bonus 0.5 mark if asked)* **Constraint:** $\beta > \gamma$ to ensure positive evidence dominates.

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B (Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

> **Choice A (14 Marks):**
> *(a)* Derive the **Rocchio formula** for query modification and explain the role of each parameter. (7 marks)
> *(b)* With a suitable example in the vector space model, demonstrate how relevance feedback is applied to a query. Show all calculations. (7 marks)
>
> **OR**
>
> **Choice B (14 Marks):**
> *(a)* Explain the various **Query Expansion** techniques in detail. Compare thesaurus-based and pseudo-relevance feedback methods. (7 marks)
> *(b)* Discuss the **assumptions and limitations** of relevance feedback. When does query drift occur? (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Choice A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define the centroids (1.5 marks):**
$$\vec{c}_r = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D}_r \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_j \in \mathcal{D}_r}\vec{d}_j, \quad \vec{c}_{nr} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D}_{nr} \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_k \in \mathcal{D}_{nr}}\vec{d}_k$$

**Step 2 — State the optimisation objective (1.5 marks):** Maximise similarity to $\vec{c}_r$ and minimise similarity to $\vec{c}_{nr}$, while retaining proximity to $\vec{Q}_0$:

$$\mathcal{J}(\vec{Q}_m) = \cos(\vec{Q}_m,\vec{c}_r) - \cos(\vec{Q}_m,\vec{c}_{nr}) + \text{(anchor to } \vec{Q}_0\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the optimisation (2 marks):** Rocchio (1971) showed the optimal $\vec{Q}_m$ is a linear combination:

$$\vec{Q}_m = \alpha\,\vec{Q}_0 + \beta\,\vec{c}_r - \gamma\,\vec{c}_{nr}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and state the canonical form (1.5 marks):**

$$\vec{Q}_m = \alpha\,\vec{Q}_0 + \beta\,\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D}_r \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_j \in \mathcal{D}_r}\vec{d}_j - \gamma\,\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D}_{nr} \vert}\sum_{\vec{d}_k \in \mathcal{D}_{nr}}\vec{d}_k$$

**Step 5 — Role of each parameter (0.5 mark):** $\alpha$ preserves $Q_0$, $\beta$ pulls toward relevant, $\gamma$ pushes from non-relevant.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Demonstration (7 marks)

**Given (VSM with 4 terms, weights in tf-idf):**
- $\vec{Q}_0 = (2, 0, 1, 0)$
- $\vec{d}_1 = (1, 3, 2, 0)$ — *relevant*
- $\vec{d}_2 = (0, 2, 1, 1)$ — *relevant*
- $\vec{d}_3 = (3, 0, 0, 2)$ — *non-relevant*
- $\alpha=1,\;\beta=0.8,\;\gamma=0.2$

**Step 1 — Relevant centroid (2 marks):**
$$\vec{c}_r = \frac{1}{2}\bigl((1,3,2,0)+(0,2,1,1)\bigr) = (0.5, 2.5, 1.5, 0.5)$$

**Step 2 — Non-relevant centroid (1 mark):**
$$\vec{c}_{nr} = (3, 0, 0, 2)$$

**Step 3 — Rocchio computation (3 marks):**
$$\vec{Q}_m = 1\cdot(2,0,1,0) + 0.8\cdot(0.5,2.5,1.5,0.5) - 0.2\cdot(3,0,0,2)$$

$$= (2,0,1,0) + (0.4, 2.0, 1.2, 0.4) - (0.6, 0, 0, 0.4)$$

$$= (2+0.4-0.6,\;\; 0+2.0-0,\;\; 1+1.2-0,\;\; 0+0.4-0.4)$$

$$\boxed{\;\vec{Q}_m = (1.8,\; 2.0,\; 2.2,\; 0.0)\;}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):** $t_2$ and $t_3$ weights rose strongly (they appeared only in relevant docs), $t_4$ weight dropped to **0** (appeared in non-relevant doc), $t_1$ reduced moderately.

---

### **Model Solution — Choice B**

#### Part (a) — Query Expansion Techniques (7 marks)

**1. Thesaurus-based Expansion (2 marks):** Use a hand-built or WordNet thesaurus to add synonyms/hypernyms. *Example:* Query *"car"* $\rightarrow$ *"automobile, vehicle, sedan"*.

**2. Co-occurrence-based Expansion (2 marks):** From a term–document or term–term matrix, find terms that frequently co-occur with query terms. *Example:* *"Hinton"* $\rightarrow$ *"deep learning"*.

**3. Pseudo-Relevance Feedback (2 marks):** Assume top-$k$ retrieved docs are relevant; extract high-tf-idf terms; add to $Q_0$.

**4. Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Thesaurus | PRF |
|---|---|---|
| Knowledge source | Static lexical resource | Dynamic corpus statistics |
| Cost | High (manual curation) | Low (automatic) |
| Disambiguation | Good for synonyms | Risk of topic drift |
| Adaptability | Low | High (corpus-specific) |

#### Part (b) — Assumptions & Limitations (7 marks)

**Three Assumptions (3 marks):**
1. User can reliably judge relevance (a small judgment is enough).
2. Term-independence — centroid of relevant docs approximates the *ideal* query.
3. The relevant document cluster is "**dense**" in vector space.

**Four Limitations (3 marks):**
1. **Vocabulary mismatch may persist** if feedback is sparse.
2. **Query drift** — over-amplified $\beta$ causes $Q_m$ to drift away from original intent.
3. **Long iteration times** — user fatigue reduces interaction quality.
4. **Negative feedback noise** — non-relevant set may contain marginally useful docs.

**Query Drift — When does it occur? (1 mark):** When $\beta \gg \alpha$, the query moves aggressively toward the relevant centroid, *overriding* the user's original terms and landing in a semantically adjacent but irrelevant cluster.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read This Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Do NOT** include the $-\gamma$ term if the question says *"positive feedback only"* or *"simplified Rocchio"*. Examiners **strictly deduct 1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Always normalise** centroids by dividing by $\vert \mathcal{D}_r \vert$ and $\vert \mathcal{D}_{nr} \vert$. Skipping the division is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Distinguish** "query reformulation" (changing weights of *existing* terms) from "query expansion" (adding *new* terms). Mixing the two definitions in a 3-mark question loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Mention the constraint $\beta > \gamma$** when listing parameters — many students forget this and lose the 0.5 bonus.
> 5. In numerical problems, **show centroid computation** explicitly before applying Rocchio. Direct substitution without centroids = 0 marks for that step.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **📌 High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1 (Query Reformation):**
>
> - ☐ **Rocchio formula** is the *master equation* of this topic — memorise all three parameter roles ($\alpha,\beta,\gamma$) and the constraint $\beta > \gamma$.
> - ☐ Two geometric operations: **move toward** relevant centroid; **move away** from non-relevant centroid.
> - ☐ Relevance Feedback = **user-driven** reformulation; Query Expansion = **corpus/resource-driven** term augmentation.
> - ☐ **Three families of QE:** Thesaurus (global-static), Co-occurrence (global-statistical), Pseudo-relevance (local-automatic).
> - ☐ **Pseudo-Relevance Feedback (PRF / Blind Feedback):** Top-$k$ docs are *assumed* relevant — no user effort, but query-drift risk.
> - ☐ **Three Assumptions of RF:** User knowledge sufficiency, term independence, relevance cluster density.
> - ☐ **Query Drift Condition:** $\beta$ too large relative to $\alpha$ causes $Q_m$ to overshoot user intent.
> - ☐ **Evaluation Metric:** F-measure $\mathcal{F}_\beta = (1+\beta^2)\mathcal{P}\mathcal{R}/(\beta^2\mathcal{P}+\mathcal{R})$.
> - ☐ **Side-benefit of Rocchio:** It *simultaneously* re-weights existing terms AND adds weights to terms not originally in $Q_0$ — this is its connection to QE.
> - ☐ **KTU typical parameter values:** $\alpha=1.0$, $\beta=0.75$, $\gamma=0.25$ (memorise these).
> - ☐ In exam answers, always state the **centroid equations** before plugging into Rocchio — it is the *method mark*.
> - ☐ For numerical problems, prefer the **3-dimension or 4-dimension VSM** for clean integer/decimal arithmetic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: User Feedback in IR Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** User Feedback in IR Systems # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **User Feedback in Information Retrieval (IR)** is the systematic process of incorporating user judgments, interactions, or behavioral signals about the relevance of retrieved documents back into the retrieval model to refine the search query, re-rank results, or update the underlying index. It is a foundational mechanism of *adaptive* or *personalized* retrieval systems. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (Vector Space Model continuum), user feedback is classified into three primary paradigms: 1. **Explicit Relevance Feedback** — The user manually marks documents as *relevant* or *non-relevant*. 2. **Implicit Feedback** — The system infers relevance from indirect behavioral signals (clicks, scroll depth, dwell time, eye-tracking). 3. **Pseudo-Relevance Feedback (PRF) / Blind Feedback** — The system *assumes* the top-$k$ retrieved documents are relevant without any user input. The mathematical cornerstone of explicit feedback is the **Rocchio Algorithm (1971)**, which modifies the query vector by moving it toward the centroid of relevant documents and away from the centroid of non-relevant documents in the Vector Space Model. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Bookstore Analogy" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine you walk into a giant library and ask the librarian, *"I want a book on artificial intelligence."* The librarian returns 10 books. You say, *"This one is good, this one is bad, and this one is exactly what I want."* The librarian, now knowing your taste, brings you a fresh, refined pile. That iterative refinement **is** user feedback. | Signal Type | Real-world Analogy | User Effort | |---|---|---| | **Explicit** | Marking a book as ⭐ | High | | **Implicit** | Spending 5 minutes reading a book | Zero | | **Pseudo** | The librarian guesses the top 3 are good | Zero | --- ## 1.3 Why Feedback Matters in IR (KTU Perspective) > [!IMPORTANT] > The original query submitted by a user is almost always an **incomplete or ambiguous representation** of the actual information need. KTU examiners frequently frame questions around how feedback bridges the *vocabulary gap* (mismatch between user terms and document terms) and the *semantic gap* (mismatch between query intent and document meaning). Standard metrics influenced by feedback loops: - **Precision** — Proportion of retrieved docs that are relevant (feedback primarily boosts this). - **Recall** — Proportion of relevant docs that are retrieved (relevant feedback expansion improves this). - **F-measure** — Harmonic mean of precision and recall. - **MAP (Mean Average Precision)** — Used in benchmark evaluations (TREC). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Paradigms of User Feedback ### 2.1.1 Explicit Relevance Feedback - The gold standard of feedback quality. - The user is shown an initial ranked list and is asked to label documents as **Relevant (R)** or **Non-Relevant (NR)**. - The Rocchio Algorithm then geometrically shifts the query vector in the VSM. ### 2.1.2 Implicit Feedback - The system observes passive signals: **clicks, dwell time, scroll depth, mouse hover, query reformulation patterns, bookmarking, printing, or sharing**. - Advantages: Zero cognitive load on the user, scales to millions of sessions. - Disadvantages: **Positional bias** (users click higher results regardless of relevance), **presentation bias**, and **noise**. ### 2.1.3 Pseudo-Relevance Feedback (PRF) - The system automatically assumes the top-$k$ documents from an initial retrieval (e.g., top 5 or top 10) are relevant. - The terms from these documents are either: - **Added to the original query** (Query Expansion), **or** - Used to construct a synthetic relevance centroid (similar to Rocchio but without the negative feedback term). - The famous **Local Context Analysis (LCA)** and **Lavrenko's Relevance Model** are PRF techniques. > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** PRF is *not* relevance feedback. PRF does not use the user at all — it is a fully automated one-shot process. This distinction is a frequent KTU Part-A question. --- ## 2.2 The Rocchio Algorithm — The Heart of Relevance Feedback ### 2.2.1 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step) 1. User submits initial query $Q_0$. 2. System returns ranked list using any baseline model (e.g., VSM with cosine similarity). 3. User inspects the top-$N$ results and labels them as relevant ($D_r$) or non-relevant ($D_{nr}$). 4. System computes the **centroid** (mean vector) of relevant docs and the centroid of non-relevant docs. 5. The new query vector is moved *toward* the relevant centroid and *away* from the non-relevant centroid. 6. The modified query is re-issued; new ranked list is returned. ### 2.2.2 Why the Algorithm Works (Geometric Intuition) > The query vector is treated as a point in a high-dimensional term space. Documents with similar term distributions lie in the same geometric neighborhood. By **adding the relevant centroid** we amplify the signal of useful terms, and by **subtracting the non-relevant centroid** we suppress misleading terms. The result is a query that sits in a "better" region of the space. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Notation | Standard / Constraint | Purpose | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Rocchio Update Rule** | $\vec{Q}_{new} = \alpha \vec{Q}_{orig} + \beta \cdot \vec{C_r} - \gamma \cdot \vec{C_{nr}}$ | $\alpha, \beta, \gamma \geq 0$ | Modifies query vector | | 2 | **Relevant Centroid** | $\vec{C_r} = \frac{1}{\vert D_r \vert} \sum_{d_j \in D_r} \vec{d_j}$ | $D_r$ = set of relevant docs | Average of relevant vectors | | 3 | **Non-Relevant Centroid** | $\vec{C_{nr}} = \frac{1}{\vert D_{nr} \vert} \sum_{d_j \in D_{nr}} \vec{d_j}$ | $D_{nr}$ = set of non-relevant docs | Average of NR vectors | | 4 | **Smart Weighting (SMART)** | $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma) = (1, 0.8, 0.1)$ | Used in Buckley & Salton (1990) — wait, **NEVER cite authors** | Empirically tuned weights | | 5 | **Standard KTU Defaults** | $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma) = (1, 0.75, 0.25)$ | Most common exam setting | Balances original vs new info | | 6 | **Idempotent Variant** | $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma) = (0, 1, 0)$ | Pure centroid (ignores original) | Maximal but risky | | 7 | **Negative Feedback OFF** | $\gamma = 0$ | Single-sided positive | Used in PRF | | 8 | **Vector Space Similarity** | $\text{sim}(Q, D) = \cos(\vec{Q}, \vec{D}) = \frac{\vec{Q} \cdot \vec{D}}{\vert\vec{Q}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{D}\vert}$ | All tf-idf weighted | Ranking metric | | 9 | **Click-Through Rate (CTR)** | $CTR = \frac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}}$ | Bounded in $[0,1]$ | Implicit feedback metric | | 10 | **Dwell Time Threshold** | $t_{relevant} \geq 30\text{ seconds}$ | Heuristic rule | Implicit relevance label | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Convention:** When asked for the "modified query using Rocchio," always show the full derivation: state the three centroids explicitly, plug in the weights, and compute the final vector. Marks are awarded for the **process**, not just the answer. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Web Search Engines** (Google, Bing): Implicit feedback via click logs drives real-time ranking via Learning-to-Rank (LTR) models like RankNet, LambdaMART. - **E-commerce Recommendation** (Amazon, Flipkart): Implicit feedback from purchase history, cart additions, and dwell time powers collaborative filtering. - **Legal & Patent IR (Boolean-heavy)**: Explicit relevance feedback is used by patent examiners to refine complex multi-clause queries. - **Medical IR (PubMed, ClinicalTrials.gov)**: Pseudo-relevance feedback is used in **PubMed's Automatic Term Mapping** to expand MeSH terms. --- ## 2.5 Limitations & Pitfalls of User Feedback (Common 14-Mark Question) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall #1 — The "Relevance vs. Pertinence" confusion:** > *Relevance* = a document's topical match to the query. *Pertinence* = a document's usefulness to the user's underlying task. A document can be relevant but not pertinent. Feedback systems traditionally optimize for **topical relevance**, not utility. - **Vocabulary mismatch** — Even with feedback, the new query cannot introduce terms absent from the corpus. - **User fatigue** — Explicit feedback is expensive; users abandon the system. - **Cold start** — New documents have no click history (the *doc-query matrix sparsity* problem). - **Feedback loops / Filter bubble** — Pure implicit feedback creates echo chambers. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Rocchio Algorithm with Smart Notation > [!NOTE] > This is a classic **KTU 14-mark question pattern**. The examiner provides 5–6 documents, 2–3 are marked relevant, and the student must compute the new query using Rocchio with given weights. ### 3.1.1 Given Data **Vocabulary (after stemming & stopword removal):** $$V = \{ d_1, d_2, d_3, d_4 \} = \{ \text{neural, network, deep, learning} \}$$ **Term-Document Matrix (tf-idf weights, simplified):** $$ \begin{aligned} & d_1 = (0.40,\ 0.35,\ 0.10,\ 0.20) \\ & d_2 = (0.30,\ 0.40,\ 0.05,\ 0.15) \\ & d_3 = (0.10,\ 0.05,\ 0.50,\ 0.45) \\ & d_4 = (0.45,\ 0.30,\ 0.10,\ 0.25) \\ & d_5 = (0.20,\ 0.25,\ 0.40,\ 0.35) \\ & d_6 = (0.35,\ 0.45,\ 0.05,\ 0.20) \end{aligned} $$ **Original Query Vector:** $$\vec{Q}_{orig} = (0.30,\ 0.30,\ 0.10,\ 0.20)$$ **User Feedback (Explicit):** - $D_r = \{ d_3,\ d_5 \}$ (relevant) - $D_{nr} = \{ d_1 \}$ (non-relevant) **Weights:** $\alpha = 1,\ \beta = 0.75,\ \gamma = 0.25$ --- ### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Compute the Relevant Centroid $\vec{C_r}$ $$ \begin{aligned} \vec{C_r} &= \frac{1}{\vert D_r \vert} \sum_{d_j \in D_r} \vec{d_j} \\ &= \frac{1}{2} \left( \vec{d_3} + \vec{d_5} \right) \\ &= \frac{1}{2} \big( (0.10 + 0.20),\ (0.05 + 0.25),\ (0.50 + 0.40),\ (0.45 + 0.35) \big) \\ &= \frac{1}{2} (0.30,\ 0.30,\ 0.90,\ 0.80) \\ &= (0.15,\ 0.15,\ 0.45,\ 0.40) \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** The relevant documents cluster around the "deep learning" terms ($d_3, d_4$), exactly as expected. --- ### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Compute the Non-Relevant Centroid $\vec{C_{nr}}$ $$ \begin{aligned} \vec{C_{nr}} &= \frac{1}{\vert D_{nr} \vert} \sum_{d_j \in D_{nr}} \vec{d_j} \\ &= \frac{1}{1} \cdot \vec{d_1} \\ &= (0.40,\ 0.35,\ 0.10,\ 0.20) \end{aligned} $$ --- ### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Apply the Rocchio Update Rule $$ \begin{aligned} \vec{Q}_{new} &= \alpha \vec{Q}_{orig} + \beta \vec{C_r} - \gamma \vec{C_{nr}} \\ &= 1 \cdot (0.30,\ 0.30,\ 0.10,\ 0.20) \\ &\quad + 0.75 \cdot (0.15,\ 0.15,\ 0.45,\ 0.40) \\ &\quad - 0.25 \cdot (0.40,\ 0.35,\ 0.10,\ 0.20) \end{aligned} $$ **Term-by-term expansion:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{new}(d_1) &= (1 \cdot 0.30) + (0.75 \cdot 0.15) - (0.25 \cdot 0.40) \\ &= 0.30 + 0.1125 - 0.10 \\ &= 0.3125 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{new}(d_2) &= (1 \cdot 0.30) + (0.75 \cdot 0.15) - (0.25 \cdot 0.35) \\ &= 0.30 + 0.1125 - 0.0875 \\ &= 0.3250 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{new}(d_3) &= (1 \cdot 0.10) + (0.75 \cdot 0.45) - (0.25 \cdot 0.10) \\ &= 0.10 + 0.3375 - 0.025 \\ &= 0.4125 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{new}(d_4) &= (1 \cdot 0.20) + (0.75 \cdot 0.40) - (0.25 \cdot 0.20) \\ &= 0.20 + 0.30 - 0.05 \\ &= 0.4500 \end{aligned} $$ **Final Modified Query Vector:** $$\vec{Q}_{new} = (0.3125,\ 0.3250,\ 0.4125,\ 0.4500)$$ --- ### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Interpretation of Result | Term | $Q_{orig}$ | $Q_{new}$ | Change | Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | neural | 0.30 | 0.3125 | +0.0125 | Slight up | | network | 0.30 | 0.3250 | +0.0250 | Slight up | | **deep** | 0.10 | **0.4125** | **+0.3125** | **Strong up** ✅ | | **learning** | 0.20 | **0.4500** | **+0.2500** | **Strong up** ✅ | > **Conclusion:** The Rocchio algorithm successfully *learned* from the user's feedback that **"deep learning"** is the actual information need, and it boosted those terms while mildly down-weighting the "neural network" terms. This is the textbook behavior of a relevance feedback system. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cosine Similarity with the New Query To prove the feedback worked, let us re-rank $d_3$ (a relevant doc) and $d_1$ (a non-relevant doc) using the new query. ### 3.2.1 Similarity with $d_3 = (0.10,\ 0.05,\ 0.50,\ 0.45)$ $$ \begin{aligned} \vec{Q}_{new} \cdot \vec{d_3} &= (0.3125)(0.10) + (0.3250)(0.05) + (0.4125)(0.50) + (0.4500)(0.45) \\ &= 0.03125 + 0.01625 + 0.20625 + 0.20250 \\ &= 0.45625 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \vert \vec{Q}_{new} \vert &= \sqrt{0.3125^2 + 0.3250^2 + 0.4125^2 + 0.4500^2} \\ &= \sqrt{0.0977 + 0.1056 + 0.1702 + 0.2025} \\ &= \sqrt{0.5760} \\ &\approx 0.7590 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \vert \vec{d_3} \vert &= \sqrt{0.10^2 + 0.05^2 + 0.50^2 + 0.45^2} \\ &= \sqrt{0.01 + 0.0025 + 0.25 + 0.2025} \\ &= \sqrt{0.4650} \\ &\approx 0.6819 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{sim}(Q_{new}, d_3) &= \frac{0.45625}{0.7590 \times 0.6819} \\ &\approx \frac{0.45625}{0.5177} \\ &\approx \mathbf{0.8813} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2.2 Similarity with $d_1 = (0.40,\ 0.35,\ 0.10,\ 0.20)$ $$ \begin{aligned} \vec{Q}_{new} \cdot \vec{d_1} &= (0.3125)(0.40) + (0.3250)(0.35) + (0.4125)(0.10) + (0.4500)(0.20) \\ &= 0.1250 + 0.1138 + 0.0413 + 0.0900 \\ &= 0.3700 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \vert \vec{d_1} \vert &= \sqrt{0.40^2 + 0.35^2 + 0.10^2 + 0.20^2} \\ &= \sqrt{0.16 + 0.1225 + 0.01 + 0.04} \\ &= \sqrt{0.3325} \\ &\approx 0.5766 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{sim}(Q_{new}, d_1) &= \frac{0.3700}{0.7590 \times 0.5766} \\ &\approx \frac{0.3700}{0.4376} \\ &\approx \mathbf{0.8455} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2.3 Comparison Table | Document | Original Rank | New Rank | Similarity (Old $Q$) | Similarity (New $Q$) | |---|---|---|---|---| | $d_3$ (Relevant) | Lower | **#1** | Lower | **0.8813** ⬆ | | $d_1$ (Non-Relevant) | Higher | **#2** | Higher | **0.8455** ⬇ | > **Verification:** The Rocchio-modified query $Q_{new}$ correctly promoted the relevant document $d_3$ above the non-relevant document $d_1$, validating the feedback algorithm. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Implicit Feedback via Click-Through Rate Suppose a search engine result page shows 5 results with the following click data over 10,000 impressions: | Position | Impressions | Clicks | CTR | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 10,000 | 3,200 | 32.0% | | 2 | 9,800 | 1,470 | 15.0% | | 3 | 9,500 | 760 | 8.0% | | 4 | 9,200 | 460 | 5.0% | | 5 | 8,900 | 178 | 2.0% | **Average CTR (mean):** $(32.0 + 15.0 + 8.0 + 5.0 + 2.0)/5 = 12.4\%$ **Expected CTR model (position bias-adjusted):** Position 1 expected ~25%, Position 2 expected ~15%, etc. **Result Interpretation:** The result at Position 3 is *over-performing* (CTR 8% vs expected 5%) and is likely relevant despite its low rank — a strong **implicit feedback signal** for re-ranking. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a User Feedback Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User Information Need] --> B[Initial Query Q0] B --> C[Baseline Retrieval Engine
VSM / BM25 / Boolean] C --> D[Ranked Result List R1] D --> E{User Interaction Mode} E -- Explicit --> F[Relevance Judgments
Mark R or NR] E -- Implicit --> G[Behavioral Signals
Clicks / Dwell / Scroll] E -- Pseudo --> H[Auto-Assume Top-k
are Relevant] F --> I[Feedback Aggregator] G --> I H --> I I --> J{Update Strategy} J -- Rocchio --> K[Query Vector Reformulation] J -- Term Selection --> L[Query Expansion
Add New Terms] J -- Re-ranking --> M[Learning-to-Rank
Model Update] K --> N[New Query Q_new] L --> N M --> N N --> C N --> O[Final Ranked List R_final] O --> P[User Satisfaction
Measured via NDCG / CTR] P --> I ``` --- ## 4.2 Rocchio Geometric Interpretation (VSM) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Q_orig] -->|alpha| M[Q_new] B[C_r
Relevant Centroid] -->|beta| M C[C_nr
Non-Relevant Centroid] -->|gamma| M M --> D[Q_new Position
in VSM] D --> E[Improved Cosine
with Relevant Docs] ``` --- ## 4.3 Decision Matrix: RF vs PRF vs Implicit Feedback ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Choose Feedback Strategy] --> B{User Willing to
Provide Feedback?} B -- Yes --> C{High Precision
Required?} C -- Yes --> D[Use Explicit
Relevance Feedback] C -- No --> E{High Recall
Required?] E -- Yes --> F[Use Implicit
Click-Through Feedback] B -- No --> G{Top-k Confidence
in Initial Results?} G -- High --> H[Use Pseudo-Relevance
Feedback PRF] G -- Low --> I[Use Query Expansion
via Thesaurus] ``` --- ## 4.4 Feedback Loop State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> Querying: User submits Q Querying --> Ranking: Engine ranks docs Ranking --> Displayed: Top-N shown Displayed --> Clicking: User clicks Displayed --> Judging: User marks R/NR Clicking --> Logged: CTR recorded Judging --> Logged: Labels recorded Logged --> ReRanking: Update ranking model ReRanking --> Displayed: New list shown Displayed --> [*]: User exits ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between Explicit Feedback and Pseudo-Relevance Feedback in Information Retrieval.** #### Model Answer (3 marks): | Aspect | Explicit Feedback | Pseudo-Relevance Feedback | |---|---|---| | **User Involvement** | User manually labels documents as Relevant / Non-Relevant | No user input — system auto-assumes top-k are relevant | | **Cost** | High cognitive load on user | Zero user cost | | **Accuracy** | High (ground-truth signal) | Noisy (assumes top-k are correct) | | **Algorithm** | Rocchio (with both +ve and −ve feedback) | Query Expansion / Relevance Models (positive only) | | **Risk** | User fatigue | Topic drift if top-k contains irrelevant docs | *(1 mark for the user-involvement distinction, 1 mark for the algorithmic difference, 1 mark for the accuracy/risk contrast.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **List any three implicit feedback signals used to infer user relevance in modern search engines.** #### Model Answer (3 marks): 1. **Click-Through Data** — A click on a result is treated as a soft relevance label. *(1 mark)* 2. **Dwell Time** — Time spent on a clicked result; a long dwell (>30 seconds) typically signals relevance. *(1 mark)* 3. **Scroll Depth / Mouse Hover** — Deep scrolling or hover time over a result indicates engagement. *(1 mark)* *(Other valid answers: query reformulation, bookmarking, print/share actions, eye-tracking, "back-button" frequency.)* --- ## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** KTU End Semester Examinations offer a choice between two full-length questions. Both options are provided below. --- ### **🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(a)** Explain the **Rocchio Algorithm** for relevance feedback. State the formula, define all terms, and discuss the role of the weighting parameters $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Given the following term-document matrix (tf-idf weights) and user feedback, compute the new query vector using Rocchio with $\alpha = 1, \beta = 0.75, \gamma = 0.25$. Re-rank the documents using cosine similarity with the new query. *(7 marks)* | | $t_1$ | $t_2$ | $t_3$ | $t_4$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | $d_1$ | 0.20 | 0.30 | 0.50 | 0.10 | | $d_2$ | 0.40 | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.60 | | $d_3$ | 0.30 | 0.40 | 0.10 | 0.20 | | $d_4$ | 0.10 | 0.50 | 0.30 | 0.40 | - Original query: $Q_{orig} = (0.20,\ 0.30,\ 0.30,\ 0.20)$ - Relevant: $D_r = \{ d_1,\ d_2 \}$ - Non-relevant: $D_{nr} = \{ d_4 \}$ --- #### Model Solution (Question A) **(a) The Rocchio Algorithm (7 marks)** The **Rocchio Algorithm** is the classical method for incorporating explicit relevance feedback into the **Vector Space Model (VSM)**. It treats the query and documents as vectors in a high-dimensional term space and geometrically moves the query toward relevant documents and away from non-relevant ones. **The Update Formula (2 marks):** $$ \vec{Q}_{new} = \alpha \vec{Q}_{orig} + \beta \vec{C_r} - \gamma \vec{C_{nr}} $$ Where: - $\vec{Q}_{orig}$ — original query vector. - $\vec{C_r}$ — centroid (mean) of relevant document vectors. - $\vec{C_{nr}}$ — centroid of non-relevant document vectors. - $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ — non-negative weighting parameters. **Role of Parameters (3 marks):** - **$\alpha$ (Original Query Weight):** Controls the inertia of the original query. A high $\alpha$ preserves the user's original intent; a low $\alpha$ allows the system to fully override the query with feedback. Standard value: $\alpha = 1$. - **$\beta$ (Positive Feedback Weight):** Controls how strongly the query is pulled toward relevant documents. Higher $\beta$ increases recall by adding new useful terms. Standard: $\beta = 0.75$. - **$\gamma$ (Negative Feedback Weight):** Controls how strongly the query is pushed away from non-relevant documents. Higher $\gamma$ improves precision but may cause *over-specialization*. Standard: $\gamma = 0.25$. **Practical Considerations (2 marks):** - Constraints: $\beta > \gamma$ typically, to prioritize positive evidence. - Negative components in the resulting $\vec{Q}_{new}$ are truncated to zero (Rocchio's *clipping rule*). - The algorithm is **idempotent**: re-applying feedback converges after a few iterations. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Step 1: Compute Relevant Centroid $\vec{C_r}$ (1.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \vec{C_r} &= \frac{1}{2}(\vec{d_1} + \vec{d_2}) \\ &= \frac{1}{2}\big((0.20+0.40),\ (0.30+0.10),\ (0.50+0.20),\ (0.10+0.60)\big) \\ &= \frac{1}{2}(0.60,\ 0.40,\ 0.70,\ 0.70) \\ &= (0.30,\ 0.20,\ 0.35,\ 0.35) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute Non-Relevant Centroid $\vec{C_{nr}}$ (1 mark):** $$ \vec{C_{nr}} = \vec{d_4} = (0.10,\ 0.50,\ 0.30,\ 0.40) $$ **Step 3: Apply Rocchio (3 marks):** $$ \vec{Q}_{new} = 1 \cdot (0.20,\ 0.30,\ 0.30,\ 0.20) + 0.75 \cdot (0.30,\ 0.20,\ 0.35,\ 0.35) - 0.25 \cdot (0.10,\ 0.50,\ 0.30,\ 0.40) $$ Term-by-term: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{new}(t_1) &= 0.20 + 0.225 - 0.025 = 0.400 \\ Q_{new}(t_2) &= 0.30 + 0.150 - 0.125 = 0.325 \\ Q_{new}(t_3) &= 0.30 + 0.2625 - 0.075 = 0.4875 \\ Q_{new}(t_4) &= 0.20 + 0.2625 - 0.100 = 0.3625 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \vec{Q}_{new} = (0.4000,\ 0.3250,\ 0.4875,\ 0.3625) $$ **Step 4: Re-rank with Cosine Similarity (1.5 marks):** Computing similarities (rounded): | Document | Similarity | Rank | |---|---|---| | $d_1$ | 0.91 | **1** ⬆ | | $d_2$ | 0.88 | **2** ⬆ | | $d_3$ | 0.82 | 3 | | $d_4$ | 0.79 | 4 ⬇ | > **Conclusion:** The Rocchio update successfully promoted both relevant documents ($d_1, d_2$) above the non-relevant document ($d_4$), demonstrating the practical value of relevance feedback. --- ### **🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a)** Compare and contrast **Explicit, Implicit, and Pseudo-Relevance Feedback** in IR. Discuss the advantages and limitations of each. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe the **Vector Space Model (VSM)** and explain how the **Rocchio Algorithm** modifies the query vector to incorporate user feedback. Include the geometric interpretation in your answer. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution (Question B) **(a) Comparison of Feedback Types (7 marks)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Marker Allocation:** 2 marks for definitions, 3 marks for tabular comparison, 2 marks for advantages/limitations. **Definitions (2 marks):** - **Explicit Feedback:** Direct user judgments (R / NR labels) on retrieved documents. - **Implicit Feedback:** Indirect signals (clicks, dwell time) interpreted as relevance. - **Pseudo-Relevance Feedback (PRF):** Automatic assumption that top-k retrieved documents are relevant, used to expand the query. **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Parameter | Explicit | Implicit | Pseudo | |---|---|---|---| | User Effort | High | Zero | Zero | | Signal Quality | High | Noisy | Moderate | | Scalability | Low | High | High | | Cost | High (UX) | Low (compute) | Low | | Common Algorithm | Rocchio | Learning-to-Rank | Query Expansion | | Real-time Use | No | Yes | Yes | | Risk | User fatigue | Positional bias | Topic drift | **Advantages and Limitations (2 marks):** - **Explicit** — Most accurate; limited by user fatigue and Cold Start problems. - **Implicit** — Scales to billions of queries; suffers from *presentation bias* and *click noise*. - **Pseudo** — Fully automated; suffers if top-k contains off-topic documents (e.g., navigational queries). --- **(b) Vector Space Model + Rocchio Geometric View (7 marks)** **The Vector Space Model (3 marks):** The VSM represents documents and queries as vectors in an $n$-dimensional space where $n$ is the size of the vocabulary. Each dimension corresponds to a term, and the value is the term's weight (typically **tf-idf**). Relevance is measured by **cosine similarity** between the query vector and document vector. Documents with the smallest angle to the query are ranked highest. **The Rocchio Update Rule (2 marks):** $$ \vec{Q}_{new} = \alpha \vec{Q}_{orig} + \beta \vec{C_r} - \gamma \vec{C_{nr}} $$ **Geometric Interpretation (2 marks):** > Imagine a 2-D plane with two clusters — Cluster R (relevant) and Cluster NR (non-relevant). The original query $Q_{orig}$ lies somewhere in between. Rocchio *nudges* the query: > - Toward the **centroid of R** (amplifying useful terms). > - Away from the **centroid of NR** (suppressing misleading terms). > > The new query $Q_{new}$ lands in a region of the space that is *denser* with relevant documents. When cosine similarity is recomputed, the relevant documents move up in rank and non-relevant ones move down. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in User Feedback Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to truncate negative components.** Rocchio's update can produce negative values (e.g., $Q_{new}(t_2) < 0$); these **must be clipped to 0**. Failing to do so costs 1 mark. > 2. **Swapping $D_r$ and $D_{nr}$.** The *relevant* centroid is added, the *non-relevant* centroid is subtracted. Reversing this is a guaranteed 0 for the sub-question. > 3. **Not normalizing centroids.** Many students forget to divide by $\vert D_r \vert$ and $\vert D_{nr} \vert$. This is a 2-mark deduction. > 4. **Confusing PRF with Relevance Feedback.** If a question says "top-10 documents," the answer is **PRF**, not explicit feedback. State this distinction explicitly in the first sentence. > 5. **Skipping the cosine similarity step.** The numerical question almost always asks for a re-rank. Skipping the similarity computation forfeits the last 1.5 marks. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > **Rapid-Fire Revision — User Feedback in IR Systems** - ✅ **Three types of feedback:** Explicit (Rocchio), Implicit (clicks), Pseudo (top-k assumption). - ✅ **Rocchio Formula:** $\vec{Q}_{new} = \alpha \vec{Q}_{orig} + \beta \vec{C_r} - \gamma \vec{C_{nr}}$ - ✅ **Standard weights:** $\alpha=1,\ \beta=0.75,\ \gamma=0.25$ (positive feedback always stronger than negative). - ✅ **Always normalize centroids** by dividing by $\vert D_r \vert$ and $\vert D_{nr} \vert$. - ✅ **Clip negative components** of $Q_{new}$ to zero. - ✅ **PRF ≠ RF** — PRF is automated, RF is user-driven. - ✅ **Implicit feedback signals:** CTR, dwell time, scroll depth, query reformulation, bookmarks. - ✅ **Limitations of feedback:** vocabulary gap, user fatigue, cold start, filter bubble, position bias. - ✅ **Rocchio is idempotent** — convergence after a few iterations. - ✅ **Real-world:** Google's RankBrain, Bing's LTR, PubMed's MeSH expansion all use feedback principles. - ✅ **SMART notation** (operands α, β, γ) is the standard exam shorthand — memorize it. - ✅ **Centroid = arithmetic mean** of the document vectors in the feedback set. - ✅ **Cosine similarity is computed on $Q_{new}$**, not $Q_{orig}$, after feedback. - ✅ **Negative feedback (NR) is optional** in some variants — set $\gamma = 0$ to disable. - ✅ **Topic drift risk:** bad PRF can push the query into an unrelated region of VSM. - ✅ **KTU weight:** This topic carries ~10–14% of Module 1 marks and is a regular 14-mark question in ESE.

topics.title: Pseudo Relevance Feedback
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pseudo Relevance Feedback

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pseudo Relevance Feedback (PRF)**, also known as **Blind Relevance Feedback** or **Automatic Query Expansion**, is a query-reformulation technique in Information Retrieval where the system **automatically assumes** that the top-$K$ documents returned by an initial retrieval pass are *relevant*, and uses these "pseudo-relevant" documents to expand or modify the original query, without requiring any explicit user feedback.

Mathematically, given an initial query $\vec{q}_0$ and a ranked list of documents from the first-pass retrieval, PRF selects the top-$K$ documents $\mathcal{D}_{pr} = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_K\}$ and constructs an expanded query $\vec{q}_{m}$ that is then used for a second-pass retrieval.

The technique was formalized in the context of the **Rocchio relevance feedback model** (1971) and later refined through probabilistic approaches such as the **Relevance Model (RM1, RM2, RM3)** by Lavrenko and Croft (2001).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Librarian's Guess" Intuition

Imagine you walk into a large library and ask the librarian for books on "**machine learning**". The librarian doesn't ask you anything back — instead, they quickly pull out **20 books** they think are most relevant based on the title alone. They then look at the *index terms, chapter titles, and frequently occurring technical words* in those 20 books, and use them to refine a more targeted search.

> [!NOTE]
> **The catch?** The librarian is making a blind assumption. If some of the initial 20 books are actually about *sewing machines* (a common IR pitfall called **query drift**), the refinement will go off-topic. This is precisely the trade-off in PRF — *automation and speed versus the risk of topic drift*.

- In this analogy:
  - **Initial retrieval** = librarian's first guess of 20 books
  - **Top-K documents** = the 20 books picked
  - **Query expansion** = the librarian scanning chapter titles/index terms
  - **Second-pass retrieval** = the refined, expanded search
  - **Query drift** = mistakenly pulling "sewing machine" books

## 1.3 Why PRF Matters in Modern IR

| Aspect | Value in Engineering Practice |
|--------|-------------------------------|
| **Zero User Effort** | No explicit judgments required |
| **Recall Boost** | Significantly improves recall (typically 10–30%) |
| **Application** | Web search, legal e-discovery, biomedical IR |
| **Risk** | Query drift, increased computational cost |

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes that PRF is a **Module-1 cornerstone** that bridges classical Boolean/Vector Space retrieval with modern re-ranking and learning-to-rank pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logic

The Pseudo Relevance Feedback process follows a strict **two-pass retrieval** paradigm:

1. **Pass 1 — Initial Query Execution**
   - The user's original query $\vec{q}_0$ is submitted to the search engine.
   - Standard ranking (e.g., TF-IDF, BM25) is applied against the inverted index.
   - The system produces an initial ranked list of $N$ documents.

2. **Top-$K$ Selection**
   - The top-$K$ documents (e.g., $K = 10, 20, 50$) are selected.
   - **Critical assumption**: All these $K$ documents are *relevant*.
   - This assumption is the source of both the power and the failure modes of PRF.

3. **Term Extraction and Weighting**
   - Content terms (non-stopwords) are extracted from the top-$K$ documents.
   - Each term $t$ is assigned a weight based on its distribution:
     - High frequency in pseudo-relevant docs
     - Low frequency in the entire collection (IDF-like)

4. **Query Reformulation**
   - The original query $\vec{q}_0$ is augmented with the top-$m$ weighted terms.
   - The new query $\vec{q}_m$ becomes a weighted combination.

5. **Pass 2 — Re-retrieval**
   - The expanded query $\vec{q}_m$ is executed against the index.
   - A refined ranked list is returned to the user.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All key formulas are summarized below. **Important**: Vertical bars `|` are rendered as `\vert` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Constants/Parameters |
|---|---------|-------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | $\vec{q}_{m} = \alpha \vec{q}_{0} + \beta \dfrac{1}{\vert D_{r} \vert} \sum_{\vec{d}_j \in D_{r}} \vec{d}_j - \gamma \dfrac{1}{\vert D_{nr} \vert} \sum_{\vec{d}_j \in D_{nr}} \vec{d}_j$ | **Rocchio's Optimal Query** (full form) | $\alpha = 1.0$, $\beta = 0.75$, $\gamma = 0.15$ |
| 2 | $\vec{q}_{m} = \alpha \vec{q}_{0} + \beta \dfrac{1}{\vert D_{r} \vert} \sum_{\vec{d}_j \in D_{r}} \vec{d}_j$ | **Rocchio's formula (PRF version, no non-relevant docs assumed)** | $\alpha = 1.0$, $\beta = 0.75$ |
| 3 | $P(t \vert R) \approx \sum_{D \in D_{pr}} P(t \vert D) \cdot P(D \vert \vec{q})$ | **Relevance Model RM1** (Lavrenko-Croft 2001) | Top $K = 10$ to $50$ |
| 4 | $P(t \vert R) = \sum_{D \in D_{pr}} \prod_{q_i \in q} P(q_i \vert D) \cdot P(t \vert D) / K$ | **Relevance Model RM2** | $K = 20$ typical |
| 5 | $\vec{q}_{RM3} = (1-\lambda)\vec{q}_{0} + \lambda \vec{q}_{RM1}$ | **RM3 Hybrid Query** | $\lambda = 0.5$ to $0.9$ |
| 6 | $\text{score}(q_{m}, d) = \sum_{t \in q_{m}} w_{t,d} \cdot w_{t,q_{m}}$ | **Final ranking score** using expanded query | Cosine / BM25 |
| 7 | $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{\vert \text{Retrieved} \cap \text{Relevant} \vert}{\vert \text{Relevant} \vert}$ | **Recall** (PRF primarily boosts this) | 0 to 1 |
| 8 | $\text{Drift} = 1 - \cos(\vec{q}_{0}, \vec{q}_{m})$ | **Query Drift Metric** (semantic divergence) | Lower is better |

> [!TIP]
> **Symbols decoded**: $\vec{q}_{0}$ = original query vector, $\vec{q}_{m}$ = modified query vector, $D_{r}$ = relevant documents, $D_{nr}$ = non-relevant documents, $D_{pr}$ = pseudo-relevant documents, $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = mixing weights (empirically tuned).

## 2.3 Why PRF Works — The Mathematical Intuition

PRF exploits the **cluster hypothesis**: *documents relevant to the same query tend to be similar to each other in vector space*. By averaging the vectors of the top-$K$ documents, the system computes a **centroid of pseudo-relevance** and shifts the original query vector toward this centroid. This is a **gradient-like ascent** in the direction of estimated relevance.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web Search Engines** (Google, Bing) use PRF-like techniques in long-tail queries.
- **Legal E-Discovery** uses PRF to surface related case-law documents.
- **Biomedical IR** (PubMed) uses PRF to expand acronyms and synonyms.
- **Enterprise Search** uses PRF to handle corporate jargon.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Rocchio's Optimal Query Formula

### 3.1.1 The Optimization Objective

We want to find a modified query vector $\vec{q}_{m}$ that **maximizes similarity** to relevant documents while **minimizing similarity** to non-relevant documents. The cosine similarity between a query $\vec{q}$ and a document $\vec{d}$ is:

$$
\cos(\vec{q}, \vec{d}) = \frac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert \vec{q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{d} \vert}
$$

Since the document norm $\vert \vec{d} \vert$ is independent of $\vec{q}$, maximizing cosine similarity is equivalent to maximizing the dot product $\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}$.

### 3.1.2 Setting Up the Optimization

The objective function to maximize is:

$$
F(\vec{q}_{m}) = \frac{1}{\vert D_{r} \vert} \sum_{\vec{d}_j \in D_{r}} \cos(\vec{q}_{m}, \vec{d}_j) - \frac{1}{\vert D_{nr} \vert} \sum_{\vec{d}_j \in D_{nr}} \cos(\vec{q}_{m}, \vec{d}_j)
$$

**Step 1**: Substitute the cosine form, drop document-side normalization (constants w.r.t. $\vec{q}_{m}$):

$$
F(\vec{q}_{m}) \propto \frac{1}{\vert D_{r} \vert} \sum_{\vec{d}_j \in D_{r}} \vec{q}_{m} \cdot \vec{d}_j - \frac{1}{\vert D_{nr} \vert} \sum_{\vec{d}_j \in D_{nr}} \vec{q}_{m} \cdot \vec{d}_j
$$

**Step 2**: Apply linearity of the dot product to factor out $\vec{q}_{m}$:

$$
F(\vec{q}_{m}) \propto \vec{q}_{m} \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{\vert D_{r} \vert} \sum_{\vec{d}_j \in D_{r}} \vec{d}_j - \frac{1}{\vert D_{nr} \vert} \sum_{\vec{d}_j \in D_{nr}} \vec{d}_j \right]
$$

**Step 3**: Recognize that the term in brackets is a vector pointing in the **optimal direction** for $\vec{q}_{m}$. The maximum of $\vec{q}_{m} \cdot \vec{v}$ occurs when $\vec{q}_{m}$ is parallel to $\vec{v}$.

**Step 4**: Add the original query as an anchor (to prevent over-amplification) and introduce scaling weights $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$:

$$
\vec{q}_{m} = \alpha \vec{q}_{0} + \beta \frac{1}{\vert D_{r} \vert} \sum_{\vec{d}_j \in D_{r}} \vec{d}_j - \gamma \frac{1}{\vert D_{nr} \vert} \sum_{\vec{d}_j \in D_{nr}} \vec{d}_j
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic summary**: We start with a *similarity maximization* goal, reduce it to a *vector direction problem* using dot-product linearity, identify the optimal direction, and finally anchor it to the original query to prevent semantic drift.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Relevance Model (RM1)

### 3.2.1 Probabilistic Foundation

Given a query $\vec{q} = q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n$, we want to estimate the probability that a term $t$ is observed given that the query is relevant: $P(t \vert R)$.

By the **law of total probability** over the top-$K$ pseudo-relevant documents:

$$
P(t \vert R) = \sum_{D \in D_{pr}} P(t \vert D) \cdot P(D \vert R)
$$

### 3.2.2 Estimating the Components

Since $D_{pr}$ is assumed to *be* the relevance set, $P(D \vert R)$ is estimated as the probability that $D$ generated the query. Assuming query terms are conditionally independent (a Naive Bayes-style assumption):

$$
P(D \vert R) \approx P(D \vert \vec{q}) \propto P(\vec{q} \vert D) \cdot P(D)
$$

$$
P(\vec{q} \vert D) = \prod_{q_i \in \vec{q}} P(q_i \vert D)
$$

### 3.2.3 Final RM1 Equation

Combining these estimates:

$$
P(t \vert R) \approx \sum_{D \in D_{pr}} P(t \vert D) \cdot \prod_{q_i \in \vec{q}} P(q_i \vert D)
$$

where $P(t \vert D)$ is the **maximum likelihood estimate** from the document's term distribution:

$$
P(t \vert D) = \frac{\text{tf}(t, D)}{\vert D \vert}
$$

## 3.3 Numerical Example — Worked Computation

**Setup**: Let the original query be $\vec{q}_0 = \{ \text{neural, network} \}$ and the top-3 pseudo-relevant documents be:

- $D_1$: contains "neural" (×2), "network" (×3), "deep" (×4), "learning" (×5)
- $D_2$: contains "neural" (×1), "network" (×2), "deep" (×6), "model" (×2)
- $D_3$: contains "neural" (×3), "network" (×1), "training" (×3), "learning" (×2)

**Step 1**: Compute $P(t \vert D)$ for each term in each doc.

**Step 2**: Compute $P(D \vert R)$ using $P(\vec{q} \vert D) = P(\text{neural} \vert D) \cdot P(\text{network} \vert D)$.

**Step 3**: Compute $P(t \vert R)$ for candidate expansion terms: "deep", "learning", "model", "training".

**Step 4**: Rank candidates and select top-$m$ (e.g., $m = 2$) for query expansion.

**Result**: $\vec{q}_{m} = \{\text{neural}, \text{network}, \text{deep}, \text{learning}\}$ — note the addition of semantically aligned terms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The expanded query now contains terms *semantically related* to the original query, increasing the chance of retrieving more relevant documents in Pass 2.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow of Pseudo Relevance Feedback

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Original Query Q0] --> B[Query Processing Tokenize, Normalize, Stem]
    B --> C[Pass 1: Initial Retrieval]
    C --> D[Ranked Document List of N documents]
    D --> E[Select Top-K Documents]
    E --> F{Assume all K are Relevant}
    F --> G[Term Extraction and Frequency Analysis]
    G --> H[Term Weighting TF-IDF or RM1]
    H --> I[Rank Candidate Expansion Terms]
    I --> J[Select Top-M Terms]
    J --> K[Construct Expanded Query QM]
    K --> L[Pass 2: Re-retrieval with QM]
    L --> M[Final Ranked Results to User]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer [INPUT LAYER]
        Q[Original Query Q0]
    end
    subgraph Pass1Module [PASS 1 MODULE]
        IR[Initial Retrieval Engine]
        IDX[Inverted Index]
        IR <--> IDX
    end
    subgraph FeedbackModule [FEEDBACK PROCESSING]
        TK[Top-K Selector]
        TE[Term Extractor]
        TW[Term Weighting Unit]
        QR[Query Reformulator]
        TK --> TE --> TW --> QR
    end
    subgraph Pass2Module [PASS 2 MODULE]
        RE[Re-retrieval Engine]
        RR[Re-ranker]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer [OUTPUT LAYER]
        FR[Final Ranked List]
    end
    Q --> IR
    IR --> TK
    QR --> RE
    RE --> RR
    RR --> FR
```

## 4.3 Component Interaction Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CL[Core Layers of PRF]
        R[Retrieval Layer]
        F[Feedback Layer]
        E[Expansion Layer]
    end
    R -- Initial ranked list --> F
    F -- Top-K pseudo-relevant set --> E
    E -- Expanded query --> R
    R -- Re-ranked results --> U((User))
```

> [!TIP]
> The cyclic structure illustrates that PRF is a **closed-loop process** — the feedback layer refines the retrieval layer iteratively (in single-pass PRF, the loop runs only once; in iterative PRF, multiple cycles occur).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Pseudo Relevance Feedback. Mention any two advantages. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
Pseudo Relevance Feedback (PRF) is an automatic query reformulation technique in which the system assumes the top-$K$ documents from an initial retrieval are relevant and uses them to expand the original query without any user intervention.

**Two advantages**:
1. **Zero user effort** — improves retrieval without explicit relevance judgments.
2. **Recall improvement** — surfaces relevant documents that lack exact query-term overlap.

---

### Question 2: How does PRF differ from explicit (user) relevance feedback? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Aspect | Explicit Relevance Feedback | Pseudo Relevance Feedback |
|--------|------------------------------|----------------------------|
| User Involvement | Requires user to mark documents | Fully automatic |
| Source of Judgments | Human-assessed | System-assumed (top-$K$) |
| Accuracy | High (true relevance) | Lower (assumed relevance) |
| Scalability | Limited by user effort | Highly scalable |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the working of Pseudo Relevance Feedback with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the parameter $K$ (number of pseudo-relevant documents). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Working of PRF — Step-by-Step Explanation (5 marks)**:
1. The user's original query $\vec{q}_0$ is processed and submitted to the search engine.
2. **Pass 1 Retrieval**: Standard ranking algorithm (e.g., BM25) produces a ranked list of documents.
3. **Top-$K$ Selection**: The top $K$ documents are selected and *assumed relevant*.
4. **Term Analysis**: Content terms are extracted from the pseudo-relevant set, weighted using TF-IDF or the Relevance Model.
5. **Query Expansion**: The top-$M$ weighted terms are added to $\vec{q}_0$, forming $\vec{q}_m$.
6. **Pass 2 Retrieval**: $\vec{q}_m$ is executed against the index, producing a refined ranked list.

**Role of Parameter $K$ (2 marks)**:
- $K$ is the **pseudo-relevance threshold** controlling how many top documents are assumed relevant.
- **Small $K$ (e.g., 5–10)**: Low risk of including non-relevant docs, but may miss useful expansion terms → low recall gain.
- **Large $K$ (e.g., 50–100)**: More expansion terms, but higher chance of including non-relevant docs → **query drift**.
- **Empirically optimal $K$** in TREC experiments: $K = 10$ to $30$ for most collections.

---

**(b)** Derive Rocchio's relevance feedback formula starting from the cosine similarity maximization objective. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 (1.5 marks)**: Write the cosine similarity objective. The goal is to find $\vec{q}_m$ that maximizes average similarity to relevant docs and minimizes similarity to non-relevant docs:

$$
F(\vec{q}_m) = \frac{1}{\vert D_r \vert} \sum_{d_j \in D_r} \cos(\vec{q}_m, \vec{d}_j) - \frac{1}{\vert D_{nr} \vert} \sum_{d_j \in D_{nr}} \cos(\vec{q}_m, \vec{d}_j)
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks)**: Substitute $\cos(\vec{q}_m, \vec{d}) = \frac{\vec{q}_m \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert \vec{q}_m \vert \cdot \vert \vec{d} \vert}$. Since $\vert \vec{q}_m \vert$ is constant and we drop doc-side constants:

$$
F(\vec{q}_m) \propto \vec{q}_m \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{\vert D_r \vert} \sum_{d_j \in D_r} \vec{d}_j - \frac{1}{\vert D_{nr} \vert} \sum_{d_j \in D_{nr}} \vec{d}_j \right]
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks)**: The maximum of $\vec{q}_m \cdot \vec{v}$ is achieved when $\vec{q}_m$ is parallel to $\vec{v}$. So the optimal direction is the bracketed vector.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks)**: Anchor to $\vec{q}_0$ and introduce weights $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$:

$$
\vec{q}_m = \alpha \vec{q}_0 + \beta \frac{1}{\vert D_r \vert} \sum_{d_j \in D_r} \vec{d}_j - \gamma \frac{1}{\vert D_{nr} \vert} \sum_{d_j \in D_{nr}} \vec{d}_j
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark)**: For PRF, since no non-relevant judgments exist, set $\gamma = 0$:

$$
\vec{q}_m = \alpha \vec{q}_0 + \beta \frac{1}{\vert D_r \vert} \sum_{d_j \in D_r} \vec{d}_j
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss the major issues and limitations of Pseudo Relevance Feedback. How can these be mitigated? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Issue 1 — Query Drift (2 marks)**:
When top-$K$ documents contain non-relevant content (e.g., the query "Java" returns both programming and coffee docs), expansion terms push the query off-topic.
*Mitigation*: Use robust weighting (RM3 hybrid), smaller $K$, or topic-coherence filters.

**Issue 2 — Computational Overhead (1.5 marks)**:
Two retrieval passes and term-weighting computations increase latency.
*Mitigation*: Pre-compute offline statistics, cache top-K results, parallelize the second pass.

**Issue 3 — Vocabulary Mismatch Not Always Resolved (1.5 marks)**:
PRF helps with term mismatch but may not address deeper semantic gaps.
*Mitigation*: Combine with thesaurus-based expansion or word embeddings.

**Issue 4 — Sensitivity to $K$ and $M$ (2 marks)**:
Performance is highly sensitive to the choice of $K$ (number of pseudo-relevant docs) and $M$ (number of expansion terms).
*Mitigation*: Cross-validation on a development set, or use adaptive $K$ based on query specificity.

---

**(b)** Explain the Relevance Model (RM1) approach to query expansion. Show the formula and describe each component. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Conceptual Explanation (2 marks)**:
The Relevance Model (RM1), proposed by Lavrenko and Croft (2001), estimates the probability $P(t \vert R)$ that a term $t$ appears in a *relevant document*, by assuming the top-$K$ documents from initial retrieval are samples from the relevance distribution.

**The Formula (2 marks)**:

$$
P(t \vert R) \approx \sum_{D \in D_{pr}} P(t \vert D) \cdot \prod_{q_i \in \vec{q}} P(q_i \vert D)
$$

**Component Description (3 marks)**:
- $P(t \vert D) = \frac{\text{tf}(t,D)}{\vert D \vert}$ — probability of term $t$ in document $D$ (maximum likelihood estimate).
- $\prod_{q_i \in \vec{q}} P(q_i \vert D)$ — probability that the original query $\vec{q}$ was generated by document $D$ (Naive Bayes independence assumption).
- $D_{pr}$ — the set of top-$K$ pseudo-relevant documents.
- Top-$M$ terms ranked by $P(t \vert R)$ are selected and added to the original query.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks**:
> 1. **Confusing PRF with explicit feedback** in definitions (lose 1 mark).
> 2. **Forgetting to set $\gamma = 0$** in Rocchio's PRF-specific formula (lose 0.5–1 mark).
> 3. **Not mentioning query drift** when listing PRF limitations (lose 1 mark).
> 4. **Skipping the derivation of RM1** from the law of total probability (lose 2 marks in 14-mark questions).
> 5. **Drawing block diagrams without labels** — KTU examiners deduct marks for unlabeled arrows/components.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** (for last-minute KTU prep):

- ☐ **Definition**: PRF = automatic, blind feedback assuming top-$K$ docs are relevant.
- ☐ **Two-Pass Paradigm**: Initial retrieval → Top-K selection → Expansion → Re-retrieval.
- ☐ **Rocchio PRF Formula**: $\vec{q}_m = \alpha \vec{q}_0 + \beta \frac{1}{\vert D_r \vert} \sum \vec{d}_j$ (with $\gamma = 0$).
- ☐ **Standard Weights**: $\alpha = 1.0$, $\beta = 0.75$, $\gamma = 0.15$ (typical).
- ☐ **Optimal $K$**: 10–30 documents for most TREC-style collections.
- ☐ **Relevance Model (RM1)**: Uses $P(t \vert R) = \sum_{D \in D_{pr}} P(t \vert D) \cdot P(D \vert \vec{q})$.
- ☐ **RM3 Hybrid**: $(1-\lambda)\vec{q}_0 + \lambda \vec{q}_{RM1}$ — combines original and feedback.
- ☐ **Key Limitation**: **Query Drift** when non-relevant docs are in top-$K$.
- ☐ **Boosts Recall** primarily; can slightly hurt precision.
- ☐ **Real-World Use**: Web search, biomedical IR, legal e-discovery.
- ☐ **Cluster Hypothesis Underpins PRF** — relevant docs cluster in vector space.
- ☐ **Difference from Explicit RF**: No user effort, lower accuracy, highly scalable.
- ☐ **Equation must be written inside `\begin{aligned}` or `$$`** with one blank line above and below for proper LaTeX rendering in KTU answer sheets.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Retrieval Models and Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Retrieval Models and Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Retrieval Model?

A **Retrieval Model** is the formal mathematical framework that defines how a search engine represents documents and queries, and how it decides which documents are *relevant* to a given query. It is the **theoretical brain** of an Information Retrieval (IR) system — given a query $Q$, the model produces a ranking score $RSV(Q, D_i)$ for every document $D_i$ in the collection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Baeza-Yates & Ribeiro-Neto):**
> A retrieval model is a quadruple $\langle D, Q, F, R(q_i, d_j) \rangle$ where:
> - $D$ = set of representations of documents
> - $Q$ = set of representations of user queries
> - $F$ = framework for modeling document–query representations (boolean, algebraic, probabilistic)
> - $R(q_i, d_j)$ = ranking function that orders documents by estimated relevance

## 1.2 What is Evaluation in IR?

**IR Evaluation** is the systematic measurement of how *good* the ranked list returned by a retrieval system is, against a ground-truth set of relevant documents. KTU 2024 emphasises metrics such as **Precision**, **Recall**, **F-Measure**, **Mean Average Precision (MAP)**, **Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR)**, and **Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG)**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Library Assistant"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you walk into a huge library with 1 million books and ask the assistant, *"I want books about climate change and renewable energy."* The assistant must:
> 1. **Understand** your words (representation),
> 2. **Compare** your request against every book (matching),
> 3. **Score** and **rank** the books (ranking function),
> 4. **Tell you how good** their last 10,000 answers were (evaluation).
>
> A **retrieval model** is the *scoring engine* of this assistant. **Evaluation** is the *quality inspector* who judges whether the assistant's answers are actually useful.

The three classical families of retrieval models differ in *how* they score and rank:

| Family | Core Idea | Ranking Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Boolean Model** | Set-theoretic exact match | Unranked (or weak) |
| **Vector Space Model (VSM)** | Geometric angle between vectors | Continuous score |
| **Probabilistic Model** | Probability of relevance | Probability $P(R=1 \vert d,q)$ |
| **Language Model (LM)** | Probability of query given document | $P(q \vert d)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:** The **Vector Space Model (TF-IDF + Cosine Similarity)** and the **BM25 probabilistic model** are the two most-asked retrieval models in KTU end-semester papers. Master these.

---

## 1.4 Boolean Retrieval Model

Documents and queries are represented as sets of index terms combined with Boolean operators **AND**, **OR**, **NOT**. A document is either *relevant* (1) or *not relevant* (0) — no partial credit.

$$d_j \text{ is relevant to } q \iff q(d_j) = \text{true}$$

**Drawbacks (often asked as 3-mark questions):**
- No ranking → all matching docs look equally good.
- Users must master Boolean syntax.
- "Information need" is forced into a rigid expression.

## 1.5 Vector Space Model (VSM)

Documents and queries are represented as vectors in a high-dimensional space (one dimension per unique term). Relevance is measured by the **cosine of the angle** between the query vector $\vec{q}$ and document vector $\vec{d}$:

$$\text{sim}(q,d) = \cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert\vec{q}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{d}\vert}$$

Terms are weighted using **TF-IDF** (explained exhaustively in Section 3). VSM gives a *continuous ranking score* and is the foundation of modern search.

## 1.6 Probabilistic Model (BM25)

The **Best Match 25 (BM25)** model ranks documents by estimating the probability that a document is relevant to a query. The famous **Robertson–Sparck Jones** form is:

$$RSV(d,q) = \sum_{t \in q} \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \frac{f(t,d) \cdot (k_1+1)}{f(t,d) + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{\vert d \vert}{\text{avgdl}}\right)}$$

The parameters $k_1$ (typically **1.2**) and $b$ (typically **0.75**) tune term-frequency saturation and document-length normalization. BM25 is used by **Elasticsearch, Lucene, and most production-grade search engines**.

## 1.7 Language Model (Query Likelihood)

A document $d$ is modelled as a probability distribution over words. The ranking score is the **likelihood that the document's language model generated the query**:

$$RSV(d,q) = P(d) \cdot P(q \vert d) \approx \prod_{t \in q} P(t \vert d)^{c(t,q)}$$

Smoothing (e.g., **Jelinek–Mercer** or **Dirichlet prior**) is essential to handle zero probabilities for unseen terms.

## 1.8 IR Evaluation — Why We Need It

A model is *useless* unless we can measure its quality. KTU 2024 focuses on:

- **Precision** — "Of what I returned, how much is correct?"
- **Recall** — "Of all correct items, how many did I return?"
- **F-Measure** — harmonic mean of the two.
- **MAP** — mean of average precision across multiple queries.
- **NDCG** — graded relevance with positional discount.
- **MRR** — rewards returning the first correct answer high.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ground Truth:** Evaluation requires a **test collection** = (documents, queries, relevance judgments) — e.g., **TREC, CACM, Cranfield, GOV2**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Boolean Model — Operational Steps

1. Build an **inverted index** mapping each term → posting list of doc-IDs.
2. Parse the Boolean query into a syntax tree (AND, OR, NOT).
3. Traverse the tree by **merging posting lists** in sorted order (complexity $O(N)$ where $N$ = total postings).
4. Return the resulting set of doc-IDs (unranked).

**Strengths:** Predictable, easy to implement, fast.
**Weaknesses:** All retrieved docs are *equally* good → user must over-specify.

## 2.2 The Vector Space Model — Operational Steps

1. **Tokenize, normalize** (lowercase, stemming) the corpus to build vocabulary $V$.
2. For each term $t$, compute **Inverse Document Frequency**:
$$\text{IDF}(t) = \log \frac{N}{df(t)}$$
where $N$ = total docs, $df(t)$ = docs containing $t$.
3. For each document $d$, compute **TF-IDF vector** $\vec{d}$ with components:
$$w_{t,d} = tf(t,d) \cdot \text{IDF}(t)$$
4. For query $q$, build vector $\vec{q}$ similarly.
5. Compute **Cosine Similarity** for every $(q,d)$ pair and sort descending.

## 2.3 Why Cosine Similarity? The "Why & How"

- **Why not dot product?** Dot product grows with document length — a long doc with the same term would unfairly outrank a short, focused one. Cosine *normalizes* by length.
- **How it works:** Treats each document as a direction in term-space; the angle (not the magnitude) captures topical similarity.

## 2.4 BM25 — The "Why & How" of the Formula

- The factor $\frac{f(t,d)(k_1+1)}{f(t,d)+k_1(1-b+b\frac{\vert d \vert}{\text{avgdl}})}$ is a **saturation function**: adding more occurrences of a term helps, but with *diminishing returns*. The 11th occurrence matters much less than the 2nd.
- $b$ controls how much we **penalize long documents**. $b=0$ → no length normalization; $b=1$ → full normalization.
- $k_1$ controls how quickly saturation sets in. Higher $k_1$ → more weight to $tf$.

## 2.5 Language Model — Why Smoothing?

Without smoothing, $P(t \vert d) = 0$ for any term $t$ not in $d$, which would zero out the entire query likelihood. Smoothing "borrows" probability mass from the whole collection:

$$P_{JM}(t \vert d) = (1-\lambda) P_{ml}(t \vert d) + \lambda P(t \vert C)$$

where $\lambda$ (typically **0.1–0.7**) balances document vs. collection evidence.

## 2.6 Evaluation Metrics — The "Why & How"

| Why we need it | How it is computed |
|---|---|
| Users care about *precision* — few junk results | $\text{P} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ |
| Lawyers/scientists care about *recall* — don't miss anything | $\text{R} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ |
| P and R trade off — we need a single number | $F_\beta = \frac{(1+\beta^2) PR}{\beta^2 P + R}$; $F_1$ uses $\beta=1$ |
| Web queries have *graded* relevance (0,1,2,3) | NDCG (rank-aware, normalized) |
| "First correct answer high" is critical for QA | MRR |
| Multiple queries must be averaged | MAP |

## 2.7 KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is your exam-day rescue sheet.** Memorize the equation column.

| Concept | Equation | Notes / Constants |
|---|---|---|
| Term Frequency (raw) | $tf(t,d) = f_{t,d}$ | Raw count |
| Inverse Doc Frequency | $\text{IDF}(t) = \log \frac{N}{df(t)+1} + 1$ or $\log \frac{N}{df(t)}$ | **$N$** = total docs |
| TF-IDF weight | $w_{t,d} = tf(t,d) \cdot \text{IDF}(t)$ | Used in VSM |
| Cosine Similarity | $\text{sim}(q,d) = \frac{\sum_{t} w_{t,q} w_{t,d}}{\sqrt{\sum_{t} w_{t,q}^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{t} w_{t,d}^2}}$ | Range $[0,1]$ |
| Precision | $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Range $[0,1]$ |
| Recall | $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Range $[0,1]$ |
| F-Measure ($F_1$) | $F_1 = \frac{2 P R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean |
