| $F_\beta$ | $F_\beta = \frac{(1+\beta^2) P R}{\beta^2 P + R}$ | $\beta > 1$ weighs recall more |
| Average Precision | $AP = \frac{1}{\vert R \vert} \sum_{k: \text{rel}(k)=1} P@k$ | Per query |
| MAP | $MAP = \frac{1}{\vert Q \vert} \sum_{q \in Q} AP(q)$ | Over all queries |
| MRR | $MRR = \frac{1}{\vert Q \vert} \sum_{q} \frac{1}{\text{rank}_q}$ | Rank of first hit |
| NDCG | $NDCG@k = \frac{DCG@k}{IDCG@k}$, $DCG@k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{rel_i}{\log_2(i+1)}$ | Graded relevance |
| BM25 score | $\sum_{t \in q} \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \frac{f(t,d)(k_1+1)}{f(t,d)+k_1(1-b+b\frac{\vert d \vert}{\text{avgdl}})}$ | $k_1 \approx 1.2$, $b \approx 0.75$ |
| Query Likelihood LM | $P(q \vert d) = \prod_{t \in q} P(t \vert d)^{c(t,q)}$ | With smoothing |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web Search Engines** (Google, Bing) use **BM25 + neural re-rankers**.
- **E-commerce Search** (Amazon, Flipkart) uses **VSM + click-through signals**.
- **Legal/Patent Search** prioritizes **Recall** (use $F_2$).
- **Conversational QA / Chatbots** optimize for **MRR** (first correct answer matters most).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Building a TF-IDF Index and Ranking with Cosine Similarity

> [!NOTE]
> **Corpus:** 4 tiny documents
> - $D_1$: "the cat sat on the mat"
> - $D_2$: "the dog sat on the rug"
> - $D_3$: "the cat and the dog played"
> - $D_4$: "the mat the rug the mat"
>
> **Query $Q$:** "the cat"
> **Task:** Rank documents using VSM with cosine similarity.

### Step 1 — Build the vocabulary and document frequency

Vocabulary $V = \{ \text{the, cat, sat, on, mat, dog, rug, and, played} \}$, so $\vert V \vert = 9$.

**Total documents $N = 4$.**

| Term | $df(t)$ | Appears in |
|---|---|---|
| the | 4 | $D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$ |
| cat | 2 | $D_1, D_3$ |
| sat | 2 | $D_1, D_2$ |
| on | 2 | $D_1, D_2$ |
| mat | 2 | $D_1, D_4$ |
| dog | 2 | $D_2, D_3$ |
| rug | 2 | $D_2, D_4$ |
| and | 1 | $D_3$ |
| played | 1 | $D_3$ |

### Step 2 — Compute $\text{IDF}(t) = \log_{10} \frac{N}{df(t)}$

Use base-10 logarithm (as per Manning/Raghavan convention).

$$\text{IDF}(\text{the}) = \log_{10}\frac{4}{4} = \log_{10}(1) = 0$$

$$\text{IDF}(\text{cat}) = \log_{10}\frac{4}{2} = \log_{10}(2) \approx 0.301$$

$$\text{IDF}(\text{sat}) = \log_{10}\frac{4}{2} = 0.301$$

$$\text{IDF}(\text{on}) = \log_{10}\frac{4}{2} = 0.301$$

$$\text{IDF}(\text{mat}) = \log_{10}\frac{4}{2} = 0.301$$

$$\text{IDF}(\text{dog}) = \log_{10}\frac{4}{2} = 0.301$$

$$\text{IDF}(\text{rug}) = \log_{10}\frac{4}{2} = 0.301$$

$$\text{IDF}(\text{and}) = \log_{10}\frac{4}{1} \approx 0.602$$

$$\text{IDF}(\text{played}) = \log_{10}\frac{4}{1} \approx 0.602$$

> Notice $\text{IDF}(\text{the}) = 0$ — the word "the" carries no discriminative power and gets filtered out automatically. This is a core intuition you **must** explain in exams.

### Step 3 — Compute TF-IDF vectors for each document

Weight for a term = $tf \cdot \text{IDF}$.

**Document $D_1$ = "the cat sat on the mat"** — term counts: the=3, cat=1, sat=1, on=1, mat=1.

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{the,D_1} &= 3 \times 0 = 0 \\
w_{cat,D_1} &= 1 \times 0.301 = 0.301 \\
w_{sat,D_1} &= 1 \times 0.301 = 0.301 \\
w_{on,D_1} &= 1 \times 0.301 = 0.301 \\
w_{mat,D_1} &= 1 \times 0.301 = 0.301
\end{aligned}
$$

**Document $D_3$ = "the cat and the dog played"** — the=2, cat=1, and=1, dog=1, played=1.

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{the,D_3} &= 2 \times 0 = 0 \\
w_{cat,D_3} &= 1 \times 0.301 = 0.301 \\
w_{and,D_3} &= 1 \times 0.602 = 0.602 \\
w_{dog,D_3} &= 1 \times 0.301 = 0.301 \\
w_{played,D_3} &= 1 \times 0.602 = 0.602
\end{aligned}
$$

**Document $D_2$ = "the dog sat on the rug"** — contains neither "cat" nor "the" as a weight-bearing term. $w_{cat,D_2} = 0$.

**Document $D_4$ = "the mat the rug the mat"** — the=3, mat=2, rug=1. $w_{cat,D_4} = 0$.

### Step 4 — Build the query vector $Q$ = "the cat"

Query counts: the=1, cat=1.

$$
w_{the,Q} = 1 \times 0 = 0 \quad ; \quad w_{cat,Q} = 1 \times 0.301 = 0.301
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Cosine Similarity $\text{sim}(Q, D_i)$ for each document

Recall the cosine similarity:

$$\text{sim}(Q, D) = \frac{\sum_{t \in V} w_{t,Q} \cdot w_{t,D}}{\sqrt{\sum_{t} w_{t,Q}^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{t} w_{t,D}^2}}$$

**For $D_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= w_{the,Q} w_{the,D_1} + w_{cat,Q} w_{cat,D_1} \\
&= (0)(0) + (0.301)(0.301) = 0.0906 \\
\text{Denom } \vert Q \vert &= \sqrt{0^2 + 0.301^2} = 0.301 \\
\vert D_1 \vert &= \sqrt{0^2 + 0.301^2 + 0.301^2 + 0.301^2 + 0.301^2} \\
&= \sqrt{4 \times 0.301^2} = 2 \times 0.301 = 0.602 \\
\text{sim}(Q, D_1) &= \frac{0.0906}{0.301 \times 0.602} = \frac{0.0906}{0.1812} = 0.500
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $D_3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= w_{cat,Q} w_{cat,D_3} = (0.301)(0.301) = 0.0906 \\
\vert Q \vert &= 0.301 \\
\vert D_3 \vert &= \sqrt{0.301^2 + 0.602^2 + 0.301^2 + 0.602^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.0906 + 0.3624 + 0.0906 + 0.3624} = \sqrt{0.906} \approx 0.952 \\
\text{sim}(Q, D_3) &= \frac{0.0906}{0.301 \times 0.952} = \frac{0.0906}{0.2866} \approx 0.316
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $D_2$ and $D_4$:** Both have $w_{cat} = 0$, so **numerator = 0** → similarity = **0**.

### Step 6 — Final Ranking

| Document | $\text{sim}(Q, D)$ | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| $D_1$ | **0.500** | 🥇 1 |
| $D_3$ | **0.316** | 🥈 2 |
| $D_2$ | 0 | 3 |
| $D_4$ | 0 | 3 |

> **Intuition:** $D_1$ outranks $D_3$ because the term "cat" is more *concentrated* in $D_1$ (it has fewer other terms to dilute it). Cosine captures this beautifully.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Computing Precision, Recall, F1, MAP

> **Test Set:** Suppose for 5 queries, the ground truth relevant docs and the system returned docs are:

| Query | Relevant Docs (R) | Returned Docs (A) |
|---|---|---|
| $Q_1$ | $\{D_1, D_2, D_5\}$ | $\{D_1, D_3, D_5, D_2\}$ |
| $Q_2$ | $\{D_4, D_7\}$ | $\{D_4, D_8\}$ |
| $Q_3$ | $\{D_2, D_3, D_6, D_9\}$ | $\{D_2, D_3, D_7, D_9\}$ |

### Step 1 — Compute TP, FP, FN per query

**Query $Q_1$:** $A \cap R = \{D_1, D_2, D_5\}$ → **TP = 3**. Returned $=4$ → **FP = 1**. Relevant $=3$ → **FN = 0**.

$$
P_{Q_1} = \frac{3}{3+1} = 0.75 \quad ; \quad R_{Q_1} = \frac{3}{3+0} = 1.00
$$

**Query $Q_2$:** $A \cap R = \{D_4\}$ → **TP = 1**. Returned $=2$ → **FP = 1**. Relevant $=2$ → **FN = 1**.

$$
P_{Q_2} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.50 \quad ; \quad R_{Q_2} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.50
$$

**Query $Q_3$:** $A \cap R = \{D_2, D_3, D_9\}$ → **TP = 3**. Returned $=4$ → **FP = 1**. Relevant $=4$ → **FN = 1**.

$$
P_{Q_3} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75 \quad ; \quad R_{Q_3} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75
$$

### Step 2 — F1 per query

$$
F_1(Q_1) = \frac{2 \times 0.75 \times 1.00}{0.75 + 1.00} = \frac{1.50}{1.75} \approx 0.857
$$

$$
F_1(Q_2) = \frac{2 \times 0.50 \times 0.50}{0.50 + 0.50} = \frac{0.50}{1.00} = 0.500
$$

$$
F_1(Q_3) = \frac{2 \times 0.75 \times 0.75}{0.75 + 0.75} = \frac{1.125}{1.50} = 0.750
$$

### Step 3 — Average Precision and MAP

Average Precision requires a *ranked* list. Suppose the system returns for $Q_1$ in this order: $[D_1, D_3, D_5, D_2]$.

| Rank $k$ | Doc | Relevant? | Precision@k |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_1$ | ✅ | $1/1 = 1.00$ |
| 2 | $D_3$ | ❌ | $1/2 = 0.50$ |
| 3 | $D_5$ | ✅ | $2/3 \approx 0.667$ |
| 4 | $D_2$ | ✅ | $3/4 = 0.75$ |

$$
AP(Q_1) = \frac{1}{\vert R \vert} \sum_{k:\text{rel}(k)=1} P@k = \frac{1}{3}(1.00 + 0.667 + 0.75) = \frac{2.417}{3} \approx 0.806
$$

Assuming $AP(Q_2) = 1.00$ (if $D_4$ at rank 1) and $AP(Q_3) = 0.806$ (similar):

$$
MAP = \frac{AP(Q_1) + AP(Q_2) + AP(Q_3)}{3} = \frac{0.806 + 1.00 + 0.806}{3} \approx 0.871
$$

### Step 4 — MRR Example

If for $Q_1$ the first relevant doc is at rank 1, for $Q_2$ at rank 2, for $Q_3$ at rank 1:

$$
MRR = \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{1}\right) = \frac{2.5}{3} \approx 0.833
$$

### Step 5 — NDCG Example (Graded Relevance)

Suppose a ranking with relevance grades $[3, 1, 2, 0, 2]$ and ideal ranking $[3, 2, 2, 1, 0]$ at $k=5$.

$$
DCG@5 = \frac{3}{\log_2 2} + \frac{1}{\log_2 3} + \frac{2}{\log_2 4} + \frac{0}{\log_2 5} + \frac{2}{\log_2 6}
$$

$$
DCG@5 = \frac{3}{1} + \frac{1}{1.585} + \frac{2}{2} + 0 + \frac{2}{2.585} = 3 + 0.631 + 1 + 0 + 0.774 = 5.405
$$

$$
IDCG@5 = \frac{3}{1} + \frac{2}{1.585} + \frac{2}{2} + \frac{1}{2.585} + \frac{0}{2.807} = 3 + 1.262 + 1 + 0.387 + 0 = 5.649
$$

$$
NDCG@5 = \frac{DCG@5}{IDCG@5} = \frac{5.405}{5.649} \approx 0.957
$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of an Information Retrieval System with Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Query] --> B[Query Preprocessor]
    B -->|tokens| C[Query Representation]
    C -->|vector q| D[Retrieval Engine]
    G[Document Collection] --> H[Inverted Index Builder]
    H -->|postings| D
    D -->|BM25 / VSM / LM| I[Ranking Module]
    I -->|ranked docs| J[Output to User]
    J --> K[User Click Log]
    K --> L[Evaluation Module]
    M[Ground Truth qrels] --> L
    L --> N[Precision / Recall / F1 / MAP / NDCG / MRR]
    N --> O[System Performance Report]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **upper branch** (A → B → C) is the *query pipeline* — text becomes a vector.
- The **left branch** (G → H) is the *indexing pipeline* — corpus becomes an inverted index.
- These merge at the **Retrieval Engine**, which applies a **Retrieval Model** (Boolean / VSM / Probabilistic / LM).
- The **ranking module** outputs a list, and the **evaluation module** measures quality using a ground-truth benchmark.

## 4.2 Comparative Flow of the Four Classical Retrieval Models

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Document d and Query q] --> B{Which Model?}
    B -->|Boolean| C[Set Operations AND OR NOT]
    C --> C1[Unranked set of docs]
    B -->|Vector Space| D[Build TF-IDF Vectors]
    D --> D1[Compute Cosine Similarity]
    D1 --> D2[Ranked list by angle]
    B -->|Probabilistic BM25| E[Compute Term Saturation]
    E --> E1[Apply Length Normalization]
    E1 --> E2[Ranked list by RSV]
    B -->|Language Model| F[Estimate P q given d]
    F --> F1[Apply Smoothing JM or Dirichlet]
    F1 --> F2[Ranked list by likelihood]
    C1 --> G[Evaluation: P R F1 MAP NDCG]
    D2 --> G
    E2 --> G
    F2 --> G
```

## 4.3 The Evaluation Process — Confusion-Matrix-to-Metric Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Output: Ranked List] --> B[Compare with Ground Truth qrels]
    B --> C[Build Confusion Matrix]
    C -->|TP FP FN TN| D[Compute Basic Metrics]
    D --> E[Precision]
    D --> F[Recall]
    E --> G[F-Measure]
    F --> G
    D --> H[Precision-Recall Curve]
    H --> I[Average Precision per query]
    I --> J[MAP across queries]
    A --> K[Track Rank of First Hit]
    K --> L[MRR across queries]
    A --> M[Apply Logarithmic Discount]
    M --> N[DCG]
    N --> O[IDCG from ideal ranking]
    O --> P[NDCG normalized score]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the Boolean Retrieval Model and the Vector Space Model. List two advantages of VSM over the Boolean model.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Boolean Model | Vector Space Model |
|---|---|---|
| Representation | Sets of terms with AND/OR/NOT | Vectors of weighted terms |
| Matching | Exact match (binary) | Partial match (continuous score) |
| Ranking | No ranking (unranked output) | Ranked by cosine similarity |
| Term weighting | None (presence/absence only) | TF-IDF weighting |
| Query logic | Rigid Boolean expression | Natural keyword list |
| User expertise | Must know Boolean operators | No special syntax needed |

**Two advantages of VSM over Boolean:**
1. **Continuous relevance score** — partial matches are rewarded; the user gets a *ranked* output instead of a flat set.
2. **Term weighting via TF-IDF** — common words like "the" are automatically downweighted, while discriminative words are boosted. Boolean model treats "the" and a rare keyword as equally important.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "VSM is faster" — *wrong*. Boolean is usually faster because of inverted-index set operations. VSM's advantage is *quality*, not speed.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the concept of Precision and Recall with a suitable example. Why is there a trade-off between them?**

**Model Answer:**

**Precision** = "Of all the documents I *returned*, how many are *actually relevant*?"

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

**Recall** = "Of all the documents that *are relevant*, how many did I *manage to return*?"

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**Example:** Suppose 10 documents are truly relevant and the system returns 8 documents, of which 6 are relevant.

$$
P = \frac{6}{8} = 0.75 \quad ; \quad R = \frac{6}{10} = 0.60
$$

**Why the trade-off exists:** If the system returns *more* documents to boost Recall (catch all 10), it will inevitably also include some non-relevant ones, lowering Precision. Conversely, if it returns only the *most confident* few to boost Precision, it risks missing some relevant ones, lowering Recall. They move in **opposite directions** as you vary the result-list size $k$ — hence the **Precision-Recall curve**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** A typical Module-2 long question carries 14 marks with sub-parts of 7+7. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Consider the following four documents:**
- $D_1$: "information retrieval is fun"
- $D_2$: "retrieval systems index information"
- $D_3$: "search engines use information retrieval"
- $D_4$: "indexing is essential for retrieval"

**Query $Q$:** "information retrieval"

**(a) [7 Marks] Build the TF-IDF vectors for the documents and the query. Compute the cosine similarity between the query and each document. Show all working clearly.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Vocabulary and Document Frequencies**

Vocabulary $V = \{ \text{information, retrieval, is, fun, systems, index, search, engines, use, indexing, essential, for} \}$. $N = 4$.

| Term | $df(t)$ | IDF = $\log_{10}\frac{N}{df(t)}$ |
|---|---|---|
| information | 3 | $\log_{10}(4/3) \approx 0.124$ |
| retrieval | 4 | $\log_{10}(4/4) = 0$ |
| is | 2 | $\log_{10}(4/2) \approx 0.301$ |
| fun | 1 | $\log_{10}(4/1) \approx 0.602$ |
| systems | 1 | $\log_{10}(4/1) \approx 0.602$ |
| index | 1 | $\log_{10}(4/1) \approx 0.602$ |
| indexing | 1 | $\log_{10}(4/1) \approx 0.602$ |
| search | 1 | $\log_{10}(4/1) \approx 0.602$ |
| engines | 1 | $\log_{10}(4/1) \approx 0.602$ |
| use | 1 | $\log_{10}(4/1) \approx 0.602$ |
| essential | 1 | $\log_{10}(4/1) \approx 0.602$ |
| for | 1 | $\log_{10}(4/1) \approx 0.602$ |

**Step 2 — Term Frequencies and TF-IDF**

For $D_1$: tf(information)=1, tf(retrieval)=1, tf(is)=1, tf(fun)=1.

$$
w_{info,D_1} = 1 \times 0.124 = 0.124 \quad ; \quad w_{retr,D_1} = 1 \times 0 = 0
$$

For $D_2$: tf(information)=1, tf(retrieval)=1, tf(systems)=1, tf(index)=1.

$$
w_{info,D_2} = 0.124 \quad ; \quad w_{retr,D_2} = 0
$$

For $D_3$: tf(information)=1, tf(retrieval)=1, tf(search)=1, tf(engines)=1, tf(use)=1.

$$
w_{info,D_3} = 0.124 \quad ; \quad w_{retr,D_3} = 0
$$

For $D_4$: tf(retrieval)=1, tf(is)=1, tf(indexing)=1, tf(essential)=1, tf(for)=1.

$$
w_{info,D_4} = 0 \quad ; \quad w_{retr,D_4} = 0
$$

**Query $Q$:** "information retrieval" → $w_{info,Q} = 0.124$, $w_{retr,Q} = 0$.

**Step 3 — Cosine Similarity (only the "information" term contributes since retrieval's weight is 0)**

$$
\text{sim}(Q, D) = \frac{w_{info,Q} \cdot w_{info,D}}{\sqrt{w_{info,Q}^2} \cdot \sqrt{w_{info,D}^2 + \text{(other weighted terms)}^2}}
$$

For $D_1$:

$$
\text{sim}(Q, D_1) = \frac{0.124 \times 0.124}{0.124 \times \sqrt{0.124^2 + 0^2 + 0.301^2 + 0.602^2}} = \frac{0.124}{\sqrt{0.0154 + 0 + 0.0906 + 0.3624}} = \frac{0.124}{0.685} \approx 0.181
$$

For $D_2$:

$$
\text{sim}(Q, D_2) = \frac{0.124}{0.124 \times \sqrt{0.124^2 + 0.602^2 + 0.602^2 + 0.602^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.0154 + 0.3624 + 0.3624 + 0.3624}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.1026}} \approx 0.953
$$

For $D_3$:

$$
\text{sim}(Q, D_3) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.0154 + 0 + 0.602^2 + 0.602^2 + 0.602^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.0154 + 1.0872}} = \frac{1}{1.051} \approx 0.951
$$

For $D_4$:

$$
\text{sim}(Q, D_4) = 0 \quad \text{(no "information" in } D_4)
$$

**Final ranking:** $D_2 \succ D_3 \succ D_1 \succ D_4$.

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the BM25 ranking formula. Using the same corpus above, compute the BM25 score for each document with respect to the query "information retrieval" (assume $k_1 = 1.2$, $b = 0.75$, and average document length avgdl = 4.5).**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — The BM25 formula**

$$
RSV(d,q) = \sum_{t \in q} \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \frac{f(t,d)(k_1+1)}{f(t,d) + k_1\left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{\vert d \vert}{\text{avgdl}}\right)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute BM25 components**

The query has two unique terms: "information" and "retrieval".

- $k_1 + 1 = 1.2 + 1 = 2.2$
- $\text{IDF}(\text{information}) \approx 0.124$
- $\text{IDF}(\text{retrieval}) = 0$

Since $\text{IDF}(\text{retrieval}) = 0$, only the "information" term contributes non-trivially to the score.

**Step 3 — Score each document**

For $D_1$ ($\vert d \vert = 4$): the saturation denominator is

$$
k_1 \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{4}{4.5}\right) = 1.2 \left(1 - 0.75 + 0.75 \cdot 0.8889\right) = 1.2 \times (0.25 + 0.6667) = 1.2 \times 0.9167 \approx 1.100
$$

$$
RSV(D_1) = 0.124 \times \frac{1 \times 2.2}{1 + 1.100} = 0.124 \times \frac{2.2}{2.100} \approx 0.124 \times 1.048 = 0.130
$$

For $D_2$ ($\vert d \vert = 4$): same length, $f(\text{info}) = 1$.

$$
RSV(D_2) \approx 0.130
$$

For $D_3$ ($\vert d \vert = 5$):

$$
k_1 \left(1 - 0.75 + 0.75 \cdot \frac{5}{4.5}\right) = 1.2 \times (0.25 + 0.8333) = 1.2 \times 1.0833 \approx 1.300
$$

$$
RSV(D_3) = 0.124 \times \frac{2.2}{1 + 1.300} = 0.124 \times \frac{2.2}{2.300} \approx 0.124 \times 0.9565 = 0.119
$$

For $D_4$ ($\vert d \vert = 5$): $f(\text{info}) = 0$, so $RSV(D_4) = 0$.

**Final BM25 ranking:** $D_1 \approx D_2 \succ D_3 \succ D_4$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — BM25:**
> 1. Many students forget to **include the IDF factor** in front of the saturation term — that's a 2-mark loss.
> 2. Some students wrongly add $f(t,d)$ in the numerator *and* denominator of the inner ratio — the numerator is $f(t,d)(k_1+1)$, not $f(t,d)+k_1+1$.
> 3. The denominator uses $\vert d \vert$ (doc length in *words*, not characters). Forgetting the $b$-normalization also costs 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the Information Retrieval system evaluation methodology. Define Precision, Recall, F-Measure, MAP, and NDCG with formulas. Discuss the difference between "set-based" and "rank-based" metrics.**

**Model Solution:**

**Information Retrieval Evaluation Methodology**

To evaluate an IR system, the following are required:
1. **A document collection** (corpus).
2. **A set of queries** representative of user needs.
3. **Relevance judgments (qrels)** — for every query, the ground-truth list of relevant documents (often binary or graded).

The system is then run on each query, and its output is compared with the qrels using **metrics** that fall into two categories:

**Set-Based Metrics (do not consider rank position)**

**Precision:** the fraction of retrieved documents that are relevant.

$$
P = \frac{\vert R \cap A \vert}{\vert A \vert} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

**Recall:** the fraction of relevant documents that are retrieved.

$$
R = \frac{\vert R \cap A \vert}{\vert R \vert} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**F-Measure:** the harmonic mean, which penalizes extreme values more than the arithmetic mean.

$$
F_\beta = \frac{(1+\beta^2) P R}{\beta^2 P + R} \quad ; \quad F_1 = \frac{2 P R}{P + R}
$$

**Rank-Based Metrics (consider position in the ranked list)**

**Average Precision (AP):** for a single query, the average of $P@k$ at every rank where a relevant document appears.

$$
AP = \frac{1}{\vert R \vert} \sum_{k:\text{rel}(k)=1} P@k
$$

**Mean Average Precision (MAP):** mean of AP over all queries $Q$.

$$
MAP = \frac{1}{\vert Q \vert} \sum_{q \in Q} AP(q)
$$

**NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain):** handles graded relevance. Position discount via $\log_2$ ensures early ranks are weighted more.

$$
DCG@k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{rel_i}{\log_2(i+1)} \quad ; \quad NDCG@k = \frac{DCG@k}{IDCG@k}
$$

where $IDCG$ is the DCG of the *ideal* ranking.

**Set-Based vs Rank-Based — Key Difference**

| Aspect | Set-Based | Rank-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Considers rank order? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Sensitive to reordering? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Examples | P, R, F1 | MAP, MRR, NDCG |
| Use case | Boolean retrieval, filtering | Web search, QA |

A system returning $[R_1, J_1, R_2, J_2]$ has the same P/R as one returning $[R_1, R_2, J_1, J_2]$, but the latter is *better* under MAP/NDCG.

**(b) [7 Marks] A search system returns the following ranked list of 8 documents for a query where the ground truth relevant documents are $\{D_1, D_3, D_5, D_7\}$:**

$$\text{Ranked Output: } [D_3, D_2, D_1, D_6, D_5, D_4, D_7, D_8]$$

**Compute Precision, Recall, F1 at rank $k = 5$, then calculate Average Precision (AP) and MAP (assuming this is the only query).**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Build a relevance table for the output**

| Rank $k$ | Doc | Relevant? | $TP@k$ | $FP@k$ | $FN@k$ | $P@k$ | $R@k$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_3$ | ✅ | 1 | 0 | 3 | 1.000 | 0.250 |
| 2 | $D_2$ | ❌ | 1 | 1 | 3 | 0.500 | 0.250 |
| 3 | $D_1$ | ✅ | 2 | 1 | 2 | 0.667 | 0.500 |
| 4 | $D_6$ | ❌ | 2 | 2 | 2 | 0.500 | 0.500 |
| 5 | $D_5$ | ✅ | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0.600 | 0.750 |

**Step 2 — Precision, Recall, F1 at $k = 5$**

$$
P@5 = \frac{3}{5} = 0.600 \quad ; \quad R@5 = \frac{3}{4} = 0.750
$$

$$
F_1@5 = \frac{2 \times 0.600 \times 0.750}{0.600 + 0.750} = \frac{0.900}{1.350} \approx 0.667
$$

**Step 3 — Average Precision**

Relevant documents appear at ranks **1, 3, 5, 7**.

$$
AP = \frac{1}{\vert R \vert} \sum_{k:\text{rel}(k)=1} P@k = \frac{1}{4}(P@1 + P@3 + P@5 + P@7)
$$

Compute $P@7$: at rank 7 we have retrieved 4 relevant documents (at positions 1, 3, 5, 7).

$$
P@7 = \frac{4}{7} \approx 0.571
$$

Therefore:

$$
AP = \frac{1}{4}(1.000 + 0.667 + 0.600 + 0.571) = \frac{2.838}{4} \approx 0.710
$$

**Step 4 — MAP (single query)**

$$
MAP = AP = 0.710
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Evaluation:**
> 1. **Confusing $P@k$ with overall $P$:** $P@k$ uses the top-$k$ *retrieved*, not the *relevant* set — denominator is $k$, not $\vert A \vert$.
> 2. **Forgetting to divide by $\vert R \vert$ in AP:** AP is normalized by the *total number of relevant docs*, not by the number retrieved.
> 3. **Using arithmetic mean instead of harmonic mean** for F1 — they are *not* the same and yield different values. Harmonic mean is *lower* and more conservative.
> 4. **Forgetting the log discount in NDCG** — using $i$ instead of $\log_2(i+1)$ in the denominator loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 2**

- [ ] A **Retrieval Model** is the theoretical framework mapping queries to ranked documents.
- [ ] **Boolean Model** = set-based, unranked, exact match; lacks partial credit.
- [ ] **Vector Space Model** = documents and queries as TF-IDF vectors; relevance = cosine similarity.
- [ ] **TF-IDF** = $tf(t,d) \cdot \log(N/df(t))$ — rewards *frequent-in-doc* and *rare-in-corpus*.
- [ ] **Cosine similarity** = $\frac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert\vec{q}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{d}\vert}$ — normalizes for document length.
- [ ] **BM25** = adds term-frequency *saturation* ($k_1 \approx 1.2$) and length *normalization* ($b \approx 0.75$).
- [ ] **Language Model** ranks by $P(q \vert d)$; **smoothing** (Jelinek–Mercer, Dirichlet) is mandatory.
- [ ] **Precision** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$; **Recall** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$.
- [ ] **F1** = harmonic mean of P and R; **$F_\beta$** weights recall $\beta$ times more.
- [ ] **MAP** = mean of AP across queries; AP uses *precision at every relevant rank*.
- [ ] **MRR** = $\frac{1}{\vert Q \vert} \sum \frac{1}{\text{rank of first hit}}$.
- [ ] **NDCG** uses **logarithmic discount** and **graded relevance**; always normalize by IDCG.
- [ ] **Set-based metrics** (P, R, F1) ignore ranking; **rank-based metrics** (MAP, MRR, NDCG) don't.
- [ ] Standard test collections: **TREC, CACM, Cranfield, GOV2**.
- [ ] **Stop-word filtering** (e.g., removing "the") is automatic by TF-IDF since $\text{IDF}=0$.
- [ ] In BM25, the numerator is $f(t,d)(k_1+1)$, **not** $f(t,d)+k_1+1$.
- [ ] In cosine similarity, the **denominator** normalizes by both query and document lengths.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vector Space Model (VSM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vector Space Model (VSM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — VECTOR SPACE MODEL (VSM)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📌 Formal Academic Definition

The **Vector Space Model (VSM)** is an algebraic representation of text documents used in Information Retrieval. In this model, every document and every query is represented as a vector of weighted term frequencies in a high-dimensional space, where each dimension corresponds to a unique term in the vocabulary of the collection. The relevance of a document to a query is computed as the **similarity** (typically cosine similarity) between the two vectors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECDT745 — Module 2):**
> *The Vector Space Model represents documents and queries as vectors in a multi-dimensional term space. Retrieval is based on computing a similarity coefficient (cosine, dot product, or Jaccard) between the query vector and every document vector, and ranking documents in descending order of similarity.*

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **library with thousands of books**, and you want to find which book is most relevant to your query *"machine learning algorithms"*.

- Picture a giant 3D coordinate system (in reality, it has *N* dimensions, where *N* = vocabulary size).
- Each **book (document)** is plotted as a single point in this space.
- The position of the point is determined by **how often** specific words appear in that book.
- Your **query** is also plotted as a point.
- The "best match" is the book whose point lies **closest in direction** to your query point.

> 🧭 **Think of it like a compass:** A book is "north" of your query if it talks about the same things, and the closer the direction, the better the match. The angle between the two arrows (vectors) is what we measure — the **smaller the angle, the more relevant the document**.

### 🧮 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Term (t)** | A word or token extracted from documents after preprocessing |
| **Document (d)** | A single text unit (article, page, paragraph) to be indexed |
| **Vocabulary (V)** | The set of all unique terms in the collection $\vert V \vert = n$ |
| **Term-Document Matrix** | An $m \times n$ matrix where entry $w_{ij}$ is the weight of term $i$ in document $j$ |
| **Query Vector (q)** | A vector representing the user's information need |
| **Document Vector (d_j)** | A vector representing document $j$ |
| **Similarity Score** | A numeric value indicating how relevant $d_j$ is to $q$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The VSM is also called the **Term Vector Model** and was popularized by **Gerard Salton** and colleagues in the 1970s through the **SMART Information Retrieval System** at Cornell University.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of VSM — Step-by-Step Logic

### 🔹 Step 1: Indexing (Document Processing)
1. Collect the entire document collection $\mathcal{D} = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_m\}$.
2. Apply text preprocessing:
   - **Tokenization** → split text into terms
   - **Stop-word removal** → drop common words (*the, is, a, an*)
   - **Stemming / Lemmatization** → reduce words to root form (*running → run*)
   - **Case folding** → convert to lowercase
3. Build the **vocabulary** $V = \{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n\}$.

### 🔹 Step 2: Term Weighting
For each term $t_i$ in each document $d_j$, compute a weight $w_{ij}$. The most widely used scheme is **tf-idf**:

$$w_{ij} = tf_{ij} \cdot idf_i$$

where:
- $tf_{ij}$ = frequency of term $t_i$ in document $d_j$
- $idf_i$ = inverse document frequency of term $t_i$ across the corpus

### 🔹 Step 3: Vector Construction
- Each document $d_j$ is represented as:
$$\vec{d_j} = (w_{1j}, w_{2j}, \ldots, w_{nj})$$
- Each query $q$ is represented as:
$$\vec{q} = (w_{1q}, w_{2q}, \ldots, w_{nq})$$

### 🔹 Step 4: Similarity Computation
Compute a similarity score $sim(\vec{q}, \vec{d_j})$ between query and every document.

### 🔹 Step 5: Ranking
Sort documents in **descending order** of similarity score. The top-$k$ documents are returned to the user.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **KTU board-exam essential**. Memorize the symbols and the order of operations.

| # | Formula | Description | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $tf_{ij} = f_{ij}$ | Raw term frequency (count of $t_i$ in $d_j$) | $\geq 0$ |
| 2 | $tf_{ij}^{norm} = \dfrac{f_{ij}}{\max_k f_{kj}}$ | Normalized term frequency (length normalized) | $[0, 1]$ |
| 3 | $df_i = \vert \{d_j : t_i \in d_j\} \vert$ | Document frequency of term $t_i$ | $[1, m]$ |
| 4 | $idf_i = \log \dfrac{m}{df_i}$ | Inverse document frequency | $\geq 0$ |
| 5 | $idf_i = \log \dfrac{m}{1 + df_i}$ | Smoothed IDF (avoids division by zero) | $> 0$ |
| 6 | $w_{ij} = tf_{ij} \cdot idf_i$ | Standard tf-idf weight | $\geq 0$ |
| 7 | $\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{iq} \cdot w_{ij}$ | Inner product / Dot product | $\geq 0$ |
| 8 | $\vert \vec{x} \vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2}$ | Euclidean norm (L2) | $\geq 0$ |
| 9 | $\cos(\vec{q}, \vec{d_j}) = \dfrac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_j}}{\vert \vec{q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{d_j} \vert}$ | **Cosine Similarity** (most used) | $[-1, 1]$ |
| 10 | $J(\vec{q}, \vec{d_j}) = \dfrac{\vert \vec{q} \cap \vec{d_j} \vert}{\vert \vec{q} \cup \vec{d_j} \vert}$ | Jaccard Coefficient (binary) | $[0, 1]$ |

### 🎯 Boundary Conditions
- If $df_i = m$ (term in every document) → $idf_i = \log(1) = 0$ → term contributes nothing.
- If $df_i = 1$ (term in only one document) → $idf_i = \log(m)$ → maximum discriminative power.
- Cosine similarity equals **1** when vectors are identical, **0** when orthogonal, **-1** when opposite (rare in IR).

### 🌐 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of VSM |
|---|---|
| 🔎 **Web Search Engines** | Ranking pages (Google's early ranking used vector-style scoring) |
| 📄 **Document Classification** | Documents as feature vectors for SVM / k-NN |
| 🛒 **Recommender Systems** | User-item profile matching |
| 🧬 **Bioinformatics** | Gene-expression profile similarity |
| 💬 **Plagiarism Detection** | Document-to-document similarity scoring |
| 📧 **Spam Filtering** | Email content vectorized and classified |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Cosine Similarity Score

The cosine similarity between two vectors $\vec{q}$ and $\vec{d_j}$ measures the **cosine of the angle** between them. It is derived from the Euclidean inner product rule.

### 📐 Derivation

By the **definition of the Euclidean inner product** for two vectors:

$$\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_j} = \vert \vec{q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{d_j} \vert \cdot \cos \theta$$

Solving for $\cos \theta$:

$$\cos \theta = \dfrac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_j}}{\vert \vec{q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{d_j} \vert}$$

Substituting the component forms:

$$\cos \theta = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{iq} \cdot w_{ij}}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{iq}^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ij}^{2}}}$$

This is the **canonical cosine similarity** used in VSM.

> [!NOTE]
> Cosine similarity is **length-invariant** — two documents with the same term distribution but different lengths will have cosine $= 1$, which is desirable in IR.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)

The intuition behind IDF is that a term appearing in **every** document is useless for discrimination, while a term appearing in **few** documents is highly discriminative.

### 📐 Derivation

Start with the basic ratio:

$$\text{idf}_i = \log \dfrac{m}{df_i}$$

where $m$ is total number of documents and $df_i$ is the number of documents containing $t_i$.

**Justification steps:**

1. If $df_i \to m$ (term in all docs) → $\log(m/m) = \log 1 = 0$ → no weight.
2. If $df_i \to 1$ (term unique to one doc) → $\log(m/1) = \log m$ → maximum weight.
3. Logarithm is used to **dampen** the effect (otherwise the ratio can be huge).
4. The base of the log is not critical (often base 2 in IR, base $e$ in statistics).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **smoothed** version $\log(m / (1 + df_i))$ is used in practice to avoid the $\log(0)$ singularity when $df_i = 0$ (i.e., the term is absent from the corpus).

---

## 3.3 ✅ Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

### 📘 Problem Statement
Given a corpus of **4 documents** and vocabulary of **4 terms**, compute the **cosine similarity** between the query and each document. Rank the documents.

**Documents:**

| | $t_1$ (gold) | $t_2$ (silver) | $t_3$ (price) | $t_4$ (market) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $d_1$ | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| $d_2$ | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| $d_3$ | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| $d_4$ | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 |

**Query:** $q = $ (1, 0, 1, 0) → user wants terms $t_1$ and $t_3$

### 🪜 Step A: Compute IDF for each term

$$m = 4$$

- $df_1 = 3$ (in $d_1, d_2, d_4$) → $idf_1 = \log(4/3) = 0.1249$
- $df_2 = 3$ (in $d_1, d_3, d_4$) → $idf_2 = \log(4/3) = 0.1249$
- $df_3 = 3$ (in $d_2, d_3, d_4$) → $idf_3 = \log(4/3) = 0.1249$
- $df_4 = 2$ (in $d_1, d_2$) → $idf_4 = \log(4/2) = 0.3010$

> Using natural log. All $idf$ values rounded to 4 decimal places.

### 🪜 Step B: Build Weighted Term-Document Matrix

Using $w_{ij} = tf_{ij} \times idf_i$:

| | $d_1$ | $d_2$ | $d_3$ | $d_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $t_1$ | 0.1249 | 0.1249 | 0 | 0.1249 |
| $t_2$ | 0.1249 | 0 | 0.1249 | 0.1249 |
| $t_3$ | 0 | 0.1249 | 0.1249 | 0.1249 |
| $t_4$ | 0.3010 | 0.3010 | 0 | 0 |

### 🪜 Step C: Construct Query Vector

Query $q = (1, 0, 1, 0)$ → weighted query:

$$\vec{q} = (1 \cdot 0.1249,\; 0 \cdot 0.1249,\; 1 \cdot 0.1249,\; 0 \cdot 0.3010)$$

$$\vec{q} = (0.1249,\; 0,\; 0.1249,\; 0)$$

### 🪜 Step D: Compute Euclidean Norms

For $\vec{q}$:

$$\vert \vec{q} \vert = \sqrt{0.1249^2 + 0^2 + 0.1249^2 + 0^2}$$

$$\vert \vec{q} \vert = \sqrt{0.0156 + 0.0156} = \sqrt{0.0312} = 0.1766$$

For $d_1 = (0.1249, 0.1249, 0, 0.3010)$:

$$\vert \vec{d_1} \vert = \sqrt{0.0156 + 0.0156 + 0 + 0.0906} = \sqrt{0.1218} = 0.3490$$

For $d_2 = (0.1249, 0, 0.1249, 0.3010)$:

$$\vert \vec{d_2} \vert = \sqrt{0.0156 + 0 + 0.0156 + 0.0906} = \sqrt{0.1218} = 0.3490$$

For $d_3 = (0, 0.1249, 0.1249, 0)$:

$$\vert \vec{d_3} \vert = \sqrt{0 + 0.0156 + 0.0156 + 0} = \sqrt{0.0312} = 0.1766$$

For $d_4 = (0.1249, 0.1249, 0.1249, 0)$:

$$\vert \vec{d_4} \vert = \sqrt{0.0156 + 0.0156 + 0.0156 + 0} = \sqrt{0.0468} = 0.2163$$

### 🪜 Step E: Compute Dot Products

$\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_1} = (0.1249)(0.1249) + (0)(0.1249) + (0.1249)(0) + (0)(0.3010) = 0.0156$

$\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_2} = (0.1249)(0.1249) + (0)(0) + (0.1249)(0.1249) + (0)(0.3010) = 0.0312$

$\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_3} = (0.1249)(0) + (0)(0.1249) + (0.1249)(0.1249) + (0)(0) = 0.0156$

$\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_4} = (0.1249)(0.1249) + (0)(0.1249) + (0.1249)(0.1249) + (0)(0) = 0.0312$

### 🪜 Step F: Compute Cosine Similarities

$$\cos(\vec{q}, \vec{d_1}) = \dfrac{0.0156}{0.1766 \times 0.3490} = \dfrac{0.0156}{0.0616} = 0.2532$$

$$\cos(\vec{q}, \vec{d_2}) = \dfrac{0.0312}{0.1766 \times 0.3490} = \dfrac{0.0312}{0.0616} = 0.5065$$

$$\cos(\vec{q}, \vec{d_3}) = \dfrac{0.0156}{0.1766 \times 0.1766} = \dfrac{0.0156}{0.0312} = 0.5000$$

$$\cos(\vec{q}, \vec{d_4}) = \dfrac{0.0312}{0.1766 \times 0.2163} = \dfrac{0.0312}{0.0382} = 0.8167$$

### 🪜 Step G: Final Ranking

| Rank | Document | Cosine Score |
|---|---|---|
| 🥇 1 | $d_4$ | **0.8167** |
| 🥈 2 | $d_2$ | 0.5065 |
| 🥉 3 | $d_3$ | 0.5000 |
| 4 | $d_1$ | 0.2532 |

> ✅ **Conclusion:** $d_4$ is the most relevant document for the query, which makes intuitive sense because $d_4$ contains both query terms ($t_1$ and $t_3$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level VSM Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Document Collection D] --> B[Text Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Tokenization]
    B1 --> B2[Stop-word Removal]
    B2 --> B3[Stemming and Lemmatization]
    B3 --> C[Build Vocabulary V]
    C --> D[Construct Term-Document Matrix]
    D --> E[Apply TF-IDF Weighting]
    E --> F[Document Vectors dj]
    G[User Query q] --> H[Query Preprocessing]
    H --> I[Build Query Vector qvec]
    I --> J[Similarity Computation]
    F --> J
    J --> K[Cosine Similarity Score]
    K --> L[Ranked List of Documents]
    L --> M[Return Top-k Results to User]
```

## 4.2 TF-IDF Weighting Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Raw Term Frequency tf] --> M1[Multiply]
    T2[Inverse Document Frequency idf] --> M1
    M1 --> OUT[TF-IDF Weight wij]
    subgraph Inputs
        T1
        T2
    end
    subgraph Output
        OUT
    end
```

## 4.3 Similarity Computation Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Query Vector q] --> DOT[Dot Product]
    B[Document Vector dj] --> DOT
    A --> NQ[Norm of q]
    B --> ND[Norm of dj]
    NQ --> DIV[Division]
    ND --> DIV
    DOT --> DIV
    DIV --> SC[Similarity Score]
    SC --> RK[Rank Documents]
    RK --> RES[Top-k Retrieved Docs]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Vector Space Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VSpace[High-Dimensional Term Space]
        O((Origin)) --- Q[Query Vector q]
        O --- D1[Document d1]
        O --- D2[Document d2]
        O --- D3[Document d3]
        Q -. Small Angle .-> D2
        D1 -. Large Angle .-> Q
        D3 -. Medium Angle .-> Q
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The angle between vectors represents **semantic dissimilarity**. Smaller angle ⇒ higher cosine similarity ⇒ more relevant document.

## 4.5 SMART Notation Summary Table

| Component | Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Term frequency | **n** | Raw count |
| | **l** | Log-normalized: $1 + \log(tf)$ |
| | **a** | Augmented: $0.5 + 0.5 \cdot \frac{tf}{\max tf}$ |
| | **b** | Boolean: 1 if present, 0 otherwise |
| | **L** | Length-normalized |
| Inverse document frequency | **n** | No IDF (weight = 1) |
| | **t** | Standard IDF: $\log(m / df)$ |
| | **p** | Probabilistic IDF |
| Document normalization | **n** | None |
| | **c** | Cosine normalization |
| | **u** | Pivoted unique normalization |

> Example: **ltc** = log-tf, standard idf, cosine normalization. The **ltc.lnc** scheme is the most widely used classical VSM setting.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the Vector Space Model. List any two similarity measures used in VSM.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Vector Space Model (VSM)** is an algebraic model for Information Retrieval in which documents and queries are represented as vectors of term weights in a multi-dimensional space, and the relevance of a document to a query is determined by a similarity function computed between their vectors.

Two similarity measures:
1. **Cosine Similarity:** $\cos(\vec{q}, \vec{d_j}) = \dfrac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_j}}{\vert \vec{q} \vert \vert \vec{d_j} \vert}$
2. **Dot Product:** $\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{iq} w_{ij}$

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for listing the two measures)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What is tf-idf weighting? Why is IDF used in place of raw term frequency alone?**

**Model Answer:**
**Tf-idf** stands for **term frequency – inverse document frequency**, computed as:

$$w_{ij} = tf_{ij} \times idf_i$$

**Need for IDF:** Raw term frequency treats all terms equally, but common words like *"the"*, *"is"*, *"of"* appear very frequently in every document and are useless for distinguishing relevant from non-relevant documents. The IDF factor down-weights such globally common terms and up-weights rare, discriminative terms, leading to a better ranking.

*(1.5 marks for formula + 1.5 marks for justification)*

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**
> 
> **(a)** Explain the architecture of the Vector Space Model with a neat block diagram. Discuss the term weighting scheme **tf-idf** in detail. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Given the following documents and a query, compute the **cosine similarity** between the query and each document using **tf-idf** weights. Rank the documents. **(7 marks)**

| | $t_1$ | $t_2$ | $t_3$ | $t_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $d_1$ | 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| $d_2$ | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| $d_3$ | 0 | 2 | 1 | 1 |
| $d_4$ | 1 | 1 | 2 | 0 |
| $q$ | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question A

### Part (a) — VSM Architecture & tf-idf (7 marks)

**VSM Architecture (3 marks):**
The VSM pipeline consists of:
1. **Indexing Phase:** Documents → Tokenization → Stop-word removal → Stemming → Vocabulary construction → Term-Document matrix → Weight assignment.
2. **Query Phase:** User query undergoes the same preprocessing → converted to query vector.
3. **Retrieval Phase:** Compute similarity score (e.g., cosine) between query vector and every document vector → rank documents → return top-k.

**tf-idf in detail (4 marks):**
- **Term frequency $tf_{ij}$** = number of times term $t_i$ occurs in document $d_j$.
- **Document frequency $df_i$** = number of documents containing $t_i$.
- **Inverse document frequency $idf_i = \log(m / df_i)$** assigns higher weight to rare terms.
- **Final weight:** $w_{ij} = tf_{ij} \cdot \log(m / df_i)$.
- The product balances **local importance** (tf) with **global discriminative power** (idf).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The block diagram from SECTION 4.1 can be reproduced here for full marks.

### Part (b) — Cosine Similarity Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute df and idf for each term**

$m = 4$

- $df_1$ = docs containing $t_1$ = $\{d_1, d_2, d_4\}$ → 3
- $df_2$ = docs containing $t_2$ = $\{d_2, d_3, d_4\}$ → 3
- $df_3$ = docs containing $t_3$ = $\{d_1, d_3, d_4\}$ → 3
- $df_4$ = docs containing $t_4$ = $\{d_1, d_3\}$ → 2

Using $idf = \log(m / df)$ with base 10:

- $idf_1 = \log(4/3) = 0.1249$
- $idf_2 = \log(4/3) = 0.1249$
- $idf_3 = \log(4/3) = 0.1249$
- $idf_4 = \log(4/2) = 0.3010$

**Step 2: Compute tf-idf weights**

$w_{ij} = tf_{ij} \times idf_i$

| | $d_1$ | $d_2$ | $d_3$ | $d_4$ | $q$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $t_1$ | 0.2498 | 0.1249 | 0 | 0.1249 | 0.1249 |
| $t_2$ | 0 | 0.1249 | 0.2498 | 0.1249 | 0 |
| $t_3$ | 0.1249 | 0 | 0.1249 | 0.2498 | 0.1249 |
| $t_4$ | 0.3010 | 0 | 0.3010 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 3: Compute norms**

- $\vert \vec{q} \vert = \sqrt{0.0156 + 0 + 0.0156 + 0} = 0.1766$
- $\vert \vec{d_1} \vert = \sqrt{0.0624 + 0 + 0.0156 + 0.0906} = \sqrt{0.1686} = 0.4106$
- $\vert \vec{d_2} \vert = \sqrt{0.0156 + 0.0156 + 0 + 0} = \sqrt{0.0312} = 0.1766$
- $\vert \vec{d_3} \vert = \sqrt{0 + 0.0624 + 0.0156 + 0.0906} = \sqrt{0.1686} = 0.4106$
- $\vert \vec{d_4} \vert = \sqrt{0.0156 + 0.0156 + 0.0624 + 0} = \sqrt{0.0936} = 0.3059$

**Step 4: Compute dot products**

- $\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_1} = (0.1249)(0.2498) + (0)(0) + (0.1249)(0.1249) + (0)(0.3010) = 0.0312 + 0 + 0.0156 + 0 = 0.0468$
- $\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_2} = (0.1249)(0.1249) + 0 + 0 + 0 = 0.0156$
- $\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_3} = 0 + 0 + (0.1249)(0.1249) + 0 = 0.0156$
- $\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_4} = (0.1249)(0.1249) + 0 + (0.1249)(0.2498) + 0 = 0.0156 + 0.0312 = 0.0468$

**Step 5: Compute cosine similarity**

$$\cos(\vec{q}, \vec{d_1}) = \frac{0.0468}{0.1766 \times 0.4106} = \frac{0.0468}{0.0725} = 0.6455$$

$$\cos(\vec{q}, \vec{d_2}) = \frac{0.0156}{0.1766 \times 0.1766} = \frac{0.0156}{0.0312} = 0.5000$$

$$\cos(\vec{q}, \vec{d_3}) = \frac{0.0156}{0.1766 \times 0.4106} = \frac{0.0156}{0.0725} = 0.2152$$

$$\cos(\vec{q}, \vec{d_4}) = \frac{0.0468}{0.1766 \times 0.3059} = \frac{0.0468}{0.0540} = 0.8667$$

**Step 6: Final Ranking**

| Rank | Document | Cosine Score |
|---|---|---|
| 🥇 1 | $d_4$ | **0.8667** |
| 🥈 2 | $d_1$ | 0.6455 |
| 🥉 3 | $d_2$ | 0.5000 |
| 4 | $d_3$ | 0.2152 |

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
> 
> **(a)** With a suitable example, explain the **tf-idf** term weighting scheme. How does it improve over simple term frequency? **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Explain the **cosine similarity measure** with derivation. Why is cosine preferred over the dot product in VSM? **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

### Part (a) — tf-idf scheme (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** Tf-idf is a numerical statistic that reflects how important a word is to a document in a collection. It is the product of two statistics:
- **Term Frequency (tf):** $tf_{ij}$ = count of $t_i$ in $d_j$
- **Inverse Document Frequency (idf):** $idf_i = \log(m / df_i)$

$$w_{ij} = tf_{ij} \times \log \frac{m}{df_i}$$

**Example (3 marks):** Suppose $m = 1000$ documents.
- A term *"the"* appears in 900 documents → $idf = \log(1000/900) = 0.046$ (almost no weight).
- A term *"quantum"* appears in 5 documents → $idf = \log(1000/5) = 2.30$ (high weight).

Hence, a document with many occurrences of "quantum" is ranked higher than one with many occurrences of "the".

**Why tf-idf is better than raw tf (2 marks):**
- Raw tf favours long documents and common words.
- tf-idf penalises common terms and rewards rare, discriminative terms.
- It produces a **sparse, meaningful** weight vector and improves precision.

### Part (b) — Cosine Similarity (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** Cosine similarity measures the cosine of the angle between two non-zero vectors in a multi-dimensional space.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Starting from the inner product identity:
$$\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_j} = \vert \vec{q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{d_j} \vert \cdot \cos \theta$$

Rearranging:
$$\cos \theta = \frac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_j}}{\vert \vec{q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{d_j} \vert} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{iq} w_{ij}}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{iq}^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ij}^{2}}}$$

**Why cosine is preferred over dot product (2 marks):**
- Dot product is **not normalized** — a longer document with more terms will get a higher score purely due to length, not relevance.
- Cosine **normalizes by vector magnitude**, making it invariant to document length.
- Two documents covering the same topic with different lengths still score near 1.0.
- Cosine is bounded in $[-1, 1]$, providing a normalized ranking scale.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply tf by idf** — many students compute only $tf$ and skip the IDF factor. Loss: up to 2 marks.
> 2. **Using raw dot product** instead of cosine similarity — examiners expect normalization. Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong sign / wrong formula for IDF** — using $idf = \log(df/m)$ instead of $\log(m/df)$ inverts the weighting logic.
> 4. **Arithmetic errors in dot product / norm** — show every step clearly to avoid losing process marks.
> 5. **Not writing units / ranges** — always mention that cosine similarity lies in $[-1, 1]$ and that all weights are $\geq 0$.
> 6. **Forgetting to include the block diagram** in part (a) questions — a neat, labelled diagram is **mandatory** for full marks.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Vector Space Model**

- ✅ VSM represents documents and queries as **vectors in term space**.
- ✅ Dimensions = vocabulary size $\vert V \vert$; entries = term weights.
- ✅ **tf-idf weight:** $w_{ij} = tf_{ij} \times \log(m / df_i)$.
- ✅ **Cosine similarity** is the standard retrieval function: $sim(\vec{q}, \vec{d_j}) = \frac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_j}}{\vert \vec{q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{d_j} \vert}$.
- ✅ **IDF rationale:** rare terms are more discriminative → higher weight.
- ✅ Cosine is bounded in $[-1, 1]$; **1 = identical**, **0 = orthogonal (no shared terms)**.
- ✅ Cosine is **length-invariant**; dot product is not.
- ✅ Ranking = sort documents by **descending similarity score**, return top-$k$.
- ✅ **SMART notation** (ltc.lnc) is the classical VSM weighting scheme.
- ✅ Preprocessing pipeline: **Tokenize → Stop-word removal → Stemming → Vocabulary**.
- ✅ **Advantages:** simple, mathematically elegant, supports partial matching, ranked output.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** assumes term independence, no semantic understanding, high dimensionality, sparsity.
- ✅ Common exam formula: always carry at least **4 decimal places** in intermediate steps.
- ✅ KTU 2024 expects: **block diagram + tf-idf derivation + cosine computation** in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probabilistic Retrieval Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Probabilistic Retrieval Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2 — Probabilistic Retrieval Model

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Probabilistic Information Retrieval (PIR)** is a formal, mathematically grounded retrieval framework that ranks documents in **decreasing order of their estimated probability of being relevant** to a given query, in direct accordance with the **Probability Ranking Principle (PRP)** proposed by **Stephen E. Robertson** in **1977**.

The foundational theorem upon which the entire model is constructed is:

> [!NOTE]
> **Probability Ranking Principle (PRP):** *"If a retrieval system's response to each query is a ranking of the documents in the collection in order of decreasing probability of relevance to the user who submitted the query, where the probabilities are estimated as accurately as possible on the basis of whatever data has been made available to the system for this purpose, then the overall effectiveness of the system, measured in terms of the expected precision obtained at any given recall level, will be the best that can be obtained under the data available."*
> — Robertson (1977)

Formally, PRP states that a document $d$ should be ranked ahead of document $d'$ if and only if:

$$P(R=1 \mid d,q) > P(R=1 \mid d',q)$$

where:
- $R \in \{0,1\}$ is a **binary relevance variable** ($R=1$ means relevant, $R=0$ means non-relevant).
- $q$ is the user query.
- $d, d'$ are candidate documents.
- $P(R=1 \mid d,q)$ is the **posterior probability of relevance** given the document and the query.

---

## 1.2 The Three Foundational Probabilistic Retrieval Models

| Model | Acronym | Key Idea |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Binary Independence Model** | **BIM** | Treats documents and queries as binary term-incidence vectors; assumes term independence. |
| **BM25 (Best Match 25)** | **BM25** | Probabilistic model with term frequency saturation and document length normalization. |
| **Language Models for IR** | **LM** | Estimates $P(q \mid d)$ — the probability that the query's terms would be generated by a document's language model. |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Binary Independence Model (BIM)** is the **canonical implementation** of PRP and is the **core sub-topic** most frequently examined under the *Probabilistic Retrieval Model* heading in KTU Module 2.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **🩺 The Doctor-Diagnosis Analogy:**
> Imagine you are a **doctor** trying to decide which of two patients ($d$ and $d'$) is more likely to have a particular disease (relevance $R=1$). Each patient has certain symptoms (terms $t_i$). You estimate the **probability of the disease given the observed symptoms**.
>
> *Patient A* has fever + cough + chest pain → very high probability of pneumonia.
> *Patient B* has only mild fatigue → low probability of pneumonia.
>
> You rank **Patient A** higher because $P(\text{pneumonia} \mid \text{symptoms of A})$ is greater.
>
> The **PIR system** behaves identically: given query symptoms (terms), it computes the **posterior relevance probability** of each document and ranks accordingly. The "Bayesian diagnosis" is the document's relevance score.

### Why is this approach powerful?

- **Theoretically optimal:** Under PRP, it minimizes the **expected loss** (or equivalently, maximizes expected precision) at any given recall threshold.
- **Principled foundation:** Grounded in Bayesian decision theory — not heuristics.
- **Strong empirical results:** BM25 (a refined descendant of BIM) remains a **state-of-the-art baseline** even in modern IR systems (Elasticsearch, Lucene, Solr).

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology Anchors (Must Memorize for KTU)

- **Relevance** ($R$): A binary judgment — a document is either relevant or not.
- **Posterior probability of relevance** $P(R=1 \mid d,q)$: The probability that $d$ is relevant after observing its content.
- **Prior probability of relevance** $P(R=1)$: The probability that any randomly chosen document is relevant (often taken as a constant across the collection).
- **Odds of relevance** $O(R)$: The ratio $\dfrac{P(R=1)}{1 - P(R=1)}$ — measures the *betting odds* in favor of relevance.
- **Retrieval Status Value (RSV)**: The numerical score assigned to a document by the probabilistic model. Higher RSV = higher ranked.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Probability Ranking Principle (PRP) — Why It Is Optimal

PRP is derived from **Bayes' optimal decision rule** under **0-1 loss** (no penalty for correct ranking, equal penalty for any incorrect order). The optimal action for the system is to rank by the **posterior probability of relevance** $P(R=1 \mid d, q)$.

If we denote:
- $C_0$ = cost of retrieving a non-relevant document.
- $C_1$ = cost of failing to retrieve a relevant document.

Then the expected cost of retrieving document $d$ is:

$$\text{ExpectedCost}(d) = C_0 \cdot P(R=0 \mid d, q) + C_1 \cdot P(R=1 \mid d, q)$$

To minimize expected cost, the system should retrieve $d$ **before** $d'$ whenever:

$$C_0 \cdot P(R=0 \mid d, q) + C_1 \cdot P(R=1 \mid d, q) \;<\; C_0 \cdot P(R=0 \mid d', q) + C_1 \cdot P(R=1 \mid d', q)$$

Assuming **equal cost of errors** ($C_0 = C_1$), this inequality reduces precisely to the PRP ordering: $P(R=1 \mid d, q) > P(R=1 \mid d', q)$. ∎

---

## 2.2 The Binary Independence Model (BIM) — Assumptions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The BIM makes **four strict assumptions**, and **exam questions frequently test whether you can list and explain these**.

1. **Binary document representation:** Each document is represented as a vector of binary term occurrences $d = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ where $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ indicates the presence (1) or absence (0) of term $t_i$. Term **frequency** within a document is ignored.
2. **Binary query representation:** The query is similarly a binary vector $q = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$. Only the **presence/absence** of query terms matters.
3. **Term Independence (Naive Bayes assumption):** Terms occur independently in documents — both relevant and non-relevant. That is:
$$P(x \mid R) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid R)$$
4. **Relevance is independent of query, given document:** All estimates are conditioned only on $d$ and $R$; the query's role is reduced to selecting which terms enter the product.

---

## 2.3 The RSV Derivation Roadmap (Logical Steps)

| Step | Logical Operation | Purpose |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **1** | Convert $P(R=1 \mid d, q)$ to **odds** $O(R \mid d, q)$ | Odds are monotonic with probability, so ranking by odds = ranking by probability. |
| **2** | Apply **Bayes' Theorem** | Express posterior in terms of likelihoods and priors. |
| **3** | Apply **term independence** | Factorize the joint probability into a product of per-term probabilities. |
| **4** | Convert to **log-space (log-odds)** | Replace multiplication with summation for numerical stability and computational speed. |
| **5** | Eliminate query-absent terms | The terms with $x_i = 0$ contribute a **document-independent constant** to all scores and can be dropped for ranking. |
| **6** | Simplify to a **sum over query terms only** | Yield the final RSV formula. |

> The fully rigorous algebraic derivation is provided in **Section 3**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains **every key formula** the KTU examiner can reasonably expect for a 14-mark question on Probabilistic Retrieval. All symbols are standard and consistent with **Manning, Raghavan & Schütze — Introduction to Information Retrieval (Cambridge University Press)**.

### Table 1 — Core PRP and Bayes' Formulas

| # | Formula | Description |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | $P(R=1 \mid d, q) \;>\; P(R=1 \mid d', q)$ | **PRP ranking rule** — document $d$ ranked above $d'$ |
| 2 | $O(A) = \dfrac{P(A)}{1 - P(A)}$ | **Odds** of event $A$ |
| 3 | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$ | **Bayes' Theorem** |
| 4 | $P(R=1 \mid d) = \dfrac{P(d \mid R=1) \cdot P(R=1)}{P(d)}$ | Posterior in terms of likelihoods and priors |

### Table 2 — BIM Per-Term Probabilities

| Symbol | Meaning | Estimation in Practice |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $p_i$ | $P(x_i = 1 \mid R = 1, q)$ | Probability that term $t_i$ is present in a **relevant** document |
| $q_i$ | $P(x_i = 1 \mid R = 0, q)$ | Probability that term $t_i$ is present in a **non-relevant** document |
| $u_i$ | $P(x_i = 1 \mid R = 1, \neg q)$ | Probability of term $t_i$ in a relevant doc **without** assuming the term is in the query (usually estimated by $p_i$) |
| $r_i$ | $P(x_i = 1 \mid R = 1, q)$ | Often aliased to $p_i$ in the simplified derivation |
| $s_i$ | $P(x_i = 1 \mid R = 0, q)$ | Often aliased to $q_i$ in the simplified derivation |

### Table 3 — The BIM Ranking Formula (THE most important equation in this module)

| # | Formula | Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 5 | $\text{RSV}_d = \sum_{i : x_i = q_i = 1} x_i \cdot \log \left( \dfrac{p_i \, (1 - q_i)}{q_i \, (1 - p_i)} \right)$ | **Final BIM score** — sum is over terms present in BOTH document and query. |
| 6 | $\text{RSV}_d = \sum_{i \in q \cap d} \log \left( \dfrac{p_i \, (1 - q_i)}{q_i \, (1 - p_i)} \right)$ | Since $x_i = 1$ for matched terms, the explicit $x_i$ is often dropped. |
| 7 | $c_i = \log \left( \dfrac{p_i \, (1 - q_i)}{q_i \, (1 - p_i)} \right)$ | Per-term **relevance weight** — analogous to TF-IDF weight but probabilistic. |
| 8 | $\text{RSV}_d = \sum_{i \in q \cap d} c_i$ | **Linear additive form** — the system is essentially a linear classifier over binary term features. |

### Table 4 — Practical Estimation of $p_i$ and $q_i$

| Estimate | Formula | Used When |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **$p_i$ (relevant estimate)** | $p_i \approx 0.5$ | **Default fallback** when no relevance information is available. |
| **$q_i$ (non-relevant estimate)** | $q_i = \dfrac{n_i}{N}$ | $n_i$ = number of docs containing $t_i$, $N$ = total docs in collection. This assumes documents are non-relevant a priori. |
| **With relevance feedback (Rocchio-style)** | $p_i = \dfrac{r_i}{R}$ | $r_i$ = number of relevant docs containing $t_i$, $R$ = total relevant docs. |
| **With relevance feedback** | $q_i = \dfrac{n_i - r_i}{N - R}$ | Ratio of (non-relevant docs containing $t_i$) to (total non-relevant docs). |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where Is This Used Today?

- **Elasticsearch, Apache Solr, Lucene** — the BM25 ranking function (a probabilistic successor of BIM with TF saturation and length normalization) is the **default scoring algorithm**.
- **Legal e-discovery** — relevance judgments are explicit and binary, making PRP-based models ideal.
- **Patent search** — Boolean-like relevance expectations map naturally onto BIM.
- **Precision-oriented filtering** — when the user requires only the *first* few results to be relevant, PRP is provably optimal.
- **Foundation for Learning-to-Rank** — modern LambdaMART and LambdaRank models descend from the same probabilistic lineage, with the per-term weight $c_i$ replaced by a learned gradient-boosted score.

# 📘 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic transition below is **explicitly written out**. No step is skipped. The derivation follows the canonical path taught in *Introduction to Information Retrieval* by Manning, Raghavan & Schütze (Chapter 11).

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the BIM Retrieval Status Value (RSV)

### Step 0 — Setup and Notation

Let the document $d$ be represented by a binary vector $\vec{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ where $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ indicates the presence of term $t_i$ in $d$. The query $q$ is represented by $\vec{q} = (q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n)$.

The relevance variable is $R \in \{0, 1\}$.

We want to compute $\text{RSV}_d \;\propto\; P(R=1 \mid \vec{x}, \vec{q})$ and rank documents by this quantity.

---

### Step 1 — Convert Probability to Odds (Monotonic Transform)

Ranking by $P(R=1 \mid \vec{x}, \vec{q})$ is equivalent to ranking by the **odds** $O(R=1 \mid \vec{x}, \vec{q})$ because the odds function $\phi(p) = \dfrac{p}{1-p}$ is **strictly monotonically increasing** for $p \in (0,1)$.

$$O(R=1 \mid \vec{x}, \vec{q}) = \dfrac{P(R=1 \mid \vec{x}, \vec{q})}{P(R=0 \mid \vec{x}, \vec{q})}$$

$$\text{RSV}_d \;=\; O(R=1 \mid \vec{x}, \vec{q})$$

---

### Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Theorem to the Numerator

By the definition of conditional probability and Bayes' theorem:

$$P(R=1 \mid \vec{x}, \vec{q}) = \dfrac{P(\vec{x} \mid R=1, \vec{q}) \cdot P(R=1 \mid \vec{q})}{P(\vec{x} \mid \vec{q})}$$

Similarly:

$$P(R=0 \mid \vec{x}, \vec{q}) = \dfrac{P(\vec{x} \mid R=0, \vec{q}) \cdot P(R=0 \mid \vec{q})}{P(\vec{x} \mid \vec{q})}$$

---

### Step 3 — Form the Odds Ratio

The denominator $P(\vec{x} \mid \vec{q})$ cancels when we form the odds:

$$O(R=1 \mid \vec{x}, \vec{q}) = \dfrac{P(\vec{x} \mid R=1, \vec{q})}{P(\vec{x} \mid R=0, \vec{q})} \cdot \dfrac{P(R=1 \mid \vec{q})}{P(R=0 \mid \vec{q})}$$

The second fraction $\dfrac{P(R=1 \mid \vec{q})}{P(R=0 \mid \vec{q})}$ is a **query-dependent constant** — it does not vary across documents for a fixed query. Therefore, for **ranking purposes only**, it can be safely dropped.

$$\text{RSV}_d \;\propto\; \dfrac{P(\vec{x} \mid R=1, \vec{q})}{P(\vec{x} \mid R=0, \vec{q})}$$

---

### Step 4 — Apply the Naive Bayes (Term Independence) Assumption

The Naive Bayes assumption states that the terms are conditionally independent given the relevance class. Hence:

$$P(\vec{x} \mid R=1, \vec{q}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid R=1, \vec{q})$$

$$P(\vec{x} \mid R=0, \vec{q}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid R=0, \vec{q})$$

Substituting:

$$\text{RSV}_d \;\propto\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{P(x_i \mid R=1, \vec{q})}{P(x_i \mid R=0, \vec{q})}$$

---

### Step 5 — Define Per-Term Probabilities

For each term $t_i$, define:
- $p_i = P(x_i = 1 \mid R = 1, \vec{q})$ — probability that $t_i$ is present in a **relevant** document.
- $q_i = P(x_i = 1 \mid R = 0, \vec{q})$ — probability that $t_i$ is present in a **non-relevant** document.

Then $1 - p_i$ and $1 - q_i$ are the corresponding absence probabilities. For a specific observation $x_i \in \{0,1\}$:

$$\dfrac{P(x_i \mid R=1, \vec{q})}{P(x_i \mid R=0, \vec{q})} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{p_i}{q_i} & \text{if } x_i = 1 \\[6pt] \dfrac{1 - p_i}{1 - q_i} & \text{if } x_i = 0 \end{cases}$$

---

### Step 6 — Split the Product into "Query-Match" and "Query-Absent" Subsets

Let $V$ = set of indices where $q_i = 1$ (terms in the query) and $V'$ = set where $q_i = 0$ (terms NOT in the query). Then:

$$\text{RSV}_d \;\propto\; \prod_{i \in V} \dfrac{P(x_i \mid R=1)}{P(x_i \mid R=0)} \cdot \prod_{i \notin V} \dfrac{P(x_i \mid R=1)}{P(x_i \mid R=0)}$$

A standard simplification in BIM assumes that for terms **not in the query**, the term's presence in the document carries no information about relevance. Formally, $P(x_i \mid R=1) = P(x_i \mid R=0)$ for $i \notin V$. Hence the second product equals **1** and disappears:

$$\text{RSV}_d \;\propto\; \prod_{i \in V} \dfrac{P(x_i \mid R=1)}{P(x_i \mid R=0)}$$

---

### Step 7 — Convert to Log-Space (Sum of Weights)

To convert multiplication into addition (improving numerical stability and matching the additive nature of standard IR ranking), take the logarithm:

$$\log \text{RSV}_d = \sum_{i \in V} \log \left( \dfrac{P(x_i \mid R=1)}{P(x_i \mid R=0)} \right) + \text{const}$$

---

### Step 8 — Separate the $x_i = 1$ and $x_i = 0$ Sub-Cases

For each $i \in V$, substitute the appropriate ratio:

$$\log \text{RSV}_d = \sum_{i \in V : x_i = 1} \log \left( \dfrac{p_i}{q_i} \right) \;+\; \sum_{i \in V : x_i = 0} \log \left( \dfrac{1 - p_i}{1 - q_i} \right) + \text{const}$$

The second summation depends only on the **query** (it does not depend on which document is being scored). Hence for **ranking** it is a constant and can be dropped.

---

### Step 9 — Final Cleaned BIM Formula

$$\boxed{\;\text{RSV}_d = \sum_{i : x_i = q_i = 1} \log \left( \dfrac{p_i \, (1 - q_i)}{q_i \, (1 - p_i)} \right)\;}$$

This is the **canonical BIM ranking function**. Documents are ranked in **decreasing order of $\text{RSV}_d$**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic KTU 14-mark problem type**. You are given a tiny collection, relevance judgments, and asked to compute RSV.

**Collection Statistics:**

| Document | Terms Present |
|:---:|:---|
| $d_1$ | $\{A, B, C\}$ |
| $d_2$ | $\{A, C\}$ |
| $d_3$ | $\{B\}$ |
| $d_4$ | $\{A, B\}$ |
| $d_5$ | $\{C\}$ |

- Total documents: $N = 5$.
- Query $q = \{A, B\}$.
- Known relevance: $d_1, d_4$ are **relevant**; $d_2, d_3, d_5$ are **non-relevant**.

**Step A — Compute document frequencies $n_i$:**

- $n_A = 3$ (in $d_1, d_2, d_4$)
- $n_B = 3$ (in $d_1, d_3, d_4$)
- $n_C = 3$ (in $d_1, d_2, d_5$)

**Step B — Compute $r_i$ (count of relevant docs containing $t_i$):**

- $r_A = 2$ ($d_1$ and $d_4$)
- $r_B = 2$ ($d_1$ and $d_4$)
- $r_C = 1$ ($d_1$ only)

**Step C — Compute $p_i$ and $q_i$ using relevance feedback formulas:**

- Total relevant docs: $R = 2$
- Total non-relevant docs: $N - R = 3$

$$
p_A = \dfrac{r_A}{R} = \dfrac{2}{2} = 1.00
$$

$$
q_A = \dfrac{n_A - r_A}{N - R} = \dfrac{3 - 2}{3} = \dfrac{1}{3} \approx 0.333
$$

$$
p_B = \dfrac{r_B}{R} = \dfrac{2}{2} = 1.00
$$

$$
q_B = \dfrac{n_B - r_B}{N - R} = \dfrac{3 - 2}{3} = \dfrac{1}{3} \approx 0.333
$$

**Step D — Compute per-term weights $c_i$:**

$$
c_A = \log \left( \dfrac{p_A \, (1 - q_A)}{q_A \, (1 - p_A)} \right) = \log \left( \dfrac{1.00 \times 0.667}{0.333 \times 0.00} \right) = \log(\infty) \to \infty
$$

Because $1 - p_A = 0$, the weight is undefined. Apply the standard **smoothing fix**: replace $p_i = 0.5$ when $p_i$ hits the boundary. (This is the standard convention; examine the marking scheme of your KTU paper for the version adopted.)

With $p_A = 0.5$ and $q_A = 0.333$:

$$
c_A = \log \left( \dfrac{0.5 \times 0.667}{0.333 \times 0.5} \right) = \log(2.0) \approx 0.693
$$

Similarly for $c_B$:

$$
c_B = \log(2.0) \approx 0.693
$$

**Step E — Compute RSV for each document:**

| Doc | Terms in $d$ | Terms in $q \cap d$ | $\text{RSV}_d$ | Value |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| $d_1$ | $\{A,B,C\}$ | $\{A, B\}$ | $c_A + c_B$ | $0.693 + 0.693 = 1.386$ |
| $d_2$ | $\{A, C\}$ | $\{A\}$ | $c_A$ | $0.693$ |
| $d_3$ | $\{B\}$ | $\{B\}$ | $c_B$ | $0.693$ |
| $d_4$ | $\{A, B\}$ | $\{A, B\}$ | $c_A + c_B$ | $1.386$ |
| $d_5$ | $\{C\}$ | $\emptyset$ | $0$ | $0.000$ |

**Step F — Final Ranking (decreasing RSV):**

$$
d_1 = d_4 \;>\; d_2 = d_3 \;>\; d_5
$$

✅ Documents $d_1$ and $d_4$ (the known relevant ones) receive the highest scores — the model works correctly.

# 📘 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The BIM Retrieval Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The diagram below shows the **end-to-end architecture** of a probabilistic retrieval system based on BIM, from query input to ranked output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[User Query] --> QBIN[Query Binarizer]
    QBIN --> QVEC[Binary Query Vector q]
    COL[(Document Collection)] --> PARSE[Indexer]
    PARSE --> DBIN[Document Binarizer]
    DBIN --> DVECS[(Inverted Index of Binary Vectors)]
    DVECS --> SCORER
    QVEC --> SCORER
    REL[(Relevance Judgments)] --> EST[Parameter Estimator p and q]
    EST --> SCORER[BM Score Computation Module]
    SCORER --> RSV[RSV per Document]
    RSV --> RANK[Ranker Decreasing RSV]
    RANK --> OUT[Top K Ranked Documents]
    SCORER -->|Uses| F1[Log odds formula]
    EST -->|Uses| F2[Relevance Feedback Counts]
    DVECS -->|Uses| F3[Document Frequency n]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

1. The **query** is converted into a binary vector by the Query Binarizer.
2. The **collection** is indexed offline; each document is reduced to a binary term-incidence vector stored in the **Inverted Index**.
3. **Relevance judgments** (from training data or user feedback) drive the **Parameter Estimator**, which computes $p_i$ and $q_i$.
4. The **Score Computation Module** uses the three formulas $F_1, F_2, F_3$ shown at the right to compute the **RSV** for each candidate document.
5. The **Ranker** orders documents by decreasing RSV and returns the top $K$ to the user.

---

## 4.2 The PRP Decision Boundary (Conceptual Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Document d] --> B[Compute P R is 1 given d q]
    A2[Document d prime] --> B2[Compute P R is 1 given d prime q]
    B --> C{Compare Posterior}
    B2 --> C
    C -->|Higher in d| D[Rank d Above d prime]
    C -->|Higher in d prime| E[Rank d prime Above d]
    C -->|Equal| F[Tie Break by Document ID]
```

**Interpretation:** The PRP is essentially a **pairwise decision rule**. For every pair $(d, d')$ in the candidate set, the system computes the posterior probability of relevance and ranks the higher one first.

---

## 4.3 Term Independence Assumption Visualization (Naive Bayes Structure)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Relevance R] --> P1[P x1 is 1 given R]
    R --> P2[P x2 is 1 given R]
    R --> P3[P x3 is 1 given R]
    R --> Pn[P xn is 1 given R]
    P1 --> JOIN[Joint Factorization]
    P2 --> JOIN
    P3 --> JOIN
    Pn --> JOIN
    JOIN --> RSV[Final RSV sum over i]
```

**Interpretation:** Each term $x_i$ is modeled as **independent** given the relevance class $R$. The joint distribution factorizes into a product of marginals — this is the graphical-model view of the Naive Bayes assumption underlying BIM.

# 📘 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State and explain the Probability Ranking Principle (PRP). Why is it considered the theoretical foundation of probabilistic information retrieval?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** The Probability Ranking Principle (PRP), formulated by S. E. Robertson in 1977, states that *an information retrieval system should rank documents in decreasing order of their estimated probability of relevance to the user's query*, where the probabilities are estimated as accurately as possible from available data.
>
> Formally, a document $d$ should be ranked ahead of $d'$ iff $P(R=1 \mid d, q) > P(R=1 \mid d', q)$.
>
> **Theoretical Optimality (1 mark):** PRP is theoretically optimal because it minimizes the expected cost of retrieval under a 0-1 loss function (assuming equal cost of false positives and false negatives). It is derived directly from Bayes' decision theory, making it the most principled foundation for any ranking-based IR system.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List the four main assumptions of the Binary Independence Model (BIM) used in probabilistic information retrieval.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The four assumptions of BIM (½ mark each):
>
> 1. **Binary document representation:** Each document is represented as a vector of binary term occurrences; term frequencies within a document are ignored.
> 2. **Binary query representation:** The query is similarly represented as a binary term-incidence vector.
> 3. **Term independence (Naive Bayes):** The presence/absence of different terms in a document are statistically independent given the relevance class.
> 4. **Non-query terms are non-discriminative:** Terms absent from the query contribute a constant factor across all documents and can be ignored for ranking purposes.
>
> These assumptions allow the BIM to factorize the joint probability distribution and derive a tractable linear ranking function.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### 🔵 Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]*

**Q. (a)** With suitable derivation, explain how the **Probability Ranking Principle (PRP)** is used to derive the **Binary Independence Model (BIM)** ranking function in information retrieval. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Given a small document collection, compute the **Retrieval Status Value (RSV)** for each document using the BIM model, and produce the final ranking. Show all working. **(7 marks)**

**Collection Data:**

| Document | Terms |
|:---:|:---|
| $D_1$ | information, retrieval, system |
| $D_2$ | information, system |
| $D_3$ | retrieval, model |
| $D_4$ | information, retrieval, model |
| $D_5$ | system, model |

- **Query** $q$ = {information, retrieval}
- **Known relevant documents** = {$D_1$, $D_4$}
- **Total documents** $N = 5$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q. A(a) — PRP and BIM Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Statement of PRP (1 mark):**

> Document $d$ is ranked above $d'$ iff $P(R=1 \mid d, q) > P(R=1 \mid d', q)$, where $R$ is the binary relevance variable.

**Step 2 — Conversion to Odds (1 mark):**

$$\text{RSV}_d = \dfrac{P(R=1 \mid d, q)}{1 - P(R=1 \mid d, q)} = \dfrac{P(R=1 \mid d, q)}{P(R=0 \mid d, q)}$$

**Step 3 — Bayes' Theorem applied (1 mark):**

$$\text{RSV}_d = \dfrac{P(d \mid R=1, q) \cdot P(R=1 \mid q)}{P(d \mid R=0, q) \cdot P(R=0 \mid q)}$$

The prior ratio $P(R=1 \mid q) / P(R=0 \mid q)$ is a query-dependent constant and is dropped for ranking.

**Step 4 — Term Independence Assumption (1 mark):**

$$\text{RSV}_d \propto \dfrac{\prod_i P(x_i \mid R=1, q)}{\prod_i P(x_i \mid R=0, q)}$$

**Step 5 — Eliminate Query-Absent Terms (1 mark):**

Only query terms matter. The product over $i \notin q$ contributes a constant (since the model assumes non-query terms are equally distributed in relevant and non-relevant docs).

**Step 6 — Log-Space Conversion (1 mark):**

$$\text{RSV}_d = \sum_{i : x_i = q_i = 1} \log \left( \dfrac{p_i (1 - q_i)}{q_i (1 - p_i)} \right)$$

**Step 7 — Assumptions Statement (1 mark):**

> 1. Binary document/query vectors.
> 2. Term independence given relevance.
> 3. Relevance is binary.
> 4. Non-query terms carry no information about relevance.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q. A(b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute document frequencies $n_i$ (1 mark):**

- $n_{\text{info}} = 3$ (in $D_1, D_2, D_4$)
- $n_{\text{retr}} = 3$ (in $D_1, D_3, D_4$)

**Step 2 — Compute relevance counts $r_i$ (1 mark):**

- $r_{\text{info}} = 2$ ($D_1, D_4$ are relevant)
- $r_{\text{retr}} = 2$ ($D_1, D_4$ are relevant)

**Step 3 — Compute $p_i$ and $q_i$ (1 mark):**

With $R = 2$ relevant docs and $N - R = 3$ non-relevant docs:

$$
p_{\text{info}} = \dfrac{r_{\text{info}}}{R} = \dfrac{2}{2} = 1.0
$$

$$
q_{\text{info}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{info}} - r_{\text{info}}}{N - R} = \dfrac{3 - 2}{3} = \dfrac{1}{3} \approx 0.333
$$

$$
p_{\text{retr}} = \dfrac{2}{2} = 1.0
$$

$$
q_{\text{retr}} = \dfrac{3 - 2}{3} \approx 0.333
$$

**Step 4 — Apply smoothing $p_i \to 0.5$ for boundary cases (1 mark):**

Since $1 - p_i = 0$ creates a divide-by-zero, use $p_i = 0.5$:

$$
p_{\text{info}} = 0.5, \quad q_{\text{info}} = 0.333
$$

$$
p_{\text{retr}} = 0.5, \quad q_{\text{retr}} = 0.333
$$

**Step 5 — Compute per-term weights $c_i$ (1 mark):**

$$
c_{\text{info}} = \log \left( \dfrac{0.5 \times 0.667}{0.333 \times 0.5} \right) = \log(2.0) \approx 0.693
$$

$$
c_{\text{retr}} = \log \left( \dfrac{0.5 \times 0.667}{0.333 \times 0.5} \right) = \log(2.0) \approx 0.693
$$

**Step 6 — Compute RSV and rank (2 marks):**

| Doc | Query Terms Present | $\text{RSV}_d$ | Value |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| $D_1$ | {info, retr} | $c_{\text{info}} + c_{\text{retr}}$ | $1.386$ |
| $D_2$ | {info} | $c_{\text{info}}$ | $0.693$ |
| $D_3$ | {retr} | $c_{\text{retr}}$ | $0.693$ |
| $D_4$ | {info, retr} | $c_{\text{info}} + c_{\text{retr}}$ | $1.386$ |
| $D_5$ | $\emptyset$ | $0$ | $0.000$ |

**Final Ranking (decreasing RSV):**

$$D_1 = D_4 \;>\; D_2 = D_3 \;>\; D_5$$

---

### 🟢 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **Probability Ranking Principle (PRP)**. Derive it formally from **Bayes' decision theory** and show why it minimizes the expected cost of retrieval. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Discuss the **strengths and limitations** of the **Binary Independence Model (BIM)**. How does the **BM25 model** address some of BIM's limitations? **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q. B(a) — PRP from Bayes' Decision Theory (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define the Cost Structure (1 mark):**

> Let $C_0$ = cost of retrieving a non-relevant document (false positive), $C_1$ = cost of NOT retrieving a relevant document (false negative).

**Step 2 — Expected Cost of Retrieving a Document (2 marks):**

$$\mathbb{E}[\text{Cost} \mid d, q] = C_0 \cdot P(R=0 \mid d, q) + C_1 \cdot P(R=1 \mid d, q)$$

**Step 3 — Pairwise Ranking Inequality (1 mark):**

Document $d$ should be retrieved before $d'$ iff:

$$C_0 P(R=0 \mid d, q) + C_1 P(R=1 \mid d, q) < C_0 P(R=0 \mid d', q) + C_1 P(R=1 \mid d', q)$$

**Step 4 — Specialize to Equal Costs (1 mark):**

Setting $C_0 = C_1$, the inequality reduces to:

$$P(R=0 \mid d, q) + P(R=1 \mid d, q) < P(R=0 \mid d', q) + P(R=1 \mid d', q)$$

Since $P(R=0 \mid d, q) + P(R=1 \mid d, q) = 1$, and the same for $d'$, this is trivially equal. The rigorous derivation uses the **0-1 loss** form, yielding:

$$P(R=1 \mid d, q) > P(R=1 \mid d', q)$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (2 marks):**

> The PRP is therefore the **Bayes-optimal decision rule** for ranking documents under symmetric loss. It is theoretically optimal in the sense that **no other ranking function using the same data can produce a better expected effectiveness** (e.g., higher expected precision at any recall level).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q. B(b) — Strengths, Limitations, and BM25 (7 marks)

**Strengths of BIM (2 marks):**

> 1. **Theoretically optimal** under PRP and 0-1 loss.
> 2. **Probabilistically principled** — every term weight has a clear Bayesian interpretation.
> 3. **Simple and efficient** — reduces to a sum of per-term weights; trivially parallelizable.
> 4. **Strong empirical baseline** that competitive with TF-IDF in many benchmarks.

**Limitations of BIM (3 marks):**

> 1. **Binary term representation** discards term-frequency (TF) information, losing the intuition that a term occurring 10 times is more relevant than one occurring once.
> 2. **Term independence assumption** is unrealistic — terms are correlated in natural language (e.g., "data" and "mining").
