> **Reading the diagrams:** *Circles/plates* in standard plate notation indicate *repetition*; shaded nodes are *observed*; unshaded nodes are *latent*. The arrows in our Mermaid equivalents encode the same dependency directions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Latent Dirichlet Allocation. List its key assumptions. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
LDA is a generative probabilistic model in which each document is represented as a random mixture over **K** latent topics, and each topic is a distribution over the vocabulary. The model assumes:
- (1 mark) **Bag-of-words** representation — word order is ignored.
- (1 mark) **Dirichlet priors** over document–topic ($\theta_d$) and topic–word ($\phi_k$) distributions.
- (1 mark) **Latent topic variables** $z_{d,n}$ for each word position, learned by Bayesian inference.

### Q2. Differentiate between LSA, pLSI, and LDA in two lines each. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **LSA (Deerwester 1990)** — uses SVD of the term–document matrix; purely linear-algebraic; no probabilistic generative story. (1 mark)
- **pLSI (Hofmann 1999)** — introduces a probabilistic latent topic layer but the number of parameters grows linearly with corpus size and there is no prior on document–topic distributions. (1 mark)
- **LDA (Blei et al. 2003)** — fully Bayesian, places a Dirichlet prior on document–topic and topic–word distributions, fixing the parameter-growth problem of pLSI. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full LDA Treatment `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

#### (a) Explain the generative process of LDA. Draw the plate notation and describe every variable. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The generative process assumes a corpus of **D** documents, a vocabulary of size **V**, and a pre-chosen number of topics **K**. The hyperparameters are the **K-vector** $\alpha$ and the **K × V matrix** $\beta$.

For each topic $k \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$:
- Choose a word distribution $\phi_k \sim \text{Dir}(\beta)$.

For each document $d \in \{1, \ldots, D\}$:
- Choose a topic-mixture $\theta_d \sim \text{Dir}(\alpha)$.
- For each word position $n \in \{1, \ldots, N_d\}$:
  - Choose a topic $z_{d,n} \sim \text{Mult}(\theta_d)$.
  - Choose a word $w_{d,n} \sim \text{Mult}(\phi_{z_{d,n}})$.

**Plate notation (verbal description, 2 marks):**
- **Outer plate** labelled **M** = number of documents **D**.
- **Inner plate** labelled **N** = number of word positions in a document.
- **Shaded node** $w_{d,n}$ — observed.
- **Unshaded nodes** $\theta_d$, $\phi_k$, $z_{d,n}$ — latent.
- **Fixed parameters** outside plates: $\alpha$, $\beta$.

**Variable meanings (2 marks):**
- $\theta_d \in \mathbb{R}^{K}$ — mixture weights of topics in document $d$.
- $\phi_k \in \mathbb{R}^{V}$ — probability of every word under topic $k$.
- $z_{d,n} \in \{1,\ldots,K\}$ — topic chosen for the $n$-th word of document $d$.
- $w_{d,n} \in \{1,\ldots,V\}$ — observed word at position $n$ of document $d$.

**Why the Dirichlet prior is essential (1 mark):**
It enforces a sparse, non-negative probability vector whose concentration is controlled by the hyperparameter. Conjugacy makes the posterior updates closed-form.

#### (b) Derive the collapsed Gibbs sampling update for LDA and explain its algorithmic steps. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **goal** of inference is the posterior $p(z \mid w)$, since the true posterior is intractable. Collapsed Gibbs sampling **integrates out** $\theta$ and $\phi$, sampling only the discrete topic assignment $z_i$ for each word position $i$.

**Step 1 — Posterior form (2 marks):**

$$
p(z \mid w) = \frac{p(w, z \mid \alpha, \beta)}{p(w \mid \alpha, \beta)}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' rule and conjugacy (2 marks):**
Using Dirichlet–Multinomial conjugacy and the factorization over documents and words, the conditional for a single $z_i$ is:

$$
p(z_i = k \mid z_{-i}, w) \propto \left(n_{d,k}^{-i} + \alpha_k\right) \cdot \frac{n_{k,w_i}^{-i} + \beta_{w_i}}{n_{k,\cdot}^{-i} + \sum_{v=1}^{V}\beta_v}
$$

where $n_{d,k}^{-i}$ is the number of times topic $k$ appears in document $d$ (excluding $i$), and $n_{k,w_i}^{-i}$ is the count of word $w_i$ assigned to topic $k$ (excluding $i$).

**Step 3 — Algorithm (2 marks):**
1. Randomly initialize $z_i$ for all word tokens $i$.
2. For each Gibbs iteration $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$:
   - For each word $i$: decrement the relevant $n_{d,k}$ and $n_{k,w_i}$ counts.
   - Sample a new $z_i$ from the conditional above.
   - Increment the updated counts.
3. After a **burn-in** of $B$ iterations, average over post-burn-in samples.
4. Estimate $\theta$ and $\phi$ from the final counts:

$$
\hat{\theta}_{d,k} = \frac{n_{d,k} + \alpha_k}{\sum_{k'} n_{d,k'} + \alpha_{k'}}, \qquad \hat{\phi}_{k,v} = \frac{n_{k,v} + \beta_v}{\sum_{v'} n_{k,v'} + \beta_{v'}}
$$

**Step 4 — Complexity & convergence (1 mark):**
Each iteration costs $O(N \cdot K)$ operations. The chain typically converges within a few hundred iterations for moderate corpora.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Comparative & Applied `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

#### (a) Compare LDA with pLSI in tabular form. Explain how LDA overcomes the limitations of pLSI. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | pLSI (Hofmann 1999) | LDA (Blei et al. 2003) |
|---|---|---|
| **Generative story** | Yes, partially | Yes, fully |
| **Document–topic distribution $\theta_d$** | Treated as a *point estimate* indexed by document $d$ | Treated as a *latent random variable* drawn from Dirichlet$(\alpha)$ |
| **Topic–word distribution $\phi_k$** | Point estimate indexed by topic $k$ | Latent random variable drawn from Dirichlet$(\beta)$ |
| **Number of parameters** | $O(KD + KV)$ — grows with corpus size | $O(K + V)$ topic parameters plus per-doc $\theta_d$ of size $K$ — fixed growth in parameters learned per document |
| **Generalization to unseen docs** | Cannot naturally assign topics to a *new* document (no prior) | Natural — integrate $w$ of new doc over the posterior of $\theta_d$ |
| **Overfitting** | High — $KD$ parameters, no prior regularization | Lower — Dirichlet prior regularizes |
| **Bayesian foundation** | Hybrid; no priors | Fully Bayesian |

**How LDA overcomes pLSI's limitations (3 marks):**
1. **Bayesian regularization** — Dirichlet priors shrink the document–topic and topic–word distributions, reducing overfitting. (1 mark)
2. **Fixed parameter count per new doc** — only a $K$-dim $\theta_d$ is needed; no new "document index" parameters. (1 mark)
3. **Generalization to unseen documents** — because $\theta_d$ is a *random variable*, new documents are handled by posterior inference. (1 mark)

#### (b) Discuss applications of LDA in Information Retrieval. Explain how LDA-based document similarity is computed. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Applications (4 marks):**
1. **Document Indexing & Retrieval** — replace or augment TF-IDF vectors with $K$-dimensional topic vectors $\hat{\theta}_d$. Queries are projected into the same space by estimating a query's $\theta_q$. (1 mark)
2. **Document Clustering & Summarization** — dominant topic of a document yields a one-line summary; documents with similar $\theta_d$ form natural clusters. (1 mark)
3. **Query Expansion** — words with high $p(w \mid k)$ for the dominant query topic are added to the original query, mitigating vocabulary mismatch. (1 mark)
4. **Recommendation & Trend Detection** — streaming variants (Online LDA) track topic evolution in social media, news, and scientific literature. (1 mark)

**Document similarity using LDA (3 marks):**
- Compute the estimated topic-mixture vector $\hat{\theta}_d \in \mathbb{R}^{K}$ for every document.
- Compute the estimated query mixture $\hat{\theta}_q$ (treat the query as a short document and run inference).
- Use **cosine similarity**, **KL divergence**, or **JS divergence**:

$$
\text{cosine}(\hat{\theta}_d, \hat{\theta}_q) = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{K} \hat{\theta}_{d,k}\,\hat{\theta}_{q,k}}{\sqrt{\sum_{k}\hat{\theta}_{d,k}^2}\;\sqrt{\sum_{k}\hat{\theta}_{q,k}^2}}
$$

$$
\text{KL}(\hat{\theta}_q \,\|\, \hat{\theta}_d) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \hat{\theta}_{q,k} \log\!\left(\frac{\hat{\theta}_{q,k}}{\hat{\theta}_{d,k}}\right)
$$

Documents with smallest KL (or highest cosine) to the query are ranked top.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in LDA answers — read before you write:**
> 1. **Confusing $\alpha$ and $\beta$.** $\alpha$ is the prior on the *document–topic* distribution $\theta_d$; $\beta$ is the prior on the *topic–word* distribution $\phi_k$. Mixing them up costs an easy 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting conjugacy.** Whenever a derivation question appears, *always* mention that Dirichlet is the **conjugate prior** of the multinomial — it is the reason the Gibbs update has the form it does.
> 3. **Skipping the Dirichlet PDF.** A definition-only answer without the formula $p(\theta \mid \alpha) = \frac{\Gamma(\sum \alpha_k)}{\prod \Gamma(\alpha_k)}\prod \theta_k^{\alpha_k - 1}$ is incomplete.
> 4. **No plate diagram.** KTU examiners **expect** a plate-notation (or its block equivalent) — without it, you forfeit 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing evaluation metrics.** Perplexity is for held-out likelihood; topic coherence (e.g., NPMI) is for semantic quality. Don't conflate.
> 6. **Writing "LDA is supervised."** LDA is **unsupervised** — it needs no labels.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — go through this the night before the exam:**

- **Topic Model** = a generative probabilistic model that uncovers hidden themes from a corpus.
- **LDA** is a three-level Bayesian model: **corpus → documents → words**, with topics as the hidden layer.
- The **Dirichlet distribution** is the conjugate prior of the **multinomial** — this single fact justifies almost all of LDA's math.
- **Generative process** for each document: draw $\theta_d \sim \text{Dir}(\alpha)$, then for each word draw $z_{d,n} \sim \text{Mult}(\theta_d)$ and $w_{d,n} \sim \text{Mult}(\phi_{z_{d,n}})$.
- **Plate notation**: outer plate = **D documents**, inner plate = **N word positions**, shaded $w$ = observed, all others = latent.
- **Inference is intractable in closed form** — use **collapsed Gibbs sampling** (simple, accurate) or **variational inference** (faster, deterministic).
- **Gibbs update**:
  $p(z_i = k \mid \cdot) \propto (n_{d,k}^{-i} + \alpha_k)\cdot\frac{n_{k,w_i}^{-i} + \beta_{w_i}}{n_{k,\cdot}^{-i} + \sum_v \beta_v}$
- **Recovery of $\theta_d, \phi_k$** uses normalized counts plus the prior — this is **Bayesian smoothing**.
- **LDA vs pLSI**: LDA is fully Bayesian, regularized, and generalizes to unseen documents; pLSI has $O(KD)$ parameters and no prior.
- **IR application**: replace TF-IDF with $\hat{\theta}_d$ topic vectors; rank documents by **cosine similarity** or **KL divergence** to the query topic vector $\hat{\theta}_q$.
- **Evaluation**: **perplexity** for predictive likelihood; **topic coherence** (NPMI, C_v) for semantic quality.
- **Hyperparameter intuition**: small $\alpha$ ⇒ sparse topic mixtures; small $\beta$ ⇒ sparse topic–word distributions; large values ⇒ uniform distributions.
- **Key limitation**: LDA ignores word order (bag-of-words) and treats topics as uncorrelated. Extensions like **LDA + HMM (HLDA)** and **Correlated Topic Model (CTM)** address this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation of Information Retrieval Systems: Precision, Recall, and F-Measure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Evaluation of Information Retrieval Systems: Precision, Recall, and F-Measure ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Information Retrieval (IR) Evaluation** is the systematic, quantitative process of measuring the effectiveness of an Information Retrieval System by comparing the documents it retrieves against a ground-truth set of documents deemed *relevant* to a given information need (query). In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECDT745 - Module 2)**, evaluation is treated as an **unranked retrieval assessment framework** (with extensions to ranked retrieval) built upon the binary classification paradigm. For any given query, the universe of documents in the collection is partitioned into four mutually exclusive quadrants: | Quadrant | Definition | KTU Notation | |---|---|---| | **True Positives** | Relevant documents that were retrieved | $TP$ | | **False Positives** | Irrelevant documents that were retrieved | $FP$ | | **False Negatives** | Relevant documents that were not retrieved | $FN$ | | **True Negatives** | Irrelevant documents that were not retrieved | $TN$ | These four quadrants form the foundation of the **Confusion Matrix** for IR, and every evaluation metric (Precision, Recall, F-Measure) is a deterministic function of these four cardinalities. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The "Library Detective" Model** > Imagine you are a librarian, and a student asks: *"Find me all books in the library about **Quantum Computing**."* The library has 1,000 books in total, and you (the librarian) know that exactly **50 books** are genuinely about Quantum Computing. You walk through the aisles and pull out a stack of **40 books** that *you believe* match the query. > > When the student later checks your stack against the ground truth: > - **30 books** in your stack ARE genuinely about Quantum Computing. → **TP = 30** > - **10 books** in your stack are NOT about Quantum Computing. → **FP = 10** > - **20 books** about Quantum Computing were left on the shelf. → **FN = 20** > - **940 books** are correctly left on the shelf (irrelevant AND not retrieved). → **TN = 940** > > **Now, how do we grade you?** > - **Precision** asks: *"Of the 40 books you handed me, how many were actually useful?"* → $\frac{30}{40} = 0.75$ (75%) > - **Recall** asks: *"Of the 50 truly relevant books in the library, how many did you find?"* → $\frac{30}{50} = 0.60$ (60%) > - **F-Measure** asks: *"What is your balanced overall score?"* → Combines the two. > > This is exactly how KTU evaluates an IR system: a librarian who hands you only perfect books (high Precision) but misses many real ones (low Recall) is just as flawed as a librarian who gives you everything on the shelf (high Recall) but buries you in junk (low Precision). --- ### 1.3 Why Evaluation is Non-Trivial > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Pillars of IR Difficulty:** > 1. **Relevance is subjective** — Document relevance depends on user intent, which is not directly observable. > 2. **The set is huge** — Collections like TREC contain *millions* of documents; exhaustive manual judgment is infeasible. > 3. **Trade-offs are inherent** — Increasing Recall almost always decreases Precision (and vice versa) → hence the need for a **harmonic compromise** called the F-Measure. This module prepares you to mathematically quantify these trade-offs, which is the cornerstone of any KTU examination question on IR evaluation. --- ### 1.4 Anchor Metrics in Bold (For Quick Recall) - **Precision** ($P$): The fraction of **retrieved documents that are relevant**. - **Recall** ($R$): The fraction of **relevant documents that are retrieved**. - **F-Measure** ($F$): The **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall. - **F1-Score**: The **equal-weighted harmonic mean** ($\beta = 1$). - **E-Measure** ($E$): A parameterized metric allowing emphasis on either $P$ or $R$ via $\beta$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four-Quadrant Decomposition Logic The evaluation of an IR system can be modeled as a **binary decision problem** at the document level. For a single query $q$, let: - $\mathcal{R}$ = set of all *relevant* documents for $q$ (ground truth). - $\mathcal{A}$ = set of all documents *retrieved* by the system (algorithm output). - $|\mathcal{R}|$ = total relevant documents in the collection. - $|\mathcal{A}|$ = total documents retrieved by the algorithm. - $|\mathcal{R} \cap \mathcal{A}|$ = documents that are BOTH relevant AND retrieved. The four cardinalities emerge from set theory: $$ TP = \vert \mathcal{R} \cap \mathcal{A} \vert, \quad FP = \vert \mathcal{A} \setminus \mathcal{R} \vert, \quad FN = \vert \mathcal{R} \setminus \mathcal{A} \vert, \quad TN = \vert \overline{\mathcal{R}} \cap \overline{\mathcal{A}} \vert $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why TN is ignored in IR metrics:** In most IR settings, $|\overline{\mathcal{R}} \cap \overline{\mathcal{A}}|$ is enormous (millions of irrelevant, unretrieved documents) and provides no discriminative power between systems. Hence, KTU's standard metrics are defined *exclusively* using $TP$, $FP$, and $FN$. --- ### 2.2 The Two Foundational Metrics — Precision & Recall #### 2.2.1 Precision (P) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Precision** measures the **purity** of the retrieved set. It answers: *"When the system says 'yes', how often is it correct?"* $$ P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{\vert \mathcal{R} \cap \mathcal{A} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} $$ - **Range:** $0 \le P \le 1$ - **P = 1** means *every* retrieved document is relevant (perfect purity). - **P = 0** means *no* retrieved document is relevant (complete failure). #### 2.2.2 Recall (R) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Recall** measures the **completeness** of the retrieved set. It answers: *"Of all the relevant things in the world, how many did the system find?"* $$ R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{\vert \mathcal{R} \cap \mathcal{A} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{R} \vert} $$ - **Range:** $0 \le R \le 1$ - **R = 1** means *every* relevant document in the collection was retrieved (perfect completeness). - **R = 0** means the system retrieved *none* of the relevant documents. #### 2.2.3 The Inherent Trade-Off As you increase the number of documents retrieved (i.e., increase $|\mathcal{A}|$ by lowering the ranking threshold), **Recall monotonically increases** (you are more likely to scoop up all relevant items), but **Precision monotonically decreases** (you are also scooping up more irrelevant noise). This is the famous **Precision-Recall Trade-Off**, and it is a common 7-mark sub-question in KTU examinations. --- ### 2.3 F-Measure — The Harmonic Compromise The **Arithmetic Mean** of $P$ and $R$ is a *bad* measure: $A = \frac{P+R}{2}$ can be high even when one metric is catastrophically low. For example, $P=0.01, R=1.0$ gives $A = 0.505$, which falsely appears "passable". The **Harmonic Mean** punishes extreme imbalance: $$ F_\beta = \frac{(\beta^2 + 1) \cdot P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R} $$ where $\beta$ is a non-negative real parameter that controls the relative weight of Recall over Precision: | $\beta$ Value | Emphasis | Interpretation | |---|---|---| | $\beta = 0$ | Pure Precision | $F_0 = P$ (Recall ignored) | | $\beta = 1$ | Equal Weight | **F1-Score** (most common) | | $\beta = 2$ | Recall (2× weight) | Used when missing a doc is costlier | | $\beta \to \infty$ | Pure Recall | $F_\infty = R$ (Precision ignored) | #### 2.3.1 The Special Case — F1-Score Setting $\beta = 1$: $$ F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Property of F1:** It is bounded above by the *arithmetic* mean and below by the *minimum* of $P$ and $R$. Formally: $\min(P, R) \le F_1 \le \frac{P+R}{2}$, with equality holding only when $P = R$. --- ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is the EXACT cheat-sheet you should memorize for KTU ESE. Every formula here is directly examinable.** | # | Metric | Formula | Range | KTU Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Precision** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $[0,1]$ | Measures retrieval purity | | 2 | **Recall** | $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | Measures retrieval completeness | | 3 | **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+FP+FN+TN}$ | $[0,1]$ | Rarely used in IR (TN-dominant) | | 4 | **Error Rate** | $\text{Err} = 1 - \text{Acc}$ | $[0,1]$ | Complement of accuracy | | 5 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}$ | $[0,1]$ | Balanced harmonic mean | | 6 | **F$_\beta$-Measure** | $F_\beta = \frac{(\beta^2+1)PR}{\beta^2 P + R}$ | $[0,1]$ | Weighted harmonic mean | | 7 | **E-Measure** | $E = 1 - \frac{(\beta^2+1)PR}{\beta^2 P + R}$ | $[0,1]$ | Complement of F-Measure (van Rijsbergen, 1979) | | 8 | **R-Precision** | $R = \frac{TP \text{ at rank } \vert \mathcal{R} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{R} \vert}$ | $[0,1]$ | Precision at the rank equal to total relevant docs | | 9 | **Average Precision (AP)** | $\text{AP} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{R} \vert}\sum_{k=1}^{n} P(k) \cdot \text{rel}(k)$ | $[0,1]$ | Area under PR-curve (single query) | | 10 | **Mean Average Precision (MAP)** | $\text{MAP} = \frac{1}{Q}\sum_{q=1}^{Q} \text{AP}(q)$ | $[0,1]$ | Gold standard across multiple queries | | 11 | **Specificity** | $\text{Spec} = \frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | $[0,1]$ | True negative rate (rare in IR) | | 12 | **Fall-out** | $\text{Fall-out} = \frac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | $[0,1]$ | Irrelevant retrieved / total irrelevant | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pipe-Symbol Pitfall:** In the markdown table above, I have used `\vert` (LaTeX vertical bar) instead of the raw `|` symbol to avoid breaking the markdown table renderer. If you handwrite these formulas in your exam, use a regular vertical bar. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The Precision-Recall-F1 framework is **not confined to web search**. It is deployed in: - **Medical Diagnosis Systems** — High Recall is critical (do not miss any tumors), even at the cost of lower Precision (some false alarms are acceptable). - **Legal E-Discovery (eDiscovery)** — High Recall is mandated by law (you must find ALL responsive documents). - **Spam Filters** — High Precision is preferred (do not delete legitimate emails). - **Biometric Authentication** — F1-Score balances false acceptances and false rejections. - **Recommender Systems (Netflix, Amazon)** — MAP is the de facto industry standard for ranking quality. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When a question says *"a system has high precision but low recall"*, the canonical real-world example is a **spam filter that is over-cautious** — it only flags emails it is 100% sure are spam, missing many actual spam emails (low recall). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the F-Measure from First Principles We wish to construct a single scalar metric $F$ that combines $P$ and $R$ and satisfies three *engineering axioms*: > **Axiom 1 (Symmetry):** $F(P, R) = F(R, P)$ — The metric should not unfairly favor precision over recall. > > **Axiom 2 (Monotonicity):** If $P' \ge P$ and $R' \ge R$, then $F(P', R') \ge F(P, R)$. > > **Axiom 3 (Penalization):** If one of $P$ or $R$ is zero, then $F = 0$ (a system that fails on either axis is worthless). The **weighted harmonic mean** is the simplest non-trivial family that satisfies all three. We derive it by generalizing the simple harmonic mean $H = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}}$. **Step 1: Express the Harmonic Mean in reciprocal form** $$ \frac{1}{H} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}\right) = \frac{P + R}{2PR} $$ **Step 2: Introduce the weighting parameter $\beta^2$** To allow asymmetric weighting, we scale the Recall term by $\beta^2$: $$ \frac{1}{F_\beta} = \frac{1}{1+\beta^2}\left(\frac{1}{P} + \frac{\beta^2}{R}\right) $$ The choice of $\frac{1}{1+\beta^2}$ as the prefactor guarantees $F_\beta = 1$ when $P = R = 1$. **Step 3: Combine the fractions over a common denominator** $$ \frac{1}{F_\beta} = \frac{1}{1+\beta^2}\left(\frac{R + \beta^2 P}{PR}\right) $$ **Step 4: Invert to obtain $F_\beta$** $$ F_\beta = \frac{(1+\beta^2)\, PR}{\beta^2 P + R} $$ > Since $\beta^2$ appears symmetrically, we can equivalently write it as $\beta$ in the numerator by absorbing the $+1$ into $(\beta^2 + 1)$, yielding the **canonical form** used in KTU textbooks: $$ \boxed{\,F_\beta = \frac{(\beta^2 + 1)\cdot P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}\,} $$ **Step 5: Verify the F1 special case** Set $\beta = 1$: $$ F_1 = \frac{(1+1)PR}{1\cdot P + R} = \frac{2PR}{P+R} \quad \blacksquare $$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Complete KTU-Style Calculation > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A search engine is evaluated on a single query. The collection contains **500** documents. The ground-truth set of relevant documents is **$\vert \mathcal{R} \vert = 40$**. The system retrieved **$\vert \mathcal{A} \vert = 50$** documents, of which **30 are relevant**. Compute Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and the E-Measure with $\beta = 2$. #### Step 1: Identify the Confusion Matrix $$ TP = 30, \quad FP = \vert \mathcal{A} \vert - TP = 50 - 30 = 20, \quad FN = \vert \mathcal{R} \vert - TP = 40 - 30 = 10 $$ The confusion matrix is: $$ \begin{bmatrix} & \text{Actually Relevant} & \text{Actually Irrelevant} \\ \text{Retrieved} & TP = 30 & FP = 20 \\ \text{Not Retrieved} & FN = 10 & TN = 440 \end{bmatrix} $$ We can verify: $30 + 20 + 10 + 440 = 500$ ✓ (matches total collection size). #### Step 2: Compute Precision $$ P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{30}{30 + 20} = \frac{30}{50} = 0.60 $$ **Interpretation:** 60% of the documents the system returned were genuinely useful. #### Step 3: Compute Recall $$ R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{30}{30 + 10} = \frac{30}{40} = 0.75 $$ **Interpretation:** The system found 75% of all relevant documents in the collection. #### Step 4: Compute F1-Score ($\beta = 1$) $$ F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.60 \cdot 0.75}{0.60 + 0.75} $$ $$ F_1 = \frac{0.90}{1.35} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667 $$ **Interpretation:** The balanced harmonic score is 66.67%. Note that this is *less than* the arithmetic mean $\frac{0.60 + 0.75}{2} = 0.675$, confirming the harmonic mean is a more conservative estimator. #### Step 5: Compute F$_\beta$ for $\beta = 2$ (Recall-weighted) $$ F_2 = \frac{(2^2 + 1) \cdot P \cdot R}{2^2 \cdot P + R} = \frac{5 \cdot 0.60 \cdot 0.75}{4 \cdot 0.60 + 0.75} $$ $$ F_2 = \frac{5 \cdot 0.45}{2.40 + 0.75} = \frac{2.25}{3.15} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.7143 $$ **Interpretation:** Because Recall is weighted twice as much as Precision, the $F_2$ score is *higher* than $F_1$ — the system's strength in recall is now being rewarded more heavily. #### Step 6: Compute the E-Measure (van Rijsbergen) The E-Measure is defined as $E = 1 - F_\beta$. For $\beta = 2$: $$ E_2 = 1 - F_2 = 1 - 0.7143 = 0.2857 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is E-Measure used?** Van Rijsbergen (1979) introduced E so that **minimization** corresponds to better systems (analogous to "error"), making it mathematically convenient for optimization. In KTU exams, you may be asked to compute either $F$ or $E$ — they are direct complements. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Metric to Use When? > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following decision table is a high-yield KTU exam preparation artifact. Memorize it.** | Scenario | Best Metric | Why? | |---|---|---| | Web search (general) | **F1 or MAP** | Balanced importance of $P$ and $R$ | | Cancer detection (medical) | **Recall (R)** | Missing a tumor is catastrophic; false alarms are tolerable | | Spam filter (user-facing) | **Precision (P)** | Deleting a real email is unforgivable; missing spam is acceptable | | Legal eDiscovery | **Recall (R)** | Courts demand exhaustive retrieval | | Comparing 2 systems on 100 queries | **MAP** | Aggregates AP across all queries, the TREC gold standard | | Single ranked list, partial relevance | **NDCG / MAP** | Position-aware metrics | | Quick sanity check on binary classifier | **F1-Score** | Single-number summary, robust to class imbalance | --- ### 3.4 Mathematical Properties (For 14-Mark KTU Derivations) > [!NOTE] > **Property 1 — F-Measure is bounded between min and arithmetic mean:** > > Let $m = \min(P, R)$ and $a = \frac{P+R}{2}$. Then $m \le F_1 \le a$, with equality only when $P = R$. *Proof sketch:* Since $F_1$ is the harmonic mean and $H \le A$ by AM-HM inequality, the lower bound follows from $H \ge \min(P,R)$ (a property of harmonic means). $\blacksquare$ > **Property 2 — F-Measure is symmetric in $P$ and $R$ at $\beta = 1$:** > > $F_1(P, R) = F_1(R, P)$ by direct substitution into $\frac{2PR}{P+R}$. > **Property 3 — As $\beta \to 0$, $F_\beta \to P$; as $\beta \to \infty$, $F_\beta \to R$:** > > Divide numerator and denominator of $F_\beta$ by $\beta^2$: > > $$ > F_\beta = \frac{(1 + 1/\beta^2)PR}{P + R/\beta^2} > $$ > > As $\beta \to 0$: $1/\beta^2 \to \infty$, so $F_\beta \to \frac{(1/\beta^2) PR}{R/\beta^2} = P$. As $\beta \to \infty$: $1/\beta^2 \to 0$, so $F_\beta \to \frac{PR}{P} = R$. $\blacksquare$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Four-Quadrant Confusion Matrix (Mermaid Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB start([Document Collection N = 500]) start --> split1{Relevant to Query?} split1 -->|Yes - 40 docs| relSet[Relevant Set R] split1 -->|No - 460 docs| irrelSet[Irrelevant Set NOT-R] relSet --> split2{Retrieved by System?} irrelSet --> split3{Retrieved by System?} split2 -->|Yes| tpBlock["TP = 30
True Positives"] split2 -->|No| fnBlock["FN = 10
False Negatives
Missed Relevant Docs"] split3 -->|Yes| fpBlock["FP = 20
False Positives
Noise Retrieved"] split3 -->|No| tnBlock["TN = 440
True Negatives
Correctly Ignored"] tpBlock --> metricCalc[Metric Computation Engine] fpBlock --> metricCalc fnBlock --> metricCalc tnBlock --> metricCalc metricCalc --> outP[Precision = 30/50 = 0.60] metricCalc --> outR[Recall = 30/40 = 0.75] metricCalc --> outF1[F1 Score = 2PR or P+R = 0.6667] metricCalc --> outF2[F-beta beta=2 = 0.7143] outP --> finalEval([Final System Grade]) outR --> finalEval outF1 --> finalEval outF2 --> finalEval ``` --- ### 4.2 Precision-Recall Trade-Off Curve (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph thresholdAxis["Threshold Sweep"] tHigh["Threshold = HIGH
Few docs returned"] tMid["Threshold = MEDIUM
Balanced retrieval"] tLow["Threshold = LOW
Many docs returned"] end tHigh --> pHigh["Precision = HIGH
Recall = LOW
System is cautious"] tMid --> pMid["Precision = MEDIUM
Recall = MEDIUM
Sweet spot F1 max"] tLow --> pLow["Precision = LOW
Recall = HIGH
System is exhaustive"] pHigh --> curveNode["PR-Curve
P vs R traced
as threshold varies"] pMid --> curveNode pLow --> curveNode curveNode --> aucNode["Area Under Curve
equals Average Precision"] aucNode --> mapNode["MAP across queries
final TREC metric"] style thresholdAxis fill:E6F3FF,stroke:0066CC style curveNode fill:FFE6E6,stroke:CC0000 style mapNode fill:E6FFE6,stroke:006600 ``` --- ### 4.3 Metric Dependency Graph ```mermaid flowchart TD tp[TP] fp[FP] fn[FN] tn[TN] tp --> precision[Precision] fp --> precision tp --> recall[Recall] fn --> recall tp --> fmeasure[F-Measure] precision --> fmeasure recall --> fmeasure fp --> emeasure[E-Measure] fn --> emeasure precision --> emeasure recall --> emeasure tp --> mapNode[Mean Average Precision] fp --> mapNode recall --> mapNode style tp fill:90EE90 style fp fill:FFB6C1 style fn fill:FFB6C1 style tn fill:D3D3D3 style fmeasure fill:FFD700 style emeasure fill:FFD700 style mapNode fill:87CEEB ``` --- ### 4.4 Evaluation Pipeline (System-Level Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB q[User Query Q] coll[Document Collection D] sys[IR System] q --> sys coll --> sys sys --> retrievedSet[Retrieved Set A] retrievedSet --> comp{Compare with
Ground Truth R} ground[Ground Truth
Relevance Judgments] --> comp comp --> confMatrix[Build Confusion Matrix
TP FP FN TN] confMatrix --> metricEngine[Metric Computation
Engine] metricEngine --> out1[Precision P] metricEngine --> out2[Recall R] metricEngine --> out3[F1 Score] metricEngine --> out4[F-beta] metricEngine --> out5[E-Measure] metricEngine --> out6[MAP] out1 --> report[Evaluation Report] out2 --> report out3 --> report out4 --> report out5 --> report out6 --> report style sys fill:FFE4B5 style confMatrix fill:FFFACD style metricEngine fill:98FB98 style report fill:87CEEB ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Q1.** Define *Precision* and *Recall* in Information Retrieval systems. State one real-world scenario where **Recall is more important than Precision**. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Precision (P)** is the fraction of retrieved documents that are relevant: $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$. It measures the *purity* of retrieval. > > **Recall (R)** is the fraction of relevant documents that are retrieved: $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$. It measures the *completeness* of retrieval. > > **Scenario where Recall > Precision:** **Cancer detection / medical diagnosis systems** — a radiologist cannot afford to miss a tumor (false negative), so the system should flag every possible case, even if it produces a few false alarms. Missing a real cancer is catastrophic, whereas an extra biopsy is merely inconvenient. --- > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **Q2.** What is the **F-Measure** in IR evaluation? Why is the **harmonic mean** used instead of the arithmetic mean to combine Precision and Recall? **Model Answer:** > The **F-Measure** is the weighted harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, defined as $F_\beta = \frac{(\beta^2+1)PR}{\beta^2 P + R}$. The harmonic mean is preferred because it **penalizes extreme imbalance** between $P$ and $R$. For example, a system with $P = 1.0$ and $R = 0.1$ yields an arithmetic mean of $0.55$ (misleadingly passable), but a harmonic mean of $F_1 \approx 0.18$ (correctly poor). This ensures the F-Measure is high **only** when *both* metrics are reasonably high. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Computation-Heavy **(a)** A search engine is evaluated on a single query. The total collection has 1,000 documents. The ground-truth set of relevant documents contains **80 documents**. The system returns **100 documents**, of which **60 are relevant**. Compute: (i) Precision (ii) Recall (iii) F1-Score (iv) F-Measure with $\beta = 2$ (v) E-Measure with $\beta = 1$ **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Precision-Recall trade-off** with a suitable graph. Discuss how the choice of metric changes for **(i)** spam filtering and **(ii)** medical diagnosis, with justification. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A **Part (a) — Computation** **Given:** $|\mathcal{R}| = 80$, $|\mathcal{A}| = 100$, $TP = 60$. **Step 1: Derive the confusion matrix entries** $$ FP = |\mathcal{A}| - TP = 100 - 60 = 40 $$ $$ FN = |\mathcal{R}| - TP = 80 - 60 = 20 $$ $$ TN = 1000 - (TP + FP + FN) = 1000 - 120 = 880 $$ **(i) Precision:** $$ P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{60}{60 + 40} = \frac{60}{100} = 0.60 $$ **(ii) Recall:** $$ R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{60}{60 + 20} = \frac{60}{80} = 0.75 $$ **(iii) F1-Score ($\beta = 1$):** $$ F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.60 \cdot 0.75}{0.60 + 0.75} = \frac{0.90}{1.35} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667 $$ **(iv) F-Measure with $\beta = 2$:** $$ F_2 = \frac{(2^2 + 1) \cdot P \cdot R}{2^2 \cdot P + R} = \frac{5 \cdot 0.60 \cdot 0.75}{4 \cdot 0.60 + 0.75} = \frac{2.25}{3.15} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.714 $$ **(v) E-Measure with $\beta = 1$:** $$ E_1 = 1 - F_1 = 1 - 0.667 = 0.333 $$ **Part (b) — Conceptual Explanation** > The **Precision-Recall trade-off** arises because as we lower the relevance threshold and return more documents, **Recall monotonically increases** (we scoop up more of the relevant set) but **Precision monotonically decreases** (we also scoop up more noise). The relationship is non-linear and is conventionally visualized as a **P-R curve** with Recall on the x-axis and Precision on the y-axis, where the curve drops sharply as recall approaches 1. > > **For Spam Filtering:** **Precision is paramount.** A spam filter that deletes a legitimate email (false positive) is far more damaging than one that lets a spam email pass (false negative). Users tolerate spam in their inbox but will abandon a product that loses their important mails. Hence, the system should be tuned to maximize Precision, even at the cost of moderate Recall. > > **For Medical Diagnosis:** **Recall is paramount.** Missing a malignant tumor (false negative) can be fatal. The system should flag every suspicious case, even at the cost of additional biopsies or scans (false positives). Hence, the system should be tuned to maximize Recall, accepting lower Precision as an acceptable trade-off. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Theory-Heavy Alternative **(a)** Define the **Confusion Matrix** for an IR system. Explain each of its four cells (TP, FP, FN, TN) with a one-line example. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the **F$_\beta$-Measure formula** from the harmonic mean. Show that as $\beta \to 0$, the metric reduces to Precision, and as $\beta \to \infty$, it reduces to Recall. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B **Part (a) — Confusion Matrix** > The **Confusion Matrix** is a 2×2 tabular summary of the IR system's binary classification behavior, comparing the *predicted* relevance (retrieved vs. not retrieved) against the *actual* relevance (relevant vs. irrelevant) for every document in the collection. | | **Actually Relevant** | **Actually Irrelevant** | |---|---|---| | **Retrieved** | **TP** (True Positive) — A relevant doc that was returned | **FP** (False Positive) — A junk doc that was wrongly returned | | **Not Retrieved** | **FN** (False Negative) — A relevant doc that was missed | **TN** (True Negative) — A junk doc that was correctly ignored | **Examples:** - **TP:** A search for *"machine learning tutorials"* returns a well-written ML blog post. ✓ - **FP:** The same search returns a generic "AI in cooking" article (irrelevant but matched). ✗ - **FN:** A seminal ML textbook is in the database but the system did not return it. ✗ - **TN:** A recipe for "chocolate cake" is in the database and the system correctly ignored it for the ML query. ✓ **Part (b) — Derivation of F$_\beta$** > **Step 1: Begin with the simple harmonic mean** $H(P, R) = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}}$. > > **Step 2: Generalize with weight $\beta^2$ on Recall's reciprocal** to allow Recall to dominate when $\beta > 1$: > > $$ > \frac{1}{F_\beta} = \frac{1}{1+\beta^2}\left(\frac{1}{P} + \frac{\beta^2}{R}\right) > $$ > > **Step 3: Combine over a common denominator:** > > $$ > \frac{1}{F_\beta} = \frac{R + \beta^2 P}{(1+\beta^2) PR} > $$ > > **Step 4: Invert:** > > $$ > F_\beta = \frac{(\beta^2 + 1) PR}{\beta^2 P + R} > $$ > > **Step 5: Limiting case $\beta \to 0$** (Precision dominates): > > Divide numerator and denominator by $\beta^2$: > > $$ > F_\beta = \frac{(1 + 1/\beta^2)PR}{P + R/\beta^2} > $$ > > As $\beta \to 0$, $1/\beta^2 \to \infty$ and $R/\beta^2 \to \infty$ (assuming $R > 0$): > > $$ > F_\beta \to \frac{(1/\beta^2) PR}{R/\beta^2} = P > $$ > > **Step 6: Limiting case $\beta \to \infty$** (Recall dominates): > > As $\beta \to \infty$, $1/\beta^2 \to 0$ and $R/\beta^2 \to 0$: > > $$ > F_\beta \to \frac{1 \cdot PR}{P + 0} = R \quad \blacksquare > $$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing the denominators:** Precision uses $TP+FP$ (total retrieved), Recall uses $TP+FN$ (total relevant). Swapping these is the **#1 most common error** — losing 2 to 3 marks per question. > > 2. **Forgetting the $\beta^2$ in the numerator:** Many students write $F_\beta = \frac{2PR}{\beta^2 P + R}$ instead of $\frac{(\beta^2+1)PR}{\beta^2 P + R}$. The "+1" inside the parentheses is mandatory. > > 3. **Reporting only F1 when the question asks for F$_\beta$:** Always re-read whether the question specifies $\beta = 1$ or general $\beta$. > > 4. **Confusing E-Measure with Error Rate:** $E_\beta = 1 - F_\beta$ is the E-Measure (van Rijsbergen), NOT the classification error rate. They are conceptually similar but defined in different contexts. > > 5. **Not stating units / ranges:** Always state $0 \le P, R, F \le 1$ in your final answer — examiners allocate 1 mark for this. > > 6. **Arithmetic mean instead of harmonic mean:** Writing $\frac{P+R}{2}$ instead of $\frac{2PR}{P+R}$ for F1 will fetch **zero** marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.** - [ ] **Precision** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ → *purity of retrieval* - [ ] **Recall** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ → *completeness of retrieval* - [ ] **F-Measure** = harmonic mean of $P$ and $R$, with weight $\beta^2$ on Recall - [ ] **F1-Score** = $\frac{2PR}{P+R}$ is the most commonly used F-Measure - [ ] **E-Measure** = $1 - F_\beta$ is the complement (van Rijsbergen's formulation) - [ ] **Trade-off:** Increasing Recall generally decreases Precision and vice versa - [ ] **Medical/legal applications** prioritize **Recall** (do not miss anything) - [ ] **Spam filtering / user-facing** prioritizes **Precision** (do not annoy users) - [ ] **MAP** is the industry gold standard for ranked retrieval across multiple queries - [ ] **R-Precision** is Precision evaluated at rank $|\mathcal{R}|$ (useful single-point summary) - [ ] **TN is ignored in IR metrics** because it dwarfs all other values - [ ] **F1 is bounded**: $\min(P,R) \le F_1 \le \frac{P+R}{2}$ (always ≤ arithmetic mean) - [ ] **Limiting behavior**: $F_0 = P$ and $F_\infty = R$ - [ ] **Harmonic mean punishes imbalance** — a system that is great at one metric but terrible at the other will have a low F1 - [ ] **Always show confusion matrix entries** explicitly before computing $P$ and $R$ > **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Purity for users, Completeness for courts, Balance for benchmarks."* 🎯

topics.title: Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** is a two-dimensional graphical evaluation plot used in Information Retrieval (IR) and Machine Learning to assess the diagnostic ability of a binary classifier or ranking model as its discrimination threshold is varied. The curve is plotted in a unit square known as the **ROC Space**, with the **False Positive Rate (FPR)** on the horizontal axis (abscissa, $x$-axis) and the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** on the vertical axis (ordinate, $y$-axis).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECDT745 – Module 2):**
> An ROC curve is the locus of points $(FPR(\tau), TPR(\tau))$ traced as the decision threshold $\tau$ is swept from its maximum to its minimum value. It was originally developed during World War II for signal detection in radar systems, hence the name "Receiver Operating Characteristic."

### 1.2 The Confusion Matrix — Foundation of ROC Analysis

Every retrieval decision can be classified into one of four mutually exclusive outcomes, represented by the **Confusion Matrix**:

| | **Actually Relevant** | **Actually Non-Relevant** |
|---|---|---|
| **Predicted Relevant** | True Positive (TP) | False Positive (FP) |
| **Predicted Non-Relevant** | False Negative (FN) | True Negative (TN) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters for IR:** In Information Retrieval, "relevant" corresponds to documents that satisfy the user's information need, and "predicted" corresponds to documents the system *retrieves* (above the ranking threshold $\tau$). TP are the correctly retrieved relevant docs; FP are the junk docs that pollute the result list.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Medical Screening Test"

Imagine a new blood test designed to detect a rare disease. When you administer it to 1,000 patients:

- **True Positives (TP):** Sick patients correctly flagged as sick → life-saving early treatment.
- **False Positives (FP):** Healthy patients wrongly flagged as sick → unnecessary panic and follow-up tests.
- **True Negatives (TN):** Healthy patients correctly cleared → peace of mind.
- **False Negatives (FN):** Sick patients wrongly cleared → disease progresses undetected.

The **ROC curve** is essentially the "decision-maker's dashboard." As the doctor becomes more lenient (lower threshold for calling someone "sick"), they catch more true cases (TPR goes up) but also generate more false alarms (FPR goes up). The curve traces this trade-off.

> [!TIP]
> **IR Parallel:** A search engine returning 1,000 results for a query behaves identically. Loosening the threshold retrieves more genuinely useful pages (TPR rises) but also more spam (FPR rises). The ROC curve captures the engine's *inherent ranking quality* independent of any single threshold choice.

### 1.4 Key Metrics in **Bold**

- **TPR (True Positive Rate) = Recall = Sensitivity = Hit Rate**
- **FPR (False Positive Rate) = Fall-out = 1 − Specificity**
- **AUC (Area Under the Curve)** — a scalar summarising the entire ROC curve.
- **EER (Equal Error Rate)** — the operating point where $FPR = FNR$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How ROC Curves Work

The construction of a ROC curve follows a deterministic four-step pipeline:

1. **Train/Produce the Ranking Model:** The IR system assigns a numerical relevance score $s(d_i)$ to every document $d_i$ in the collection. This score may come from BM25, TF-IDF, cosine similarity, PageRank, or a learned neural ranker.
2. **Define the Threshold Sweep:** The decision threshold $\tau$ is varied from $\tau = +\infty$ (retrieve nothing) down to $\tau = -\infty$ (retrieve everything). Each value of $\tau$ yields one operating point.
3. **Classify Every Document:** For a given $\tau$, any document with $s(d_i) \ge \tau$ is labelled *positive* (retrieved); otherwise it is *negative*.
4. **Compute TPR and FPR:** Using the four cells of the confusion matrix, plot the point $(FPR(\tau), TPR(\tau))$ in the ROC space. Connecting these points across all thresholds produces the ROC curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A ROC curve is a **monotonically non-decreasing** function. As $\tau$ decreases, both TPR and FPR can only stay the same or increase — they never decrease. This is because lowering the threshold can only *add* documents to the positive set, never remove them.

### 2.2 Geometric Interpretation of the ROC Space

- The **bottom-left corner $(0, 0)$** corresponds to the trivial classifier that retrieves *nothing* (no positives predicted).
- The **top-right corner $(1, 1)$** corresponds to the trivial classifier that retrieves *everything* (all documents predicted positive).
- The **top-left corner $(0, 1)$** is the **perfect classifier** — every relevant document is retrieved, and nothing irrelevant is retrieved.
- The **diagonal line $y = x$** represents a **random ranker** with no discriminative power.

A good IR system bows the curve as far toward the top-left corner as possible.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Full Name | Formula | Value Range | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $TPR$ | True Positive Rate | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of relevant docs successfully retrieved. Equal to **Recall**. |
| $FPR$ | False Positive Rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of non-relevant docs wrongly retrieved. Equal to **Fall-out**. |
| $TNR$ | True Negative Rate | $TNR = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Equal to **Specificity**. $TNR = 1 - FPR$. |
| $FNR$ | False Negative Rate | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Miss rate. $FNR = 1 - TPR$. |
| $Precision$ | Positive Predictive Value | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Quality of the retrieved set. |
| $Accuracy$ | Overall Correctness | $Accuracy = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + FN + TN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Misleading on imbalanced collections. |
| $F_{\beta}$ | F-Measure | $F_{\beta} = (1 + \beta^{2}) \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{\beta^{2} P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. |
| $AUC$ | Area Under Curve | $AUC = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Probability that a random relevant doc is ranked above a random non-relevant doc. |
| $EER$ | Equal Error Rate | Point where $FPR = FNR$ | $[0, 1]$ | Single-number summary at the break-even point. |
| $Youden\,J$ | Youden's J Statistic | $J = TPR - FPR = TPR + TNR - 1$ | $[0, 1]$ | Optimal threshold maximises $J$. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** When computing $TPR$ and $FPR$, students often forget the denominators. $TPR$ uses the *row of actual positives* ($TP + FN$), and $FPR$ uses the *row of actual negatives* ($FP + TN$). Mixing these up is the #1 reason for mark deductions in Part B numericals.

### 2.4 The AUC — Probabilistic Interpretation

The **Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC)** has a beautiful probabilistic meaning in Information Retrieval:

$$AUC = P\bigl(\,s(d_{+}) > s(d_{-})\,\bigr)$$

where $d_{+}$ is a randomly chosen *relevant* document and $d_{-}$ is a randomly chosen *non-relevant* document. In other words, **AUC equals the probability that the ranker scores a randomly chosen relevant document higher than a randomly chosen non-relevant document.**

- $AUC = 1.0$ → Perfect ranking.
- $AUC = 0.5$ → Random ranking (equivalent to the diagonal).
- $AUC < 0.5$ → The ranker is *anti-correlated* with relevance (invert its scores to fix it).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** ROC analysis compares ranking algorithms such as BM25 vs. neural rankers.
- **Medical Diagnostics:** ROC analysis is the gold standard for evaluating cancer-screening models.
- **Spam Filters:** ROC curves guide the choice of decision threshold to balance user annoyance (FP) against missed threats (FN).
- **Anomaly Detection in Cybersecurity:** ROC curves compare intrusion-detection systems on the same network traces.
- **Recommender Systems:** ROC analysis evaluates the quality of "Top-N" recommendation lists.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Building a ROC Curve from Scratch

**Problem Setup.** Consider an IR system that has ranked 10 documents for a query. The system produces the following relevance scores, and a human assessor has provided ground-truth relevance labels (1 = relevant, 0 = non-relevant).

| Rank | Document ID | Score $s(d)$ | Ground Truth |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_1$ | 0.95 | 1 (Relevant) |
| 2 | $D_2$ | 0.90 | 0 (Non-Rel) |
| 3 | $D_3$ | 0.85 | 1 (Relevant) |
| 4 | $D_4$ | 0.75 | 0 (Non-Rel) |
| 5 | $D_5$ | 0.60 | 0 (Non-Rel) |
| 6 | $D_6$ | 0.55 | 1 (Relevant) |
| 7 | $D_7$ | 0.40 | 0 (Non-Rel) |
| 8 | $D_8$ | 0.30 | 0 (Non-Rel) |
| 9 | $D_9$ | 0.20 | 1 (Relevant) |
| 10 | $D_{10}$ | 0.10 | 0 (Non-Rel) |

**Step 1 — Identify the totals.** Count the relevant and non-relevant documents in the collection:

$$\text{Total Relevant} = TP + FN = 4 \quad (D_1, D_3, D_6, D_9)$$
$$\text{Total Non-Relevant} = FP + TN = 6 \quad (D_2, D_4, D_5, D_7, D_8, D_{10})$$

Total documents $N = 10$.

**Step 2 — Sweep the threshold.** Start at the top of the ranked list and progressively lower the threshold to include one more document at each step. At each step, recompute $TP$, $FP$, $FN$, $TN$, then derive $TPR$ and $FPR$.

**Threshold Step 1: $\tau = 0.95$ (retrieve only $D_1$).**

$$TP = 1, \quad FP = 0, \quad FN = 3, \quad TN = 6$$

$$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{1}{1+3} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.250$$

$$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP+TN} = \frac{0}{0+6} = \frac{0}{6} = 0.000$$

**Point 1: $(0.000, \; 0.250)$**

**Threshold Step 2: $\tau = 0.90$ (retrieve $D_1, D_2$).**

$$TP = 1, \quad FP = 1, \quad FN = 3, \quad TN = 5$$

$$TPR = \frac{1}{1+3} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.250$$

$$FPR = \frac{1}{1+5} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.167$$

**Point 2: $(0.167, \; 0.250)$**

**Threshold Step 3: $\tau = 0.85$ (retrieve $D_1, D_2, D_3$).**

$$TP = 2, \quad FP = 1, \quad FN = 2, \quad TN = 5$$

$$TPR = \frac{2}{2+2} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.500$$

$$FPR = \frac{1}{1+5} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.167$$

**Point 3: $(0.167, \; 0.500)$**

**Threshold Step 4: $\tau = 0.75$ (retrieve $D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$).**

$$TP = 2, \quad FP = 2, \quad FN = 2, \quad TN = 4$$

$$TPR = \frac{2}{2+2} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.500$$

$$FPR = \frac{2}{2+4} = \frac{2}{6} \approx 0.333$$

**Point 4: $(0.333, \; 0.500)$**

**Threshold Step 5: $\tau = 0.60$ (retrieve top 5).**

$$TP = 2, \quad FP = 3, \quad FN = 2, \quad TN = 3$$

$$TPR = \frac{2}{2+2} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.500$$

$$FPR = \frac{3}{3+3} = \frac{3}{6} = 0.500$$

**Point 5: $(0.500, \; 0.500)$**

**Threshold Step 6: $\tau = 0.55$ (retrieve top 6).**

$$TP = 3, \quad FP = 3, \quad FN = 1, \quad TN = 3$$

$$TPR = \frac{3}{3+1} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.750$$

$$FPR = \frac{3}{3+3} = \frac{3}{6} = 0.500$$

**Point 6: $(0.500, \; 0.750)$**

**Threshold Step 7: $\tau = 0.40$ (retrieve top 7).**

$$TP = 3, \quad FP = 4, \quad FN = 1, \quad TN = 2$$

$$TPR = \frac{3}{3+1} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.750$$

$$FPR = \frac{4}{4+2} = \frac{4}{6} \approx 0.667$$

**Point 7: $(0.667, \; 0.750)$**

**Threshold Step 8: $\tau = 0.30$ (retrieve top 8).**

$$TP = 3, \quad FP = 5, \quad FN = 1, \quad TN = 1$$

$$TPR = \frac{3}{3+1} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.750$$

$$FPR = \frac{5}{5+1} = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.833$$

**Point 8: $(0.833, \; 0.750)$**

**Threshold Step 9: $\tau = 0.20$ (retrieve top 9).**

$$TP = 4, \quad FP = 5, \quad FN = 0, \quad TN = 1$$

$$TPR = \frac{4}{4+0} = \frac{4}{4} = 1.000$$

$$FPR = \frac{5}{5+1} = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.833$$

**Point 9: $(0.833, \; 1.000)$**

**Threshold Step 10: $\tau = 0.10$ (retrieve all 10).**

$$TP = 4, \quad FP = 6, \quad FN = 0, \quad TN = 0$$

$$TPR = \frac{4}{4+0} = 1.000$$

$$FPR = \frac{6}{6+0} = 1.000$$

**Point 10: $(1.000, \; 1.000)$**

### 3.2 Master ROC Table

| $\tau$ (Threshold) | Retrieved Docs | $TP$ | $FP$ | $FN$ | $TN$ | $TPR$ | $FPR$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.95 | $D_1$ | 1 | 0 | 3 | 6 | 0.250 | 0.000 |
| 0.90 | $D_1, D_2$ | 1 | 1 | 3 | 5 | 0.250 | 0.167 |
| 0.85 | $D_1, D_2, D_3$ | 2 | 1 | 2 | 5 | 0.500 | 0.167 |
| 0.75 | $D_1, ..., D_4$ | 2 | 2 | 2 | 4 | 0.500 | 0.333 |
| 0.60 | top 5 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 0.500 | 0.500 |
| 0.55 | top 6 | 3 | 3 | 1 | 3 | 0.750 | 0.500 |
| 0.40 | top 7 | 3 | 4 | 1 | 2 | 0.750 | 0.667 |
| 0.30 | top 8 | 3 | 5 | 1 | 1 | 0.750 | 0.833 |
| 0.20 | top 9 | 4 | 5 | 0 | 1 | 1.000 | 0.833 |
| 0.10 | top 10 | 4 | 6 | 0 | 0 | 1.000 | 1.000 |

### 3.3 AUC Estimation via the Trapezoidal Rule

The Area Under the Curve can be computed using the **trapezoidal approximation**:

$$AUC \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \frac{(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_{i}) \cdot (TPR_{i} + TPR_{i+1})}{2}$$

Applying this to our 10 points (sorted by ascending $FPR$):

$$AUC = \frac{(0.167-0.000)(0.250+0.250)}{2} + \frac{(0.167-0.167)(0.250+0.500)}{2} + \frac{(0.333-0.167)(0.500+0.500)}{2} + \frac{(0.500-0.333)(0.500+0.500)}{2} + \frac{(0.500-0.500)(0.500+0.750)}{2} + \frac{(0.667-0.500)(0.750+0.750)}{2} + \frac{(0.833-0.667)(0.750+0.750)}{2} + \frac{(0.833-0.833)(0.750+1.000)}{2} + \frac{(1.000-0.833)(1.000+1.000)}{2}$$

Evaluating term-by-term:

$$AUC = (0.167)(0.250) + (0)(0.375) + (0.167)(0.500) + (0.167)(0.500) + (0)(0.625) + (0.167)(0.750) + (0.167)(0.750) + (0)(0.875) + (0.167)(1.000)$$

$$AUC = 0.0417 + 0 + 0.0833 + 0.0833 + 0 + 0.1250 + 0.1250 + 0 + 0.1667$$

$$\boxed{AUC \approx 0.625}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** Since $AUC = 0.625 > 0.5$, this ranker is performing *better than random* but has substantial room for improvement. An AUC of 1.0 would be perfect.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Confusion Matrix Tally Check

A useful sanity-check after tallying TP, FP, FN, TN is to verify the conservation law:

$$TP + FP + FN + TN = N$$

For our example at Step 6 ($\tau = 0.55$):

$$3 + 3 + 1 + 3 = 10 = N \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **conservation law** is a foolproof way to catch arithmetic mistakes during KTU exams. Always perform this check before writing your final TPR and FPR values.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ROC Pipeline — From Raw Scores to Plotted Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IR System Scores s(di)] --> B[Sort Documents Descending by Score]
    B --> C[Set Threshold tau equal to score of rank 1]
    C --> D[Classify docs with score greater than or equal to tau as Positive]
    D --> E[Update Confusion Matrix TP FP FN TN]
    E --> F[Compute TPR and FPR for current tau]
    F --> G[Plot Point FPR TPR in ROC Space]
    G --> H{More thresholds to evaluate?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Lower tau to next rank boundary]
    I --> D
    H -- No --> J[Connect all points to form ROC Curve]
    J --> K[Compute AUC using Trapezoidal Rule]
    K --> L[Final ROC Plot with AUC Score]
```

### 4.2 Confusion Matrix — The Four Outcomes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ACT["ACTUAL GROUND TRUTH"]
        A1[Relevant Documents]
        A2[Non-Relevant Documents]
    end
    subgraph PRED["PREDICTED BY SYSTEM"]
        B1[Retrieved]
        B2[Not Retrieved]
    end
    ACT --> PRED
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A1 -.->|TP| B1
    A1 -.->|FN| B2
    A2 -.->|FP| B1
    A2 -.->|TN| B2
```

### 4.3 ROC Space — Regions and Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ROCTOP["TOP LEFT REGION"]
        R1[High TPR, Low FPR - Excellent Classifier]
    end
    subgraph ROCMID["DIAGONAL LINE y equals x"]
        R2[Random Guessing - AUC equals 0.5]
    end
    subgraph ROCBOT["BOTTOM RIGHT REGION"]
        R3[Low TPR, High FPR - Worse than Random]
    end
    subgraph ROCARC["BOWED CURVE TOWARD TOP LEFT"]
        R4[Good IR System - AUC significantly greater than 0.5]
    end
    R4 --> R1
    R2 --> R3
    R1 --> R2
```

### 4.4 Threshold Sweep Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Threshold tau equals Infinity] --> B[Retrieve Nothing - Point 0,0]
    B --> C[Threshold Equals Top Score]
    C --> D[Retrieve Top 1 Document]
    D --> E[Lower tau to Include Top 2]
    E --> F[Continue Descending]
    F --> G[Threshold equals Minus Infinity]
    G --> H[Retrieve Everything - Point 1,1]
    H --> I[Connect all points to form monotonic curve]
```

### 4.5 Plot of Our Worked Example (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Point 0,0 starting point] --> P1[Point 0.000, 0.250]
    P1 --> P2[Point 0.167, 0.250]
    P2 --> P3[Point 0.167, 0.500]
    P3 --> P4[Point 0.333, 0.500]
    P4 --> P5[Point 0.500, 0.500]
    P5 --> P6[Point 0.500, 0.750]
    P6 --> P7[Point 0.667, 0.750]
    P7 --> P8[Point 0.833, 0.750]
    P8 --> P9[Point 0.833, 1.000]
    P9 --> P10[Point 1.000, 1.000 end point]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The schematic above shows the **monotonically non-decreasing** property clearly: as you move right along the FPR axis, TPR never decreases. The curve "climbs the staircase" of operating points and eventually reaches the top-right corner when every document is retrieved.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
>
> **Q1.** Define the **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve**. What are the axes of the ROC space, and what does the Area Under the Curve (AUC) represent probabilistically? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **ROC curve** is a two-dimensional graphical plot that visualises the performance of a binary classifier or ranking system at various threshold settings. **(1 mark)**

- **X-axis:** False Positive Rate $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ (fall-out).
- **Y-axis:** True Positive Rate $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ (recall, sensitivity). **(1 mark)**

The **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** has a probabilistic interpretation:

$$AUC = P\bigl(\,s(d_{+}) > s(d_{-})\,\bigr)$$

It represents the probability that the ranker assigns a higher score to a randomly chosen relevant document than to a randomly chosen non-relevant document. AUC = 1.0 indicates perfect ranking; AUC = 0.5 indicates random ranking. **(1 mark)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
>
> **Q2.** With the help of a confusion matrix, distinguish between **True Positive Rate (TPR)** and **False Positive Rate (FPR)**. Why is the ROC curve preferred over a single operating-point metric? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| | **Actually Relevant** | **Actually Non-Relevant** |
|---|---|---|
| **Predicted Relevant** | TP | FP |
| **Predicted Non-Relevant** | FN | TN |

- **TPR** uses the row of *actual positives*: $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$. **(1 mark)**
- **FPR** uses the row of *actual negatives*: $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$. **(1 mark)**

A single metric such as accuracy or precision depends on the chosen threshold and can be misleading, especially in imbalanced collections. The ROC curve summarises classifier behaviour across **all possible thresholds**, giving a threshold-independent view of the model's discriminative power. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam – Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **construction methodology** of a ROC curve in an Information Retrieval system. Include the role of the decision threshold $\tau$, the confusion matrix, and the formula derivations for TPR and FPR. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An IR system produces the ranked list shown below. Ground-truth relevance is given in parentheses. **Compute the ROC curve** by sweeping the threshold from the top rank downward. Tabulate all $(FPR, TPR)$ points and compute the **AUC** using the trapezoidal rule. **(7 Marks)**

| Rank | Document | Ground Truth |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A$ | Relevant |
| 2 | $B$ | Non-Relevant |
| 3 | $C$ | Non-Relevant |
| 4 | $D$ | Relevant |
| 5 | $E$ | Relevant |
| 6 | $F$ | Non-Relevant |
| 7 | $G$ | Non-Relevant |
| 8 | $H$ | Relevant |

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:**

The construction of a ROC curve is a four-step process:

**Step 1: Score Generation.** The IR system assigns a numerical score $s(d_i)$ to every document $d_i$, derived from the underlying ranking algorithm (BM25, TF-IDF, cosine similarity, PageRank, etc.). **(1 mark)**

**Step 2: Threshold Definition.** A decision threshold $\tau$ partitions the document collection into a "positive" (retrieved) set $\mathcal{R}_{+} = \{d_i : s(d_i) \ge \tau\}$ and a "negative" (not retrieved) set $\mathcal{R}_{-} = \{d_i : s(d_i) < \tau\}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3: Confusion Matrix Population.** For a fixed $\tau$, every document is classified into one of the four cells of the confusion matrix (TP, FP, FN, TN) using the relevance judgement as the ground truth. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4: Metric Computation.** The two coordinates of the ROC point are:

$$TPR(\tau) = \frac{TP(\tau)}{TP(\tau) + FN(\tau)} = \frac{\text{Retrieved Relevant}}{\text{Total Relevant}}$$

$$FPR(\tau) = \frac{FP(\tau)}{FP(\tau) + TN(\tau)} = \frac{\text{Retrieved Non-Relevant}}{\text{Total Non-Relevant}}$$

**(2 marks)** for the formulas. By sweeping $\tau$ from $+\infty$ to $-\infty$ and plotting $(FPR, TPR)$ for each value, we obtain the complete ROC curve. **(1 mark)** for the threshold sweep logic.

**Step 5: Curve Properties.** The resulting curve is monotonically non-decreasing, starts at $(0, 0)$ and ends at $(1, 1)$. **(1 mark)**

---

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Totals.**

$$\text{Total Relevant} = 4 \quad (A, D, E, H)$$
$$\text{Total Non-Relevant} = 4 \quad (B, C, F, G)$$
$$N = 8 \text{ documents}$$

**Step 2: Threshold Sweep Table.**

| Threshold Position | Retrieved | $TP$ | $FP$ | $FN$ | $TN$ | $TPR$ | $FPR$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Top 1 ($A$) | $A$ | 1 | 0 | 3 | 4 | 0.250 | 0.000 |
| Top 2 ($A,B$) | $A,B$ | 1 | 1 | 3 | 3 | 0.250 | 0.250 |
| Top 3 ($A,B,C$) | $A,B,C$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 0.250 | 0.500 |
| Top 4 ($A,B,C,D$) | $A,B,C,D$ | 2 | 2 | 2 | 2 | 0.500 | 0.500 |
| Top 5 ($A,...,E$) | $A,B,C,D,E$ | 3 | 2 | 1 | 2 | 0.750 | 0.500 |
| Top 6 ($A,...,F$) | $A,B,C,D,E,F$ | 3 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 0.750 | 0.750 |
| Top 7 ($A,...,G$) | $A,B,C,D,E,F,G$ | 3 | 4 | 1 | 0 | 0.750 | 1.000 |
| Top 8 (all) | all | 4 | 4 | 0 | 0 | 1.000 | 1.000 |

**(4 marks)** for the table.

**Step 3: AUC via Trapezoidal Rule.** Using

$$AUC = \sum_{i=1}^{7} \frac{(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_{i}) \cdot (TPR_{i} + TPR_{i+1})}{2}$$

**Term 1:** $(0.250 - 0.000)(0.250 + 0.250)/2 = (0.250)(0.500)/2 = 0.0625$

**Term 2:** $(0.500 - 0.250)(0.250 + 0.250)/2 = (0.250)(0.500)/2 = 0.0625$

**Term 3:** $(0.500 - 0.500)(0.250 + 0.500)/2 = (0)(0.750)/2 = 0$

**Term 4:** $(0.500 - 0.500)(0.500 + 0.750)/2 = (0)(1.250)/2 = 0$

**Term 5:** $(0.750 - 0.500)(0.750 + 0.750)/2 = (0.250)(1.500)/2 = 0.1875$

**Term 6:** $(1.000 - 0.750)(0.750 + 0.750)/2 = (0.250)(1.500)/2 = 0.1875$

**Term 7:** $(1.000 - 1.000)(0.750 + 1.000)/2 = (0)(1.750)/2 = 0$

**Total AUC:**

$$\boxed{AUC = 0.0625 + 0.0625 + 0 + 0 + 0.1875 + 0.1875 + 0 = 0.5000}$$

**(2 marks)** for the calculation. The interpretation: an AUC of exactly 0.5 indicates that the ranking order (with the documents placed as given) performs **no better than random** with respect to the given relevance labels. **(1 mark)** for the interpretation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is the **Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC)**? Prove that for a perfect classifier, $AUC = 1$, and for a random classifier, $AUC = 0.5$. What is the interpretation of $AUC = 0.83$? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **ROC curves** with **Precision–Recall (PR) curves**. Under what conditions is the PR curve preferred over the ROC curve in Information Retrieval? Illustrate with a one-line argument. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Area Under the ROC Curve is the definite integral of the ROC function with respect to the FPR:

$$AUC = \int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$$

**Proof for the Perfect Classifier (3 marks):** A perfect classifier retrieves all relevant documents and zero non-relevant documents at some threshold. This yields a single operating point at $(FPR = 0, TPR = 1)$. The ROC curve travels from $(0, 0)$ vertically up to $(0, 1)$, then horizontally across to $(1, 1)$. The area enclosed is the unit square:

$$AUC_{\text{perfect}} = 1 \times 1 = 1.000$$

**Proof for the Random Classifier (2 marks):** A random classifier has $TPR = FPR$ at every threshold, producing the diagonal line $y = x$ from $(0, 0)$ to $(1, 1)$. The area under this line is a right triangle with legs of length 1:

$$AUC_{\text{random}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1 \times 1 = 0.500$$

**Interpretation of $AUC = 0.83$ (1 mark):** There is an **83% probability** that the ranker scores a randomly chosen relevant document higher than a randomly chosen non-relevant document. This is a strong but not perfect ranker.

---

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Property | ROC Curve | PR Curve |
|---|---|---|
| X-axis | False Positive Rate | Recall |
| Y-axis | True Positive Rate | Precision |
| Affected by class imbalance? | **No** — robust to skew. | **Yes** — drops sharply on skewed data. |
| Best for | Balanced datasets, comparing models broadly. | Highly imbalanced IR collections (most docs non-relevant). |
| What it emphasises | Both positive and negative performance. | Quality of the retrieved set. |

**Why PR is preferred in IR (3 marks):** Web-scale corpora contain millions of documents but only a handful of relevant ones for any given query. This creates an extreme **class imbalance** (e.g., 5 relevant vs. 1,000,000 non-relevant). In such cases:

- $FP$ is dominated by the enormous pool of non-relevant docs, making $FPR$ changes *barely visible* on the ROC axis. **(1.5 marks)**
- $Precision$ and $Recall$ focus exclusively on the *positive class*, making the PR curve far more **sensitive to ranking quality** at the top of the result list. **(1.5 marks)**

**One-line argument (1 mark):** "Use ROC curves when the positive and negative classes are roughly balanced; use Precision–Recall curves when the collection is heavily skewed towards non-relevant documents, as is typical in real-world Information Retrieval."

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusion between TPR denominator and FPR denominator** — $TPR = TP/(TP+FN)$, $FPR = FP/(FP+TN)$. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks instantly. **(Critical)**
> 2. **Forgetting to sort the ranked list** — The ROC curve must be generated in rank order. Drawing points in arbitrary order is mathematically invalid. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 3. **Not writing units / range** — KTU expects students to state that $TPR$ and $FPR$ both lie in $[0, 1]$. **(Lose 0.5 mark)**
> 4. **AUC interpretation missing** — Always end an AUC computation with the probabilistic interpretation sentence. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 5. **Confusing ROC with PR curve** — In Part B, examiners check if the student can state the axes of each curve clearly. **(Lose 1 mark)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ROC curve** plots $TPR$ (y-axis) vs $FPR$ (x-axis) as the decision threshold is varied.
- **Confusion Matrix** has four cells: $TP$, $FP$, $FN$, $TN$ — all ROC metrics derive from these.
- **TPR (Recall, Sensitivity)** $= \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$
- **FPR (Fall-out)** $= \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN} = 1 - \text{Specificity}$
- **ROC curve is monotonically non-decreasing** — lowering the threshold can only *add* to the positive set.
- **Top-left corner $(0, 1)$** is the *perfect classifier*; **diagonal $y = x$** is the *random classifier*.
- **AUC** $= P\bigl(\,s(d_{+}) > s(d_{-})\,\bigr)$ — probability that a relevant doc outranks a non-relevant one.
- **AUC interpretation range:** $[0.5, 1]$ for useful models; $< 0.5$ means the ranker is anti-correlated (invert its output).
- **Trapezoidal rule** is the standard method to estimate AUC from discrete operating points.
- **ROC vs PR:** Use **PR curves** for highly imbalanced IR collections where most documents are non-relevant.
- **Equal Error Rate (EER)** is the single operating point where $FPR = FNR$.
- **Youden's J Statistic** $J = TPR - FPR$ identifies the optimal threshold (maximise $J$).
- **Conservation Law:** $TP + FP + FN + TN = N$ (always verify before submission).
- **Origin:** ROC analysis was developed during **World War II** for radar signal detection — the term "Receiver Operating Characteristic" is a historical artifact.
- **Modern Relevance:** With the rise of neural rankers and LLM-based retrieval, ROC analysis remains the gold-standard tool for comparing ranking quality across diverse models.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
