## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: CLIR Query Likelihood with Translation Probabilities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is **high-yield** for KTU 14-mark questions. Master every step.

**Goal:** Derive the ranking function for CLIR when a query $Q$ in language $L_q$ is matched against a document $D$ in language $L_d$ using a probabilistic model that incorporates **word-level translation probabilities**.

**Step 1 — Start with the Bayesian ranking principle.**
A document $D$ is ranked by the posterior probability $P(D \mid Q)$ that it is relevant given the query.

$$
P(D \mid Q) = \frac{P(Q \mid D) \cdot P(D)}{P(Q)}
$$

This equation states that the probability of a document being relevant to a query equals the product of the likelihood of the query given the document and the prior probability of the document, divided by the probability of the query.

**Step 2 — Eliminate the query prior $P(Q)$.**
Since $P(Q)$ is **constant for all documents** in the collection during ranking, it does not affect the relative ordering and can be dropped from the computation.

$$
P(D \mid Q) \propto P(Q \mid D) \cdot P(D)
$$

This proportionality simplifies the computation because $P(Q)$ is uniform across all documents, so it does not influence the ranking.

**Step 3 — Apply the Naive Bayes (independence) assumption across query terms.**
Assume query terms $q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n$ are conditionally independent given $D$.

$$
P(Q \mid D) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(q_i \mid D)
$$

This factorization is justified by the bag-of-words assumption where the presence of one term does not influence the probability of another within a document.

**Step 4 — Introduce translation probability $P(q_i \mid w)$ for each $q_i$.**
Since $q_i \in L_q$ but $D \in L_d$, we cannot directly compute $P(q_i \mid D)$. We must sum over every word $w$ in the document language.

$$
P(q_i \mid D) = \sum_{w \in V_d} P(q_i \mid w) \cdot P(w \mid D)
$$

This is the **law of total probability**. The probability of observing a source-language query term is computed by marginalizing over all possible document-language words weighted by their likelihood in the document.

**Step 5 — Substitute back into the ranking equation.**

$$
P(D \mid Q) \propto P(D) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{w \in V_d} P(q_i \mid w) \cdot P(w \mid D)
$$

This is the **complete CLIR ranking function**. It shows that each query term is matched against every document word, weighted by both the translation probability and the document term probability.

**Step 6 — Practical smoothing with Dirichlet prior.**
To avoid zero probabilities for unseen document words, we apply **Bayesian smoothing using a Dirichlet prior** over the collection language model $P(w \mid C)$.

$$
P(w \mid D) = \frac{|D|}{|D| + \mu} \cdot P_{\text{ML}}(w \mid D) + \frac{\mu}{|D| + \mu} \cdot P(w \mid C)
$$

This equation linearly interpolates the maximum likelihood estimate from the document with a background model from the entire collection, controlled by the smoothing parameter $\mu$, typically set to the average document length.

**Step 7 — Final practical CLIR scoring equation.**

$$
\text{Score}(D, Q) = \log P(D) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log \sum_{w \in V_d} P(q_i \mid w) \cdot P(w \mid D)
$$

The logarithm is applied to convert products into summations, which prevents numerical underflow when dealing with many small probabilities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** $P(q_i \mid w)$ is the **translation model** (learned from parallel corpora), while $P(w \mid D)$ is the **document language model** (learned from the document itself). The CLIR system is therefore a **product of two statistical models**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: IBM Model 1 Translation Probability

**Step 1 — Define the alignment variable $a$.**
For a source sentence $\mathbf{e} = (e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_l)$ and target sentence $\mathbf{f} = (f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_m)$, an alignment $a$ assigns each $f_j$ to exactly one $e_{a_j}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the conditional probability of $\mathbf{f}$ given $\mathbf{e}$ under uniform alignment assumption.**

$$
P(\mathbf{f} \mid \mathbf{e}) = \frac{\varepsilon}{(l+1)^m} \prod_{j=1}^{m} t(f_j \mid e_{a_j})
$$

This equation states that the translation probability of the foreign sentence given the English sentence equals a uniform alignment prior multiplied by the product of word-level translation probabilities across all aligned word pairs.

**Step 3 — Marginalize over all alignments to learn $t(f \mid e)$.**

$$
P(\mathbf{f} \mid \mathbf{e}) = \frac{\varepsilon}{(l+1)^m} \sum_{a_1=0}^{l} \cdots \sum_{a_m=0}^{l} \prod_{j=1}^{m} t(f_j \mid e_{a_j})
$$

This marginalization sums the joint probability over all possible word-level alignments, which is necessary because the correct alignment is unobserved latent data.

**Step 4 — Exchange product and sum to get an efficient form.**

$$
P(\mathbf{f} \mid \mathbf{e}) = \frac{\varepsilon}{(l+1)^m} \prod_{j=1}^{m} \sum_{i=0}^{l} t(f_j \mid e_i)
$$

The interchange of product and summation is a critical mathematical simplification that makes IBM Model 1 computationally tractable by avoiding explicit enumeration of all alignments.

**Step 5 — Apply EM (Expectation-Maximization) to learn $t(f \mid e)$ iteratively.**

$$
t^{(n+1)}(f \mid e) = \frac{\sum_{(\mathbf{e}, \mathbf{f})} c(f \mid e; \mathbf{e}, \mathbf{f})}{\sum_{f'} \sum_{(\mathbf{e}, \mathbf{f})} c(f' \mid e; \mathbf{e}, \mathbf{f})}
$$

This EM update rule refines the translation probability by computing the expected count of word pair alignments under the current model, normalized to ensure probabilities sum to one.

where $c(f \mid e; \mathbf{e}, \mathbf{f}) = \dfrac{t(f \mid e)}{\sum_{e'} t(f \mid e')}$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — CLIR Strategies

| **Criterion** | **Query Translation (QT)** | **Document Translation (DT)** | **Interlingual (IL)** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Translation Frequency** | Once per query (online) | Once per document (offline) | Once per query + document |
| **Computational Cost** | Low (online) | Very High (offline) | Medium |
| **Storage Overhead** | None | Doubles index size | Embedding storage only |
| **Translation Quality Needed** | High (ambiguity penalty) | Moderate | N/A (semantic vectors) |
| **Scalability to N Languages** | Poor (N×N translators) | Poor (N×N translators) | Excellent (one shared space) |
| **Out-of-Vocabulary Handling** | Difficult | Easier (full sentence) | Robust via subword embeddings |
| **Domain Sensitivity** | High | High | Medium |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | **High** | **Medium** | **High (recent trend)** |
| **Typical MAP Drop vs Mono** | 15–30% | 5–15% | 5–10% |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** Suppose a query $Q = \{q_1\}$ in English has a single term "bank." The document collection in Malayalam contains words $w_1 = \text{നദിയോരം}$ (river bank) and $w_2 = \text{ബാങ്ക്}$ (financial bank). Translation probabilities are $P(q_1 \mid w_1) = 0.6$ and $P(q_1 \mid w_2) = 0.4$. Document $D$ has $P(w_1 \mid D) = 0.7$ and $P(w_2 \mid D) = 0.3$. Compute the CLIR score contribution of $q_1$.

**Solution (Exhaustive Steps):**

**Step 1 — Identify the formula.**

$$
\text{Score}(q_1, D) = \log \sum_{w \in \{w_1, w_2\}} P(q_1 \mid w) \cdot P(w \mid D)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\text{Score}(q_1, D) = \log \big[ P(q_1 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_1 \mid D) + P(q_1 \mid w_2) \cdot P(w_2 \mid D) \big]
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the inner product terms.**

$$
\text{Score}(q_1, D) = \log \big[ (0.6)(0.7) + (0.4)(0.3) \big]
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the arithmetic.**

$$
\text{Score}(q_1, D) = \log \big[ 0.42 + 0.12 \big] = \log(0.54)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the final logarithm.**

$$
\text{Score}(q_1, D) = \log(0.54) \approx -0.6162
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**
A less negative log-probability indicates a stronger match. The document $D$ has a higher score for the "bank" query when its content includes $w_1$ (river bank) which the translation model associates more strongly with the English term "bank."

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CLIR System Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below represents a generic **Query-Translation-Based CLIR Pipeline**, which is the most frequently tested architecture in KTU Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[USER QUERY IN LANGUAGE LQ] --> B[QUERY PREPROCESSING TOKENIZATION STEMMING STOPWORD REMOVAL]
    B --> C[TRANSLATION ENGINE MT OR DICTIONARY OR CORPUS BASED]
    C --> D[TRANSLATED QUERY IN LANGUAGE LD]
    D --> E[INVERTED INDEX OF DOCUMENT COLLECTION IN LD]
    E --> F[RETRIEVAL MODEL BM25 OR TFIDF OR LANGUAGE MODEL]
    F --> G[RANKING MODULE COMPUTE RELEVANCE SCORES]
    G --> H[POST PROCESSING OPTIONAL RE TRANSLATION]
    H --> I[RANKED RESULTS PRESENTED TO USER]

    subgraph KNOWLEDGE_RESOURCES
        J1[BILINGUAL DICTIONARY]
        J2[PARALLEL CORPUS]
        J3[MACHINE TRANSLATION MODEL]
    end

    J1 -.-> C
    J2 -.-> C
    J3 -.-> C

    subgraph EVALUATION_LAYER
        K1[RELEVANCE JUDGMENTS]
        K2[MAP MRR P@K BLEU METRICS]
    end

    I --> K1
    K1 --> K2
```

**Component Explanations:**

- **Node A — USER QUERY:** The input in the source language $L_q$ such as English.
- **Node B — QUERY PREPROCESSING:** Tokenization, lowercasing, stemming, and stopword elimination performed in the source language.
- **Node C — TRANSLATION ENGINE:** The core module using MT, dictionary lookup, or corpus-based alignment to map terms from $L_q$ to $L_d$.
- **Node D — TRANSLATED QUERY:** The query expressed in $L_d$ ready for matching.
- **Node E — INVERTED INDEX:** The pre-built postings index of the document collection.
- **Node F — RETRIEVAL MODEL:** The matching function (BM25, TF-IDF, or Language Model).
- **Node G — RANKING MODULE:** Computes relevance scores for all candidate documents.
- **Node H — POST PROCESSING:** Optional operations such as re-translation of results to the source language.
- **Node I — RANKED RESULTS:** The final ordered list presented to the user.
- **Subgraph KNOWLEDGE_RESOURCES:** Auxiliary resources feeding the translation engine.
- **Subgraph EVALUATION_LAYER:** Offline metrics used to measure CLIR quality.

---

### 4.2 Cross-Lingual Embedding Architecture (Interlingual Approach)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[ENGLISH QUERY] --> B1[ENCODER ENGLISH BRANCH]
    A2[MALAYALAM DOCUMENT] --> B2[ENCODER MALAYALAM BRANCH]
    A3[HINDI DOCUMENT] --> B3[ENCODER HINDI BRANCH]
    A4[FRENCH DOCUMENT] --> B4[ENCODER FRENCH BRANCH]

    B1 --> C[SHARED MULTILINGUAL EMBEDDING SPACE DIMENSION 512]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C

    C --> D[COSINE SIMILARITY COMPUTATION]
    D --> E[RANKED CROSS LINGUAL RESULTS]
```

**Architectural Notes:**

- **Node A1 through A4:** Inputs in different source languages feeding parallel encoder branches.
- **Node B1 through B4:** Language-specific encoders, often transformer-based, that project text into a shared vector space.
- **Node C — SHARED EMBEDDING SPACE:** The unified semantic manifold where semantically equivalent sentences in different languages lie close together. Typical dimension is **512** or **768**.
- **Node D — COSINE SIMILARITY:** The matching metric computed between the query embedding and every document embedding.
- **Node E — RANKED RESULTS:** The output list ordered by cross-lingual semantic similarity.
- **The arrow style from B1 through B4 to C represents the crucial step of projection into a language-agnostic space.**

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
> **Q1.** Define Cross-Language Information Retrieval (CLIR). List any **four major challenges** of CLIR systems.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Cross-Language Information Retrieval (CLIR) is the sub-field of Information Retrieval in which a query expressed in one natural language $L_q$ is used to retrieve relevant documents from a collection written in a different natural language $L_d$, where $L_q \neq L_d$. The system must bridge the linguistic gap using translation or cross-lingual semantic mapping.

**Four Major Challenges (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**

1. **Translation Ambiguity:** A single source word may map to multiple target words (e.g., English "bank" → Malayalam നദിയോരം / ബാങ്ക്).
2. **Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) Words:** Named entities, technical jargon, and newly coined words absent from the translation lexicon.
3. **Morphological Divergence:** Languages like Malayalam have agglutinative morphology, generating thousands of surface forms from a single root.
4. **Scarcity of Parallel Corpora:** Low-resource language pairs lack aligned sentence pairs needed to train translation models.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest Pattern]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **Query Translation (QT)** and **Document Translation (DT)** in CLIR. State **one advantage** and **one disadvantage** of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Query Translation** | **Document Translation** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| When translation occurs | Online, before retrieval | Offline, during indexing |
| What is translated | The short query | The entire document collection |
| Computational cost per query | Low | Low (already pre-translated) |
| Storage cost | None | Nearly doubles |
| **Advantage (0.75 M)** | Faster, no extra storage (0.75 M) | Avoids query-level ambiguity using full-sentence context (0.75 M) |
| **Disadvantage (0.75 M)** | Suffers from lack of context in queries (0.75 M) | Expensive preprocessing; storage overhead (0.75 M) |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4 Pattern]**
> **Q3. (A) (14 Marks)**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **architecture of a Cross-Language Information Retrieval (CLIR) system** based on **query translation**. Illustrate the flow using a **block diagram** and describe each component in detail.
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss in detail the **five major challenges** of CLIR with **suitable examples** for each.

#### Model Solution to Q3(A)(a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
A query-translation-based CLIR system translates the user query from the source language $L_q$ into the target document language $L_d$ before performing retrieval. The architecture consists of a sequence of well-defined processing stages that handle query preprocessing, translation, indexing, matching, and ranking.

**Component-by-Component Description (5 Marks):**

1. **Query Preprocessing Module:** Performs tokenization, lowercasing, stopword removal, and stemming on the source-language query. For instance, an English query "retrieving multilingual documents" is normalized to a clean term list.
2. **Translation Engine:** This is the heart of the CLIR system. It uses one of three approaches: (i) **Machine Translation systems** such as Google Translate or MarianNMT, (ii) **Bilingual Dictionary** lookup, or (iii) **Corpus-Based Translation** using IBM Model alignments derived from parallel corpora.
3. **Translated Query Representation:** The translated query is then expressed as a vector in the document language space, ready for matching against the pre-built index.
4. **Inverted Index:** The document collection in language $L_d$ is pre-indexed using standard IR techniques such as positional postings lists, term frequencies, and document frequencies.
5. **Retrieval and Ranking Module:** Uses a scoring function such as BM25 or a Language Model to compute the relevance between the translated query and each document. Documents are sorted in descending order of score.

**Block Diagram (1 Mark):**
A clear block diagram should show the sequential flow **User Query → Preprocessing → Translation Engine → Translated Query → Index Lookup → Scoring → Ranking → Results**. Knowledge resources such as dictionaries and parallel corpora feed into the translation engine as auxiliary inputs.

#### Model Solution to Q3(A)(b) — 7 Marks

**The five major challenges of CLIR are explained as follows:**

**1. Translation Ambiguity (1.5 Marks):**
A single source-language word can have multiple valid translations in the target language. For example, the English word "plant" can mean a botanical organism, a factory, or the verb "to place." A dictionary-based CLIR system may pick the wrong sense, leading to irrelevant results.

**2. Out-of-Vocabulary Words (1.5 Marks):**
Technical terms, named entities, and newly coined words are often absent from bilingual dictionaries. For example, the term "metaverse" may not exist in older English-Malayalam dictionaries, causing the translator to fail and the document to be missed.

**3. Morphological Divergence (1.5 Marks):**
Languages such as Malayalam, Tamil, and Hungarian are agglutinative, meaning a single root word can produce thousands of surface forms via suffixes. A CLIR system relying on word-level matching without stemming or lemmatization will miss many matches.

**4. Scarcity of Parallel Corpora (1.5 Marks):**
Statistical and neural translation models require large aligned parallel corpora. Low-resource language pairs (e.g., Malayalam-Mandarin) have very little aligned data, severely limiting the quality of the translation model.

**5. Cross-Lingual Evaluation Difficulty (1 Mark):**
Constructing **relevance judgments** for CLIR evaluation is expensive because human annotators must be proficient in *both* languages. The CLEF campaigns addressed this by pooling multilingual judgments, but the cost remains a bottleneck.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice, Model Paper Pattern]**
> **Q3. (B) (14 Marks)**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Describe the **three main approaches to CLIR** (Query Translation, Document Translation, and Interlingual). Compare them in terms of **computational cost, scalability, and translation quality requirement**.
> **(b)** [7 Marks] With a **suitable mathematical derivation**, show how the **CLIR query likelihood scoring function** incorporates **word-level translation probabilities**. Explain the role of **Dirichlet smoothing** in this context.

#### Model Solution to Q3(B)(a) — 7 Marks

**Approach 1 — Query Translation (2 Marks):**
The source-language query is translated into the document language using a translation engine. Computational cost is low because only the short query is translated online. However, query-level translation suffers from **lack of context**, requiring high translation quality to avoid cascading errors.

**Approach 2 — Document Translation (2 Marks):**
The entire document collection is pre-translated into the query language during offline indexing. This leverages **full-sentence context** for higher translation accuracy. However, the computational and storage costs are very high, and the system must be re-indexed whenever the collection changes.

**Approach 3 — Interlingual Representation (2 Marks):**
Both query and documents are projected into a shared **multilingual embedding space** using cross-lingual encoders such as mBERT or LASER. Matching is performed via **cosine similarity** in the shared space. This approach scales elegantly to dozens of languages and is robust to script differences.

**Comparative Summary (1 Mark):**
A comparison table should clearly state that **Query Translation** is cheapest but quality-sensitive, **Document Translation** is accurate but expensive, and **Interlingual** is the most scalable modern approach.

#### Model Solution to Q3(B)(b) — 7 Marks

**Mathematical Derivation (5 Marks):**

Begin with the Bayesian ranking principle.

$$
P(D \mid Q) \propto P(Q \mid D) \cdot P(D)
$$

This proportionality shows that the relevance of a document to a query is determined by the likelihood of the query given the document and the document's prior probability.

Apply the Naive Bayes assumption to factorize the query likelihood across its terms.

$$
P(Q \mid D) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(q_i \mid D)
$$

This factorization is valid under the bag-of-words assumption where query terms are conditionally independent given the document.

Introduce translation probabilities by marginalizing over all document-language words.

$$
P(q_i \mid D) = \sum_{w \in V_d} P(q_i \mid w) \cdot P(w \mid D)
$$

The law of total probability is applied because the query term is in a different language and must be expressed as a mixture over all possible translations weighted by their document-specific likelihoods.

Substitute back to obtain the **complete CLIR scoring function**.

$$
\text{Score}(D, Q) = \log P(D) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log \sum_{w \in V_d} P(q_i \mid w) \cdot P(w \mid D)
$$

The logarithm transforms products into sums, preventing numerical underflow and making the computation numerically stable.

**Role of Dirichlet Smoothing (2 Marks):**
Dirichlet smoothing addresses the **zero-probability problem** for words that do not appear in the document but are valid translations. The smoothed document language model interpolates the maximum likelihood estimate with a background collection model.

$$
P(w \mid D) = \frac{|D|}{|D| + \mu} \cdot P_{\text{ML}}(w \mid D) + \frac{\mu}{|D| + \mu} \cdot P(w \mid C)
$$

This equation linearly combines the document-specific estimate with the collection-wide prior, with the smoothing parameter $\mu$ typically set to the average document length, ensuring that unseen terms receive a small but non-zero probability.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU CLIR Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing CLIR with Multilingual MT** — CLIR is about *retrieval*, not translation. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks when students describe MT architectures instead of retrieval pipelines.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply $\log$** — In derivations, students often write $\prod$ instead of $\sum \log$. This loses 0.5 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Not mentioning translation ambiguity** — A CLIR question without mention of translation ambiguity is considered incomplete. Always include at least one example (e.g., "bank").
> 4. **Ignoring evaluation metrics** — Every KTU CLIR question expects at least one metric: **MAP, MRR, P@K, or BLEU**. Omitting these loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Missing the cross-lingual gap quantification** — When asked "why CLIR is hard," students often give vague answers. Always quantify: *"CLIR systems typically lose 15–30% MAP compared to monolingual baselines."*
> 6. **Writing the bare word "CLIR" without expansion** — Always write **"Cross-Language Information Retrieval (CLIR)"** on first mention to earn the definition mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — CLIR Module 4**

- [x] **CLIR** = retrieval across languages; $L_q \neq L_d$.
- [x] **Three architectures:** Query Translation (QT), Document Translation (DT), Interlingual (IL).
- [x] **QT** is cheap but ambiguity-prone; **DT** is expensive but accurate; **IL** is scalable and modern.
- [x] **Translation methods:** MT, Dictionary, Corpus-based (IBM Models), Hybrid.
- [x] **IBM Model 1** uses uniform alignment assumption and EM training to learn $t(f \mid e)$.
- [x] **Core CLIR equation:** $\text{Score}(D, Q) = \log P(D) + \sum_i \log \sum_w P(q_i \mid w) \cdot P(w \mid D)$.
- [x] **$P(q_i \mid w)$** = translation model; **$P(w \mid D)$** = document language model.
- [x] **Dirichlet smoothing** prevents zero probabilities for unseen words.
- [x] **Evaluation metrics:** MAP, MRR, P@K, BLEU, and the **CLIR effectiveness drop $\Delta$**.
- [x] **Five challenge categories:** Linguistic, Translation, Resource, Evaluation, Script/Morphology.
- [x] **Translation ambiguity example:** "bank" → river bank / financial bank.
- [x] **OOV example:** "metaverse" absent in older bilingual dictionaries.
- [x] **Morphological example:** Malayalam agglutinative forms of a single root.
- [x] **CLEF (Cross-Language Evaluation Forum)** is the standard international CLIR benchmark.
- [x] **Cross-lingual embeddings** (mBERT, LASER, XLM-R) enable the Interlingual approach.
- [x] **Typical MAP drop** in CLIR vs monolingual = **15–30%**.
- [x] **Remember the equation form** — Examiners expect a log-sum over products, not just a product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When asked a *comparison* question in Part B, **always include a table**. KTU examiners award a full 1 mark extra for well-structured comparative tables versus narrative-only answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Natural Language Processing (NLP) in IR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Natural Language Processing (NLP) in IR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Natural Language Processing (NLP) in IR?

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
Natural Language Processing (NLP) in Information Retrieval is the sub-field of computer science, artificial intelligence, and linguistics concerned with the **interactions between computers and human (natural) languages**. In the specific context of IR, NLP refers to the algorithmic techniques used to **preprocess, transform, understand, and index textual content** so that a search engine can match user queries with the most semantically relevant documents in a collection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):** *"NLP in IR is the application of computational linguistics techniques to bridge the gap between the unstructured, ambiguous nature of natural language queries and the structured representations of documents in an inverted index."*

## 1.2 The Core Problem NLP Solves in IR

Humans write queries using a bewildering variety of surface forms:
- **Morphological variation:** `run`, `running`, `ran`, `runs`
- **Synonymy:** `car` ≈ `automobile` ≈ `vehicle`
- **Polysemy:** `bank` (river bank) vs. `bank` (financial institution)
- **Ambiguity & Context:** `Apple` (fruit) vs. `Apple` (company)
- **Anaphora:** *"It released a new phone. **It** was expensive."* (What does *it* refer to?)

NLP provides a layered pipeline of techniques that **normalizes, enriches, and disambiguates** text before it enters the search ranking algorithm.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Multilingual Librarian 🧑‍🏫

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: The "Smart Librarian"**
> Imagine a **librarian who speaks 100 languages** but is *very literal-minded*. When you ask for a "book about fast cars", the literal-minded librarian would fail to find books titled *"High-Performance Automobiles"* or *"Velocity-Driven Vehicles"*.
>
> Now imagine a **second librarian** who, before shelving books, translates everything into a *conceptual index* (a thesaurus-based skeleton). This librarian:
> 1. **Strips** plurals and verb tenses (`running` → `run`).
> 2. **Maps synonyms** (`car` → `vehicle`).
> 3. **Tags parts of speech** so the right sense of `bank` is picked.
> 4. **Extracts named entities** (`Delhi`, `Microsoft`, `Einstein`).
>
> This second librarian **is NLP in IR**. It transforms chaotic human language into clean, query-ready tokens.

## 1.4 Where NLP Fits in the IR Pipeline

$$\text{Raw Text} \xrightarrow{\text{NLP}} \text{Indexed Tokens} \xrightarrow{\text{IR Engine}} \text{Ranked Results}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, NLP in IR covers *lexical analysis, morphological processing, syntactic processing, semantic analysis, and the role of NLP in modern neural/hybrid retrieval systems (BERT-style embeddings)*.

---

## 1.5 Key NLP Sub-Tasks Relevant to IR (Quick-Reference Matrix)

| # | NLP Sub-Task | Role in IR | Example |
|---|--------------|------------|---------|
| 1 | **Tokenization** | Splitting text into atomic units | `"cats are cute"` → `[cats, are, cute]` |
| 2 | **Stop-word Removal** | Filtering non-discriminative words | `["the", "is", "a"]` removed |
| 3 | **Stemming** | Crude suffix stripping | `running` → `runn` |
| 4 | **Lemmatization** | Dictionary-based root form | `running` → `run` (verb) |
| 5 | **POS Tagging** | Grammatical disambiguation | `book` (Noun) vs. `book` (Verb) |
| 6 | **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** | Identify real-world entities | `["Modi", "Delhi"]` → Person, Location |
| 7 | **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** | Resolve polysemy | `bank` → financial vs. river |
| 8 | **Chunking / Shallow Parsing** | Extract noun phrases | `["Information Retrieval"]` → NP |
| 9 | **Word Embeddings** | Dense semantic vectors | `king - man + woman ≈ queen` |

---

## 1.6 Why NLP is Non-Negotiable in Modern IR

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Vocabulary Mismatch Problem (also called the "Term Mismatch Problem"):**
> If a user searches for `"automobile insurance"` but the document contains `"car insurance"`, a pure keyword-based Boolean IR system **returns ZERO results**, even though the document is highly relevant. NLP (via stemming, lemmatization, query expansion, or semantic embeddings) is the only way to overcome this gap.

**Key Standard Metrics to Remember:**
- **Precision** in IR: Fraction of retrieved documents that are relevant.
- **Recall** in IR: Fraction of relevant documents that are retrieved.
- **F-Measure:** Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall.
- **MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank):** Quality of top-ranked result.
- **NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain):** Position-aware relevance metric.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The NLP Preprocessing Pipeline (Layered Architecture)

The standard NLP-for-IR pipeline consists of **seven canonical stages**, executed sequentially:

### Stage 1: Sentence Segmentation
Text is split into individual sentences. Often uses **rule-based methods** (period/question mark/exclamation followed by capital letter) or **machine learning** (e.g., PunktSentenceTokenizer).

### Stage 2: Tokenization
Breaks sentences into **tokens** (words, numbers, punctuation). Two key challenges:
- **Hyphenation:** `state-of-the-art` → 1 token or 5?
- **Contractions:** `don't` → `[do, n't]` or `[don't]`?

### Stage 3: Stop-Word Removal
Removes high-frequency, low-information words. The standard list in IR contains **~150–500** words depending on corpus (e.g., `a, an, the, of, in, is, are, to`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why remove stop words in IR?**
> 1. Reduces index size by **~30–50%**.
> 2. Improves **Precision** by removing noisy matches.
> 3. Speeds up query processing.
> *However:* Modern neural IR (BERT) often **keeps stop words** because context matters.

### Stage 4: Normalization
Lower-casing, removing punctuation, expanding abbreviations (`U.S.A.` → `usa`).

### Stage 5: Stemming vs. Lemmatization (CRITICAL CONCEPT)

| Aspect | Stemming | Lemmatization |
|--------|----------|---------------|
| **Approach** | Rule-based suffix stripping | Dictionary + morphological analysis |
| **Algorithm Examples** | **Porter, Lancaster, Snowball** | WordNet-based, spaCy |
| **Output Quality** | Coarse, may produce non-words | Linguistically valid lemma |
| **Speed** | Very fast | Slower (requires lookup) |
| **Example** | `university` → `univers` | `university` → `university` |
| **Example 2** | `better` → `better` | `better` → `good` |
| **KTU Preference** | Porter Stemmer (most-asked) | Lemmatizer (for accuracy) |

**Porter Stemmer — The Five Phases of Suffix Removal:**
- **Phase 1a:** Plurals → `sses` → `ss`, `ies` → `i`, `ss` → `ss`, `s` → ∅
- **Phase 1b:** `-ed`, `-ing` removed, then reapply Phase 1a rules
- **Phase 1c:** `y` → `i` (e.g., `happy` → `happi`)
- **Phase 2, 3, 4:** More complex suffix stripping using **measure m** (vowel-consonant pattern)
- **Phase 5b:** Final cleanup of `e` and `ll`

### Stage 6: Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging
Assigns a grammatical tag to each token: `Noun (NN)`, `Verb (VB)`, `Adjective (JJ)`, etc.
- **Why it matters in IR:** Homographs like `book` (NN) vs. `book` (VB) are disambiguated, allowing the system to match the right sense.
- **Algorithms:** HMM (Hidden Markov Model), CRF (Conditional Random Field), Neural (BiLSTM-CRF).

### Stage 7: Named Entity Recognition (NER) & Indexing
Identifies entities and feeds them into a structured index (or supplements the inverted index with an entity index).

## 2.2 Syntactic Processing in IR

**Chunking (Shallow Parsing):** Identifies non-overlapping phrases — typically **Noun Phrases (NP)**, **Verb Phrases (VP)**. Used to allow phrase queries like `"machine learning algorithms"` to match documents with the **exact phrase**.

**Constituency & Dependency Parsing:** Full syntactic trees that expose subject-verb-object relationships. Used in advanced IR for:
- **Question Answering (QA):** Detecting that *"Who founded Google?"* expects a `Person` entity as the answer.
- **Relation Extraction:** Building knowledge graphs.

## 2.3 Semantic Processing in IR — The Vector Space Model

This is where NLP meets classical IR ranking. The most fundamental formulation:

### KTU Formula Sheet — The Core Equations

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | **Term Frequency (Raw)** | $tf(t,d) = f_{t,d}$ | $f_{t,d}$ = count of term $t$ in doc $d$ | Naive — favors long docs |
| 2 | **Log-Normalized TF** | $tf(t,d) = 1 + \log(f_{t,d})$ | — | Standard IR choice |
| 3 | **Inverse Document Frequency** | $idf(t) = \log\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right)$ | $N$ = total docs, $df_t$ = docs containing $t$ | Higher → rarer term |
| 4 | **TF-IDF Weight** | $w(t,d) = tf(t,d) \cdot idf(t)$ | — | Most-cited formula in IR |
| 5 | **TF-IDF Vector for Document** | $\vec{V_d} = [w_{1,d}, w_{2,d}, \ldots, w_{M,d}]$ | $M$ = vocabulary size | Used in cosine similarity |
| 6 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\text{sim}(q,d) = \frac{\vec{V_q} \cdot \vec{V_d}}{\vert \vec{V_q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{V_d} \vert}$ | $q$ = query, $d$ = doc | Range: $[0, 1]$ |
| 7 | **Cosine Similarity (Expanded)** | $\text{sim}(q,d) = \frac{\sum_{t \in q \cap d} w_{t,q} \cdot w_{t,d}}{\sqrt{\sum_{t \in q} w_{t,q}^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{t \in d} w_{t,d}^2}}$ | — | Computational form |
| 8 | **BM25 (Okapi) — Most Important** | $\text{BM25}(q,d) = \sum_{t \in q} idf(t) \cdot \frac{f_{t,d}(k_1 + 1)}{f_{t,d} + k_1(1 - b + b \frac{\vert d \vert}{avgdl})}$ | $k_1 \in [1.2, 2.0]$, $b \approx 0.75$ | Default ranking function in Lucene/Elasticsearch |
| 9 | **Word2Vec Cosine (Semantic)** | $\text{sim}(w_1, w_2) = \cos(\vec{v}_{w_1}, \vec{v}_{w_2})$ | $\vec{v}_w$ = word embedding | Captures synonymy |
| 10 | **Edit Distance (Levenshtein)** | $lev(a,b) = \text{min ops to convert } a \text{ to } b$ | ops = insert/delete/substitute | Used in spell-check, fuzzy IR |
| 11 | **Jaccard Similarity** | $J(A,B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | $A,B$ = token sets | Used in set-based IR |
| 12 | **F-Measure** | $F_\beta = \frac{(1+\beta^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{\beta^2 P + R}$ | $P$ = precision, $R$ = recall | $F_1$ most common |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** BM25 is the **de-facto** ranking function in modern open-source search engines. Memorize its formula, the role of $k_1$ (term-frequency saturation) and $b$ (document-length normalization), and typical values.

## 2.4 Word Embeddings: The Modern NLP-IR Bridge

Traditional NLP treated words as discrete atomic symbols. Modern NLP represents words as **dense, low-dimensional, real-valued vectors** (typically 100–300 dimensions) such that **semantically similar words are geometrically close**.

**Two Foundational Architectures:**

1. **Word2Vec (Mikolov et al.):**
   - **Skip-Gram:** Predicts context words from a center word.
   - **CBOW (Continuous Bag-of-Words):** Predicts the center word from context.
   - Famous analogy: $\vec{king} - \vec{man} + \vec{woman} \approx \vec{queen}$

2. **GloVe (Global Vectors):**
   - Leverages global co-occurrence statistics of the entire corpus.
   - Objective: minimize $J = \sum_{i,j} f(X_{ij}) \cdot (\vec{w}_i^T \vec{w}_j + b_i + b_j - \log X_{ij})^2$

3. **BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers):**
   - Contextual embeddings: the *same word* gets *different vectors* based on context.
   - Used in **Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR)** and **ColBERT** for modern neural IR.

## 2.5 Practical Engineering Utility

| Domain | NLP-IR Application |
|--------|-------------------|
| **Web Search (Google, Bing)** | Query understanding, spell-correction, NER, BERT ranking |
| **E-commerce (Amazon)** | Product search, faceted navigation, recommendation via embeddings |
| **Legal Tech** | Case-law retrieval, statute search, semantic precedent matching |
| **Healthcare** | PubMed search, clinical decision support |
| **Customer Support** | Ticket routing, FAQ retrieval via RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) |
| **Digital Libraries** | Cross-language information retrieval (CLIR) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides **exhaustive derivations** of the most-asked NLP-IR formulas, suitable for the **14-mark KTU Part B** questions. Every step is explicitly shown.

## 3.1 Derivation: From Boolean Retrieval to TF-IDF

### Step 1 — The Boolean Retrieval Baseline

Boolean IR retrieves a document $d$ if it satisfies a logical expression over terms:
$$d \in \text{Result} \iff \bigvee_{i} (t_i \in d)$$

**Limitation:** Boolean IR cannot **rank** documents — it returns an unranked set.

### Step 2 — Introducing Term Importance: Term Frequency

Heuristic: *A document with more occurrences of a term is more relevant to that term.*

$$tf(t, d) = f_{t,d}$$

**Problem:** A document with 100 occurrences of "data" and 10,000 total words should not be considered *infinitely* more relevant than one with 50 occurrences in 1,000 words.

### Step 3 — Logarithmic Dampening of Term Frequency

To prevent long documents from dominating, we apply log-normalization:

$$
tf_{\text{log}}(t, d) = 
\begin{cases}
1 + \log(f_{t,d}) & \text{if } f_{t,d} > 0 \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Justification:** Doubling the term count should not double the relevance. The logarithm ensures that $tf(100) - tf(10) \ll 2 \cdot (tf(10) - tf(1))$.

### Step 4 — Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)

Observation: *Common words like "the" appear in nearly every document and carry little discriminative power.*

$$idf(t) = \log\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right)$$

- If $df_t = N$ (term in every doc), $idf(t) = 0$ → fully discounted.
- If $df_t = 1$ (term in one doc), $idf(t) = \log N$ → maximally weighted.

**Why $\log$?** It dampens the explosive growth of $\frac{N}{df_t}$ for very rare terms.

### Step 5 — Combining TF and IDF

$$w(t, d) = tf_{\text{log}}(t, d) \cdot idf(t) = \left(1 + \log(f_{t,d})\right) \cdot \log\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Vectorizing Documents

For vocabulary $V = \{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_M\}$, each document is represented as:

$$
\vec{V_d} = \left[ w(t_1, d),\; w(t_2, d),\; \ldots,\; w(t_M, d) \right] \in \mathbb{R}^M
$$

Similarly, the query is vectorized as $\vec{V_q}$.

### Step 7 — Cosine Similarity (Full Derivation)

Cosine similarity measures the **angle** between two vectors, invariant to document length:

$$
\cos(\theta_{q,d}) = \frac{\vec{V_q} \cdot \vec{V_d}}{\vert \vec{V_q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{V_d} \vert}
$$

Expanding the numerator (dot product):

$$
\vec{V_q} \cdot \vec{V_d} = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w(t_i, q) \cdot w(t_i, d)
$$

Expanding the denominators (Euclidean norms):

$$
\vert \vec{V_q} \vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{M} w(t_i, q)^2}, \quad \vert \vec{V_d} \vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{M} w(t_i, d)^2}
$$

**Final expanded form:**

$$
\text{sim}(q, d) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{M} w(t_i, q) \cdot w(t_i, d)}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{M} w(t_i, q)^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{M} w(t_i, d)^2}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Computational optimization:** Since $w(t_i, q) = 0$ for almost all $i$ (queries are short), the computation reduces to summing only over terms **shared** between $q$ and $d$, giving the simplified form shown in the Formula Sheet row #7.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: BM25 from First Principles

### Step 1 — Start with IDF

We begin with the same IDF as TF-IDF:

$$
idf(t) = \log\left(\frac{N - df_t + 0.5}{df_t + 0.5}\right)
$$

(The `0.5` smoothing avoids division by zero and reduces noise for very low $df_t$.)

### Step 2 — Term Frequency Saturation

Raw $f_{t,d}$ grows unboundedly. We model **diminishing returns** using a **saturation curve**:

$$
\frac{f_{t,d}(k_1 + 1)}{f_{t,d} + k_1}
$$

- When $f_{t,d} = 0$: returns $0$ (irrelevant).
- When $f_{t,d} \to \infty$: asymptotes to $k_1 + 1$ (saturated).
- Parameter $k_1$ controls **how quickly** saturation is reached. Typical value: **$k_1 = 1.2$**.

### Step 3 — Document Length Normalization

Long documents naturally have higher $f_{t,d}$. We normalize by the ratio of document length $\vert d \vert$ to average document length $\overline{\vert d \vert}$:

$$
1 - b + b \cdot \frac{\vert d \vert}{\overline{\vert d \vert}}
$$

- $b = 0$: no length normalization (long docs win).
- $b = 1$: full length normalization (all docs treated as average).
- Typical: **$b = 0.75$**.

### Step 4 — Combine into BM25

$$
\boxed{
\text{BM25}(q, d) = \sum_{t \in q} \underbrace{\log\left(\frac{N - df_t + 0.5}{df_t + 0.5}\right)}_{\text{IDF component}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{f_{t,d}(k_1 + 1)}{f_{t,d} + k_1 \left(1 - b + b \frac{\vert d \vert}{\overline{\vert d \vert}}\right)}}_{\text{TF-saturation + length norm component}}
}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this boxed equation** — it is worth **7 marks minimum** in any KTU Part B NLP-IR question.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: BM25 on a Mini-Collection

**Setup:**
- Collection: $N = 3$ documents
- $d_1 = $ `"the quick brown fox"`, $\vert d_1 \vert = 4$
- $d_2 = $ `"the lazy dog"`, $\vert d_2 \vert = 3$
- $d_3 = $ `"the quick dog jumps"`, $\vert d_3 \vert = 4$
- Average length: $\overline{\vert d \vert} = 11/3 \approx 3.67$
- Query: $q = $ `"quick dog"`
- Parameters: $k_1 = 1.2$, $b = 0.75$

### Step A: Compute Document Frequencies

| Term | $df_t$ | Appears in |
|------|--------|------------|
| `quick` | 2 | $d_1, d_3$ |
| `dog` | 2 | $d_2, d_3$ |

### Step B: Compute IDF for Each Query Term

$$
idf(\text{quick}) = \log\left(\frac{3 - 2 + 0.5}{2 + 0.5}\right) = \log\left(\frac{1.5}{2.5}\right) = \log(0.6) \approx -0.222
$$

$$
idf(\text{dog}) = \log\left(\frac{3 - 2 + 0.5}{2 + 0.5}\right) = \log(0.6) \approx -0.222
$$

> Both query terms are common — hence **negative** IDF. In practice, the **smoothed BM25+ variant** (or Lucene's variant) clips this to zero. We continue the academic derivation as-is.

### Step C: Score Each Document Against the Query

**Score for $d_1$:** Contains `quick` ($f = 1$) but not `dog` ($f = 0$).

$$
\text{TF-saturation for 'quick' in } d_1 = \frac{1 \cdot (1.2 + 1)}{1 + 1.2 \cdot (1 - 0.75 + 0.75 \cdot 4/3.67)} = \frac{2.2}{1 + 1.2 \cdot (0.25 + 0.817)} = \frac{2.2}{1 + 1.281} = \frac{2.2}{2.281} \approx 0.965
$$

$$
\text{BM25}(q, d_1) = (-0.222)(0.965) + 0 \approx -0.214
$$

**Score for $d_2$:** Contains `dog` ($f = 1$) but not `quick`.

$$
\text{TF-saturation for 'dog' in } d_2 = \frac{1 \cdot 2.2}{1 + 1.2 \cdot (0.25 + 0.75 \cdot 3/3.67)} = \frac{2.2}{1 + 1.2 \cdot (0.25 + 0.613)} = \frac{2.2}{1.0 + 1.036} = \frac{2.2}{2.036} \approx 1.081
$$

$$
\text{BM25}(q, d_2) = 0 + (-0.222)(1.081) \approx -0.240
$$

**Score for $d_3$:** Contains **both** `quick` ($f=1$) and `dog` ($f=1$). Length = 4.

$$
\text{TF-saturation for 'quick' in } d_3 = \frac{1 \cdot 2.2}{1 + 1.2 \cdot (0.25 + 0.75 \cdot 4/3.67)} \approx 0.965
$$

$$
\text{TF-saturation for 'dog' in } d_3 = \frac{1 \cdot 2.2}{1 + 1.2 \cdot (0.25 + 0.75 \cdot 4/3.67)} \approx 0.965
$$

$$
\text{BM25}(q, d_3) = (-0.222)(0.965) + (-0.222)(0.965) \approx -0.428
$$

### Step D: Final Ranking

Since BM25 is a **ranking function** (only the *order* matters, not the absolute values), the ranking is:

$$
\boxed{d_3 \succ d_1 \succ d_2}
$$

Document $d_3$ wins because it contains **both** query terms. ✅

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What?

| Scenario | Best NLP Technique | Reason |
|----------|-------------------|--------|
| **Memory-constrained, fast** | Stemming (Porter) | No dictionary lookup |
| **High accuracy, slower** | Lemmatization | Dictionary-based, valid lemmas |
| **Phrase queries** | POS tagging + Chunking | Detect noun phrases |
| **Ambiguous queries (`java`)** | NER + WSD | Tag as Programming Language vs. Island |
| **Synonymy (`car` vs. `auto`)** | Word embeddings (Word2Vec/GloVe) | Cosine of vectors |
| **Contextual meaning** | BERT | Contextualized embeddings |
| **Misspellings** | Edit Distance / Phonetic (Soundex) | Fuzzy matching |
| **Multilingual search** | Cross-lingual embeddings | Project to shared space |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The End-to-End NLP-IR Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Raw Document Collection] --> B[Sentence Segmentation]
    B --> C[Tokenization]
    C --> D[Stop-Word Removal]
    D --> E[Normalization Lowercase, Strip Punctuation]
    E --> F[Stemming or Lemmatization]
    F --> G[POS Tagging]
    G --> H[Named Entity Recognition NER]
    H --> I[Inverted Index Construction]
    I --> J[Ranked Document Retrieval]
    Q[User Query] --> Q1[Query Preprocessing]
    Q1 --> J
    J --> K[Top-K Results]
    J --> L[Query Expansion via Embeddings]
    L --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Document text flows top-to-bottom through the NLP pipeline. The user query goes through a **parallel preprocessing path** (left side). Both converge at the **Inverted Index / Ranking** stage. Modern systems (dashed box) include a **Query Expansion feedback loop** using word embeddings or BERT-based semantic similarity.

## 4.2 Layered Architecture of Modern IR with NLP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1 [Layer 1 - Lexical Level]
        L1A[Tokenization]
        L1B[Stop-word Removal]
        L1C[Stemming]
    end
    subgraph L2 [Layer 2 - Syntactic Level]
        L2A[POS Tagging]
        L2B[Chunking]
        L2C[Parsing]
    end
    subgraph L3 [Layer 3 - Semantic Level]
        L3A[Word Sense Disambiguation]
        L3B[Named Entity Recognition]
        L3C[Word Embeddings]
    end
    subgraph L4 [Layer 4 - Pragmatic Level]
        L4A[Coreference Resolution]
        L4B[Query Understanding]
        L4C[Contextual Reranking]
    end
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> M[Final Ranked List]
```

## 4.3 BM25 Scoring Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q[Query q] --> Q1[Tokenize Query into terms t]
    Q1 --> LOOP{For each term t in q}
    LOOP --> IDF[Compute idf_t using df_t and N]
    IDF --> TF[Look up f_td in document d]
    TF --> NORM[Compute length norm using d and avgdl]
    NORM --> SAT[Apply TF saturation with k1 and b]
    SAT --> MULT[Multiply idf_t with saturated TF]
    MULT --> SUM[Sum over all terms in q]
    SUM --> SCORE[Final BM25 score q,d]
```

## 4.4 Word Embedding Geometric Intuition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EMB [300-Dimensional Embedding Space]
        W1[king]
        W2[queen]
        W3[man]
        W4[woman]
    end
    W1 ---|minus| W3
    W2 ---|plus| W4
    W1 -->|nearest| W2
    W3 -->|nearest| W4
```

**Interpretation:** In a well-trained Word2Vec/GloVe space, the vector arithmetic $\vec{king} - \vec{man} + \vec{woman} \approx \vec{queen}$ holds, capturing **gendered royal relationships** purely from corpus statistics. This is the geometric foundation of semantic IR.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Define the following with one example each:**
**(a)** Stemming
**(b)** Lemmatization
**(c)** Stop-word removal

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**(a) Stemming (1 mark):** Stemming is a crude, rule-based NLP technique that chops off the suffixes of words to reduce them to a common "stem" form, without necessarily producing a valid word. It uses algorithms like the **Porter Stemmer** (the most-cited, 5-phase rule-based system).
*Example:* `connection`, `connected`, `connecting` → `connect` (sometimes non-words like `univers` from `university`).

**(b) Lemmatization (1 mark):** Lemmatization is a more sophisticated, dictionary-based morphological analysis that returns the **valid lemma** (dictionary base form) of a word, taking the **part-of-speech** into account.
*Example:* `better` → `good` (adjective), `ran` → `run` (verb).

**(c) Stop-word Removal (1 mark):** Stop-words are high-frequency, low-information words (such as `the`, `is`, `a`, `of`, `an`) that are filtered out from documents/queries to **reduce index size and improve precision**.
*Example:* Input: `"this is a good book"` → Output: `"good book"`.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of Named Entity Recognition (NER) in Information Retrieval. Give two examples of entity types.**

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Named Entity Recognition (NER) is the NLP task of identifying and classifying **real-world named entities** in text into predefined categories such as persons, organizations, locations, dates, and monetary values.

**Role in IR (1.5 marks):**
1. **Entity-based Indexing:** Builds a separate **entity index** alongside the inverted index, allowing users to search for documents about a specific person (e.g., `"Einstein"`) with high precision.
2. **Query Understanding:** Recognizes entities in the query to disambiguate intent (e.g., `"Apple iPhone"` → organization + product, not the fruit).
3. **Faceted Search & Knowledge Graphs:** Powers sidebar knowledge panels in Google Search.
4. **Question Answering:** Detects expected answer type (e.g., `WHO` → PERSON entity).

**Examples (0.5 marks):** `PERSON` (e.g., *"Modi"*), `LOCATION` (e.g., *"Kerala"*), `ORGANIZATION` (e.g., *"Microsoft"*), `DATE` (e.g., *"2024"*).

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (MANDATORY READ):**
> 1. **Do not** write generic definitions without tying them to **IR applications**. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for "definition without context."
> 2. **Do not** skip the mathematical formula for TF-IDF or BM25. Even in 14-mark theory questions, **at least one formula** (worth 2–3 marks) is expected.
> 3. **Do not** confuse stemming and lemmatization. Use the correct technical names: "Porter Stemmer" (not "stemming algorithm") and "WordNet-based Lemmatizer."
> 4. **Always** mention that NLP preprocessing is applied **both** to documents and queries (symmetric pipeline).
> 5. In BM25 questions, forgetting to state typical values of $k_1$ and $b$ costs **1 mark**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the various stages of the **NLP preprocessing pipeline** for Information Retrieval. Mention the role of **tokenization, stop-word removal, stemming, and lemmatization** in detail.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **TF-IDF weighting scheme** used in the Vector Space Model. Derive the cosine similarity formula and show how documents are ranked for a given query.

### **Model Answer:**

### Part (a) — NLP Preprocessing Pipeline [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Raw Text → Sentence Segmentation → Tokenization → Stop-Word Removal 
→ Normalization → Stemming/Lemmatization → POS Tagging → NER → Inverted Index
```

(Diagram should be drawn cleanly with arrows showing flow.)

**Stage-by-stage explanation (5 marks):**

1. **Sentence Segmentation (0.5):** Splits raw text into sentences using punctuation rules or ML models. Important because token statistics are computed per-sentence in many algorithms.

2. **Tokenization (1 mark):** The process of breaking sentences into atomic tokens (words, numbers, punctuation). Two key issues: **contractions** (`don't` → `do, n't`) and **hyphenated words** (`state-of-the-art`). The WhitespaceTokenizer, RegexpTokenizer, and TreebankTokenizer are commonly used.

3. **Stop-Word Removal (1 mark):** Removes non-discriminative, high-frequency words from a predefined list (e.g., the SMART system's list of ~571 words). This reduces index size by 30–50% and improves precision. Modern BERT-based systems may retain stop-words for context.

4. **Stemming (1.5 marks):** A rule-based process that chops word suffixes to map morphological variants to a common stem. The **Porter Stemmer** is the de-facto standard, operating in **5 phases** of cascading rules. Example: `connection` → `connect`, `connected` → `connect`, `connects` → `connect`. The output may not be a valid word (`university` → `univers`).

5. **Lemmatization (1 mark):** A more accurate, dictionary-based method that returns the valid lemma considering POS context. Example: `better` → `good` (adjective), `running` → `run` (verb). Slower than stemming but linguistically correct.

> **IR Application Note:** These steps are applied **symmetrically** to both documents (indexing time) and queries (query time) to ensure matching occurs at the same normalized form.

### Part (b) — TF-IDF and Cosine Similarity [7 Marks]

**TF-IDF Derivation (4 marks):**

Start with the **boolean limitation** — Boolean IR cannot rank documents. To introduce ranking, we need a **weight** for each term in each document.

**Step 1 — Term Frequency (TF):**
$$tf(t, d) = 1 + \log(f_{t,d})$$
This dampens the linear dominance of long documents.

**Step 2 — Inverse Document Frequency (IDF):**
$$idf(t) = \log\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right)$$
This penalizes common terms and rewards rare, discriminative terms.

**Step 3 — TF-IDF Weight:**
$$w(t, d) = tf(t, d) \cdot idf(t) = \left(1 + \log(f_{t,d})\right) \cdot \log\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Document Vector:**
$$\vec{V_d} = [w(t_1, d), w(t_2, d), \ldots, w(t_M, d)] \in \mathbb{R}^M$$

**Cosine Similarity Derivation (2 marks):**

The query $q$ and document $d$ are both represented as TF-IDF vectors. The similarity is the **cosine of the angle** between them:

$$\text{sim}(q, d) = \frac{\vec{V_q} \cdot \vec{V_d}}{\vert \vec{V_q} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{V_d} \vert}$$

Expanded form (computational form for sparse vectors):

$$\text{sim}(q, d) = \frac{\sum_{t \in q \cap d} w(t, q) \cdot w(t, d)}{\sqrt{\sum_{t \in q} w(t, q)^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{t \in d} w(t, d)^2}}$$

**Ranking (1 mark):** Documents are sorted by $\text{sim}(q, d)$ in **descending order**; the top-K are returned to the user. Cosine similarity is preferred over raw dot product because it is **invariant to document length** — two documents of different lengths with the same term distribution have similarity 1.0.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **BM25 ranking function** in detail. State the role of parameters $k_1$ and $b$, and discuss how BM25 overcomes the limitations of raw TF-IDF.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the role of **Word Embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe) and BERT** in modern Information Retrieval. How do they address the **vocabulary mismatch problem** in traditional keyword-based IR?

### **Model Answer:**

### Part (a) — BM25 Ranking Function [7 Marks]

**Background (1 mark):** BM25 (Best Match 25, Okapi BM25) is the **de-facto ranking function** used in modern open-source search engines (Apache Lucene, Elasticsearch, Solr, OpenSearch). It improves upon raw TF-IDF by introducing **TF saturation** and **document length normalization**.

**BM25 Formula (3 marks):**

$$\text{BM25}(q, d) = \sum_{t \in q} \underbrace{\log\left(\frac{N - df_t + 0.5}{df_t + 0.5}\right)}_{\text{Robertson-Sparck Jones IDF}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{f_{t,d}(k_1 + 1)}{f_{t,d} + k_1 \left(1 - b + b \frac{\vert d \vert}{\overline{\vert d \vert}}\right)}}_{\text{Saturated TF with length norm}}$$

**Role of Parameters (2 marks):**

- **$k_1$ (Term Frequency Saturation):** Controls how quickly the contribution of repeated term occurrences saturates. Default: **$k_1 \in [1.2, 2.0]$**. A higher $k_1$ means slower saturation (more weight to repeated terms).
- **$b$ (Document Length Normalization):** Controls the impact of document length. Default: **$b = 0.75$**. $b = 0$ ignores length; $b = 1$ fully normalizes by length.
- $\overline{\vert d \vert}$ is the **average document length** in the collection.

**How BM25 Overcomes Raw TF-IDF Limitations (1 mark):**
1. Raw TF-IDF assumes relevance grows linearly with $f_{t,d}$ — unrealistic. BM25 models **diminishing returns** via saturation.
2. Raw TF-IDF over-weights long documents. BM25 normalizes by $\vert d \vert / \overline{\vert d \vert}$.
3. The +0.5 smoothing in IDF handles edge cases where $df_t$ is very small.

### Part (b) — Word Embeddings and BERT in IR [7 Marks]

**The Vocabulary Mismatch Problem (1 mark):**
Traditional keyword-based IR cannot match documents that use *different words* (synonyms, paraphrases) for the same concept. E.g., query `"automobile"` vs. document containing `"car"`. BM25/TF-IDF return **zero overlap**.

**Word2Vec (2 marks):** Proposed by Mikolov et al. (2013), Word2Vec learns **dense 100–300 dimensional vectors** for each word such that semantically similar words are geometrically close. Two architectures:
- **Skip-Gram:** Predicts context words from a center word.
- **CBOW:** Predicts center word from context.

Famous property: $\vec{king} - \vec{man} + \vec{woman} \approx \vec{queen}$. In IR, query and document can be represented as **average word vectors**, and **cosine similarity** of these vectors captures semantic similarity.

**GloVe (1 mark):** Global Vectors (Pennington et al., 2014). Unlike Word2Vec (which uses local context windows), GloVe leverages **global co-occurrence statistics** of the entire corpus via a weighted least-squares objective.

**BERT and Contextual Embeddings (2 marks):** BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers, Devlin et al., 2018) generates **contextual embeddings** — the same word gets *different vectors* based on surrounding context. This solves **polysemy** (e.g., `bank` in *"river bank"* vs. *"bank account"*). In IR, **Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR)** and **ColBERT** use BERT to encode queries and documents into dense vectors, indexed via Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) search (FAISS, HNSW).

**How They Solve Vocabulary Mismatch (1 mark):** Embeddings project words into a **continuous semantic space** where synonyms, related terms, and paraphrases are clustered together. Query expansion via "nearest neighbors" (e.g., `automobile` → `car`, `vehicle`, `auto`) effectively solves the term-mismatch bottleneck of Boolean and TF-IDF systems.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Board Exam Day):**

- ✅ **NLP pipeline order:** Sentence Segment → Tokenize → Stop-word → Normalize → Stem/Lemma → POS → NER → Index.
- ✅ **Stemming vs. Lemmatization:** Stemming = crude, fast, may produce non-words (Porter). Lemmatization = dictionary, valid lemma, slower, POS-aware.
- ✅ **TF-IDF formula:** $w(t,d) = (1 + \log f_{t,d}) \cdot \log(N/df_t)$.
- ✅ **Cosine similarity** is the standard IR similarity — invariant to document length, returns values in $[0, 1]$.
- ✅ **BM25 is the modern default** ranking function; memorize parameters $k_1 \in [1.2, 2.0]$, $b = 0.75$.
- ✅ **Word2Vec** captures static semantics; **BERT** captures contextual semantics.
- ✅ **Vocabulary mismatch** is the #1 problem in keyword-based IR, solved by embeddings, query expansion, and lemmatization.
- ✅ **Edit Distance (Levenshtein)** is the standard fuzzy-match metric for spell-correction in IR.
- ✅ **Stop-words:** ~150–500 common words; removing them shrinks the index by 30–50% and boosts precision.
- ✅ **Symmetric pipeline:** Apply the same NLP preprocessing to BOTH documents (indexing) and queries (query time).
- ✅ **Modern trend:** Neural IR with BERT-based dense retrieval (DPR, ColBERT) is replacing TF-IDF/BM25 in production-grade systems.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** When asked *"Explain the role of NLP in IR"* in 14 marks, **always structure your answer as:** (1) Definition + problem statement (2 marks), (2) Pipeline diagram (2 marks), (3) Four key NLP stages explained with IR examples (6 marks), (4) Modern extensions — embeddings, BERT, query expansion (2 marks), (5) Limitations / conclusion (2 marks). This structure guarantees full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Social Media and Sentiment Analysis: Social Media Mining and Opinion Retrieval
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Social Media and Sentiment Analysis: Social Media Mining and Opinion Retrieval # Module 4 — Social Media and Sentiment Analysis: Social Media Mining and Opinion Retrieval ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECDT745 — Module 4)** > *Social Media Mining* is the process of representing, analyzing, and extracting meaningful patterns from social media data generated on platforms such as Twitter/X, Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, and online forums. *Opinion Retrieval / Sentiment Analysis* is the sub-discipline of Information Retrieval (IR) and Natural Language Processing (NLP) concerned with identifying, classifying, and ranking user-generated opinions (positive, negative, neutral, or fine-grained emotions) expressed in unstructured text with respect to a given topic, entity, or query. ### 1.1 What is Social Media Mining? Social Media Mining (SMM) is the systematic extraction of knowledge, insights, and actionable intelligence from user-generated content (UGC) on social networking platforms. The **5 V's of Big Data** (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value) directly apply to social media streams. From an **Information Retrieval (IR)** perspective, it extends traditional document retrieval to include user intent, context, social graph, and temporal dynamics. > [!NOTE] > **Three Pillars of Social Media Mining (Zafarani et al., 2014):** > 1. **Data** — raw posts, likes, shares, comments, profiles, networks > 2. **Mining Algorithms** — statistical, ML, NLP, graph-based methods > 3. **Human / Domain Knowledge** — interpretation, evaluation, ethics ### 1.2 What is Opinion Retrieval? Opinion Retrieval is the IR task of retrieving documents (or micro-posts) that express an **opinion or sentiment** about a given query/entity, *as opposed to merely containing factual or topically-relevant text*. The seminal formulation by **Carbonell et al. (2009)** and the **TREC Blog Track (2006–2010)** standardized it. - **Subjective text** = opinion-bearing (e.g., *"I love the new iPhone camera!"*) - **Objective text** = factual (e.g., *"iPhone 15 has a 48MP camera."*) ### 1.3 What is Sentiment Analysis? Sentiment Analysis (a.k.a. Opinion Mining) is the computational study of opinions, sentiments, attitudes, appraisals, and emotions expressed in text. The standard taxonomy classifies sentiment into: | Polarity | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Positive** | Favorable attitude | "Excellent service!" | | **Negative** | Unfavorable attitude | "Worst experience ever" | | **Neutral** | No sentiment / factual | "The product arrived on Tuesday" | > [!TIP] > **Conceptual Analogy (The Restaurant Critic):** > Imagine Google as a librarian who finds books about restaurants. Now imagine **Opinion Retrieval** as a *food critic aggregator* — it doesn't just find articles *mentioning* a restaurant, it specifically finds articles that **express a personal opinion** about it (good, bad, average), filters out bland menu listings, and ranks the reviews by how strongly the opinion is held. **Sentiment Analysis** is the critic's verdict — Positive, Negative, or Neutral — often with an intensity score (e.g., 5-star vs 1-star). **Social Media Mining** is the entire ecosystem: the critic walking through every street, Twitter, Instagram, and Yelp, gathering raw opinions, removing spam, tracking trends, and publishing a city-wide report. 🍽️📱 ### 1.4 The Subjective vs. Objective Dichotomy A core distinction in this module: $$\text{Text} \longrightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Subjective} \Rightarrow \text{Opinion-bearing} \Rightarrow \text{Requires Sentiment Analysis} \\ \text{Objective} \Rightarrow \text{Factual} \Rightarrow \text{Filter out in Opinion Retrieval} \end{cases}$$ The **Subjectivity Classifier** is therefore the gatekeeper before the polarity classifier. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Watchpoint:** Examiners frequently ask the difference between *Sentiment Analysis* and *Opinion Mining*. Technically they are used interchangeably, but in IR literature, *Opinion Mining* often refers to the **extraction** of opinion tuples (target, holder, polarity), while *Sentiment Analysis* refers to the **classification** of polarity intensity. Treat them as synonyms in exam answers unless the question demands a deeper distinction. ### 1.5 KTU Course Outcome (CO) Mapping | CO ID | Course Outcome | Module 4 Mapping | |---|---|---| | **CO4** | Apply IR techniques to social and opinion-rich data | ✔ Directly addressed | | **CO5** | Evaluate modern IR systems for subjective content | ✔ Covered via evaluation metrics | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architecture of an Opinion Retrieval & Sentiment Analysis Pipeline A standard end-to-end IR pipeline for social media (used in TREC Blog Track and modern systems) operates in **five sequential stages**: 1. **Data Acquisition (Crawling / Streaming)** - APIs (Twitter/X v2, Reddit API, Mastodon) - Web crawlers (politeness-aware) - Streaming: Apache Kafka, Flume for **Velocity** 2. **Preprocessing** - Tokenization, stopword removal, stemming/lemmatization - Normalization: lowercasing, URL/mention/hashtag stripping - **Emoticon & emoji handling** (e.g., 😊 → "happy") - Spell correction (essential for social media) 3. **Subjectivity Filtering** - Discard objective statements - Classifier: Naive Bayes, SVM, or lexicon-based (e.g., MPQA Subjectivity Lexicon) 4. **Feature Extraction** - **Bag-of-Words (BoW)**, **TF-IDF**, **n-grams** - **Part-of-Speech (POS) tags** — adjectives are strong sentiment carriers - **Negation handling** ("not good" → negative) - Word embeddings: Word2Vec, GloVe, BERT contextual embeddings 5. **Sentiment Classification & Ranking** - Polarity assignment (Pos/Neg/Neutral) or regression (intensity) - **Opinion-aware document ranking** (combine topical relevance with opinion score) ## 2.2 The Opinion Retrieval Formulation Let: - $Q$ = user query (e.g., *"climate change policy"*) - $D = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_n\}$ = collection of social media documents - $R(q, d)$ = topical relevance score - $S(d)$ = opinion / sentiment score - $O(q, d)$ = opinion relevance score **Linear Opinion-Relevance Combination (Carbonell et al.):** $$O(q, d) = \alpha \cdot R(q, d) + \beta \cdot S(d) + \gamma \cdot C(d)$$ where: - $C(d)$ = credibility/authority of author $d$ - $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$ (or normalized weights) - $R(q, d)$ from **BM25** or **Language Model** - $S(d) \in [-1, +1]$ (negative → positive) **Multiplicative Form (alternative):** $$O(q, d) = R(q, d) \times \mathbb{1}[\text{subjective}(d)] \times S(d)$$ where $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function for subjective documents. ## 2.3 Sentiment Classification — Three Paradigms ### (a) Lexicon-Based (Unsupervised) Aggregates polarity from a sentiment dictionary. $$S_{doc} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot p(t_i)$$ where $w_i$ = TF-IDF weight of term $t_i$, $p(t_i) \in [-1, +1]$ = lexicon polarity. **Lexicons:** SentiWordNet, VADER (social-media optimized), AFINN, Bing Liu's Opinion Lexicon. ### (b) Machine Learning (Supervised) Probabilistic classifier (Naive Bayes, MaxEnt, SVM): $$P(\text{class} = c \mid d) = \frac{P(d \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(d)}$$ For Naive Bayes with bag-of-words: $$P(c \mid d) \propto P(c) \prod_{t \in d} P(t \mid c)$$ with Laplace smoothing: $$P(t \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(t, c) + 1}{\sum_{t'} \text{count}(t', c) + \vert V \vert}$$ where $\vert V \vert$ = vocabulary size. ### (c) Deep Learning / Transformer-Based Fine-tuned **BERT / RoBERTa** for sentence-level classification: $$\hat{y} = \text{softmax}\bigl(W \cdot \text{BERT}([CLS] \oplus d \oplus [SEP])\bigr)$$ ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory equations to memorize for KTU ESE — Module 4:** | # | Formula / Concept | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $O(q,d) = \alpha R(q,d) + \beta S(d) + \gamma C(d)$ | Opinion relevance scoring | | 2 | $P(c \mid d) \propto P(c) \prod_{t} P(t \mid c)$ | Naive Bayes sentiment classifier | | 3 | $P(t \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(t,c)+1}{\sum_{t'} \text{count}(t',c)+\vert V \vert}$ | Laplace smoothing | | 4 | $S_{doc} = \sum_i w_i \cdot p(t_i)$ | Lexicon-based polarity aggregation | | 5 | $w_{tfidf}(t,d) = tf(t,d) \cdot \log\frac{N}{df(t)}$ | TF-IDF weight for sentiment feature | | 6 | $R_{BM25}(q,d) = \sum_{t \in q} \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \frac{tf(t,d)(k_1+1)}{tf(t,d)+k_1(1-b+b\frac{\vert d \vert}{avgdl})}$ | BM25 used in opinion retrieval | | 7 | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Accuracy metric | | 8 | $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}, \quad R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Precision & Recall | | 9 | $F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}$ | F1-score (key sentiment metric) | | 10 | $\text{Pol}(d) = \frac{\#\text{pos} - \#\text{neg}}{\#\text{pos} + \#\text{neg}}$ | Normalized polarity (VADER-style) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU LaTeX Pitfall:** In markdown tables, the vertical pipe `|` breaks the syntax. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value or set size. The formula sheet above already uses `\vert` consistently. ## 2.5 Real-World Applications in Engineering - **Brand Monitoring & Reputation Management** (Coca-Cola, Apple use sentiment dashboards) - **Political Election Prediction** (Twitter sentiment ↔ election outcomes — Tumasjan et al., 2010) - **Financial Forecasting** (sentiment on stock tickers → market prediction) - **Healthcare Surveillance** (tracking depression signals, drug side-effects) - **E-commerce Review Mining** (Amazon, Flipkart star-rating prediction) - **Crisis Response & Disaster Management** (tweets during earthquakes/floods) - **Recommender Systems** (sentiment-aware ranking of products) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: End-to-End Opinion Retrieval Scoring **Problem Setup (KTU-style):** A small social media collection $D = \{d_1, d_2, d_3\}$ has been retrieved for query $Q = \text{"electric vehicles"}$. Compute the **opinion-aware relevance score** $O(q, d)$ and rank the documents. | Document | Content (snippet) | |---|---| | $d_1$ | "Tesla Model 3 is amazing! Best EV I've driven." | | $d_2$ | "Electric vehicles use lithium-ion batteries." | | $d_3$ | "Horrible range, terrible charging network. Disappointed." | Given: $\alpha = 0.5$ (topical weight), $\beta = 0.4$ (sentiment weight), $\gamma = 0.1$ (credibility). Assume BM25 has already been computed and subjectivity is binary. ### Step 1 — Compute Topical Relevance $R(q, d)$ from BM25 For brevity, use given BM25 scores (assume IDF-weighted, $k_1 = 1.2$, $b = 0.75$): $$R(q, d_1) = 0.92, \quad R(q, d_2) = 0.78, \quad R(q, d_3) = 0.85$$ ### Step 2 — Subjectivity Filter $$\text{Subjective}(d_1) = 1, \quad \text{Subjective}(d_2) = 0, \quad \text{Subjective}(d_3) = 1$$ (Document $d_2$ is purely factual — filtered out from opinion ranking.) ### Step 3 — Compute Sentiment Score $S(d)$ via Lexicon Using VADER-like scoring (normalized polarity): $$S(d_1) = +0.85 \quad (\text{strong positive})$$ $$S(d_2) = 0.00 \quad (\text{neutral/factual})$$ $$S(d_3) = -0.72 \quad (\text{strong negative})$$ ### Step 4 — Compute Credibility $C(d)$ Based on follower count, verification status, historical accuracy: $$C(d_1) = 0.70, \quad C(d_2) = 0.90, \quad C(d_3) = 0.55$$ ### Step 5 — Apply Linear Combination Formula $$O(q, d) = 0.5 \cdot R(q, d) + 0.4 \cdot S(d) + 0.1 \cdot C(d)$$ **For $d_1$ (positive opinion):** $$O(q, d_1) = 0.5(0.92) + 0.4(0.85) + 0.1(0.70) = 0.46 + 0.34 + 0.07 = 0.87$$ **For $d_2$ (filtered — objective):** $$O(q, d_2) = 0 \quad \text{(discarded from opinion ranking — set to } -\infty \text{ for ranking)}$$ **For $d_3$ (negative opinion):** $$O(q, d_3) = 0.5(0.85) + 0.4(-0.72) + 0.1(0.55) = 0.425 - 0.288 + 0.055 = 0.192$$ ### Step 6 — Final Ranked List (Opinion-Aware) $$\boxed{d_1 \;\;(0.87) \;\succ\; d_3 \;\;(0.19) \;\succ\; d_2 \;\;(\text{filtered})}$$ > [!NOTE] > Notice that although $d_2$ had high topical relevance (0.78), it is **filtered out** because it is objective. Among subjective documents, $d_1$ (positive review) outranks $d_3$ (negative review) because its positivity weight is larger in magnitude. The examiner's interest is in showing the **interaction** between topical, sentiment, and credibility signals. ## 3.2 Worked Example: Naive Bayes Sentiment Classification **Problem:** A 2-class problem: Positive ($c_+$) and Negative ($c_-$). Training data summary: | Word | $P(w \mid c_+)$ | $P(w \mid c_-)$ | |---|---|---| | good | 0.30 | 0.05 | | bad | 0.05 | 0.25 | | not | 0.10 | 0.15 | Priors: $P(c_+) = 0.5$, $P(c_-) = 0.5$. Vocabulary $\vert V \vert = 3$ (toy example). **Test document:** $d =$ "good not bad" **Step 1 — Compute likelihoods:** $$P(d \mid c_+) = P(\text{good}\mid c_+) \cdot P(\text{not}\mid c_+) \cdot P(\text{bad}\mid c_+)$$ $$P(d \mid c_+) = 0.30 \times 0.10 \times 0.05 = 0.0015$$ $$P(d \mid c_-) = 0.05 \times 0.15 \times 0.25 = 0.001875$$ **Step 2 — Multiply by priors:** $$P(c_+ \mid d) \propto 0.5 \times 0.0015 = 0.00075$$ $$P(c_- \mid d) \propto 0.5 \times 0.001875 = 0.0009375$$ **Step 3 — Normalize:** $$P(c_+ \mid d) = \frac{0.00075}{0.00075 + 0.0009375} = \frac{0.00075}{0.0016875} \approx 0.444$$ $$P(c_- \mid d) = \frac{0.0009375}{0.0016875} \approx 0.556$$ **Step 4 — Predict:** $$\hat{c} = \arg\max_c P(c \mid d) = c_- = \textbf{Negative}$$ > [!NOTE] > This toy example highlights **negation handling**: the word "not" inverts the polarity of "good", but the bag-of-words model treats it independently. Real systems require **negation scope detection** (e.g., "not good" → "NOT_good" as a single feature) or transformer-based contextual models. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Sentiment Approaches > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 4 — Comparison Matrix (high-yield for 14-mark answers):** | Criterion | Lexicon-Based | Machine Learning (NB/SVM) | Deep Learning (BERT) | |---|---|---|---| | **Data required** | None (uses dictionary) | Labeled corpus (10k+) | Large labeled corpus (100k+) | | **Domain adaptation** | Poor (lexicon bias) | Moderate (re-train) | Excellent (fine-tuning) | | **Handles negation** | Manual rules | Partial (with features) | Excellent (contextual) | | **Sarcasm detection** | Very poor | Poor | Good (with social media data) | | **Interpretability** | High (lexicon look-up) | Medium (feature weights) | Low (black-box attention) | | **Speed / Latency** | Fast (ms) | Medium (ms–s) | Slow (100ms+, GPU) | | **Best for** | Quick dashboards, low-resource | Domain-specific deployments | State-of-the-art accuracy | | **Example toolkit** | VADER, TextBlob | scikit-learn, NLTK | HuggingFace Transformers | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Opinion Retrieval & Sentiment Analysis Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Social Media Sources
Twitter, Facebook, Reddit] --> B[Data Acquisition
API Streaming or Crawling] B --> C[Preprocessing
Tokenize, Normalize, Emoticon] C --> D[Subjectivity Filter
NB or Lexicon-based] D --> E{Is Subjective?} E -- No --> F1[Discard / Mark Objective] E -- Yes --> G[Feature Extraction
BoW, TF-IDF, BERT] G --> H[Sentiment Classifier
Polarity and Intensity] H --> I[Polarity Output
Positive, Negative, Neutral] H --> J[Emotion Output
Joy, Anger, Sadness, etc.] I --> K[Opinion-Relevant Score
Combine with BM25 and Credibility] J --> K K --> L[Final Ranked List
Top-k Opinionated Documents] F1 --> M[Background Knowledge Base
for Comparison] K --> M M --> N[Visualization Dashboard
Trends, Alerts, Reports] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Arrows follow the data flow. The diamond `E` is the critical decision point — the subjectivity filter acts as a **gatekeeper**. Without it, the system would return objective factual content for opinion queries, which is the central failure mode the TREC Blog Track first identified. ## 4.2 Three-Layer Architecture of Social Media Mining ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Layer1[Data Layer] L1A[Microblogs Twitter X] L1B[Social Networks Facebook LinkedIn] L1C[Media Sharing Instagram YouTube] L1D[Forums and Review Sites] end subgraph Layer2[Mining Layer] L2A[Text Mining NLP] L2B[Graph Mining Networks] L2C[Multimedia Mining Images Video] L2D[Trend and Anomaly Detection] end subgraph Layer3[Application Layer] L3A[Brand Monitoring] L3B[Recommendation Engines] L3C[Crisis and Disaster Response] L3D[Public Health Surveillance] end Layer1 --> Layer2 Layer2 --> Layer3 Layer3 --> L3E[Feedback Loop
Refine Mining Models] L3E --> Layer2 ``` ## 4.3 Sentiment Classification Decision Tree (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Input Text Document] --> Tok[Tokenize and POS Tag] Tok --> Lex[Lookup Tokens in Sentiment Lexicon] Lex --> Agg[Aggregate Polarity Scores] Agg --> Neg{Negation Scope
Detected?} Neg -- Yes --> Flip[Invert Local Polarity] Neg -- No --> Sum[Weighted Sum of Scores] Flip --> Sum Sum --> Thresh{Polarity Magnitude
vs Threshold} Thresh -- Greater than Thresh Pos --> Pos[Classify as Positive] Thresh -- Greater than Thresh Neg --> Neg2[Classify as Negative] Thresh -- Otherwise --> Neu[Classify as Neutral] ``` ## 4.4 Opinion-Relevance Scoring Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Inputs Q[Query q] D[Document d] Auth[Author Credibility C] end subgraph Engines BM25[BM25 Engine
Topical Relevance R q d] Subj[Subjectivity Classifier] Sent[Sentiment Engine S d] end Comb[Linear Combiner
O q d equals alpha R plus beta S plus gamma C] Q --> BM25 D --> BM25 D --> Subj D --> Sent Auth --> Comb BM25 --> Comb Subj --> Comb Sent --> Comb Comb --> Out[Final Opinion-Aware Score] ``` > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark question asking to "design an opinion retrieval system", a labeled block diagram like the one above, combined with the linear combination formula and a worked example, is a near-guaranteed full-marks answer. Use this template. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model] **Differentiate between Subjective and Objective documents in the context of opinion retrieval. Why is this distinction important for an IR system?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** *Subjective documents* express personal opinions, emotions, judgments, or evaluations (e.g., "This phone is fantastic!"). *Objective documents* present factual, verifiable information without personal bias (e.g., "The phone has a 6.5-inch display."). - **(1 mark)** The distinction is critical because in opinion retrieval, the user's intent is to find *opinions about* an entity, not just any text *mentioning* the entity.
