Returning objective documents pollutes the result set. - **(1 mark)** Hence, IR systems for opinion queries must apply a **subjectivity classifier** (typically Naive Bayes or lexicon-based) as a filtering gate *before* ranking, ensuring only opinion-bearing documents contribute to the relevance score. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model] **List and briefly explain any three challenges specific to social media mining.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per challenge):** 1. **Volume and Velocity:** Billions of posts daily; streaming infrastructure (Kafka, Flume) is mandatory; offline processing is infeasible. 2. **Noise and Veracity:** Typos, slang, abbreviations, sarcasm, bots, and spam degrade signal quality; preprocessing and credibility scoring are essential. 3. **Short and Unstructured Text:** 280-character limits truncate context; lack of co-references and disambiguation increases NLP difficulty (e.g., "Apple" the fruit vs. the company). 4. *(Bonus 4th — only if asked:)* **Ethical and Privacy Concerns:** GDPR, user consent, and data anonymization complicate collection. ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Model] **(a)** Explain the architecture of an Opinion Retrieval system. Discuss the role of the subjectivity classifier and how it integrates with the topical relevance module. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the linear opinion-relevance score $O(q, d) = \alpha R(q, d) + \beta S(d) + \gamma C(d)$. Using a small worked example with three documents, demonstrate the ranking for query "electric vehicles". **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Architecture of an Opinion Retrieval System (7 marks)** An Opinion Retrieval system extends classical ad-hoc IR by combining three signals: **topical relevance, opinion/sentiment, and source credibility.** Its architecture (refer to Section 4.1 and 4.4) has five stages: | Stage | Component | Function | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Crawler / Streamer** | Collects tweets, posts, reviews via API or crawler | | 2 | **Preprocessor** | Tokenizes, normalizes, removes stopwords, handles emojis | | 3 | **Subjectivity Classifier** | Filters objective documents (binary or probabilistic) | | 4 | **Topical Relevance Engine** | Computes $R(q,d)$ using BM25 or LM | | 5 | **Sentiment Classifier** | Assigns polarity $S(d) \in [-1, +1]$ | **Role of the Subjectivity Classifier (3 marks sub-allocation):** - It acts as a **gatekeeper** between indexing and ranking. - It uses a labeled corpus (e.g., MPQA) and supervised ML (SVM) or lexicon (e.g., OpinionFinder). - Documents flagged objective are demoted or excluded from opinion queries. - It bridges **IR and NLP**: traditional IR assumes topicality; subjectivity adds the *affective* dimension. **Integration (2 marks):** The classifier outputs a binary decision or probability $P(\text{subj}\mid d)$ which is multiplied with the topical score: $$O(q,d) = R(q,d) \times P(\text{subj}\mid d) \times S(d)$$ or added linearly with weights, ensuring that **only subjective, topically-relevant, opinion-bearing documents** rise to the top. --- **Part (b) — Derivation and Worked Example (7 marks)** **Derivation (3 marks):** The opinion-relevance score must satisfy three desiderata: 1. High when $d$ is topically relevant to $q$ → $\propto R(q,d)$ 2. High when $d$ expresses a *strong* opinion → $\propto \vert S(d) \vert$ 3. High when author is credible → $\propto C(d)$ A linear combination is the simplest convex aggregation: $$O(q,d) = \alpha \cdot R(q,d) + \beta \cdot S(d) + \gamma \cdot C(d)$$ subject to $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$ with $\alpha, \beta, \gamma \geq 0$ (weights sum to unity so scores remain comparable across queries). **Worked Example (4 marks):** Using the same setup as Section 3.1 (Documents $d_1, d_2, d_3$; query "electric vehicles"; $\alpha = 0.5, \beta = 0.4, \gamma = 0.1$): $$O(q, d_1) = 0.5(0.92) + 0.4(0.85) + 0.1(0.70)$$ $$O(q, d_1) = 0.46 + 0.34 + 0.07 = \mathbf{0.87}$$ $$O(q, d_2) = 0 \;\;(\text{filtered — objective})$$ $$O(q, d_3) = 0.5(0.85) + 0.4(-0.72) + 0.1(0.55)$$ $$O(q, d_3) = 0.425 - 0.288 + 0.055 = \mathbf{0.192}$$ **Final Ranked Opinion-Aware List:** $$\boxed{d_1 \;(0.87) \;\succ\; d_3 \;(0.19) \;\succ\; d_2 \;(\text{objective — discarded})}$$ **Interpretation:** $d_1$, a strongly positive review, ranks first. $d_3$, a strongly negative review, ranks second because it is still opinion-bearing and topically relevant. $d_2$, despite high topical relevance (0.78), is excluded because it carries no opinion. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **(a)** Compare lexicon-based, machine learning, and deep learning approaches to sentiment analysis. Discuss the trade-offs in terms of data requirements, accuracy, interpretability, and handling of context. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the Naive Bayes sentiment classifier. Given the conditional probabilities from a small training set, classify the test sentence "good not bad" as positive or negative with full mathematical steps. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Comparison of Approaches (7 marks)** Refer to the **Analytical Matrix in Section 3.3** of these notes. The examiner expects a structured 3-column or 3-row comparison covering: | Dimension | Lexicon | ML (NB/SVM) | Deep Learning (BERT) | |---|---|---|---| | **Data Need** | None | 10k+ labeled | 100k+ labeled | | **Accuracy** | 60–75% | 75–85% | 88–95% (SOTA) | | **Interpretability** | High | Medium | Low (attention visualization helps) | | **Negation / Sarcasm** | Rules only | Partial | Strong | | **Speed** | Real-time | Fast | GPU-bound, slower | | **Best Use** | Dashboards, low-resource | Custom domains | High-stakes deployments | **Key Trade-off Discussion (3 marks):** - Lexicon-based is *cheap and explainable* but fails on domain-specific slang (e.g., "sick" = positive in Gen-Z). - Classical ML is the *sweet spot* for many KTU exam scenarios — fast, requires modest data, and well-understood. - Deep learning gives *contextual understanding* (BERT handles polysemy and negation naturally) but is opaque, slow, and data-hungry — unsuitable for streaming social media dashboards. --- **Part (b) — Naive Bayes Sentiment Classifier (7 marks)** **Theory (3 marks):** Naive Bayes applies Bayes' theorem with the *naive* assumption of conditional independence between features (words) given the class. $$P(c \mid d) = \frac{P(c) \cdot P(d \mid c)}{P(d)} \propto P(c) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(t_i \mid c)$$ **Worked Computation (4 marks):** Using the toy data from Section 3.2: $$P(d \mid c_+) = 0.30 \times 0.10 \times 0.05 = 0.0015$$ $$P(d \mid c_-) = 0.05 \times 0.15 \times 0.25 = 0.001875$$ With equal priors ($P(c_+) = P(c_-) = 0.5$): $$P(c_+ \mid d) \propto 0.5 \times 0.0015 = 0.00075$$ $$P(c_- \mid d) \propto 0.5 \times 0.001875 = 0.0009375$$ Normalized: $$P(c_+ \mid d) = \frac{0.00075}{0.0016875} \approx 0.444 \quad ; \quad P(c_- \mid d) \approx 0.556$$ **Prediction:** $\hat{c} = c_- = $ **Negative** (note that the negation "not" inverts the positive "good", but the bag-of-words assumption fails to model this compositionality — a classic limitation). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Module 4:** > 1. **Conflating Sentiment Analysis with Opinion Mining** — Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you treat them as identical without explaining the nuance (extraction vs. classification). > 2. **Forgetting the Subjectivity Filter** — When asked to "design an opinion retrieval system", students often jump straight to sentiment polarity. Always include a **subjectivity classifier** before the sentiment classifier. > 3. **Missing the Linear Combination Weights** — Writing $O(q,d) = R + S + C$ without $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ loses 1 mark. Always include the weighting constraint $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$. > 4. **Skipping Normalization in Naive Bayes** — Computing $P(c \mid d)$ without normalizing the posterior loses the final 1 mark in the worked example. > 5. **Ignoring Negation & Sarcasm** — In comparison questions, students often list pros/cons but forget to discuss **negation handling** — examiners explicitly test this. > 6. **No Diagram** — A 14-mark question on architecture without a block diagram is capped at 10–11 marks. Always include a labeled flowchart. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (Social Media Mining & Opinion Retrieval):** - 🔹 **Social Media Mining** = extracting patterns from UGC on social platforms; pillars are *Data, Algorithms, Human Knowledge*. - 🔹 **5 V's of Big Data** apply: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value. - 🔹 **Opinion Retrieval** = IR task retrieving documents that express opinion about a query (vs. just topically relevant). - 🔹 **Sentiment Analysis** = classification of polarity (Pos/Neg/Neutral) or fine-grained emotions (joy, anger, sadness, fear, surprise, disgust). - 🔹 **Subjectivity vs. Objectivity** is the foundational dichotomy — subjective text carries opinion, objective text is factual. - 🔹 **Pipeline:** Crawl → Preprocess → Subjectivity Filter → Feature Extract → Classify → Rank. - 🔹 **Three paradigms:** Lexicon-based (VADER, SentiWordNet), Classical ML (NB, SVM), Deep Learning (BERT). - 🔹 **Key Formula:** $O(q,d) = \alpha R(q,d) + \beta S(d) + \gamma C(d)$ — the linear opinion-relevance combination. - 🔹 **Naive Bayes posterior:** $P(c \mid d) \propto P(c) \prod_i P(t_i \mid c)$ with **Laplace smoothing** for unseen words. - 🔹 **Evaluation metrics:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, **F1-score** (most important for imbalanced sentiment classes). - 🔹 **Key challenges:** Sarcasm, negation, domain adaptation, bot/spam filtering, ethical/privacy concerns (GDPR). - 🔹 **Applications:** Brand monitoring, election prediction, financial forecasting, healthcare surveillance, crisis response. - 🔹 **Always include a labeled block diagram** in 14-mark architecture questions — examiners reward visual structure. - 🔹 **Always normalize** posterior probabilities in Naive Bayes worked examples. - 🔹 **Always mention weights and their constraints** in opinion scoring formulas.

topics.title: Sentiment Analysis and Opinion Mining Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sentiment Analysis and Opinion Mining Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sentiment Analysis (SA)**, also known as **Opinion Mining (OM)**, is the computational study of opinions, sentiments, evaluations, attitudes, appraisals, and emotions expressed in natural language text. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, it is a sub-field of **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** and **Information Retrieval (IR)** that systematically identifies, extracts, quantifies, and studies affective states and subjective information from textual data sources.

**Opinion Mining** is generally treated as the broader umbrella discipline that encompasses the process of detecting, classifying, and summarizing opinions from unstructured text, while Sentiment Analysis focuses more narrowly on the **polarity classification** (positive, negative, neutral) of expressed feelings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the 2024 scheme, the module explicitly couples "Sentiment Analysis" with "Opinion Mining Techniques." Examiners expect students to differentiate between the two and identify their role in modern IR pipelines such as search engines, recommender systems, and social media analytics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a restaurant that has **5,000 reviews** on Google. You obviously cannot read every single one. What if an intelligent assistant could:
1. Read all 5,000 reviews for you,
2. Identify who liked the food and who disliked it,
3. Tell you **why** they liked or disliked it (taste, service, ambiance, price),
4. Give you a single summary score?

**That assistant is essentially running Sentiment Analysis and Opinion Mining.**

In technical terms:
- **Sentiment Analysis** answers: *"How does the person FEEL?"* (Positive / Negative / Neutral)
- **Opinion Mining** answers: *"What exactly are they talking about, and what is their judgment on that specific aspect?"*

Think of it like this:
> A customer writes: *"The pizza was amazing, but the delivery was terribly slow."*
> - **Sentiment Analysis** might give a mixed/neutral score because both positive and negative emotions exist.
> - **Opinion Mining** breaks this into:
>   - Target: *pizza* → Opinion: *amazing* (Positive)
>   - Target: *delivery* → Opinion: *terribly slow* (Negative)

This **aspect-level granularity** is what distinguishes modern opinion mining from simple sentiment polarity detection.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU High-Yield Definitions)

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Polarity** | The directional orientation of sentiment: Positive, Negative, or Neutral |
| **Subjectivity** | Text expressing personal opinions, feelings, or beliefs (vs. objective facts) |
| **Aspect (Feature)** | The specific attribute or entity component being evaluated |
| **Opinion Holder** | The person or entity expressing the opinion |
| **Opinion Target** | The object or entity about which the opinion is expressed |
| **Document-Level SA** | Classifies sentiment of an entire document as one polarity |
| **Sentence-Level SA** | Classifies sentiment of each individual sentence |
| **Aspect-Level SA** | Identifies sentiment toward specific aspects/entities within text |
| **Lexicon** | A predefined dictionary of words tagged with sentiment scores |

> [!NOTE]
> **Levels of Sentiment Analysis** (in increasing difficulty):
> 1. **Document Level** → Whole document has one sentiment.
> 2. **Sentence Level** → Each sentence is classified.
> 3. **Aspect/Entity Level** → Sentiment tied to specific features of an entity.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used for Evaluation

The following metrics are universally used to evaluate sentiment classifiers and are **directly examinable** in KTU assessments:

- **Accuracy** — Proportion of correctly classified instances over total instances.
- **Precision** — Fraction of relevant instances among retrieved instances.
- **Recall** — Fraction of relevant instances that were retrieved.
- **F1-Score** — Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall.
- **Confusion Matrix** — Tabular representation of TP, FP, TN, FN predictions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sentiment Analysis Pipeline (Operational Workflow)

A standard sentiment analysis system, as conceptualized under the **KTU 2024 IR Module 4**, follows a structured 5-stage pipeline:

1. **Data Acquisition / Collection**
   - Crawl data from sources: social media (Twitter/X), product reviews (Amazon), news comments, blogs.
   - In IR context: query-relevant documents may be filtered through a sentiment lens.

2. **Text Pre-processing**
   - Tokenization, stop-word removal, stemming, lemmatization.
   - Noise removal: URLs, emojis (sometimes retained as signals!), HTML tags.
   - Lowercasing, punctuation handling.

3. **Feature Engineering / Representation**
   - Bag-of-Words (BoW), TF-IDF, Word Embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe), BERT embeddings.
   - Sentiment lexicons: VADER, SentiWordNet, AFINN.

4. **Sentiment Classification / Mining**
   - **Lexicon-based scoring** (rule-based)
   - **Machine Learning classifiers** (Naïve Bayes, SVM, Logistic Regression)
   - **Deep Learning models** (LSTM, BERT, Transformer-based)
   - **Hybrid approaches**

5. **Evaluation & Aggregation**
   - Apply evaluation metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1).
   - Aspect-based aggregation for opinion summarization.

## 2.2 Core Techniques (Theory Breakdown)

### A. Lexicon-Based Approach
- Uses a **predefined dictionary** where each word has a polarity score.
- Aggregate score of words in a sentence = sentence sentiment.
- **Advantages:** Simple, interpretable, no training data needed.
- **Disadvantages:** Cannot handle sarcasm, context, domain shift.

**Example Lexicons:**
- **AFINN**: Words scored from -5 to +5.
- **VADER (Valence Aware Dictionary and sEntiment Reasoner)**: Specifically tuned for social media.
- **SentiWordNet**: Each WordNet synset assigned positivity, negativity, objectivity scores.

### B. Machine Learning Approach
- Treats sentiment classification as a **supervised text classification problem**.
- Requires labeled training data.
- **Common algorithms:**
  - Naïve Bayes (probabilistic, fast baseline)
  - Support Vector Machines (SVM) — strong for high-dimensional sparse text data
  - Logistic Regression
  - Random Forest, Gradient Boosting

### C. Deep Learning Approach
- Uses neural networks: CNN, RNN, LSTM, GRU, Transformers (BERT, RoBERTa).
- Captures sequential and contextual information.
- State-of-the-art in modern benchmarks.

### D. Hybrid Approach
- Combines lexicon + ML/DL.
- Example: Lexicon-based features + neural network classifier.

## 2.3 The Aspect-Based Opinion Mining Model (Liu's Model)

According to the widely accepted academic model by **Bing Liu**, an opinion is a quintuple:

$$(e, a, s, h, t)$$

Where:
- $e$ = **Entity** (the object being reviewed)
- $a$ = **Aspect** of the entity (a feature/attribute)
- $s$ = **Sentiment** (positive, negative, neutral — or a numeric score)
- $h$ = **Opinion Holder** (the person/entity giving the opinion)
- $t$ = **Time** (when the opinion was expressed)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This quintuple formulation is **highly examinable** in KTU 2024 Part B questions. Examiners frequently ask students to extract this quintuple from sample review sentences.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Purpose / Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness of classifier |
| **Precision** | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Quality of positive predictions |
| **Recall** | $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives |
| **F1-Score** | $F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Balanced single metric |
| **Macro F1** | $F1_{\text{macro}} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} F1_i$ | Average F1 across all classes |
| **Lexicon Polarity Score** | $S_{doc} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$ | Sum of weighted word scores |
| **TF-IDF Weight** | $w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \cdot \log \dfrac{N}{df_t}$ | Feature weight for term $t$ in doc $d$ |
| **Sigmoid (Logistic)** | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Probability output for binary SA |
| **Cosine Similarity (IR)** | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\vert\vec{A}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{B}\vert}$ | Document similarity in vector space |
| **Opinion Quintuple** | $(e, a, s, h, t)$ | Liu's opinion representation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Sentiment Analysis powers real-world IR systems at companies like Amazon (product review summarization), Twitter/X (trend detection), and Google (search result re-ranking by sentiment). It is the backbone of **brand monitoring**, **political analysis**, **financial forecasting**, and **customer experience management**.

## 2.5 Key Challenges in Sentiment Analysis

| Challenge | Explanation | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Sarcasm & Irony** | Literal words convey opposite meaning | "Great, another Monday!" (Negative) |
| **Negation Handling** | "not good" reverses sentiment | "The movie was not good" (Negative) |
| **Context Dependency** | Same word, different sentiment in domains | "Unpredictable plot" — bad for thriller, good for romance |
| **Domain Adaptation** | Lexicons trained on one domain fail in another | "Small size" — bad for laptop, good for phone |
| **Multilingualism** | Code-mixed text (Hinglish, Manglish) | "Movie was super bakwas" |
| **Comparative Sentences** | "X is better than Y" requires entity linking | "iPhone is better than Samsung" |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Lexicon-Based Sentiment Scoring

Given the following **mini lexicon** and a sample review, compute the overall sentiment polarity.

**Mini Lexicon:**
| Word | Score |
|---|---|
| amazing | +3 |
| love | +3 |
| terrible | -3 |
| slow | -2 |
| okay | +1 |
| worst | -3 |
| great | +2 |

**Sample Review:** *"The food was amazing, but the service was terribly slow."*

**Step-by-Step Computation:**

**Step 1:** Tokenize and look up each opinion-bearing word in the lexicon.

$$S = \{\text{amazing: } +3, \text{terribly: } -3, \text{slow: } -2\}$$

**Step 2:** Sum the individual polarity scores to obtain the overall document sentiment score.

$$S_{doc} = (+3) + (-3) + (-2) = -2$$

**Step 3:** Interpret the result using a threshold-based decision rule (commonly $\pm 1$ or $\pm 2$).

- If $S_{doc} > 0$ → **Positive**
- If $S_{doc} < 0$ → **Negative**
- If $S_{doc} = 0$ → **Neutral**

$$S_{doc} = -2 < 0 \implies \textbf{Negative Overall Sentiment}$$

**Step 4:** Apply **aspect-level breakdown** for granular opinion mining.

| Entity | Aspect | Opinion Word | Polarity | Score |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Restaurant | food | amazing | Positive | +3 |
| Restaurant | service | terribly slow | Negative | -5 |

This demonstrates that while the **overall document sentiment** may be classified as negative, **aspect-level opinion mining** reveals that the customer liked the food but disliked the service — a far more actionable insight.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Opinion Quintuple Extraction

**Review Text:** *"On 15th March 2024, Priya said that the battery life of the new PhoneX is excellent."*

**Step 1:** Identify the **Entity**.

$$e = \text{PhoneX}$$

**Step 2:** Identify the **Aspect / Feature**.

$$a = \text{battery life}$$

**Step 3:** Identify the **Sentiment**.

$$s = \text{Positive}$$

**Step 4:** Identify the **Opinion Holder**.

$$h = \text{Priya}$$

**Step 5:** Identify the **Time**.

$$t = \text{15th March 2024}$$

**Step 6:** Assemble the quintuple.

$$(e, a, s, h, t) = (\text{PhoneX}, \text{battery life}, \text{Positive}, \text{Priya}, \text{15 March 2024})$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Evaluation Metrics from Confusion Matrix

A Naïve Bayes sentiment classifier is evaluated on **200 test reviews**. The resulting confusion matrix is:

|  | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | 80 (TP) | 20 (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | 10 (FP) | 90 (TN) |

**Step 1:** Identify the four cardinal values.

$$TP = 80, \quad FN = 20, \quad FP = 10, \quad TN = 90$$

**Step 2:** Compute **Accuracy**.

$$\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \dfrac{80 + 90}{80 + 90 + 10 + 20} = \dfrac{170}{200} = 0.85$$

Therefore, $\text{Accuracy} = \mathbf{85\%}$.

**Step 3:** Compute **Precision**.

$$\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{80}{80 + 10} = \dfrac{80}{90} \approx 0.8889$$

Therefore, $\text{Precision} \approx \mathbf{88.89\%}$.

**Step 4:** Compute **Recall**.

$$\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{80}{80 + 20} = \dfrac{80}{100} = 0.80$$

Therefore, $\text{Recall} = \mathbf{80\%}$.

**Step 5:** Compute **F1-Score**.

$$F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.8889 \cdot 0.80}{0.8889 + 0.80}$$

$$F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.7111}{1.6889} \approx 0.8421$$

Therefore, $F1 \approx \mathbf{84.21\%}$.

**Interpretation:** The classifier has **high precision (88.89%)**, meaning when it predicts positive, it is usually correct. However, its **recall (80%)** is lower, indicating it misses 20% of actual positive reviews — a common trade-off in sentiment classification.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Sigmoid Probability for Binary Sentiment

A logistic regression model outputs a raw score $z = 1.85$ for a given review. Compute the probability of it being classified as Positive.

**Step 1:** Apply the **sigmoid function**.

$$\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $z = 1.85$.

$$\sigma(1.85) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-1.85}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the exponential term.

$$e^{-1.85} \approx 0.1572$$

**Step 4:** Compute the final probability.

$$\sigma(1.85) = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.1572} = \dfrac{1}{1.1572} \approx 0.8642$$

**Step 5:** Apply the decision rule (threshold = 0.5).

$$P(\text{Positive}) = 0.8642 > 0.5 \implies \textbf{Classified as Positive}$$

## 3.5 Comparative Analysis Matrix: Technique Selection

| Technique | Data Required | Interpretability | Handles Context | Training Cost | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lexicon-based** | None | High | Low | None | Quick prototyping, small-scale IR |
| **Naïve Bayes** | Labeled corpus | Medium | Low | Low | Baseline sentiment classification |
| **SVM** | Labeled corpus | Low | Medium | Medium | High-dimensional sparse text |
| **LSTM / RNN** | Large labeled corpus | Low | High | High | Sequential sentiment patterns |
| **BERT / Transformers** | Very large corpus | Very Low | Very High | Very High | State-of-the-art accuracy |
| **Hybrid (Lexicon + ML)** | Labeled + lexicon | Medium | Medium | Medium | Balanced performance & cost |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sentiment Analysis System Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following diagram illustrates the **end-to-end functional architecture** of a sentiment analysis system as it integrates with an Information Retrieval pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Data Sources] --> B[Text Preprocessing Module]
    B --> B1[Tokenization]
    B --> B2[Stopword Removal]
    B --> B3[Stemming and Lemmatization]
    B --> C[Feature Extraction Layer]
    C --> C1[TF-IDF Vectorizer]
    C --> C2[Word Embeddings GloVe BERT]
    C --> C3[Sentiment Lexicon Lookup]
    C --> D[Sentiment Classification Engine]
    D --> D1[Lexicon Based Scorer]
    D --> D2[Machine Learning Classifier]
    D --> D3[Deep Learning LSTM Transformer]
    D --> E[Sentiment Output Layer]
    E --> E1[Polarity Label Positive Negative Neutral]
    E --> E2[Aspect Level Opinion Extraction]
    E --> E3[Confidence Score Probability]
    E --> F[Evaluation and Validation Module]
    F --> F1[Accuracy Metric]
    F --> F2[Precision and Recall]
    F --> F3[F1 Score Computation]
    F --> G[IR System Integration]
    G --> G1[Sentiment Aware Ranking]
    G --> G2[Review Summarization]
    G --> G3[Recommendation Engine]
```

## 4.2 Aspect-Based Opinion Mining Workflow

The following diagram depicts the workflow for **extracting structured opinion quintuples** from unstructured review text.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Input Review Text] --> S1[Sentence Segmentation]
    S1 --> S2[Named Entity Recognition]
    S2 --> T1[Identify Entity E]
    S1 --> T2[Identify Aspect A]
    S2 --> T3[Identify Opinion Holder H]
    S1 --> T4[Identify Time T]
    S1 --> O1[Opinion Word Extraction]
    O1 --> O2[Polarity Lookup via Lexicon]
    O2 --> Q[Assemble Quintuple E A S H T]
    Q --> OUT[Structured Opinion Database]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Decision Flow: Choosing a Sentiment Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[New Sentiment Analysis Task] --> Q1{Is Labeled Data Available?}
    Q1 -- No --> LEX[Use Lexicon Based Approach]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is Interpretability Required?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> ML[Use Traditional ML Naive Bayes SVM Logistic Regression]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Dataset Size?}
    Q3 -- Small --> ML
    Q3 -- Large --> DL[Use Deep Learning LSTM BERT Transformer]
    LEX --> EVAL[Evaluate via Accuracy F1]
    ML --> EVAL
    DL --> EVAL
    EVAL --> DEPLOY[Deploy to IR Pipeline]
```

## 4.4 Sentiment-Aware Information Retrieval Architecture

This diagram shows how sentiment analysis is integrated into a modern search/recommendation system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[User Query] --> SE[Search Engine Module]
    SE --> DOC[Document Retrieval]
    DOC --> SA[Sentiment Analysis Filter]
    SA --> SA1[Classify Document Polarity]
    SA1 --> SA2[Aspect Based Scoring]
    SA2 --> RANK[Sentiment Aware Ranking Algorithm]
    RANK --> R1[Boost Positive Relevant Documents]
    RANK --> R2[Demote Negative Off Topic Documents]
    R1 --> RES[Final Ranked Results]
    R2 --> RES
    RES --> USER[Display to User]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The above diagrams illustrate the **block-level functional flow** of how sentiment analysis operates as a downstream analytical layer of an Information Retrieval system. Each module performs a discrete, testable function, and the entire pipeline is modular — meaning each block can be replaced or upgraded independently (e.g., swapping a lexicon-based scorer for a BERT-based deep learning classifier without altering the rest of the pipeline).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Sentiment Analysis. Differentiate between document-level, sentence-level, and aspect-level sentiment analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

**Sentiment Analysis** is the computational process of identifying, extracting, and classifying opinions, emotions, and attitudes expressed in natural language text into categories such as **positive, negative, or neutral**.

**Differentiation Table:**

| Level | Granularity | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Document-Level** | Whole document → single polarity | A product review classified as Positive overall |
| **Sentence-Level** | Each sentence → individual polarity | "Phone is great." (Positive) / "Battery drains fast." (Negative) |
| **Aspect-Level** | Specific features/attributes | Entity: Phone → Aspect: camera → Positive; Aspect: battery → Negative |

Aspect-level is the **most granular** and is widely used in modern opinion mining for detailed feedback analysis. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain three challenges in sentiment analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Sarcasm & Irony Detection:** Literal text conveys opposite meaning. Example: "Oh great, my flight got cancelled!" appears positive but is negative.
2. **Negation Handling:** Words like "not", "never" reverse polarity. "The movie was not good" is negative despite "good" being positive.
3. **Domain Adaptation:** Sentiment lexicons trained on one domain (e.g., movie reviews) often fail in another (e.g., medical reviews), as the same word carries different sentiment strength across domains.

**(3 marks — 1 mark each for naming and 2-mark depth via brief explanation)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Students often confuse "Sentiment Analysis" with "Opinion Mining" as synonyms. They are **related but distinct** — SA focuses on polarity, while OM extracts structured opinions including targets, holders, and aspects. **Always define both separately.**
> - **Mistake 2:** When asked for the opinion quintuple, students forget to include the **Time ($t$)** component. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for incomplete quintuples.
> - **Mistake 3:** In metric calculation questions, students frequently mix up Precision and Recall formulas. Memorize: **Precision = TP / (TP + FP)** and **Recall = TP / (TP + FN)**.
> - **Mistake 4:** Students often describe techniques without explaining **why** a technique is suitable for a particular scenario — KTU 2024 scheme demands **justification**, not just enumeration.

---

### **Question B1 (Option A)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Modified]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain in detail the Lexicon-Based approach to sentiment analysis. List its advantages, disadvantages, and any two popular sentiment lexicons.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Lexicon-Based Approach** uses a predefined dictionary (lexicon) where each word is associated with a **polarity score** (positive, negative) or a numerical weight. The sentiment of a document is computed by aggregating the scores of all opinion-bearing words it contains.

**Steps Involved:**
1. **Lexicon Construction/Selection:** Choose or build a sentiment dictionary (AFINN, VADER, SentiWordNet).
2. **Tokenization:** Split the text into individual words.
3. **Score Aggregation:** Sum the scores of sentiment words present in the text.
4. **Polarity Classification:** Apply a threshold to classify overall sentiment as Positive, Negative, or Neutral.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$S_{doc} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i$$

Where $s_i$ is the sentiment score of the $i^{th}$ word in the document, and $n$ is the total number of matched lexicon words.

**Two Popular Lexicons:**
- **AFINN:** Word scores range from -5 to +5; widely used for short texts.
- **VADER:** Specifically tuned for social media; handles emoticons, slang, and intensifiers.

**Advantages:**
- Simple to implement; no training data required.
- Highly interpretable — one can trace which words contributed to the score.
- Fast computation, suitable for real-time applications.

**Disadvantages:**
- Cannot handle **sarcasm, irony, or context-dependent** sentiment.
- Fails across **domain shifts** (lexicon built for movies may not work for finance).
- Requires manual lexicon curation for specialized domains.

**(7 marks — 3 marks for explanation, 2 marks for lexicons, 2 marks for advantages/disadvantages)**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Consider the review: "The camera is awesome but the price is too high. Battery life is decent." Perform aspect-based opinion mining and extract the opinion quintuples. Justify your polarity decisions.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Identify opinion-bearing sentences and their targets.

The review contains three aspect-opinion pairs:
1. *camera → awesome*
2. *price → too high*
3. *battery life → decent*

**Step 2:** Apply a sentiment lexicon lookup (assume standard scores: awesome = +3, too high = -2, decent = +1).

**Step 3:** Extract structured quintuples $(e, a, s, h, t)$.

| # | Entity ($e$) | Aspect ($a$) | Sentiment ($s$) | Holder ($h$) | Time ($t$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Phone | camera | Positive (+3) | Anonymous user | Implied (current review) |
| 2 | Phone | price | Negative (-2) | Anonymous user | Implied |
| 3 | Phone | battery life | Neutral/Positive (+1) | Anonymous user | Implied |

**Justification:**
- *"awesome"* carries strong positive connotation → classified as **Positive**.
- *"too high"* for a price is undesirable → classified as **Negative**.
- *"decent"* indicates mild satisfaction → classified as **Neutral to slightly Positive**.

**Final Assembled Quintuples:**
$$(e_1, a_1, s_1, h_1, t_1) = (\text{Phone}, \text{camera}, \text{Positive}, \text{User}, t)$$
$$(e_2, a_2, s_2, h_2, t_2) = (\text{Phone}, \text{price}, \text{Negative}, \text{User}, t)$$
$$(e_3, a_3, s_3, h_3, t_3) = (\text{Phone}, \text{battery life}, \text{Neutral}, \text{User}, t)$$

This aspect-level breakdown provides **far richer insight** than a simple document-level classification, which would have labeled the entire review as "Mixed" or "Neutral."

**(7 marks — 2 marks for identification, 3 marks for quintuple construction, 2 marks for justification)**

---

### **Question B1 (Option B)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Modified]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the Machine Learning approach to sentiment analysis. Compare Naïve Bayes and Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers in this context.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Machine Learning (ML) approach** treats sentiment analysis as a **supervised text classification** problem. Given a labeled training corpus (e.g., 10,000 movie reviews tagged as positive/negative), the ML model learns patterns and generalizes to unseen reviews.

**General Pipeline:**
1. **Data Collection:** Gather labeled review data.
2. **Preprocessing:** Tokenization, stopword removal, stemming.
3. **Feature Extraction:** Convert text to numerical vectors (BoW, TF-IDF, embeddings).
4. **Model Training:** Train a classifier on labeled data.
5. **Prediction & Evaluation:** Predict sentiment for unseen reviews; measure Accuracy, F1.

**Naïve Bayes (NB):**
- Based on Bayes' Theorem with the assumption of feature independence.
- Formula:

$$P(c \mid d) = \dfrac{P(d \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(d)}$$

Where $c$ = class (Positive/Negative) and $d$ = document.
- **Fast**, works well on small datasets, good baseline.
- Assumption of independence is often violated in real text but still yields strong results.

**Support Vector Machine (SVM):**
- Finds the **optimal hyperplane** that maximally separates classes in high-dimensional feature space.
- Highly effective for **high-dimensional sparse text data** (e.g., TF-IDF vectors).
- Often outperforms Naïve Bayes in modern sentiment benchmarks.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Naïve Bayes | SVM |
|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | Very Fast | Slower |
| **Accuracy on Text** | Good (baseline) | Often Superior |
| **Interpretability** | High (probabilistic) | Low (geometric) |
| **Data Requirement** | Works with small data | Needs more data |
| **High-Dim Performance** | Acceptable | Excellent |

**(7 marks — 3 marks for ML pipeline, 2 marks each for NB and SVM)**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A sentiment classifier is tested on 500 reviews. The confusion matrix is as follows: TP = 200, FP = 50, FN = 30, TN = 220. Compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. Interpret which metric is most suitable if both false positives and false negatives are equally costly.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Values:**

$$TP = 200, \quad FP = 50, \quad FN = 30, \quad TN = 220$$

**Step 1: Accuracy**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \dfrac{200 + 220}{200 + 220 + 50 + 30} = \dfrac{420}{500} = 0.84$$

Therefore, **Accuracy = 84%**.

**Step 2: Precision**

$$\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{200}{200 + 50} = \dfrac{200}{250} = 0.80$$

Therefore, **Precision = 80%**.

**Step 3: Recall**

$$\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{200}{200 + 30} = \dfrac{200}{230} \approx 0.8696$$

Therefore, **Recall ≈ 86.96%**.

**Step 4: F1-Score**

$$F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.80 \cdot 0.8696}{0.80 + 0.8696}$$

$$F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.6957}{1.6696} \approx 0.8333$$

Therefore, **F1-Score ≈ 83.33%**.

**Step 5: Interpretation**

When **both false positives and false negatives are equally costly**, the **F1-Score** is the most suitable metric because it balances Precision and Recall via their harmonic mean. Unlike Accuracy (which can be misleading in imbalanced datasets), the F1-Score penalizes classifiers that heavily favor one type of error. In this case, an F1 of **83.33%** indicates a well-balanced classifier.

**(7 marks — 1.5 marks each for the four metrics + 1 mark for interpretation)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Sentiment Analysis** = Polarity classification (Positive / Negative / Neutral).
- **Opinion Mining** = Broader; extracts structured opinions including targets and aspects.
- **Three Levels of SA:** Document → Sentence → Aspect (granularity increases).
- **Liu's Opinion Quintuple:** $(e, a, s, h, t)$ — **Entity, Aspect, Sentiment, Holder, Time**.
- **Lexicon-Based Approach:** Uses sentiment dictionaries (AFINN, VADER, SentiWordNet); simple, no training needed; fails on sarcasm.
- **ML-Based Approach:** Supervised classification using Naïve Bayes, SVM, Logistic Regression; requires labeled data.
- **Deep Learning Approach:** LSTM, BERT, Transformers; state-of-the-art accuracy but computationally expensive.
- **Evaluation Metrics:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score — F1 is preferred for imbalanced data.
- **Key Challenges:** Sarcasm, Negation, Context Dependency, Domain Adaptation, Multilingualism.
- **Real-World Applications:** Brand monitoring, product review mining, political sentiment, financial forecasting, IR result re-ranking, recommender systems.
- **Integration with IR:** Sentiment signals can re-rank search results, filter biased content, and enhance recommendation relevance.

> **Final Tip:** When answering KTU questions, always (1) **define the concept formally**, (2) **give a real-world example**, (3) **show a step-by-step working** for any computation, and (4) **conclude with engineering applicability**. This structure aligns with the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) marking scheme and consistently scores full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Recommendation System (RS)** is an information filtering subclass of Information Retrieval (IR) that predicts the **preference** or **rating** a user would assign to a set of items (documents, products, media). It is engineered to mitigate **information overload** by personalizing retrieval based on implicit/explicit user feedback signals.

**Collaborative Filtering (CF)** is the dominant recommendation paradigm that operates on the foundational assumption: *"Users who agreed in the past will agree in the future, and items that share user appreciation will be similarly liked."* CF relies purely on the **user-item interaction matrix** $\mathbf{R}$ and does not require domain knowledge of the items themselves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Lock:** As per Module 4 of PECDT745, the classification hierarchy is:
> **Recommendation Systems → Content-Based Filtering (CBF) | Collaborative Filtering (CF) | Hybrid Systems**
> Where CF is further bifurcated into **Memory-Based (Neighborhood)** and **Model-Based (Latent Factor)** approaches.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you walk into a new café in a city you have never visited. You do not know the menu, ingredients, or chef. However, you notice a long queue of people who look like they share your refined taste in coffee. You skip the menu, stand in that queue, and order what they are having. You have just performed **Collaborative Filtering** — you leveraged *similar people's collective wisdom* rather than analyzing the coffee itself (which would be Content-Based Filtering).

| Term | Coffee Shop Analogy | IR/R Meaning |
|------|---------------------|--------------|
| **User** $u$ | You, the customer | Querying agent / profile |
| **Item** $i$ | A specific coffee drink | Document / product |
| **Rating** $r_{u,i}$ | Star rating you would give | Implicit/Explicit feedback |
| **Neighborhood** | People in the queue | Top-K similar users/items |
| **Cold Start** | Empty café, no one to observe | New user/item with no history |
| **Sparsity** | Most tables are empty | Sparse user-item matrix |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of RS:**
> 1. **Who** is the user? (User Profiling)
> 2. **What** did they like? (Item Representation)
> 3. **Whom** are they similar to? (Similarity Computation)

## 1.3 Mathematical Setup — The User-Item Interaction Matrix

Let there be $m$ users $\mathcal{U} = \{u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_m\}$ and $n$ items $\mathcal{I} = \{i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_n\}$. The fundamental data structure of any RS is the rating matrix:

$$
\mathbf{R} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
r_{1,1} & r_{1,2} & \cdots & r_{1,n} \\
r_{2,1} & r_{2,2} & \cdots & r_{2,n} \\
\vdots  & \vdots  & \ddots & \vdots  \\
r_{m,1} & r_{m,2} & \cdots & r_{m,n}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Where each entry $r_{u,i}$ is either:
- An **observed rating** on a discrete scale (e.g., $r_{u,i} \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$), or
- A **missing value** (denoted as `?` or 0) representing non-observed user-item interaction.

The **sparsity level** of the matrix is defined as:

$$
\text{Sparsity} = 1 - \frac{\text{Number of observed ratings}}{\text{Total possible ratings}} = 1 - \frac{|\{(u,i) : r_{u,i} \neq \text{null}\}|}{m \times n}
$$

**Real-world sparsity** in commercial systems (Netflix, Amazon) typically exceeds **99%**, which is the central engineering challenge of CF.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Recommendation Approaches

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes recommendation algorithms into three principal families:

1. **Content-Based Filtering (CBF):** Recommends items similar in *features* to those a user has previously liked. Uses item profiles (TF-IDF vectors, embeddings) and user profiles (aggregated vectors of consumed items).
2. **Collaborative Filtering (CF):** Leverages the wisdom of the crowd. No content analysis needed.
   - **Memory-Based (Neighborhood) CF:** Computes similarity directly from $\mathbf{R}$.
     - User-User CF
     - Item-Item CF
   - **Model-Based CF:** Learns a compact latent model from $\mathbf{R}$.
     - Matrix Factorization (SVD, NMF, FunkSVD)
     - Probabilistic Latent Semantic Analysis (pLSA)
3. **Hybrid Systems:** Combine CBF + CF (e.g., weighted, switching, cascade, feature-augmentation).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Cosine Similarity (Users)** | $\text{sim}(u_a, u_b) = \dfrac{\sum_{i \in \mathcal{I}_{ab}} r_{a,i} r_{b,i}}{\sqrt{\sum_{i} r_{a,i}^2} \sqrt{\sum_{i} r_{b,i}^2}}$ | Treats users as vectors; ignores magnitude differences. Unitless in $[-1, 1]$. |
| 2 | **Adjusted Cosine Similarity (Items)** | $\text{sim}(i_a, i_b) = \dfrac{\sum_{u \in \mathcal{U}_{ab}} (r_{u,a} - \bar{r}_u)(r_{u,b} - \bar{r}_u)}{\sqrt{\sum_{u}(r_{u,a}-\bar{r}_u)^2}\sqrt{\sum_{u}(r_{u,b}-\bar{r}_u)^2}}$ | Subtracts user mean to remove rating bias. |
| 3 | **Pearson Correlation** | $\text{sim}(u_a, u_b) = \dfrac{\sum_{i \in \mathcal{I}_{ab}}(r_{a,i} - \bar{r}_a)(r_{b,i} - \bar{r}_b)}{\sqrt{\sum_{i}(r_{a,i} - \bar{r}_a)^2}\sqrt{\sum_{i}(r_{b,i} - \bar{r}_b)^2}}$ | Most common in KTU numericals. Captures linear correlation. |
| 4 | **Predicted Rating (User-User CF)** | $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \bar{r}_u + \dfrac{\sum_{v \in N_k(u)} \text{sim}(u,v) \cdot (r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum_{v \in N_k(u)} \vert \text{sim}(u,v) \vert}$ | $N_k(u)$ = Top-K neighbors of $u$. |
| 5 | **Predicted Rating (Item-Item CF)** | $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \dfrac{\sum_{j \in N_k(i)} \text{sim}(i,j) \cdot r_{u,j}}{\sum_{j \in N_k(i)} \vert \text{sim}(i,j) \vert}$ | $N_k(i)$ = Top-K similar items. |
| 6 | **SVD Decomposition** | $\mathbf{R} \approx \mathbf{U}_{m \times k} \, \mathbf{\Sigma}_{k \times k} \, \mathbf{V}_{n \times k}^T$ | $k$ is the latent dimension. |
| 7 | **Predicted Rating (Matrix Factorization)** | $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \mu + b_u + b_i + \mathbf{p}_u^T \mathbf{q}_i$ | $\mu$ = global mean, $b_u, b_i$ = biases, $\mathbf{p}_u, \mathbf{q}_i \in \mathbb{R}^k$. |
| 8 | **RMSE** | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{|\mathcal{T}|} \sum_{(u,i) \in \mathcal{T}} (r_{u,i} - \hat{r}_{u,i})^2}$ | Lower is better. Standard Netflix metric. |
| 9 | **MAE** | $\text{MAE} = \dfrac{1}{|\mathcal{T}|} \sum_{(u,i) \in \mathcal{T}} \vert r_{u,i} - \hat{r}_{u,i} \vert$ | Less sensitive to outliers than RMSE. |
| 10 | **Precision@K** | $\text{P@K} = \dfrac{\vert \text{Recommended} \cap \text{Relevant} \vert}{K}$ | Top-K retrieval accuracy. |
| 11 | **Recall@K** | $\text{R@K} = \dfrac{\vert \text{Recommended} \cap \text{Relevant} \vert}{\vert \text{Relevant} \vert}$ | Coverage of relevant items. |
| 12 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot \text{P} \cdot \text{R}}{\text{P} + \text{R}}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R. |
| 13 | **MAP (Mean Average Precision)** | $\text{MAP} = \dfrac{1}{m} \sum_{u=1}^{m} \dfrac{1}{\vert \mathcal{R}_u \vert} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \text{P@K}(u) \cdot \text{rel}(k)$ | Gold standard for ranked list quality. |
| 14 | **NDCG@K** | $\text{NDCG@K} = \dfrac{\text{DCG@K}}{\text{IDCG@K}}$, where $\text{DCG@K} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \dfrac{\text{rel}_k}{\log_2(k+1)}$ | Handles graded relevance. |
| 15 | **Sparsity** | $1 - \dfrac{\vert \mathcal{O} \vert}{m \times n}$ | $\mathcal{O}$ = observed entries. |
| 16 | **Coverage** | $\dfrac{\vert \text{Items recommended to at least one user} \vert}{n}$ | Catalog coverage. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** For numerical problems, expect Pearson correlation on 4×4 or 5×5 matrices, and one question on either User-User or Item-Item prediction using the weighted-neighbor formula. Always normalize using user mean $\bar{r}_u$ for Pearson.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: User-User Collaborative Filtering Prediction

We seek to predict the unobserved rating $r_{u,i}$. The intuition is to aggregate the *deviations* of the user's neighbors from their personal means, weighted by similarity.

### Step 1 — Compute User Means

For each user $v$, compute the average of all observed ratings:

$$
\bar{r}_v = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{I}_v|} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{I}_v} r_{v,j}
$$

where $\mathcal{I}_v$ is the set of items rated by user $v$.

### Step 2 — Compute Pearson Similarity

We measure the *linear correlation* between two users $u$ and $v$ over the co-rated items $\mathcal{I}_{uv} = \mathcal{I}_u \cap \mathcal{I}_v$:

$$
\text{sim}(u, v) = \frac{\sum_{j \in \mathcal{I}_{uv}} (r_{u,j} - \bar{r}_u)(r_{v,j} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sqrt{\sum_{j \in \mathcal{I}_{uv}} (r_{u,j} - \bar{r}_u)^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{j \in \mathcal{I}_{uv}} (r_{v,j} - \bar{r}_v)^2}}
$$

The numerator captures *covariance*, the denominator normalizes by *product of standard deviations* (Pearson normalization).

### Step 3 — Select Top-K Neighbors

$$
N_k(u) = \arg\max_{S \subset \mathcal{U}, |S|=k} \sum_{v \in S} \text{sim}(u,v)
$$

### Step 4 — Weighted Aggregation

The final prediction is the user's mean plus a similarity-weighted average of how neighbors deviated from their own means:

$$
\hat{r}_{u,i} = \bar{r}_u + \frac{\sum_{v \in N_k(u) \cap U_i} \text{sim}(u,v) \cdot (r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum_{v \in N_k(u) \cap U_i} \vert \text{sim}(u,v) \vert}
$$

Here $U_i$ is the set of users who have rated item $i$. The denominator normalizes so that predictions stay on the original rating scale.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

Consider the user-item rating matrix on a 1–5 scale where `0` means *not rated*:

| User | Item A | Item B | Item C | Item D |
|------|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|
| U1   |  5     |  3     |  0     |  1     |
| U2   |  4     |  0     |  0     |  1     |
| U3   |  1     |  1     |  0     |  5     |
| U4   |  0     |  3     |  4     |  4     |
| U5   |  2     |  0     |  5     |  4     |

**Target:** Predict $r_{U1, C}$ using User-User CF with Pearson correlation and $K=2$ neighbors.

### Step 1 — Compute Means

$$
\bar{r}_{U1} = \frac{5+3+1}{3} = 3.000
$$

$$
\bar{r}_{U2} = \frac{4+1}{2} = 2.500
$$

$$
\bar{r}_{U3} = \frac{1+1+5}{3} = 2.333
$$

$$
\bar{r}_{U4} = \frac{3+4+4}{3} = 3.667
$$

$$
\bar{r}_{U5} = \frac{2+5+4}{3} = 3.667
$$

### Step 2 — Co-Rated Items with U1

U1 has rated A, B, D. So we compute Pearson with U2, U3, U4, U5 using only these items.

**Common with U2:** {A, D} → $\mathcal{I}_{U1,U2} = \{A, D\}$

$$
\text{Num}_{U1,U2} = (5-3)(4-2.5) + (1-3)(1-2.5) = (2)(1.5) + (-2)(-1.5) = 3.0 + 3.0 = 6.0
$$

$$
\text{Den}_{U1,U2} = \sqrt{2^2 + (-2)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1.5^2 + (-1.5)^2} = \sqrt{8} \cdot \sqrt{4.5} = \sqrt{36} = 6.0
$$

$$
\text{sim}(U1, U2) = \frac{6.0}{6.0} = +1.000
$$

**Common with U3:** {A, B, D} → $\mathcal{I}_{U1,U3} = \{A, B, D\}$

$$
\text{Num} = (5-3)(1-2.333) + (3-3)(1-2.333) + (1-3)(5-2.333) = 2(-1.333) + 0 + (-2)(2.667)
$$

$$
= -2.667 + 0 - 5.333 = -8.000
$$

$$
\text{Den} = \sqrt{2^2 + 0^2 + (-2)^2} \cdot \sqrt{(-1.333)^2 + (-1.333)^2 + (2.667)^2} = \sqrt{8} \cdot \sqrt{11.111} \approx 2.828 \times 3.333 \approx 9.428
$$

$$
\text{sim}(U1, U3) \approx \frac{-8.000}{9.428} \approx -0.849
$$

**Common with U4:** {B, D} → $\mathcal{I}_{U1,U4} = \{B, D\}$

$$
\text{Num} = (3-3)(3-3.667) + (1-3)(4-3.667) = 0 + (-2)(0.333) = -0.667
$$

$$
\text{Den} = \sqrt{0^2 + (-2)^2} \cdot \sqrt{(-0.667)^2 + (0.333)^2} = 2 \cdot \sqrt{0.556} \approx 2 \times 0.745 = 1.491
$$

$$
\text{sim}(U1, U4) \approx \frac{-0.667}{1.491} \approx -0.447
$$

**Common with U5:** {A, D} → $\mathcal{I}_{U1,U5} = \{A, D\}$

$$
\text{Num} = (5-3)(2-3.667) + (1-3)(4-3.667) = 2(-1.667) + (-2)(0.333) = -3.333 - 0.667 = -4.000
$$

$$
\text{Den} = \sqrt{8} \cdot \sqrt{(-1.667)^2 + (0.333)^2} = 2.828 \cdot \sqrt{2.889} \approx 2.828 \times 1.700 \approx 4.808
$$

$$
\text{sim}(U1, U5) \approx \frac{-4.000}{4.808} \approx -0.832
$$

### Step 3 — Top-2 Neighbors

Sorted similarities: **U2 (+1.000), U4 (-0.447), U5 (-0.832), U3 (-0.849)**

Top-2: $\{(U2, +1.000), (U4, -0.447)\}$

### Step 4 — Predict $r_{U1, C}$

U2 has not rated C; **only U4 has rated C (rating 4)**. So $N_k(U1) \cap U_C = \{U4\}$.

$$
\hat{r}_{U1, C} = \bar{r}_{U1} + \frac{\text{sim}(U1,U4) \cdot (r_{U4, C} - \bar{r}_{U4})}{\vert \text{sim}(U1,U4) \vert}
$$

$$
\hat{r}_{U1, C} = 3.000 + \frac{(-0.447) \cdot (4 - 3.667)}{0.447} = 3.000 + (-1)(0.333) = 2.667
$$

**Final Answer:** $\hat{r}_{U1, C} \approx \mathbf{2.67}$ on a 1–5 scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:**
> - Pearson requires **co-rated items only** — common mistake is to include all items.
> - Only neighbors who have rated the **target item** are eligible for the weighted sum.
> - If the sum of absolute similarities is 0, fall back to $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \bar{r}_u$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Matrix Factorization (SVD-based Latent Model)

### Step 1 — Standard SVD

Given dense $\mathbf{R}_{m \times n}$, the truncated Singular Value Decomposition is:

$$
\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{U} \, \mathbf{\Sigma} \, \mathbf{V}^T
$$

where $\mathbf{U} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$, $\mathbf{\Sigma} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ (diagonal), $\mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$. Rank-$k$ approximation keeps the top $k$ singular values:

$$
\mathbf{R} \approx \hat{\mathbf{R}} = \mathbf{U}_k \, \mathbf{\Sigma}_k \, \mathbf{V}_k^T = \mathbf{P} \, \mathbf{Q}^T
$$

with $\mathbf{P}_{m \times k}$ = user latent factors, $\mathbf{Q}_{n \times k}$ = item latent factors.

### Step 2 — Biased MF (FunkSVD / SVD++)

Direct SVD fails on sparse matrices. **FunkSVD** learns latent vectors $\mathbf{p}_u, \mathbf{q}_i$ by minimizing regularized squared error:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{p}_*, \mathbf{q}_*, b_*} \sum_{(u,i) \in \mathcal{O}} \left( r_{u,i} - \mu - b_u - b_i - \mathbf{p}_u^T \mathbf{q}_i \right)^2 + \lambda \left( \|\mathbf{p}_u\|^2 + \|\mathbf{q}_i\|^2 + b_u^2 + b_i^2 \right)
$$

where:
- $\mu$ = global mean rating
- $b_u$ = user bias (some users rate harshly)
- $b_i$ = item bias (some items are universally liked)
- $\mathbf{p}_u, \mathbf{q}_i$ = $k$-dimensional latent vectors
- $\lambda$ = L2 regularization hyperparameter

The prediction rule is:

$$
\hat{r}_{u,i} = \mu + b_u + b_i + \mathbf{p}_u^T \mathbf{q}_i
$$

### Step 3 — Stochastic Gradient Descent Update

For each observed $(u, i, r)$:

$$
e_{u,i} = r_{u,i} - \hat{r}_{u,i}
$$

$$
b_u \leftarrow b_u + \eta (e_{u,i} - \lambda b_u)
$$

$$
b_i \leftarrow b_i + \eta (e_{u,i} - \lambda b_i)
$$

$$
\mathbf{p}_u \leftarrow \mathbf{p}_u + \eta (e_{u,i} \mathbf{q}_i - \lambda \mathbf{p}_u)
$$

$$
\mathbf{q}_i \leftarrow \mathbf{q}_i + \eta (e_{u,i} \mathbf{p}_u - \lambda \mathbf{q}_i)
$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate. After convergence, every entry (observed or unobserved) is estimated via $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \mu + b_u + b_i + \mathbf{p}_u^T \mathbf{q}_i$, solving both the **sparsity** and the **scalability** problems simultaneously.

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Method | Strengths | Weaknesses | Cold-Start Robust | Sparsity Robust |
|--------|-----------|------------|:-----------------:|:---------------:|
| **User-User CF** | Intuitive, social signals | Poor with many users | ❌ | ⚠️ |
| **Item-Item CF** | Stable item similarity, scales to Amazon-scale | Limited diversity | ❌ | ⚠️ |
| **Content-Based** | Works for new items (no CF needed) | Filter bubble | ✅ (new items) | ✅ |
| **Matrix Factorization** | Compact, accurate, scalable | Hard to interpret | ❌ | ✅ |
| **Hybrid** | Combines best of all | Engineering complexity | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Deep Learning (Neural CF)** | Captures non-linear patterns | Needs lots of data | ⚠️ | ✅ |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 | May 2024]`**

### Q1. Define Collaborative Filtering. Differentiate between User-User and Item-Item CF.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Collaborative Filtering (CF):** A recommendation technique that predicts a user's preference for an item based on the preferences of other similar users (user-user) or similar items (item-item), without requiring content/feature analysis of the items.

| Aspect | User-User CF | Item-Item CF |
|--------|:------------:|:------------:|
| **Similarity computed between** | Users | Items |
| **Prediction basis** | Ratings of similar users | User's own ratings on similar items |
| **Stability** | Users' tastes drift; similarity changes | Item similarity is more stable over time |
| **Scalability** | Poor for huge user base (Amazon-scale users) | Scales well (item catalog smaller) |
| **Preferred when** | Few users, many items | Many users, stable item set |
| **Used by** | Last.fm (early), Twitter | Amazon, Netflix |

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for comparison table)*

### Q2. What is the Cold-Start problem in Recommendation Systems? List any two solutions.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Cold-Start Problem:** The inability of a recommendation system to provide reliable suggestions when there is *insufficient historical interaction data*. It manifests in three flavors:
1. **New User Cold Start:** No ratings/history to build a profile.
2. **New Item Cold Start:** No one has rated the new item yet.
3. **New System Cold Start:** The entire platform is bootstrapping.

**Two Solutions (any two for 2 marks):**
- **Hybrid Filtering:** Fall back to Content-Based Filtering using item attributes (genre, tags) for new items, and demographic features (age, location) for new users.
- **Onboarding Questionnaires:** Ask new users to rate a small seed set of items to bootstrap their profile.
- **Popularity / Demographic Baselines:** Recommend globally popular items or use demographic clustering until enough data accrues.
- **Knowledge-Based Methods:** Use explicit user requirements (e.g., "movies released after 2020, action genre") as initial recommendations.

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU End Semester Examination — Module 4 | 2024 Scheme | NEP 2020 Aligned]`**

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Consider the following user-item rating matrix (1–5 scale, 0 = not rated). Compute the **Pearson correlation similarity** between every pair of users and predict the rating of **User 1 for Item 5** using **User-User Collaborative Filtering with K = 2 neighbors**. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

| User | Item 1 | Item 2 | Item 3 | Item 4 | Item 5 |
|------|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|
| U1   |  5     |  3     |  4     |  0     |  **?** |
| U2   |  3     |  1     |  2     |  3     |  4     |
| U3   |  4     |  3     |  4     |  3     |  5     |
| U4   |  0     |  2     |  1     |  4     |  5     |
| U5   |  2     |  1     |  0     |  5     |  4     |

#### Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute user means** *(1 mark)*

$$
\bar{r}_{U1} = \frac{5+3+4}{3} = 4.000, \quad \bar{r}_{U2} = \frac{3+1+2+3+4}{5} = 2.600
$$

$$
\bar{r}_{U3} = \frac{4+3+4+3+5}{5} = 3.800, \quad \bar{r}_{U4} = \frac{2+1+4+5}{4} = 3.000
$$

$$
\bar{r}_{U5} = \frac{2+1+5+4}{4} = 3.000
$$

**Step 2: Compute Pearson sim of U1 with U2, U3, U4, U5** *(3 marks — 0.75 per pair)*

**U1–U2 co-rated: {1, 2, 3}** (U1 has no rating for 4; U2 has 4 and 5 not in U1)

$$
\text{sim}(U1, U2) = \frac{(1)(-1.6) + (-1)(-1.6) + (0)(-0.6)}{\sqrt{1^2+1^2+0^2}\sqrt{(-1.6)^2+(-1.6)^2+(-0.6)^2}} = \frac{-1.6+1.6+0}{\sqrt{2}\sqrt{5.48}} = \frac{0}{3.310} = 0
$$

*Detailed deviation values: U1 deviations on 1,2,3: (1,-1,0); U2 deviations: (-1.6,-1.6,-0.6)*

**U1–U3 co-rated: {1, 2, 3, 5}**

$$
\text{Num} = (1)(0.2) + (-1)(-0.8) + (0)(0.2) + (r_{15} \text{ unknown for U1})
$$

Since U1 hasn't rated item 5, we use only items 1, 2, 3:

$$
\text{Num} = (1)(0.2) + (-1)(-0.8) + (0)(0.2) = 0.2 + 0.8 + 0 = 1.0
$$

$$
\text{Den} = \sqrt{1+1+0} \cdot \sqrt{0.04+0.64+0.04} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{0.72} = 1.414 \times 0.849 = 1.201
$$

$$
\text{sim}(U1, U3) = \frac{1.0}{1.201} \approx +0.833
$$

**U1–U4 co-rated: {2, 3}**

$$
\text{Num} = (-1)(-1) + (0)(-2) = 1
$$

$$
\text{Den} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{1+4} = 1.414 \times 2.236 = 3.162
$$

$$
\text{sim}(U1, U4) = \frac{1}{3.162} \approx +0.316
$$

**U1–U5 co-rated: {1, 2}**

$$
\text{Num} = (1)(-1) + (-1)(-2) = -1 + 2 = 1
$$

$$
\text{Den} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{1+4} = 3.162
$$

$$
\text{sim}(U1, U5) = \frac{1}{3.162} \approx +0.316
$$

**Step 3: Top-K=2 neighbors** *(1 mark)*

Ranked: **U3 (0.833)**, **U4 (0.316) = U5 (0.316 — tie, pick U4)**.

Top-K = {U3, U4}.

**Step 4: Predict $\hat{r}_{U1, 5}$** *(2 marks)*

Both U3 and U4 have rated Item 5: $r_{U3,5} = 5$, $r_{U4,5} = 5$.

$$
\hat{r}_{U1, 5} = \bar{r}_{U1} + \frac{0.833 \cdot (5-3.8) + 0.316 \cdot (5-3.0)}{0.833 + 0.316}
$$

$$
= 4.000 + \frac{0.833 \cdot 1.2 + 0.316 \cdot 2.0}{1.149}
$$

$$
= 4.000 + \frac{0.9996 + 0.6320}{1.149} = 4.000 + \frac{1.6316}{1.149}
$$

$$
= 4.000 + 1.420 \approx 5.42
$$

> Predicted rating is **5.42**; clipped to maximum scale 5 → $\hat{r}_{U1,5} = \mathbf{5.00}$

**(b)** Explain **Matrix Factorization (FunkSVD)** for recommendation. How does it overcome the sparsity and scalability issues of memory-based CF? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** Matrix Factorization (MF) is a *model-based* CF technique that decomposes the sparse user-item matrix $\mathbf{R}_{m \times n}$ into two lower-dimensional latent factor matrices $\mathbf{P}_{m \times k}$ and $\mathbf{Q}_{n \times k}$ such that:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{R}} = \mathbf{P} \mathbf{Q}^T
$$

where each row $\mathbf{p}_u \in \mathbb{R}^k$ is the latent vector of user $u$, and each row $\mathbf{q}_i \in \mathbb{R}^k$ is the latent vector of item $i$. The inner product $\mathbf{p}_u^T \mathbf{q}_i$ approximates $r_{u,i}$.

**FunkSVD Formulation (2 marks):** The bias-augmented prediction rule is:

$$
\hat{r}_{u,i} = \mu + b_u + b_i + \mathbf{p}_u^T \mathbf{q}_i
$$

The model parameters are learned by minimizing the regularized loss over observed ratings $\mathcal{O}$:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \sum_{(u,i) \in \mathcal{O}} \left( r_{u,i} - \mu - b_u - b_i - \mathbf{p}_u^T \mathbf{q}_i \right)^2 + \lambda \left( \|\mathbf{p}_u\|^2 + \|\mathbf{q}_i\|^2 + b_u^2 + b_i^2 \right)
$$

Solved via **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** or **Alternating Least Squares (ALS)**.

**How MF solves Sparsity (1.5 marks):**
- In memory-based CF, similarity is computed only over *co-rated items*; with sparse data, $\vert \mathcal{I}_{uv} \vert$ is tiny, leading to unreliable similarity.
- MF uses **all observed ratings** to learn the latent vectors, so a single rating influences the global model via $\mathbf{p}_u$ and $\mathbf{q}_i$. Even with 1% density, the latent factors become accurate.
- Predictions for unobserved cells are possible via the dot product — the matrix becomes *fully dense* in $\hat{\mathbf{R}}$.

**How MF solves Scalability (1.5 marks):**
- Memory-based CF requires $O(m^2)$ user-user or $O(n^2)$ item-item similarity matrices — infeasible for millions of users.
- MF stores only $\mathbf{P}$ and $\mathbf{Q}$ of size $O((m+n)k)$ where $k \approx 50$–$200$, dramatically compressing storage.
- Predictions are $O(k)$ per cell (a dot product), independent of $m$ or $n$, enabling real-time serving at scale.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a **Content-Based Filtering (CBF)** system. Compare it with Collaborative Filtering. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks)

**Architecture (4 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userA["User Profile"] --> matcher["Profile Item Matcher"]
    itemA["Item Profile Features"] --> feat["Feature Extractor TFIDF Embeddings"]
    corpus["Item Corpus"] --> feat
    feat --> matcher
    matcher --> rec["Top K Recommended Items"]
    userA --> upd["Feedback Loop"]
    rec --> upd
    upd --> userA
```

**Three-step CBF pipeline (3 marks):**

1. **Item Profiling:** Each item $i$ is represented as a feature vector $\mathbf{f}_i$ (e.g., TF-IDF for text items, embeddings for images, genre-actor-director for movies).
2. **User Profiling:** The user's profile $\mathbf{p}_u$ is the aggregated (often weighted) vector of items they have previously consumed/liked:
$$
\mathbf{p}_u = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{I}_u^+|} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{I}_u^+} w_i \mathbf{f}_i
$$
where $\mathcal{I}_u^+$ is the set of positively-rated items.
3. **Matching & Ranking:** Compute similarity (e.g., cosine) between $\mathbf{p}_u$ and all candidate $\mathbf{f}_i$, and recommend the Top-K highest-scoring items.

**Comparison with CF (in tabular form, 2 marks):**

| Criterion | Content-Based | Collaborative |
|-----------|:-------------:|:-------------:|
| **Data needed** | Item features + user history | Only user-item interactions |
| **Cold start (new item)** | ✅ Solvable (features known) | ❌ Cannot recommend |
| **Cold start (new user)** | ❌ Needs history | ❌ Needs history |
| **Diversity** | Low (filter bubble) | High (serendipity) |
| **Domain knowledge** | Required | Not required |
| **Scalability** | Good (precompute item vectors) | Good with MF |

**(b)** Discuss the role of **Latent Factor Models** in modern recommender systems. How do embeddings $\mathbf{p}_u$ and $\mathbf{q}_i$ capture hidden semantics? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks)

**Role of Latent Factor Models (3 marks):** Latent factor models (LFMs) are the *backbone of modern recommender systems* because they:

1. **Compress the rating matrix** into compact $k$-dimensional representations, achieving up to 90% storage reduction.
2. **Generalize across sparse data** by exploiting global co-occurrence patterns rather than per-pair similarities.
3. **Enable semantic retrieval:** Two users with no common rated items can still be similar if their latent vectors align.
4. **Form the foundation for advanced methods:** Neural Collaborative Filtering, DeepFM, Two-Tower models, and Transformer-based recommenders (e.g., SASRec, BERT4Rec) all build on top of latent embeddings.

**How Embeddings Capture Hidden Semantics (2 marks):** Each dimension of the latent space is *learned*, not predefined, but it tends to correspond to interpretable semantic axes. For example, in a movie recommender with $k = 10$:
- Dimension 1 might encode "action vs. romance"
- Dimension 2 might encode "mainstream vs. indie"
- Dimension 3 might encode "old classics vs. recent releases"

User vector $\mathbf{p}_u$ = coordinates expressing the user's preference on each hidden axis.
Item vector $\mathbf{q}_i$ = coordinates expressing where the item lies on these axes.

Their dot product $\mathbf{p}_u^T \mathbf{q}_i$ is high when both vectors align (user likes the *combination* of facets that the item embodies).

**Mathematical Intuition (2 marks):** The dot product is the cosine of the angle scaled by magnitudes:

$$
\mathbf{p}_u^T \mathbf{q}_i = \|\mathbf{p}_u\| \cdot \|\mathbf{q}_i\| \cdot \cos(\theta_{u,i})
$$

A high predicted rating requires (a) the user to have strong, polarized preferences (large $\|\mathbf{p}_u\|$) *and* (b) the item to be aligned with those preferences (small $\theta$). This is precisely the *collaborative signal* extracted from millions of users and items.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in CF Numericals (per KTU 2024 valuation keys):**
> 1. **Including non-co-rated items in Pearson numerator/denominator** (max -2 marks). Only the intersection of rated items counts.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract user mean** in Pearson — students often use raw cosine and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Using all users instead of Top-K** in prediction — partial credit only.
> 4. **Not clipping predictions to rating range** (e.g., predicting 5.42 when scale is 1–5) — minor -0.5 mark deduction.
> 5. **In MF theory, omitting the regularization term** $\lambda(\|\mathbf{p}\|^2 + \|\mathbf{q}\|^2)$ — examiner expects explicit mention.
> 6. **Confusing cosine similarity with adjusted cosine** in Item-Item CF.
> 7. **Spelling "Sparsity" as "Sparsness" or "Sparsness"** — surprisingly penalized in professional answer scripts.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **CF definition:** Recommends based on collective user behavior, not item content.
- [ ] **Two pillars of CF:** Memory-Based (neighborhood) + Model-Based (latent factors).
- [ ] **User-User CF Formula:** $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \bar{r}_u + \frac{\sum_v \text{sim}(u,v)(r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum_v \vert \text{sim}(u,v) \vert}$
- [ ] **Item-Item CF Formula:** $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \frac{\sum_j \text{sim}(i,j) \cdot r_{u,j}}{\sum_j \vert \text{sim}(i,j) \vert}$
- [ ] **Pearson correlation** uses *co-rated items* and *mean-centered* ratings.
- [ ] **Cosine similarity** treats users/items as vectors; ignores rating-scale bias.
- [ ] **Adjusted Cosine** subtracts user means → used for *item-item* similarity to remove user rating bias.
- [ ] **Matrix Factorization:** $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \mu + b_u + b_i + \mathbf{p}_u^T \mathbf{q}_i$
- [ ] **FunkSVD loss function** includes L2 regularization $\lambda(\|\mathbf{p}\|^2 + \|\mathbf{q}\|^2 + b_u^2 + b_i^2)$.
- [ ] **SGD update rule** for MF uses error $e_{u,i} = r_{u,i} - \hat{r}_{u,i}$.
- [ ] **Sparsity level** of real-world matrices > 99% — central CF challenge.
- [ ] **Cold Start types:** New user, new item, new system.
- [ ] **Cold Start solutions:** Hybrid filtering, popularity baseline, onboarding questions, demographic clustering.
- [ ] **RMSE** is the gold standard for rating prediction; **MAP@K** and **NDCG@K** are gold standards for ranking.
- [ ] **Item-Item CF used by Amazon** (scalability), **User-User CF used by early Last.fm** (smaller user base), **MF used by Netflix Prize winner** (2009, RMSE 0.85).
- [ ] **Hybrid approaches:** Weighted, switching, mixed, feature-augmentation, cascade, meta-level.
- [ ] **Content-Based** solves item cold-start; **CF** solves filter-bubble/diversity problem.
- [ ] **Latent dimension $k$** is a hyperparameter — too small = underfit, too large = overfit.
- [ ] **Implicit feedback datasets** (clicks, watch time) require different loss functions (BPR, WMF) compared to explicit ratings.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Collaborative Filtering and Recommendation Systems**
> **Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) — Course PECDT745**
