topics.title: Ethical Issues and Emerging Trends: Privacy and Bias in Information Retrieval
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ethical Issues and Emerging Trends: Privacy and Bias in Information Retrieval

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Ethics in Information Retrieval

Information Retrieval (IR) systems, such as search engines, recommender systems, and question-answering platforms, have become the primary gateways through which billions of users access information. As these systems grow more sophisticated, they raise profound **ethical questions** regarding two foundational pillars: **Privacy** and **Bias**. These are no longer peripheral concerns; under the **NEP 2020** framework and global data-protection regulations, ethical IR is a core competency expected of every computer science engineer.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

### A. Privacy in Information Retrieval
**Privacy in IR** is the property of an information system that ensures *user data, query logs, behavioral traces, and personal identifiers* are collected, stored, processed, and shared in a manner that is **lawful, consensual, minimal, and protected** against unauthorized access or inference.

Mathematically, privacy preservation ensures that the *posterior knowledge* an adversary gains about a user from observing the system's outputs is bounded by a small, quantifiable parameter (often denoted $\varepsilon$, the **privacy budget** in differential privacy).

### B. Bias in Information Retrieval
**Bias in IR** is a systematic skew in the *relevance judgments, ranking outputs, or content representation* produced by an information retrieval system, which causes *certain groups, viewpoints, documents, or individuals* to be unfairly advantaged or disadvantaged.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly requires students to identify, classify, and propose mitigation strategies for bias, as well as to differentiate between **personal privacy** (individual) and **group privacy** (collective), and between **algorithmic bias**, **data bias**, **pre-existing bias**, and **emergent bias**.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Analogy 1 — Privacy: The "Locked Diary" Metaphor
Imagine you walk into a vast public library and ask the librarian, *"Find me every book about my grandfather's illness."* The librarian retrieves the books, but if the librarian also **writes down your name, the time of your visit, every book you touched, and shares this list with advertisers**, your private life has been compromised. **Privacy in IR is the librarian's pledge** to forget your identity, never record your queries unnecessarily, and never leak what you read — even to the library's own marketing team.

### Analogy 2 — Bias: The "Tilted Scale" Metaphor
Consider a weighing scale used in a fruit market. If the scale is tilted, every "fair" transaction is *systematically* wrong — not by accident, but by design. **Bias in IR is a tilted scale**: the search engine *appears* neutral, but its ranking algorithm is calibrated against training data that already favours certain types of content (e.g., English sources, Western viewpoints, male-authored papers). The result is that for every 100 results returned, certain voices are consistently *under-represented* in a predictable way.

### Analogy 3 — The Filter Bubble (Bias Amplification)
A more dynamic analogy: imagine wearing tinted glasses that, over time, *only show you what you have previously looked at*. The IR system learns your preferences and **filters out diversity**, creating an echo chamber. Eli Pariser coined this the **"Filter Bubble"** — an emergent ethical failure where personalization *amplifies* bias rather than reducing it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core takeaway for KTU exams:** Privacy is about *protecting the user from the system*, whereas bias is about *the system treating users, content, or groups unequally*. Both are two sides of the same ethical coin in modern IR.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in 2024+

| Real-World Trigger | Year | Ethical Lesson |
|---|---|---|
| Cambridge Analytica scandal | 2018 | Query logs and profile data can weaponize democracy |
| GDPR enforcement (EU) | 2018 | Consent and data minimization became *legal* obligations |
| Google's "ramen gender bias" study | 2021 | Image search amplifies occupational stereotypes |
| EU AI Act | 2024 | High-risk AI (including IR) must undergo bias audits |
| DPDP Act, India | 2023 | Indian equivalent of GDPR — affects all KTU students professionally |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Framework of Privacy in IR

Privacy in IR is not a single safeguard — it is a **multi-layer architectural concern**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to identify threats at each layer.

### Layer 1: Data Collection Privacy
- **What is collected:** Query strings, click-through data, dwell time, IP addresses, cookies, device fingerprints, location, voice (for voice search).
- **Threats:** Re-identification, linkage attacks (joining two anonymized datasets to reveal identity).
- **Mitigations:** *Data minimization*, *informed consent*, *opt-in mechanisms*, *pseudonymization*.

### Layer 2: Storage Privacy
- **What is stored:** User profiles, historical queries, indexed web pages, embeddings of documents.
- **Threats:** Data breaches, insider attacks, government subpoenas.
- **Mitigations:** *Encryption-at-rest* (AES-256), *homomorphic encryption*, *secure multi-party computation*, *differential privacy*.

### Layer 3: Processing Privacy
- **What is processed:** Ranking scores, personalization vectors, embeddings, recommendation lists.
- **Threats:** Inference attacks (inferring sensitive attributes from non-sensitive behaviour), membership inference.
- **Mitigations:** *Differential privacy* (DP), *federated learning*, *on-device personalization*.

### Layer 4: Output Privacy
- **What is exposed:** Search results, autocomplete suggestions, related searches, ads.
- **Threats:** *Side-channel leakage* (e.g., autocomplete revealing what other users searched), *contextual leaks*.
- **Mitigations:** *Output perturbation*, *result diversification*, *stricter access control*.

---

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Bias in IR

Bias in IR is **multi-causal** and **multi-stage**. The KTU syllabus recognizes the following four canonical types, originally systematized by Friedman & Nissenbaum (1996) and later adapted for IR by Baeza-Yates:

### A. Pre-existing Bias (Societal Bias)
- **Origin:** Exists in the *world* before the system is built.
- **Example in IR:** The web itself has more content authored by English-speaking males; the IR system inherits this skew.
- **Manifestation:** Under-representation of marginalized voices in search results.

### B. Technical / Algorithmic Bias
- **Origin:** Introduced by the *design choices* of the algorithm.
- **Example:** TF-IDF inherently favours longer documents; BM25 partially corrects this but introduces length normalization parameters that may still be unfair across content types.
- **Manifestation:** Systematic over/under-ranking of certain document classes.

### C. Data / Training Bias
- **Origin:** Present in the *training corpus* used to build the index, embeddings, or ranker.
- **Example:** A web crawler that excludes `.in` domains biases results towards international content.
- **Manifestation:** Geographic, linguistic, and topical skew in retrieval.

### D. Emergent / Interaction Bias (User Feedback Loop)
- **Origin:** Arises from *user interaction* with the system over time.
- **Example:** Click-through data is used to re-rank results, but users click more on top-ranked items (position bias), reinforcing the original ranking.
- **Manifestation:** Filter bubbles, echo chambers, polarization.

### E. Presentation / Interface Bias
- **Origin:** Arises from the *UI design*.
- **Example:** Showing only 10 blue links on the first page hides diversity; the "People Also Ask" box surfaces stereotyped associations.
- **Manifestation:** Confirmation bias amplification.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet: Key Metrics and Formulas

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** The following table consolidates the *quantitative* metrics KTU examiners love to test. Memorize the formula, the symbol, and the *interpretation*.

| # | Metric / Concept | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Differential Privacy Budget | $\varepsilon$ | $\Pr[M(D) \in S] \le e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[M(D') \in S]$ | Smaller $\varepsilon$ $\Rightarrow$ stronger privacy. $\varepsilon \le 1$ is considered strong. |
| 2 | k-Anonymity | $k$ | Each record is indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ others | Larger $k$ $\Rightarrow$ stronger anonymity. |
| 3 | l-Diversity | $l$ | Each equivalence class has at least $l$ "well-represented" sensitive values | Prevents homogeneity attack on k-anonymity. |
| 4 | Statistical Parity Difference | $SPD$ | $SPD = \Pr[\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0] - \Pr[\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1]$ | $SPD = 0$ means perfect parity (fair). |
| 5 | Disparate Impact Ratio | $DIR$ | $DIR = \dfrac{\Pr[\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0]}{\Pr[\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1]}$ | $DIR \in [0.8, 1.25]$ is the "4/5ths rule" of fairness. |
| 6 | Equal Opportunity Difference | $EOD$ | $EOD = TPR_{A=0} - TPR_{A=1}$ | $EOD = 0$ means equal true positive rates across groups. |
| 7 | Position Bias Click Model | $P(\text{click} \mid \text{rel}, \text{pos})$ | $P(c \mid r=1, p) = \alpha(p)$; $P(c \mid r=0, p) = 0$ | Click probability depends only on position, not relevance — biases training. |
| 8 | Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain | $nDCG@k$ | $nDCG@k = \dfrac{DCG@k}{IDCG@k}$ | Used to *measure* relevance bias, but can itself embed presentation bias. |

> **Notation note:** In the above table, $\hat{Y}$ represents the IR system's predicted relevance/decision, $A$ is a protected attribute (e.g., gender, region), and $TPR$ is the True Positive Rate. The vertical separators in all formulas have been replaced with $\Pr[\cdot]$ notation to comply with markdown table rules.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Ethical IR is not academic — it is the **operational backbone** of:

1. **Search Engines (Google, Bing, DuckDuckGo):** Must comply with GDPR, DPDP Act, and CCPA. Bias audits are now quarterly events.
2. **Enterprise Search (Elastic, Solr):** Used in HR, healthcare, legal — where biased retrieval can violate anti-discrimination law.
3. **E-commerce Recommendation (Amazon, Flipkart):** Differential privacy is deployed by Apple, Google, and Microsoft to protect user-level behavioural data.
4. **Generative IR (RAG-based LLMs):** The retrieval layer of Retrieval-Augmented Generation pipelines *inherits all the bias and privacy risks* of classical IR — and adds new ones (hallucination, memorization of private data).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Differential Privacy Guarantee in IR

Differential Privacy (DP) is the **gold-standard** mathematical definition of privacy in modern IR systems. KTU examiners expect students to be able to *derive* and *interpret* the DP inequality.

### Step 1: Setup
Let $D$ and $D'$ be two neighbouring datasets that differ in exactly *one* record (one user's query history, for instance). Let $\mathcal{M}$ be a randomized mechanism (the IR ranker with added noise) and $S$ be any subset of the output space.

### Step 2: The Core Inequality
The mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ is said to be $\varepsilon$-differentially private if, for all neighbouring $D, D'$ and all measurable $S$:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \le e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]
$$

### Step 3: Interpretation of Each Symbol

- $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S]$ — the probability that the IR system, when trained on dataset $D$, produces an output belonging to set $S$.
- $D'$ — the same dataset but with *one user's* query history removed (or modified).
- $e^{\varepsilon}$ — the multiplicative bound. Because $e^{\varepsilon} \approx 1 + \varepsilon$ for small $\varepsilon$, the bound acts as a *soft* ceiling on the privacy leakage.
- $\varepsilon$ — the **privacy budget**. A smaller $\varepsilon$ means the system's output is *almost identical* whether or not any single user is in the dataset, making re-identification impossible.

### Step 4: Composition Property
If we apply two $\varepsilon_1$-DP and $\varepsilon_2$-DP mechanisms sequentially to the same dataset, the combined privacy loss is:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2
$$

This is called the **basic composition theorem** and is critical for IR pipelines that query multiple differentially private sub-systems (e.g., a private embedding lookup followed by a private re-ranker).

### Step 5: Application in IR
A practical IR example: Google uses **RAPPOR** (Randomized Aggregatable Privacy-Preserving Ordinal Response) to collect Chrome browser statistics with $\varepsilon$-DP guarantees. The result is aggregate trend data that is provably indistinguishable regardless of any individual user's browsing history.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Statistical Parity Difference (SPD) for IR Fairness

Suppose an IR system retrieves documents and decides whether to *boost* a result's rank based on an attribute $A \in \{0, 1\}$ (e.g., $A=0$ for one demographic group, $A=1$ for another). Let $\hat{Y} = 1$ denote "boosted" and $\hat{Y} = 0$ denote "not boosted".

### Step 1: Define Group-wise Boost Rates
For group $A = 0$:

$$
P_{0} = \Pr[\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 0]
$$

For group $A = 1$:

$$
P_{1} = \Pr[\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 1]
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Disparity
Subtract to get the **Statistical Parity Difference**:

$$
SPD = P_{0} - P_{1}
$$

### Step 3: Fairness Threshold
- If $SPD = 0$, the system *grants boosts at identical rates* across both groups — perfect statistical parity.
- If $SPD > 0$, group $A=0$ is *preferentially boosted* (advantaged).
- If $SPD < 0$, group $A=1$ is *preferentially boosted* (advantaged).
- In practice, the **4/5ths rule** (also called the *disparate impact* rule) is applied: $DIR = \frac{\min(P_0, P_1)}{\max(P_0, P_1)} \ge 0.8$.

### Step 4: Worked Example
A KTU-style problem: *"Out of 1000 documents authored by Group A and 1000 by Group B, the IR system boosts 400 from A and 200 from B. Compute SPD and DIR."*

$$
P_{0} = \frac{400}{1000} = 0.40, \quad P_{1} = \frac{200}{1000} = 0.20
$$

$$
SPD = 0.40 - 0.20 = 0.20
$$

$$
DIR = \frac{0.20}{0.40} = 0.50
$$

**Interpretation:** $DIR = 0.50$ is *well below* the 0.8 threshold, meaning the IR system is **legally and ethically unfair** to Group B. This kind of calculation is a classic KTU Part B (14-mark) question.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Mitigation Strategies to Threats

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU revision tool** — examiners often ask "discuss mitigation strategies" and reward systematic tabular answers.

| Threat Layer | Privacy Threat | Privacy Mitigation | Bias Type | Bias Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Data Collection | Re-identification via query logs | Pseudonymization, opt-in consent | Pre-existing | Diverse & representative web crawling |
| Indexing | Leakage of sensitive terms in inverted index | Encrypted inverted index, secret sharing | Data bias | Balanced sampling, debiasing embeddings |
| Ranking | Inference of user attributes from results | Differential privacy in ranker | Algorithmic bias | Fair ranking constraints, re-ranking |
| Output | Side-channel leaks via autocomplete | Output perturbation, truncation | Presentation bias | Diverse result sets, mix-of-rank |
| Feedback Loop | Filter bubble from click data | Federated learning, on-device processing | Emergent bias | Exploration vs. exploitation (multi-armed bandits) |
| Evaluation | Biased relevance judgments | Crowdsourced diverse assessors | Measurement bias | Multiple gold-standard test collections |

---

## 3.4 The Fair Ranking Optimization Problem (Conceptual Derivation)

A modern IR system optimizes a *joint* objective balancing relevance and fairness:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{fair}} = (1 - \lambda) \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{relevance}} + \lambda \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{fairness}}
$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{L}_{\text{relevance}}$ is typically a listwise loss such as ListMLE or LambdaLoss.
- $\mathcal{L}_{\text{fairness}}$ encodes the chosen fairness criterion (e.g., demographic parity, equal opportunity).
- $\lambda \in [0, 1]$ is a tunable hyperparameter controlling the **relevance–fairness trade-off**.

Setting $\lambda = 0$ recovers the classical IR system (maximum relevance, no fairness guarantee). Setting $\lambda = 1$ recovers a maximally fair but possibly irrelevant system. The KTU 2024 examiner often asks students to *explain this trade-off* and *justify* a choice of $\lambda$ for a real scenario (e.g., job-search IR).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Architecture: Privacy and Bias in a Modern IR Pipeline

The following Mermaid block renders the complete architecture of an ethical IR system, mapping *where* privacy risks enter, *where* bias is introduced, and *which* mitigation layer addresses each.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[User Query Input] --> A1[Privacy Layer 1: Consent and Pseudonymization]
    A1 --> A2[Query Understanding Module]
    A2 --> A3[Privacy Layer 2: Encrypted Index Lookup]
    A3 --> A4[Retrieval and Candidate Generation]
    A4 --> A5[Bias Audit Point 1: Data Bias Check]
    A5 --> A6[Ranking Model]
    A6 --> A7[Bias Audit Point 2: Algorithmic Fairness Re-ranker]
    A7 --> A8[Privacy Layer 3: Differential Privacy Noise Injection]
    A8 --> A9[Presentation Module]
    A9 --> A10[Bias Audit Point 3: Presentation Diversity Check]
    A10 --> A11[Output to User]
    A11 --> A12[Feedback Collection]
    A12 --> A13[Bias Audit Point 4: Feedback Loop Monitor]
    A13 --> A14[Periodic Re-indexing with Fairness Constraints]

    classDef privacy fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    classDef bias fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#14532d,color:#000

    class A1,A3,A8 privacy
    class A5,A7,A10,A13,A14 bias
    class A0,A2,A4,A6,A9,A11,A12 core
```

**How to read this diagram for your KTU answer:**
- The **blue nodes (Privacy Layers)** are checkpoints that protect the *user* from data leakage.
- The **red nodes (Bias Audit Points)** are checkpoints that protect *content creators and societal groups* from unfair treatment.
- The **green nodes (Core Modules)** are the classical IR pipeline steps.
- Together, they form a **"Privacy + Fairness sandwich"** — the standard architecture in any ethically-deployed 2024-grade IR system.

---

## 4.2 The Bias Amplification Feedback Loop

This second diagram shows the *cyclic* nature of emergent bias — a question examiners often ask as a 7-mark short essay.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Training Data with Pre-existing Bias] --> B2[Trained Ranker Learns Skewed Patterns]
    B2 --> B3[Ranker Outputs Skewed Results to User]
    B3 --> B4[User Clicks and Engagement Data Collected]
    B4 --> B5[Click Data Used as Re-training Signal]
    B5 --> B2
    B1 -.Mitigation.-> M1[Counterfactual Data Augmentation]
    B2 -.Mitigation.-> M2[Fairness-constrained Re-ranking]
    B3 -.Mitigation.-> M3[Diverse SERP Design]
    B4 -.Mitigation.-> M4[Counterfactual Position Bias Correction]
    B5 -.Mitigation.-> M5[Periodic Re-training with Audited Data]

    classDef cycle fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#78350f,color:#000
    classDef mit fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#14532d,color:#000
    class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5 cycle
    class M1,M2,M3,M4,M5 mit
```

**Reading guide:** The yellow loop shows *how bias compounds* with every iteration. The green "Mitigation" arrows show *where* the cycle can be broken. Note that mitigation must be applied at *multiple points* — a single fix is insufficient.

---

## 4.3 Privacy Threat-Surface Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[User Identity] --> P2[Query Log]
    P2 --> P3[Click History]
    P3 --> P4[Profile Vector]
    P4 --> P5[Personalized Results]
    P5 --> P6[Ad Auctions and Third-party Sharing]
    P6 --> P7[Re-identification Risk]

    P1 -.Privacy Control 1.-> PC1[Anonymization at Query Log]
    P2 -.Privacy Control 2.-> PC2[Encryption at Rest]
    P3 -.Privacy Control 3.-> PC3[On-device Aggregation]
    P4 -.Privacy Control 4.-> PC4[Differential Privacy in Embeddings]
    P5 -.Privacy Control 5.-> PC5[Output Perturbation]
    P6 -.Privacy Control 6.-> PC6[Strict Access Control and Consent]
    P7 -.Privacy Control 7.-> PC7[Privacy Budget Monitoring epsilon]

    classDef risk fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,color:#000
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6,P7 risk
    class PC1,PC2,PC3,PC4,PC5,PC6,PC7 control
```

**Reading guide:** Each red node is a *privacy exposure point*. Each green node is a *control mechanism* that interrupts the chain. The strongest defence is a **defence-in-depth** strategy where multiple controls are applied at every stage.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Algorithmic Bias" in Information Retrieval. Give one example. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Algorithmic bias in IR refers to a systematic and *unintended* skew in the ranking or relevance decisions of an information retrieval system, caused by the design choices of the underlying algorithm itself (such as the choice of weighting function, similarity metric, or learning objective) rather than by the data or the user.

*Example (1 mark):* The classical **TF-IDF** weighting scheme inherently favours longer documents because they accumulate more term matches; this is a form of length-bias introduced by the algorithmic design. A query on "machine learning" might rank a 5,000-word tutorial above a concise 300-word article, even though the latter may be more relevant.

*Marking scheme:* Definition 2 marks + Example 1 mark.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between "Personal Privacy" and "Group Privacy" in the context of IR. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Personal Privacy | Group Privacy |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Protects a *single* user from disclosure of their data, queries, or profile. | Protects a *community or demographic group* from being collectively profiled, stereotyped, or excluded. |
| **Threat** | Re-identification, query log leakage. | Stereotyping, filter bubbles, group-level discrimination. |
| **Example** | A user does not want their medical queries revealed. | A search for "CEO" returning only male images reinforces gender stereotypes for *all* women. |
| **Mitigation** | Anonymization, differential privacy, consent. | Fair ranking, diverse results, bias audits. |

*Marking scheme:* Tabular comparison 2 marks + valid example 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]

**Q. (a)** With a neat architectural diagram, explain the **four layers of privacy concerns** in a modern Information Retrieval system. For each layer, identify *one concrete threat* and *one mitigation strategy*. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Privacy in IR is a multi-layer concern that must be addressed at every stage of the retrieval pipeline — from data collection, through storage, processing, and finally to output. The following four-layer model is widely accepted in modern IR literature.

**Layer 1 — Data Collection Privacy (1.5 marks):**
- *Threat:* Re-identification through query logs. An adversary who knows a user made a unique query (e.g., a rare medical condition) can re-identify them from anonymized logs.
- *Mitigation:* **Pseudonymization** of user identifiers and *data minimization* (collect only what is strictly necessary).

**Layer 2 — Storage Privacy (1.5 marks):**
- *Threat:* Data breaches exposing user profiles, indexed documents, and click histories.
- *Mitigation:* **AES-256 encryption-at-rest** combined with strict access-control policies and hardware security modules (HSMs).

**Layer 3 — Processing Privacy (1.5 marks):**
- *Threat:* Inference attacks — adversaries inferring sensitive user attributes (e.g., political leaning) from non-sensitive behavioural data.
- *Mitigation:* **Differential privacy** (DP) during model training and ranking, with a controlled privacy budget $\varepsilon$.

**Layer 4 — Output Privacy (1.5 marks):**
- *Threat:* Side-channel leakage via autocomplete and "related searches" — these can reveal *other users'* queries, indirectly exposing private information.
- *Mitigation:* **Output perturbation** (adding noise to suggestion lists) and result diversification.

*Refer to Section 4.1's Mermaid diagram for the architectural sketch (extra credit).*

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **taxonomy of bias** in IR as proposed by Friedman & Nissenbaum. For each type, provide a *concrete IR example*. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Overview (1 mark):**
The Friedman & Nissenbaum (1996) taxonomy classifies bias into three categories based on its *origin*: pre-existing (societal), technical (algorithmic), and emergent (interaction-induced). The KTU 2024 framework extends this with two additional IR-specific categories: data bias and presentation bias.

**1. Pre-existing Bias (1.5 marks):**
- *Definition:* Bias that exists in society or in the source data *before* the IR system is built.
- *IR Example:* A web search for "famous scientist" returning mostly male names, reflecting the historical under-representation of women in STEM fields.

**2. Technical / Algorithmic Bias (1.5 marks):**
- *Definition:* Bias introduced by the *design* of the algorithm itself.
- *IR Example:* The PageRank algorithm preferentially surfaces pages with many incoming links, which structurally disadvantages new, niche, or non-English content. This is a *link-based* algorithmic bias.

**3. Emergent / Interaction Bias (1.5 marks):**
- *Definition:* Bias that arises from the *interaction* between users and the system over time, through feedback loops.
- *IR Example:* A user clicks only on sensationalist news headlines; the click signal retrains the ranker, which then surfaces more sensationalist content, creating a filter bubble.

**4. Data / Training Bias (1 mark — KTU extension):**
- *Definition:* Bias inherited from the training corpus used to build embeddings or index.
- *IR Example:* Word2Vec embeddings trained on Google News exhibit gender bias ("programmer" closer to "man" than "woman"), which contaminates any IR system that uses these embeddings for semantic search.

**5. Presentation / Interface Bias (0.5 marks — KTU extension):**
- *Definition:* Bias introduced by UI choices.
- *IR Example:* Showing 10 blue links per SERP hides diversity beyond the first page.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** Define **Differential Privacy**. Derive the basic composition theorem for differential privacy. Why is differential privacy important for IR systems? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ is $\varepsilon$-differentially private if, for all neighbouring datasets $D$ and $D'$ (datasets that differ in at most one record) and for all measurable subsets $S$ of the output space:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \le e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]
$$

In simpler terms, the inclusion or exclusion of any *single* user's data changes the probability of any output by at most a factor of $e^{\varepsilon}$.

**Derivation of the Basic Composition Theorem (3 marks):**
Let $\mathcal{M}_1$ be $\varepsilon_1$-DP and $\mathcal{M}_2$ be $\varepsilon_2$-DP. Consider the composed mechanism $\mathcal{M} = (\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2)$ that releases both outputs.

For any neighbouring $D, D'$ and any output pair $(s_1, s_2)$:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) = (s_1, s_2)] = \Pr[\mathcal{M}_1(D) = s_1] \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}_2(D) = s_2]
$$

Applying the DP property of $\mathcal{M}_1$:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}_1(D) = s_1] \le e^{\varepsilon_1} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}_1(D') = s_1]
$$

Applying the DP property of $\mathcal{M}_2$:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}_2(D) = s_2] \le e^{\varepsilon_2} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}_2(D') = s_2]
$$

Multiplying the two inequalities:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) = (s_1, s_2)] \le e^{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') = (s_1, s_2)]
$$

Hence the composed mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ is $(\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2)$-differentially private, proving the **basic composition theorem**:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2
$$

**Importance for IR (2 marks):**
- *Query log protection:* Search engines can publish aggregate query statistics without revealing individual queries.
- *Federated personalization:* User models can be trained on-device with DP guarantees, enabling personalization without centralized data collection.
- *Legal compliance:* DP provides a *mathematically defensible* privacy guarantee, satisfying GDPR's "data protection by design" requirement.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain **Fair Ranking** in Information Retrieval. Discuss the **relevance-fairness trade-off** with a suitable mathematical formulation. Provide a real-world example where this trade-off matters. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Fair Ranking (1.5 marks):**
Fair ranking is the design of retrieval and re-ranking algorithms that produce a *top-k* result list which is simultaneously (a) relevant to the query and (b) equitable in its exposure of protected groups (e.g., authors of different genders, regions, or political viewpoints). It moves beyond the classical "relevance-only" paradigm of IR.

**Mathematical Formulation (2.5 marks):**
The fair ranking objective is formulated as a *constrained optimization*:

$$
\min_{\pi} \; \mathcal{L}(\pi, q, D) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathcal{F}(\pi, A) \ge \tau
$$

Where:
- $\pi$ is the ranking permutation of documents $D$ for query $q$.
- $\mathcal{L}(\pi, q, D)$ is the standard relevance loss (e.g., listwise DCG loss).
- $\mathcal{F}(\pi, A)$ is the fairness metric evaluated over protected attribute $A$ (e.g., demographic parity or exposure equity).
- $\tau$ is the minimum fairness threshold (e.g., 0.8 for the 4/5ths rule).

An equivalent unconstrained Lagrangian formulation is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{fair}} = (1 - \lambda) \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{relevance}} + \lambda \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{fairness}}
$$

where $\lambda \in [0, 1]$ controls the relevance-fairness trade-off.

**The Trade-off (1.5 marks):**
- Setting $\lambda = 0$ ignores fairness and may produce highly relevant but discriminatory rankings.
- Setting $\lambda = 1$ ignores relevance and produces fair but useless results.
- The optimal $\lambda$ is *application-specific*: in job-search IR, fairness may be weighted more heavily; in medical IR, relevance dominates.

**Real-World Example (1.5 marks):**
Consider an IR system used by a university admissions office to shortlist scholarship candidates from a diverse applicant pool. A pure-relevance system ($\lambda = 0$) might systematically under-rank applicants from rural or non-English-medium backgrounds, mirroring pre-existing societal inequalities. A fair-ranking system ($\lambda > 0$) would boost the visibility of these applicants, ensuring equitable exposure — even at the cost of a small drop in average relevance score. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically references this *tension* as a key real-world concern.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic — Examiner's Pitfall List:**
>
> 1. **Conflating privacy and bias.** They are *related* but *distinct* concepts. Privacy is about *the user being protected from the system*; bias is about *the system treating entities unfairly*. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for using the two terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the fourth type of bias.** Most students list only pre-existing, technical, and emergent bias, missing **data bias** and **presentation bias**, which the 2024 scheme explicitly adds. This loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Not stating a numerical threshold.** When discussing fairness, always state the **4/5ths rule** ($DIR \ge 0.8$) and the **statistical parity threshold** ($SPD \approx 0$). Examiners reward quantitative precision.
> 4. **Omitting the architectural diagram.** A 7-mark privacy question without a labelled diagram is capped at 5 marks. Always include a layered block diagram (see Section 4.1).
> 5. **Treating differential privacy as "just adding noise."** Examiners expect the *mathematical definition* with $\varepsilon$ and the *composition theorem*. A vague "adds random noise" answer gets only 1 of 2 marks.
> 6. **Ignoring the Indian context.** Under NEP 2020 and the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act 2023, *Indian* students are expected to know that India has its own data-protection law equivalent to GDPR. Reference this for full marks.
> 7. **No mention of generative IR.** The 2024 scheme *specifically* flags ethical risks in LLM-based retrieval (RAG pipelines). At least one line about bias/memorization in RAG is rewarded.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### High-Density Revision Checklist

- **Privacy in IR** is multi-layered: Collection, Storage, Processing, Output.
- **Bias in IR** has *five* recognized types: pre-existing, technical, data, emergent, presentation.
- **Differential Privacy** is the gold-standard mathematical privacy guarantee, parameterized by privacy budget $\varepsilon$ (smaller is better; $\varepsilon \le 1$ is strong).
- **k-Anonymity** requires each record to be indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ others; **l-Diversity** adds diversity to sensitive attributes.
- **Statistical Parity Difference (SPD)** and **Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR)** are the two most-tested fairness metrics. $DIR \ge 0.8$ is the legal fairness threshold.
- **Equal Opportunity Difference (EOD)** compares true-positive rates across groups; $EOD = 0$ is fair.
- **Position bias** in click models causes emergent bias; mitigated by counterfactual click correction and on-device aggregation.
- **Fair ranking** is a constrained optimization: maximize relevance subject to a fairness threshold, or minimize a weighted sum $\mathcal{L}_{\text{fair}} = (1-\lambda)\mathcal{L}_{\text{relevance}} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{\text{fairness}}$.
- **Real-world triggers to memorize:** Cambridge Analytica (2018, privacy), Google's gender bias in image search (2021, bias), DPDP Act India (2023, privacy), EU AI Act (2024, bias audits).
- **Filter Bubble** is the canonical example of *emergent* bias in personalized IR.
- **NEP 2020 alignment:** Ethical IR is a graduate attribute — engineers must design for *privacy-by-design* and *fairness-by-design*.
- **Generative IR / RAG** inherits all classical IR ethics issues and adds: hallucination, memorization of private training data, and opaque embedding-based ranking.
- **Mnemonic for bias types:** "**P**eople **T**hink **D**ata **E**merges from **P**resentation" → Pre-existing, Technical, Data, Emergent, Presentation.

### Final KTU Mantra
> *"An ethically sound IR system is one where the user is **protected from the system's knowledge of them**, and the content is **treated equitably by the system's view of it**."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ethics of Search Engines and IR Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Ethics of Search Engines and IR Systems** is a branch of applied information ethics that critically examines the moral responsibilities, societal impact, and value-laden decisions embedded within the design, development, deployment, and operation of Information Retrieval (IR) systems — most notably web search engines, recommender systems, and question-answering platforms.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is positioned at the intersection of **Computer Science, Information Science, and Applied Philosophy**, evaluating how ranking algorithms, data collection pipelines, and result-presentation interfaces influence human cognition, autonomy, democracy, and equity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim style for exams):**
> *"Ethics of Search Engines and IR Systems refers to the systematic study of moral principles governing the behavior of algorithms that mediate access to information, including issues of bias, fairness, privacy, transparency, accountability, and manipulation, with the goal of ensuring that retrieval systems serve human flourishing without causing harm."*

The formal sub-domains covered in Module 4 are:

1. **Bias in Search** — algorithmic, data, presentation, and interaction biases.
2. **Fairness in IR** — group fairness, individual fairness, exposure fairness.
3. **Privacy** — data collection, user profiling, search history exploitation.
4. **Transparency and Explainability** — "black-box" ranking, right to explanation.
5. **Filter Bubbles & Echo Chambers** — personalization-induced information narrowing.
6. **Manipulation & Adversarial IR** — SEO spam, click fraud, ranking manipulation.
7. **Accountability and Governance** — who is responsible when IR fails.
8. **Legal Frameworks** — GDPR, EU AI Act, Indian IT Act, Section 230 (US).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Librarian" Metaphor

Imagine you walk into a massive library containing **every book ever written**. A librarian stands at the counter. You ask: *"Librarian, give me information on climate change."*

- **Scenario A (Ethical):** The librarian hands you books from **diverse authors** — scientists, economists, skeptics, activists — clearly labeled with publication dates and credentials. The librarian tells you, *"I ranked these by how often other readers checked them out, and here is why this book came first."*

- **Scenario B (Unethical):** The librarian only hands you books from **one political perspective**, hides that she was paid by an oil company to prioritize certain titles, secretly records your reading habits, and refuses to explain why she ranked a misleading book at the top.

In this analogy:
- The **librarian = the search engine / IR system**.
- The **books = documents in the corpus**.
- The **ranking order = the retrieval algorithm's output**.
- The **payment = algorithmic bias / commercial influence**.
- The **recording = privacy violation**.
- The **refusal to explain = opacity / black-box problem**.

Search engines are the **gatekeepers of the digital information age**. Unlike a human librarian, however, they serve **billions of queries per day** with algorithms no single human fully understands. Ethics asks: *Who watches the watchmen?*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Module 4 questions are typically 3-mark definitional ("Define filter bubble") or 7–14 mark analytical ("Discuss the types of bias in search engines with examples"). Always tie your answer to a **real-world case** (e.g., 2016 US election misinformation, Cambridge Analytica scandal) to score full marks.

## 1.3 Scope of the Module

| Dimension | Ethical Question |
|---|---|
| **Technical** | Are ranking algorithms fair across demographic groups? |
| **Social** | Do search engines reinforce societal inequalities? |
| **Political** | Can search rankings influence elections? |
| **Economic** | Do sponsored results unfairly dominate organic ones? |
| **Legal** | Is personal data used for IR profiling legally defensible? |
| **Philosophical** | Does personalization undermine epistemic autonomy? |

## 1.4 Why KTU 2024 Includes This Topic

The 2024 scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) mandates **ethics, sustainability, and human values** as cross-cutting themes. PECDT745 (Information Retrieval) — being an **Program Elective (PECD T)** — is expected to prepare engineers who build IR systems. The module thus ensures graduates can:

- Recognize bias in their own algorithms.
- Comply with Indian and global data protection laws.
- Design for **fairness, accountability, and transparency (FAccT)**.
- Articulate the societal consequences of their technical decisions.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Taxonomy of Ethical Concerns in IR

Ethical issues in IR can be organized into a hierarchical taxonomy. This is the **single most exam-relevant framework** in this module.

### 2.1.1 Bias in Search Engines

**Definition (KTU style):** *Systematic skew in the retrieval or ranking of documents that systematically disadvantages certain groups, viewpoints, or types of content.*

**Types of Bias:**

| # | Bias Type | Definition | Real-World Example | Root Cause |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Algorithmic Bias** | Bias embedded in the ranking function itself | Google's 2015 autocomplete scandal (anti-Semitic suggestions) | Mathematical formulation of relevance |
| 2 | **Data / Corpus Bias** | Bias present in the training/indexed data | Gender bias: "CEO" → male results dominate | Web demography is not uniform |
| 3 | **Presentation Bias** | Bias from how results are shown (top vs. bottom) | 90% of clicks go to top-3 results | Position bias in user attention |
| 4 | **Interaction Bias** | Bias learned from user clicks | Trending results stay trending | Reinforcement loops |
| 5 | **Selection Bias** | Bias from which documents are crawled/indexed | Deep web vs. surface web | Crawler coverage gaps |
| 6 | **Commercial / Advertising Bias** | Paid results mixed with organic ones | "Ad" labels often missed by users | Revenue-driven ranking |
| 7 | **Confirmation Bias (user-side)** | IR amplifies user pre-existing beliefs | Filter bubble creation | Personalization algorithms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Note:**
> The *Exposure Fairness* principle states: **"Protected demographic groups should receive equal exposure to relevant opportunities through retrieval systems."** This is mathematically formalized as the **Disparate Exposure Ratio (DER)**.

### 2.1.2 Fairness in IR

Two competing formalizations:

**A. Group Fairness (Demographic Parity)**

$$P(\text{rel} \mid g_1) = P(\text{rel} \mid g_2) = \dots = P(\text{rel} \mid g_k)$$

where $g_i$ are demographic groups (e.g., male, female, non-binary) and "rel" denotes being judged relevant.

**B. Individual Fairness (Dwork et al., 2012)**

$$d_{\mathcal{Y}}(R(x_i), R(x_j)) \leq L \cdot d_{\mathcal{X}}(x_i, x_j)$$

where $R(\cdot)$ is the ranker, $d_{\mathcal{X}}$ and $d_{\mathcal{Y}}$ are distance metrics, and $L$ is a Lipschitz constant. *Similar individuals must receive similar treatment.*

**C. Exposure Fairness (Singh & Joachims, 2018)**

For position $p$ with attention weight $a_p$:

$$\text{Exposure}(d) = \sum_{p=1}^{n} \frac{a_p}{\log_2(p+1)} \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{doc } d \text{ at position } p]$$

The goal is to ensure proportional exposure to demographic representation.

### 2.1.3 Privacy

**Three Pillars of Privacy in IR (OECD framework):**

1. **Collection Limitation Principle** — Data should be collected only with consent.
2. **Purpose Specification Principle** — Data used only for stated retrieval purposes.
3. **Use Limitation Principle** — Data not disclosed/shared without consent.

Search engines collect: query logs, click-through rates, location, device fingerprint, IP address, cookies, behavioral patterns. Each item is a **potential privacy vector**.

**Differential Privacy (DP)** is the gold-standard mathematical framework:

$$\Pr[M(D) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[M(D') \in S] + \delta$$

where $D$ and $D'$ differ by one record, $\epsilon$ is the privacy budget (smaller = more private), $\delta$ is failure probability, and $M$ is a randomized mechanism.

### 2.1.4 Transparency & Explainability

The **Right to Explanation** (GDPR Article 22) entitles users to "meaningful information about the logic involved" in automated decisions.

Three levels of explainability:

| Level | Audience | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Algorithmic** | Engineers | Model internals, feature importance |
| **Local** | Affected user | "Why was I denied a loan?" |
| **Global** | Public/Regulators | Aggregate behavior across users |

### 2.1.5 Filter Bubbles & Echo Chambers

**Filter Bubble** (Eli Pariser, 2011): A state of intellectual isolation that occurs when algorithms personalize information such that users are exposed only to opinions and content aligned with their prior beliefs.

**Echo Chamber** (Sunstein, 2001): A social environment where users encounter only opinions reinforcing their own, due to self-selection plus algorithmic amplification.

Mathematical model of personalization-induced narrowing:

$$H_{\text{post}}(U) \leq H_{\text{pre}}(U)$$

where $H$ is the Shannon entropy of the user's information diet $U$. Post-personalization entropy is **bounded above** by pre-personalization entropy.

$$H(U) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(u_i) \log_2 p(u_i)$$

A drop in $H$ means **information diversity loss** — the filter bubble effect.

### 2.1.6 Manipulation & Adversarial IR

- **Search Engine Optimization (SEO) spam** — Manipulating ranking through link farms, keyword stuffing, cloaking.
- **Click fraud** — Automated clicks on ads to drain competitor budgets.
- **Adversarial ranking** — Crafting documents that exploit model weaknesses.
- **Astroturfing** — Fake grassroots reviews to game recommender systems.

### 2.1.7 Accountability

The **"Accountability Gap"** occurs when:

- The algorithm is proprietary (trade secret).
- The training data is not public.
- The harm is distributed across millions of users.
- The decision is non-deterministic.

This makes it difficult to assign moral or legal responsibility.

### 2.1.8 Legal & Regulatory Frameworks (KTU-relevant)

| Regulation | Region | Key Principle for IR |
|---|---|---|
| **GDPR** | EU | Right to explanation, right to be forgotten |
| **CCPA** | California, US | Data access and deletion rights |
| **EU AI Act** | EU | High-risk AI classification, transparency |
| **Indian IT Act 2000 + DPDP Act 2023** | India | Consent-based data processing |
| **Section 230** | US | Platform liability shield (debated) |
| **Right to Be Forgotten** | Multiple | Article 17 GDPR — delisting from search results |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative formula you may need for Module 4 numerical or analytical questions. The vertical pipe symbol has been replaced with `\mid` to preserve table formatting.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Shannon Entropy (filter bubble metric)** | $H(U) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(u_i) \log_2 p(u_i)$ | $p(u_i)$: probability of topic $u_i$ | Measure information diet diversity |
| 2 | **Group Fairness (Demographic Parity)** | $P(\text{rel} \mid g_1) = P(\text{rel} \mid g_2)$ | $g_i$: demographic group | Test if ranking is fair across groups |
| 3 | **Individual Fairness (Lipschitz)** | $d_{\mathcal{Y}}(R(x_i), R(x_j)) \leq L \cdot d_{\mathcal{X}}(x_i, x_j)$ | $R$: ranker, $L$: Lipschitz constant | Similar queries → similar rankings |
| 4 | **Exposure of Document** | $\text{Exposure}(d) = \sum_{p=1}^{n} \dfrac{a_p}{\log_2(p+1)} \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{doc } d \text{ at pos } p]$ | $a_p$: attention at position $p$ | Quantify demographic exposure |
| 5 | **Disparate Exposure Ratio (DER)** | $\text{DER} = \dfrac{\text{Exposure}(g_1)}{\text{Exposure}(g_2)}$ | $g_1, g_2$: protected groups | DER $\approx 1$ = fair |
| 6 | **Differential Privacy** | $\Pr[M(D) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[M(D') \in S] + \delta$ | $\epsilon$: privacy budget, $\delta$: failure | Quantify privacy guarantee |
| 7 | **Click-Through Rate (CTR)** | $\text{CTR} = \dfrac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}}$ | Clicks, Impressions | Measure interaction bias |
| 8 | **NDCG (revisited for fairness)** | $\text{NDCG@k} = \dfrac{\text{DCG@k}}{\text{IDCG@k}}$ | DCG, IDCG | Combine relevance and fairness |
| 9 | **Position Bias Discount** | $a_p = \dfrac{1}{\log_2(p+1)}$ | $p$: rank position | Model attention by position |
| 10 | **Bias Amplification Factor** | $\text{BAF} = \dfrac{\text{Bias}_{\text{output}}}{\text{Bias}_{\text{input}}}$ | Output vs. input bias | Measure how much model amplifies bias |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These ethical principles are not abstract — they are **engineering specifications**:

- **Search engine developers** at Google, Bing, Yandex, Baidu use exposure fairness metrics to audit rankings.
- **HR-tech startups** building resume retrieval must use demographic-parity testing (US EEOC compliance).
- **Healthcare IR systems** (e.g., PubMed, UpToDate) must apply differential privacy to patient-derived queries.
- **Recommender systems** on Flipkart/Amazon use filter-bubble mitigation techniques to surface diverse results.
- **Legal-tech IR** (Westlaw, LexisNexis) must implement right-to-be-forgotten delisting mechanisms.
- **Indian government IR** (e.g., UMANG, DigiLocker search) must comply with the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "Why is ethics important in IR?", always anchor your answer to **three concrete technical mechanisms** (e.g., ranking, personalization, profiling) and **one societal consequence** (e.g., democratic erosion, exclusion).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Position-Bias Discount and Exposure Fairness

We derive the standard **exposure formula** used in fairness-aware ranking, starting from a behavioral model of how users scan search results.

### Step 1: Model User Attention by Position

Empirical eye-tracking studies (e.g., Joachims et al., 2007) show that users examine position $p$ with probability inversely proportional to $\log_2(p+1)$. Define the **position-bias weight**:

$$a_p = \frac{1}{\log_2(p + 1)}$$

This is sometimes called the **cascade click model weight** or the **DCG discount**. For $p=1$, $a_1 = 1/\log_2 2 = 1$. For $p=10$, $a_{10} = 1/\log_2 11 \approx 0.289$.

**Logic:** Position 1 is examined with weight 1.0; position 10 with weight 0.29. The discount follows a logarithmic decay — not linear, not quadratic — because users scan top results more carefully, then browse with diminishing focus.

### Step 2: Define Exposure of a Document

A document $d$ is placed at some position $p$ in a ranking of length $n$. It receives exposure equal to the position bias weight, but only if it is present at that position (an indicator):

$$\text{Exposure}(d) = \sum_{p=1}^{n} a_p \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{doc } d \text{ at position } p]$$

**Logic:** Sum across all positions; the indicator $\mathbb{1}$ ensures the term contributes only when $d$ is at $p$.

### Step 3: Aggregate Exposure to a Group

A demographic group $g$ consists of a set of documents $\mathcal{D}_g$. The total exposure to group $g$ is:

$$\text{Exposure}(g) = \sum_{d \in \mathcal{D}_g} \text{Exposure}(d) = \sum_{d \in \mathcal{D}_g} \sum_{p=1}^{n} a_p \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{doc } d \text{ at position } p]$$

Swapping the order of summation (a standard technique):

$$\text{Exposure}(g) = \sum_{p=1}^{n} a_p \cdot \underbrace{\sum_{d \in \mathcal{D}_g} \mathbb{1}[\text{doc } d \text{ at position } p]}_{\text{count of group-}g \text{ docs at position } p}$$

**Logic:** Either way of summing is valid. The double-counting interpretation helps connect to **disparate impact audits**.

### Step 4: Define Disparate Exposure Ratio (DER)

For two groups $g_1$ and $g_2$:

$$\text{DER}(g_1, g_2) = \frac{\text{Exposure}(g_1)}{\text{Exposure}(g_2)}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $\text{DER} = 1$: Groups receive equal exposure. **Fair.**
- $\text{DER} \gg 1$: $g_1$ is over-exposed (favored).
- $\text{DER} \ll 1$: $g_1$ is under-exposed (disadvantaged).

A common threshold is the **"four-fifths rule"** (US EEOC, 1978) — a selection rate less than 80% of the majority group's rate constitutes adverse impact:

$$\text{DER} \geq 0.8 \quad \text{(or equivalently } \text{DER} \leq 1.25\text{)}$$

### Step 5: Worked Example

Consider 5 ranked positions. Documents $A, B$ are in group $g_1$; documents $C, D, E$ are in group $g_2$. Suppose the ranking is: $A, C, B, D, E$.

Position weights:
$$a_1 = 1.000, \quad a_2 = 0.631, \quad a_3 = 0.500, \quad a_4 = 0.431, \quad a_5 = 0.387$$

Exposure for $g_1$ (docs $A$ at pos 1, $B$ at pos 3):

$$\text{Exposure}(g_1) = a_1 + a_3 = 1.000 + 0.500 = 1.500$$

Exposure for $g_2$ (docs $C$ at pos 2, $D$ at pos 4, $E$ at pos 5):

$$\text{Exposure}(g_2) = a_2 + a_4 + a_5 = 0.631 + 0.431 + 0.387 = 1.449$$

Disparate Exposure Ratio:

$$\text{DER} = \frac{1.500}{1.449} \approx 1.035$$

Since $\text{DER} \approx 1$, the ranking is **fair** in terms of exposure. (Recall: actual fairness also requires accounting for relevance, not just exposure.)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** This derivation is a **favourite for 14-mark questions**. Be prepared to derive or apply these steps, possibly with different numerical inputs.

## 3.2 Derivation: Filter Bubble Entropy Reduction

We derive how personalization **reduces** the Shannon entropy of a user's information diet.

### Step 1: Pre-Personalization Distribution

Suppose a user is interested in 4 topics — Politics ($T_1$), Science ($T_2$), Sports ($T_3$), Arts ($T_4$) — with equal prior probability 0.25 each. Pre-personalization entropy:

$$H_{\text{pre}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{4} 0.25 \cdot \log_2 0.25 = -4 \cdot (0.25 \cdot (-2)) = 2.000 \text{ bits}$$

**Logic:** Maximum entropy for a uniform distribution over 4 outcomes is $\log_2 4 = 2$ bits.

### Step 2: Apply Personalization

A personalization algorithm infers the user prefers Politics ($T_1$) and Science ($T_2$). It re-weights the distribution, e.g., $p(T_1) = 0.45, p(T_2) = 0.40, p(T_3) = 0.10, p(T_4) = 0.05$.

### Step 3: Compute Post-Personalization Entropy

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{post}} &= -(0.45 \log_2 0.45 + 0.40 \log_2 0.40 + 0.10 \log_2 0.10 + 0.05 \log_2 0.05) \\
&= -(0.45 \cdot (-1.152) + 0.40 \cdot (-1.322) + 0.10 \cdot (-3.322) + 0.05 \cdot (-4.322)) \\
&= -(-0.5184 - 0.5288 - 0.3322 - 0.2161) \\
&= 1.5955 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Each term $p \log_2 p$ is negative (since $p < 1$), so the leading minus sign makes $H$ positive.

### Step 4: Compute Entropy Reduction

$$\Delta H = H_{\text{pre}} - H_{\text{post}} = 2.000 - 1.596 = 0.404 \text{ bits}$$

Percentage reduction:

$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{\Delta H}{H_{\text{pre}}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.404}{2.000} \times 100\% = 20.2\%$$

**Interpretation:** The user's information diet has lost **20.2% of its diversity** due to personalization — the filter bubble effect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If the question says "personalization leads to $p = (0.7, 0.2, 0.05, 0.05)$," you must recompute. Always show each $\log_2$ step explicitly in the exam — examiners award partial marks for method.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Bias Mitigation Strategies

| Bias Type | Detection Method | Mitigation Strategy | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Algorithmic** | Counterfactual fairness tests | Adversarial debiasing, fairness constraints in loss function | May reduce relevance accuracy |
| **Data** | Corpus demographic audits | Re-sampling, synthetic data, reweighting | Cost of data collection |
| **Presentation** | Click distribution analysis | Re-ranking, diversification (MMR, PM-2) | May hurt relevance for some users |
| **Interaction** | Click debiasing models | Inverse propensity scoring | Requires reliable propensity estimates |
| **Selection** | Coverage analysis | Improve crawl, federated search | Cannot crawl private web |
| **Commercial** | A/B test sponsored vs. organic | Clearer labels, separate sections | Revenue impact |
| **Confirmation** | Diversity metrics | Filter bubble "burst" features, "diverse perspectives" | User pushback |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Differential Privacy for Query Logs

### Step 1: Define the Mechanism

A query log dataset $D$ contains $n$ users' queries. We want to release aggregate statistics (e.g., top-100 queries) while protecting individual privacy.

Define a **randomized mechanism** $M$ that, on input $D$, adds calibrated noise to query counts:

$$M(D) = f(D) + \text{Laplace}\left(0, \frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon}\right)$$

where $f(D)$ is the true count of a query, $\Delta f$ is the **sensitivity** (maximum change in $f$ when one user's record is added/removed, typically $\Delta f = 1$ for counts), and $\epsilon$ is the privacy budget.

### Step 2: Compute Noise Scale

For $\Delta f = 1$ and $\epsilon = 1$:

$$\text{Scale} = \frac{1}{1} = 1$$

The Laplace distribution $\text{Laplace}(0, 1)$ has PDF:

$$p(x) = \frac{1}{2} e^{-|x|}$$

**Logic:** $\Delta f$ governs how much one record can change the output; dividing by $\epsilon$ scales the noise to guarantee the privacy budget.

### Step 3: Verify Privacy Guarantee

For two datasets $D, D'$ differing by one record, and any output $S$:

$$\frac{\Pr[M(D) \in S]}{\Pr[M(D') \in S]} \leq e^{\epsilon} = e \approx 2.718$$

This satisfies the definition of $\epsilon$-differential privacy.

### Step 4: Trade-off

Smaller $\epsilon$ = more noise = better privacy but lower utility. KTU exam may ask: *"What is the noise scale for $\epsilon = 0.1$?"* — Answer: $1/0.1 = 10$, ten times more noise.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always state that $\Delta f = 1$ for "counting queries" (a single user contributes at most 1 to any count). For sum queries, $\Delta f$ equals the maximum user contribution.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ethical Decision Flow in an IR System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Issues Query] --> B[Query Processing]
    B --> C[Retrieve Candidate Documents]
    C --> D[Apply Ranking Algorithm]
    D --> E{Apply Fairness Filters}
    E -->|Pass| F[Apply Personalization Layer]
    E -->|Fail| G[Re-rank for Fairness]
    G --> F
    F --> H{Privacy Audit}
    H -->|DP-compliant| I[Apply Differential Privacy Noise]
    H -->|Non-compliant| J[Anonymize / Suppress Logs]
    I --> K[Generate Final Ranking]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Present Results to User]
    L --> M[Log Interaction Data]
    M --> N[Feedback Loop to Ranker]
    N -. Bias Amplification .-> D
    N -. Privacy Leakage .-> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation:** The dashed arrows from the feedback loop indicate **ethical risks** — the system can amplify bias and leak privacy through the feedback loop. Ethical design must break or audit these loops.


## 4.3 Filter Bubble Formation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Issues Query] --> B[System Logs Behavior]
    B --> C[Build/Update User Profile]
    C --> D[Personalize Rankings]
    D --> E[User Sees Personalized Results]
    E --> F[User Clicks on Familiar Content]
    F --> G[More Behavior Logged]
    G --> C
    C -. Reinforcement .-> D
    D -. Reduced Diversity .-> E
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **self-reinforcing personalization loop** that creates filter bubbles. Note the feedback path $G \to C$, which closes the loop and intensifies narrowing over time.

## 4.4 Fairness-Aware Ranking Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Query + Document Corpus] --> PRED[Predict Relevance Scores]
    PRED --> RANK[Standard Ranker]
    RANK --> FAIR{Are Fairness Constraints Satisfied?}
    FAIR -->|Yes| OUT[Output Ranking]
    FAIR -->|No| RE[Re-ranking Optimizer]
    RE --> RANK
    OUT --> AUDIT[Post-hoc Fairness Audit]
    AUDIT --> REPORT[Compliance Report]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU exam link:** This diagram supports 14-mark answers on "Design an ethical IR pipeline." The key is to show that **fairness is enforced at two stages** — pre-hoc (constraints during ranking) and post-hoc (audits).
