# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the term "Filter Bubble" in the context of Information Retrieval. Give one real-world example.**

**Model Answer:**

A **filter bubble** is a state of intellectual isolation that occurs when an information retrieval or recommendation system personalizes content such that a user is progressively exposed only to information aligned with their pre-existing beliefs, preferences, or behavior history. The concept was popularized by internet activist Eli Pariser (2011).

The mechanism works as follows: when a user interacts with an IR system (clicks, likes, dwell time), the system builds a profile of inferred preferences. Future retrievals are then re-ranked to favor content consistent with this profile, suppressing diverse or contradictory viewpoints. Over time, the user's "information diet" becomes narrower — measured as a drop in Shannon entropy.

**Real-world example:** A user who frequently watches political news from a left-leaning outlet on YouTube will, over time, receive more recommendations of similar content, while conservative or centrist perspectives are deprioritized. This was empirically studied in **FilterBubbles.org (2015)** by the American Institute for Behavioral Research and Technology, which found YouTube's recommendation algorithm gradually channels users toward more extreme content.

**Closing remark (for full marks):** Filter bubbles raise ethical concerns because they limit **epistemic autonomy**, polarize public discourse, and can entrench misinformation. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the concept of "Right to be Forgotten" in the context of search engines. Mention the relevant legal provision.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Right to be Forgotten (RTBF)** is a legal principle that grants individuals the right to request the removal of personal information from search engine results and other digital platforms, particularly when the information is outdated, irrelevant, or no longer serves a public interest.

**Origin and Legal Provision:** The RTBF was established as a fundamental right under **Article 17 of the European Union's General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)**, which came into force in May 2018. The legal precedent stems from the 2014 case of *Google Spain SL, Google Inc. v AEPD and Mario Costeja González* (Case C-131/12), where the Court of Justice of the European Union ruled that individuals have the right to delist search results that contain personal data that is "inadequate, irrelevant, or no longer relevant."

**Mechanism:** A user submits a delisting request to the search engine. The engine evaluates it against criteria — public interest, role in public life, time elapsed, accuracy — and decides whether to remove the link from search results for queries containing the user's name. Note: the underlying web page is **not deleted**; only the search result link is suppressed.

**Indian context:** While India does not yet have an explicit constitutional RTBF, the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act)** introduces data-erasure rights, and the **Supreme Court of India** in *Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017)* recognized privacy as a fundamental right, providing a constitutional foundation for RTBF in India. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Discuss the different types of bias that can occur in search engine rankings. Provide an example for each. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Bias in search engines refers to **systematic skews** in the retrieval or ranking of documents that systematically disadvantage certain groups, viewpoints, or content types. Below are the major types:

**1. Algorithmic Bias (2 marks)**
Bias embedded in the mathematical formulation of the ranking function itself. Example: A ranking function that uses click-through rate as a relevance signal will systematically favor popular documents, even if they are not the most relevant for niche queries. Google's 2015 autocomplete issue — where typing "are Jews" produced anti-Semitic suggestions — is an example of algorithmic bias learned from biased training data.

**2. Data / Corpus Bias (1 mark)**
The indexed web itself is biased. For example, Wikipedia entries about female scientists tend to be shorter and have fewer references than those about male counterparts. Since search engines index Wikipedia prominently, this bias propagates to the search results. A query for "famous physicists" returns a male-dominated list, reflecting the underlying corpus demographics.

**3. Presentation Bias (1 mark)**
Users overwhelmingly click on the top-3 results (studies show ~75% of clicks), so the position of a result determines its visibility. This creates a feedback loop: top results get more clicks, more clicks signal relevance, and the system reinforces their top placement. Example: Amazon product search where the first-listed product sells disproportionately more, regardless of quality.

**4. Interaction Bias (1 mark)**
Learned from user clicks — if users click on a result, the system assumes relevance, but clicks can reflect position (a user clicks the top result without reading alternatives). This is called **position bias** in click models.

**5. Selection Bias (1 mark)**
Search engines cannot index the deep web (databases, private networks, paywalled content). Thus, "CEO" queries over-represent CEOs with significant online presence, often skewing toward male, Western executives. The "invisible web" introduces structural bias.

**6. Commercial / Advertising Bias (1 mark)**
Sponsored results are mixed with organic results, often in identical formatting. Studies (e.g., IMK, 2013) show users often miss "Ad" labels, leading to inadvertent trust in commercial content. Google's policy of separating ads is a partial mitigation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Many students conflate "algorithmic bias" with all other types. Examiners expect **distinct, technically correct definitions** for each type. **Loses 2 marks** if you mix up presentation bias with interaction bias, or skip commercial bias entirely.

---

#### (b) Explain the concept of Fairness in Information Retrieval. Compare Group Fairness, Individual Fairness, and Exposure Fairness with suitable mathematical formulations. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Fairness in IR refers to the principle that retrieval systems should **not systematically discriminate** against individuals or groups based on protected attributes such as gender, race, age, religion, or socioeconomic status. It is a multi-dimensional concept formalized in different ways.

**1. Group Fairness (Demographic Parity) (2 marks)**
Group fairness requires that the probability of being judged relevant (or being retrieved) is **equal across demographic groups**.

$$P(\text{rel} \mid g_i) = P(\text{rel} \mid g_j) \quad \forall \text{ groups } g_i, g_j$$

**Example:** A resume-search IR system for hiring must retrieve an equal proportion of equally-qualified male and female candidates. If 30% of relevant resumes are female in the corpus, then 30% of top-ranked resumes should be female.

**Strength:** Easy to measure and audit.
**Weakness:** Treats all group members identically, ignoring merit differences.

**2. Individual Fairness (Dwork et al., 2012) (2.5 marks)**
Individual fairness states that **similar individuals should receive similar treatment** by the ranker. Formally, if two queries/documents $x_i$ and $x_j$ are similar in the input space (distance $d_{\mathcal{X}}$), then their outputs (rankings) should also be similar (distance $d_{\mathcal{Y}}$), bounded by a Lipschitz constant $L$:

$$d_{\mathcal{Y}}(R(x_i), R(x_j)) \leq L \cdot d_{\mathcal{X}}(x_i, x_j)$$

**Example:** Two identical job-seekers (same experience, same skills) must receive the same ranking from a job-board IR system, regardless of demographic attributes that are not job-relevant.

**Strength:** Honors individual merit, harder to "game" by group labels.
**Weakness:** Requires a meaningful distance metric in both input and output spaces, which is hard to design.

**3. Exposure Fairness (Singh & Joachims, 2018) (2.5 marks)**
Exposure fairness focuses on **the attention that documents receive in ranked lists**. Position $p$ has attention weight $a_p = 1/\log_2(p+1)$. Exposure of a document is:

$$\text{Exposure}(d) = \sum_{p=1}^{n} a_p \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{doc } d \text{ at position } p]$$

The principle states that **protected demographic groups should receive exposure proportional to their relevance**, or that **disparate exposure ratio (DER)** should be close to 1.

**Example:** In image-search for "CEO", the demographic distribution of CEOs in top-100 results should match the demographic distribution in the population of qualified CEOs. If women are 30% of qualified CEOs but receive 10% exposure, the system is unfair.

**Comparison Table (must be in answer):**

| Dimension | Group | Individual | Exposure |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit of analysis** | Demographic group | Single individual | Documents and groups |
| **Mathematical form** | Probability equality | Lipschitz bound | Attention-weighted sum |
| **Strength** | Easy to audit | Honors merit | Captures ranking-specific issues |
| **Weakness** | Ignores merit | Hard distance metrics | Requires relevance data |
| **Best for** | Hiring, lending, regulatory compliance | Personalized systems | Search, recommender systems |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Loss of Marks:** Students often describe all three notions qualitatively but **omit the mathematical formulation**. KTU 2024 ESE explicitly tests mathematical precision — always include at least one equation per fairness type. Also, do NOT confuse exposure fairness with "click fairness" — exposure is **received attention**, not actual clicks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Alternative Choice]`

#### (a) What is Privacy in the context of Information Retrieval? Explain Differential Privacy with a suitable example. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Privacy in IR:** Privacy in IR refers to the **protection of user information** that is collected, processed, or stored during the act of retrieving information. Search engines collect: query terms, click patterns, IP addresses, location data, device fingerprints, and session durations. Each is a potential privacy vector.

The **OECD Privacy Framework (1980, revised 2013)** lays out core principles: collection limitation, purpose specification, use limitation, data quality, transparency, individual participation, and accountability. India's **DPDP Act 2023** aligns with these.

**Why is privacy a problem in IR?**

- Search queries are highly sensitive (e.g., medical, financial, political).
- Aggregated query logs can re-identify individuals (Netflix Prize 2007 de-anonymization case).
- Profiling enables manipulation, discrimination, and surveillance.

**Differential Privacy (DP) — Formal Definition (4 marks):**

A randomized mechanism $M$ provides $\epsilon$-differential privacy if for all datasets $D, D'$ differing on one record, and for any set of outputs $S$:

$$\Pr[M(D) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[M(D') \in S] + \delta$$

where $\epsilon$ is the **privacy budget** (smaller = stronger privacy) and $\delta$ is the probability of catastrophic failure (usually negligible, e.g., $10^{-5}$).

**Mechanism — Laplace Mechanism (2 marks):**

For numerical queries $f(D)$ (e.g., count of "diabetes" queries), noise is added:

$$M(D) = f(D) + \text{Laplace}\left(0, \frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon}\right)$$

where $\Delta f$ is the sensitivity (max change from one record). For counting queries, $\Delta f = 1$.

**Worked Example (1 mark):**

Suppose a hospital publishes the top-100 most-queried medical terms from its search portal, without DP. An attacker who knows their neighbor searched for "psychiatrist appointment" can verify this in the top-100. With DP at $\epsilon = 1$ and $\Delta f = 1$, the published count for "psychiatrist appointment" becomes:

$$M(D) = 50 + \text{Laplace}(0, 1) = 50 + 0.7 = 50.7 \text{ (or any noisy value) }$$

The noise hides the true count, making re-identification infeasible. Apple, Google, and the US Census Bureau all use DP in production systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students write "$e^{-\epsilon}$" instead of "$e^{\epsilon}$" — the correct bound uses $e^{\epsilon}$ in the **numerator** (i.e., output probabilities from neighboring datasets are at most $e^{\epsilon}$ times more likely). Mixing this up loses 1 mark.

---

#### (b) Discuss the ethical implications of search engine manipulation and filter bubbles on democratic processes. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Search engines mediate access to information for billions of users. Their rankings shape public opinion, voting behavior, and democratic discourse. Two key ethical concerns are **search result manipulation** and **filter bubble formation**.

**1. Search Result Manipulation (2.5 marks)**

Search engine manipulation refers to deliberate alteration of rankings — by either internal (corporate) or external (adversarial) actors — to influence user beliefs or behavior.

- **State-sponsored manipulation:** Authoritarian regimes (e.g., China's "Great Firewall" or Russia's Yandex political ranking) suppress dissent, censor opposition candidates, and amplify state propaganda through search results.
- **Corporate manipulation:** Google was fined €2.42 billion by the EU (2017) for illegally favoring its own comparison-shopping service in search results — an antitrust and ethical violation of result neutrality.
- **SEO manipulation:** Astroturfing, link farms, and content farms can game algorithms to elevate misleading or low-quality content. In 2016, fake news stories outperformed legitimate news on Facebook and Google during the US election cycle.
- **Cambridge Analytica (2018):** Used search and social-media profiling to micro-target political ads based on psychometric models — a clear case of IR-driven manipulation of democratic processes.

**2. Filter Bubbles (2.5 marks)**

Filter bubbles arise when personalization algorithms narrow a user's information exposure. As derived in Section 3.2, post-personalization entropy is **lower** than pre-personalization entropy, meaning the user sees a less diverse set of viewpoints.

**Effects on democracy:**

- **Polarization:** When users see only ideologically aligned content, opposing viewpoints are never encountered, hardening political divisions. Studies (e.g., Bakshy et al., 2015, *Science*) show Facebook's News Feed algorithm **reduced cross-cutting exposure by 5–17%**, while user self-selection accounted for the rest.
- **Echo chambers:** Reinforce existing beliefs, making voters less receptive to counter-arguments. This undermines the **deliberative ideal** of democracy, in which citizens engage with diverse perspectives.
- **Misinformation amplification:** Within filter bubbles, false information circulates without fact-checkers' corrections reaching the user, leading to misinformed voting.
- **Epistemic injustice:** Marginalized voices are systematically under-represented in personalized feeds, reducing their democratic participation.

**3. Mitigation Strategies (1 mark)**

- **Search-diversification algorithms:** Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) re-ranking to surface diverse perspectives.
- **Serendipity features:** "News from across the aisle" or "Opposing view" buttons (used by some news aggregators).
- **Regulatory interventions:** EU's Digital Services Act (DSA) and AI Act mandate risk assessments for recommender systems.
- **Media literacy:** Educating users to recognize personalization effects.
- **Algorithmic audits:** Independent third-party audits of ranking fairness and exposure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students answer this question as a general "social media" essay and forget the **IR-specific mechanisms** (ranking, exposure, profile inference). Always anchor to **retrieval-system mechanisms** for full marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in This Module:**
> 1. **No concrete examples** — Examiners expect 1 real-world case per bias type (e.g., "2016 US election", "Google autocomplete scandal", "Amazon hiring bias").
> 2. **Confusing filter bubble with echo chamber** — A filter bubble is **algorithm-induced**; an echo chamber is a **broader social phenomenon** (algorithm + self-selection).
> 3. **Forgetting mathematical formulation** — For any named principle (DP, exposure fairness, individual fairness), you must include the equation. Without math, expect 50% marks.
> 4. **Writing in generalities about "AI bias"** — Stay specific to **IR systems** (ranking, retrieval, indexing). Generic AI-ethics answers get partial credit.
> 5. **Ignoring Indian legal context** — KTU is an Indian university; the 2023 DPDP Act, 2017 *Puttaswamy* judgment, and IT Act 2000 are **frequently tested**. Always localize.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Ethics of Search Engines and IR Systems**

- ✅ **Bias has 7 types:** Algorithmic, Data, Presentation, Interaction, Selection, Commercial, Confirmation — know one example for each.
- ✅ **Fairness has 3 formalizations:** Group (probability equality), Individual (Lipschitz bound), Exposure (attention-weighted).
- ✅ **Exposure formula:** $\text{Exposure}(d) = \sum_{p=1}^{n} a_p \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{doc at pos } p]$, where $a_p = 1/\log_2(p+1)$.
- ✅ **Filter bubble formula:** Reduce Shannon entropy of user information diet: $H_{\text{post}} < H_{\text{pre}}$.
- ✅ **Differential Privacy:** $\Pr[M(D) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[M(D') \in S] + \delta$. Smaller $\epsilon$ = more private.
- ✅ **Right to be Forgotten:** Article 17 GDPR; *Google Spain* case (2014). India: DPDP Act 2023 + *Puttaswamy* (2017).
- ✅ **Explainability has 3 levels:** Algorithmic (engineers), Local (affected user), Global (regulators).
- ✅ **Cambridge Analytica (2018)** = classic example of IR-based political manipulation.
- ✅ **EU AI Act (2024)** classifies search/recommender systems as **limited-risk** but with transparency obligations.
- ✅ **Position bias:** ~75% of clicks go to top-3 results — drives presentation bias and feedback loops.
- ✅ **Indian law stack:** IT Act 2000, DPDP Act 2023, *Puttaswamy* (2017) for fundamental right to privacy.
- ✅ **Algorithmic audit requirement:** Pre-hoc constraints (during ranking) AND post-hoc audits.
- ✅ **Trade-off in DP:** Privacy ↑ ⇒ Utility ↓ (more noise).
- ✅ **Echo chamber ≠ Filter bubble:** Echo = social + algorithmic; Filter bubble = purely algorithmic.
- ✅ **FAccT principles** (Fairness, Accountability, Transparency) are the foundational design pillars.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Ethics of Search Engines and IR Systems**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AI in Information Retrieval: Deep Learning and Neural IR Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** AI in Information Retrieval: Deep Learning and Neural IR Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Neural Information Retrieval (Neural IR)** is the application of deep learning architectures—particularly neural network models trained via representation learning—to the classical Information Retrieval pipeline, with the goal of mapping queries and documents into a shared semantic vector space where relevance can be computed via geometric similarity.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context (Course Outcome CO4 / PECDT745, Module 4), the topic is officially framed as: *"Application of Artificial Intelligence paradigms—specifically deep neural networks and representation learning—to bridge the lexical gap inherent in traditional keyword-based retrieval systems."*

The core paradigm shift can be summarized as:

| Aspect | Classical (Lexical) IR | Neural (Semantic) IR |
|---|---|---|
| Matching Mechanism | Exact token overlap | Distributed semantic similarity |
| Document Representation | Sparse vector (TF-IDF, BM25) | Dense embedding vector |
| Vocabulary Problem | Vocabulary mismatch (synonymy) | Solved via embeddings |
| Generalization | Bag-of-words assumption | Context-aware via Transformers |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Library Analogy (The "Smart Librarian" vs. "Card Catalogue"):**
>
> Imagine a vast library. The **classical IR system** is a robotic librarian who can only find books whose titles contain *exactly* the words you ask for. If you ask for "automobile repair" but the book is titled "car maintenance," the robot returns nothing.
>
> A **Neural IR system** is a *human* librarian who has *read* and *understood* every book. When you ask for "automobile repair," the librarian instantly knows that "car maintenance," "vehicle servicing," and "auto mechanics" all mean the same thing—and returns the relevant books even if the words don't match. The librarian has built a **mental map (embedding space)** where conceptually similar words sit close together.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Block

- **Word Embedding**: A dense, low-dimensional, real-valued vector representation of a word, learned such that semantically similar words have similar vectors. Standard dimensionality: **d = 100, 200, 300**.
- **Dense Vector**: A vector where most components are non-zero, in contrast to the high-dimensional sparse vectors of TF-IDF.
- **Encoder (fθ)**: A neural network parameterized by weights θ that maps an input text sequence to a fixed-dimensional embedding.
- **Cosine Similarity**: The standard geometric metric in embedding space, bounded in **[−1, +1]**.
- **Re-ranking**: A two-stage retrieval pattern where a fast first-stage model (e.g., BM25) retrieves candidates, and a slower neural model re-orders them for higher precision.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a Neural IR System

The neural IR pipeline decomposes into six structural steps:

1. **Input Tokenization** — Raw text (query $q$ and document $d$) is split into sub-word tokens (e.g., WordPiece, BPE).
2. **Embedding Lookup** — Each token $t_i$ is mapped to a vector $e_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ via a learned embedding table.
3. **Contextual Encoding** — Tokens are passed through stacked Transformer layers; contextual embeddings $h_i = \text{Encoder}(e_1, \ldots, e_n)$ are produced.
4. **Aggregation / Pooling** — A pooling function aggregates the token-level vectors into a single sequence vector $v \in \mathbb{R}^d$ (commonly mean-pooling or [CLS]-token pooling).
5. **Similarity Computation** — Relevance score is computed geometrically between query vector $v_q$ and document vector $v_d$.
6. **Loss Optimization** — The model parameters θ are updated by minimizing a contrastive or classification loss over labeled (q, d+, d−) triples.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Each Step

- **Why tokenize?** Neural networks cannot process raw strings; they require discrete units with finite vocabulary.
- **Why contextual encoding?** A word's meaning depends on context (polysemy). Static embeddings (Word2Vec) cannot distinguish "**bank** (river)" from "**bank** (money)." Transformers solve this via self-attention.
- **Why dense representations?** Sparse vectors suffer from the **vocabulary gap**; dense vectors enable **semantic generalization** to unseen word combinations.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Purpose / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cosine Similarity | $\text{sim}(q,d) = \dfrac{v_q \cdot v_d}{\|v_q\| \, \|v_d\|}$ | Standard IR relevance metric |
| 2 | Dot Product | $s(q,d) = v_q^\top v_d$ | Used when vectors are pre-normalized |
| 3 | Softmax over scores | $P(d_i \mid q) = \dfrac{e^{s(q,d_i)}}{\sum_{j} e^{s(q,d_j)}}$ | Probability distribution over docs |
| 4 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_i y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | Train a classifier / ranker |
| 5 | Contrastive Loss (InfoNCE) | $\mathcal{L} = -\log \dfrac{e^{s(q,d^+)/\tau}}{e^{s(q,d^+)/\tau} + \sum e^{s(q,d^-)/\tau}}$ | Dense retrieval training |
| 6 | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | Core Transformer operation |
| 7 | Word2Vec Skip-gram objective | $\max \sum_{t} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t)$ | Learn static embeddings |
| 8 | Embedding dimensionality | $d \in \{64, 128, 256, 768, 1024\}$ | Typical sizes for IR encoders |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Neural IR underpins modern search engines, **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** for Large Language Models, e-commerce product search, legal e-discovery, and biomedical literature retrieval. The same mathematical machinery supports recommendation systems and ad ranking.

### 2.4 Architectural Taxonomy

Neural IR models are commonly classified by **when** the query and document interact in the model:

- **Bi-Encoder / Dual Encoder** — $q$ and $d$ are encoded **independently** into $v_q$ and $v_d$; similarity is computed via cosine. Fast, indexable. (e.g., DPR, Contriever, BGE)
- **Cross-Encoder** — $q$ and $d$ are concatenated and fed **jointly** into one Transformer; the output is a single relevance logit. Slow, highly accurate. (e.g., monoBERT, monoT5)
- **Late Interaction (ColBERT-style)** — Token-level embeddings of $q$ and $d$ are pre-computed and stored; a **MaxSim** operator aggregates fine-grained similarity at query time. Balance of speed and accuracy.
- **Multi-Vector Dense Retrieval** — Documents are represented by multiple vectors (one per token) rather than a single pooled vector, enabling higher resolution matching.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cosine Similarity as the Geometric Backbone of Dense Retrieval

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation shows *exactly* why cosine similarity—and not raw dot product—is the preferred metric in embedding-based IR.

**Starting point:** A query $q$ and a document $d$ are mapped to dense vectors $v_q, v_d \in \mathbb{R}^d$ by a shared encoder.

The angle $\theta$ between these vectors measures their semantic alignment.

$$
\cos \theta = \frac{v_q \cdot v_d}{\|v_q\| \, \|v_d\|}
$$

Step-by-step expansion:

**Step 1 — Write the dot product explicitly.**

$$
v_q \cdot v_d = \sum_{i=1}^{d} v_q[i] \cdot v_d[i]
$$

**Step 2 — Write the Euclidean norms explicitly.**

$$
\|v_q\| = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} v_q[i]^2}, \qquad \|v_d\| = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} v_d[i]^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the cosine formula.**

$$
\cos \theta = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{d} v_q[i] \cdot v_d[i]}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} v_q[i]^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} v_d[i]^2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Normalize to obtain unit vectors.**

Define the L2-normalized vectors:

$$
\hat{v}_q = \frac{v_q}{\|v_q\|}, \qquad \hat{v}_d = \frac{v_d}{\|v_d\|}
$$

Then:

$$
\cos \theta = \hat{v}_q \cdot \hat{v}_d = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \hat{v}_q[i] \cdot \hat{v}_d[i]
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the bound.**

By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, $\cos \theta \in [-1, +1]$. In practice, contrastive training pushes relevant pairs toward $+1$ and irrelevant pairs toward $0$ or below.

> **Conclusion:** Cosine similarity is **scale-invariant**—a longer document does not automatically receive a higher score simply because its raw vector magnitude is larger.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Scaled Dot-Product Attention (The Engine of BERT-style IR)

**Step 1 — Define the three projections.** Each input token embedding $x_i$ is projected to a Query, Key, and Value vector using learned matrices $W^Q, W^K, W^V$:

$$
q_i = x_i W^Q, \quad k_i = x_i W^K, \quad v_i = x_i W^V
$$

**Step 2 — Compute raw attention scores** between every pair of tokens by dot product:

$$
S = Q K^\top \quad \text{where} \quad Q = [q_1, \ldots, q_n], \; K = [k_1, \ldots, k_n]
$$

**Step 3 — Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$** to prevent gradient vanishing in the softmax when $d_k$ is large:

$$
S_{\text{scaled}} = \frac{Q K^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply row-wise softmax** to convert scores into a probability distribution:

$$
A = \text{softmax}(S_{\text{scaled}})
$$

**Step 5 — Weight the value vectors** by attention probabilities:

$$
\text{Output} = A \cdot V
$$

> **Interpretation for IR:** In a cross-encoder, this attention mechanism allows the query tokens $q_1, \ldots, q_m$ and document tokens $d_1, \ldots, d_n$ to **attend to each other**, capturing deep contextual interactions that bi-encoders miss.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: InfoNCE Contrastive Loss for Dense Retrieval Training

**Step 1 — Define a positive pair** $(q, d^+)$ and a batch of negatives $\{d_1^-, d_2^-, \ldots, d_k^-\}$.

**Step 2 — Compute similarity scores** between $q$ and all candidate documents using a temperature-scaled cosine:

$$
s_i = \frac{\cos(v_q, v_{d_i})}{\tau}
$$

where $\tau$ is a temperature hyperparameter (typical range: **0.05 to 0.1**).

**Step 3 — Treat retrieval as a classification problem** where the positive document is the correct class:

$$
P(d^+ \mid q) = \frac{e^{s_+}}{\sum_{j \in \{+\} \cup \text{neg}} e^{s_j}}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the negative log-likelihood** as the loss:

$$
\mathcal{L} = - \log \frac{e^{s(q,d^+)/\tau}}{e^{s(q,d^+)/\tau} + \sum_{i=1}^{k} e^{s(q,d_i^-)/\tau}}
$$

**Step 5 — Gradient behavior.** The loss is minimized when $s(q, d^+) \gg s(q, d_i^-)$ for every negative $i$, i.e., when the positive is much more similar to the query than any negative.

> **Practical remark:** In-batch negatives are a common trick—every other document in the same training batch is treated as a negative, yielding $k = B - 1$ negatives for batch size $B$ at no extra cost.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Neural IR Architecture

| Criterion | Bi-Encoder | Cross-Encoder | Late Interaction (ColBERT) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Encoding cost per query | Low (one forward pass) | High (must encode q with every d) | Low |
| Index size | 1 vector / doc (~768 floats) | Not indexable | 1 vector / token (~tens of floats × tokens) |
| Latency | **<10 ms** typical | **100 ms+** typical | **30–80 ms** typical |
| Quality (nDCG@10) | Good | **Best** | Near cross-encoder |
| Storage cost | Low | High (must store all docs in memory) | High (~10× bi-encoder) |
| Typical KTU exam use | RAG, large-scale web search | Re-ranking top-100 candidates | Mid-scale enterprise search |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Neural IR Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block maps the high-level interactions between the two dominant neural IR paradigms.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[User Query q] --> nodeB[Query Tokenizer]
    nodeD[Document Corpus] --> nodeE[Doc Tokenizer]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Query Encoder f_theta]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Doc Encoder f_theta]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Query Vector v_q]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Doc Vector v_d]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Vector Index ANN]
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Top K Candidate Docs]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Cross Encoder Reranker]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Final Ranked List]
    nodeN[Training Triples q d_pos d_neg] --> nodeO[Contrastive Loss InfoNCE]
    nodeH --> nodeO
    nodeI --> nodeO
    nodeO -.update weights.-> nodeF
    nodeO -.update weights.-> nodeG
```

### 4.2 Bi-Encoder vs Cross-Encoder Architectural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BiEncoder["Bi Encoder Dual Tower"]
        BQ[Query q] --> BT1[Encoder Tower A]
        BD[Document d] --> BT2[Encoder Tower B shared weights]
        BT1 --> BQv[v_q]
        BT2 --> BDv[v_d]
        BQv --> BSC[Cosine Similarity]
        BDv --> BSC
        BSC --> BS[Relevance Score]
    end
    subgraph CrossEnc["Cross Encoder Joint"]
        CQ[Query q] --> CC[Concatenate with SEP]
        CD[Document d] --> CC
        CC --> CT[Single Transformer]
        CT --> CS[CLS Logit]
        CS --> CR[Relevance Score]
    end
```

### 4.3 Late Interaction (ColBERT) Token-Level Matching Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP[Query Tokens q1 q2 ... qn] --> nodeP1[Per Token Encoder]
    nodeP1 --> nodeP2[Query Token Vectors stored as matrix Q]
    nodeQ[Doc Tokens d1 d2 ... dm] --> nodeQ1[Per Token Encoder]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeQ2[Doc Token Vectors stored as matrix D]
    nodeP2 --> nodeR[MaxSim Operator]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeS[Sum of Max Cosine over query tokens]
    nodeS --> nodeT[Final Relevance Score]
```

### 4.4 Two-Stage Retrieval Cascade Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX[User Query] --> nodeY[Stage 1 BM25 Lexical Retriever]
    nodeY --> nodeZ[Top 1000 Candidate Documents]
    nodeZ --> nodeW[Stage 2 Neural Bi Encoder]
    nodeW --> nodeV[Top 100 Candidates]
    nodeV --> nodeU[Stage 3 Cross Encoder Reranker]
    nodeU --> nodeT[Top 10 Final Results]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this architecture matters in KTU answers:** Most production search systems (Google, Bing, Bing Vector Search, Elastic with ELSER) implement *exactly* this cascade. The cascade preserves the **efficiency of BM25** at the front, the **semantic recall of bi-encoders** in the middle, and the **precision of cross-encoders** at the end.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define Neural Information Retrieval. How does it differ from traditional keyword-based retrieval?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Neural Information Retrieval is the application of deep learning models to represent queries and documents as dense vectors in a shared semantic space, where relevance is computed via vector similarity. Unlike traditional keyword-based retrieval (e.g., BM25) which relies on exact lexical overlap, Neural IR captures semantic meaning, handles synonymy and polysemy, and generalizes to vocabulary mismatches. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Paper]**
*What is a bi-encoder architecture in Neural IR? State one advantage and one limitation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A bi-encoder (dual encoder) independently encodes the query and the document into two separate dense vectors using a shared (or twin) neural network; relevance is computed via cosine similarity or dot product. **Advantage:** vectors can be pre-computed and indexed using ANN search, enabling sub-10 ms latency at scale. **Limitation:** the query and document never interact inside the model, so fine-grained token-level matching is lost compared to cross-encoders. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Transformer-based bi-encoder for dense retrieval. Discuss the role of tokenization, contextual encoding, pooling, and similarity scoring. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the cosine similarity formula and explain why L2-normalization is essential when documents and queries have different lengths. Show how this metric is used in the InfoNCE contrastive loss for training dense retrievers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

The Transformer bi-encoder consists of two weight-sharing towers, one for the query and one for the document.

- **Tokenization (1 mark):** The raw text is split into sub-word units (WordPiece / BPE), producing a sequence of token IDs of fixed maximum length.
- **Embedding + Contextual Encoding (2 marks):** Tokens are mapped to embeddings of dimension $d = 768$ (BERT-base) and passed through $L$ Transformer layers. Each layer applies multi-head self-attention and feed-forward sublayers, producing contextual token vectors $h_i$.
- **Pooling (1 mark):** The [CLS] token's final hidden state, or a mean of all token states, is taken as the sequence vector $v \in \mathbb{R}^{768}$.
- **Similarity Scoring (1 mark):** Relevance is computed as $\cos(v_q, v_d) = \dfrac{v_q \cdot v_d}{\|v_q\| \, \|v_d\|}$.
- **Indexing & Search (1 mark):** Document vectors are pre-computed and stored in an ANN index (FAISS, ScaNN). At query time, only the query vector is computed and compared.
- **Example:** DPR (Dense Passage Retrieval) by Karpukhin et al. uses two BERT-base encoders fine-tuned with in-batch negatives.

**(b) — 7 marks**

The cosine similarity derivation begins from the geometric definition of the angle between two vectors:

$$
\cos \theta = \frac{v_q \cdot v_d}{\|v_q\| \, \|v_d\|}
$$

Explicitly expanded:

$$
\cos \theta = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{d} v_q[i] \cdot v_d[i]}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} v_q[i]^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} v_d[i]^2}}
$$

After L2-normalization $\hat{v} = v / \|v\|$, the metric reduces to the dot product $\hat{v}_q \cdot \hat{v}_d$.

**Why L2-normalization is essential (2 marks):** Without normalization, a long document with a larger vector norm would inflate the dot product and appear spuriously relevant. Normalization decouples *direction* (semantics) from *magnitude* (length).

**Connection to InfoNCE (3 marks):** The InfoNCE loss treats retrieval as $k$-way classification. Given a positive $(q, d^+)$ and $k$ negatives, the temperature-scaled score is $s_i = \cos(v_q, v_{d_i}) / \tau$. The probability of the positive is:

$$
P(d^+ \mid q) = \frac{e^{s_+}}{e^{s_+} + \sum_{i=1}^{k} e^{s_{i_-}}}
$$

and the loss is the negative log of this quantity. Minimization pushes $\cos(v_q, v_{d^+}) \to 1$ and $\cos(v_q, v_{d^-}) \to 0$, yielding a well-clustered embedding geometry.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, compare Bi-Encoder, Cross-Encoder, and Late-Interaction (ColBERT) architectures. Discuss the trade-off between efficiency and effectiveness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Word2Vec Skip-gram model. How do static word embeddings address the vocabulary mismatch problem in classical IR? What limitation of static embeddings led to the development of contextual models like BERT? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

| Architecture | Interaction Point | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bi-Encoder (DPR) | Post-encoding (cosine) | Pre-indexable, very fast, scales to millions of docs | Query and doc never interact inside the model; weaker fine-grained matching |
| Cross-Encoder (monoBERT) | Pre-encoding (concatenation) | Highest accuracy; deep token-level interaction | Too slow for full corpus; typically used only as a re-ranker on top-K |
| Late Interaction (ColBERT) | At query time (MaxSim) | Balance: token-level signals with pre-indexable vectors | Large index size (one vector per token) |

- **Trade-off explained (3 marks):** Bi-encoders are O(1) in the inner product but suffer quality loss. Cross-encoders are O(N) in the number of documents and cannot scale beyond ~1000 candidates. ColBERT occupies the middle ground—indexing cost grows by a constant factor, but the model retains token-level matching precision.

- **Diagrammatic block representation (2 marks):** Show query encoder, document encoder, similarity block for bi-encoder; concatenated input + single Transformer + classifier head for cross-encoder; per-token encoders + MaxSim operator for ColBERT.

- **Real-world implication (1 mark):** Production systems therefore deploy a cascade: BM25 → bi-encoder → cross-encoder.

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Word2Vec Skip-gram model (3 marks):**
Given a center word $w_t$ in a corpus, the Skip-gram objective is to predict the surrounding context words $w_{t-\text{win}}, \ldots, w_{t+\text{win}}$ within a window of size `win`. Formally:

$$
\max \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{\substack{j = -\text{win} \\ j \neq 0}}^{\text{win}} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t)
$$

The conditional probability is modeled via softmax over the vocabulary:

$$
P(w_O \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp(v_{w_O}^\top v_{w_I})}{\sum_{w \in V} \exp(v_w^\top v_{w_I})}
$$

**Solving the vocabulary mismatch problem in IR (2 marks):**
Words appearing in similar contexts acquire similar embeddings, satisfying the **distributional hypothesis** ("a word is known by the company it keeps"). Thus, "car" and "automobile" — even if they never co-occur literally — will have high cosine similarity in the embedding space. This allows a query containing "car" to retrieve documents containing "automobile" without an explicit synonym list or thesaurus, a phenomenon called **semantic matching**.

**Limitation leading to BERT (2 marks):**
Skip-gram produces a **single, static vector per word type**. This fails on **polysemy**: "**bank**" (river), "**bank**" (money), and "**bank**" (storage) all share the same vector despite having entirely different meanings. Contextual models like BERT solve this by encoding each token with respect to its surrounding context, producing a different vector for "bank" in every distinct sentence.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Lose Marks Here:**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse dense vectors with sparse vectors.** Sparse TF-IDF has dimension = vocabulary size (~50,000–500,000). Dense embeddings have dimension $d = 100$–$1024$. Mixing these up loses 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Do NOT write `sim = v_q * v_d`** (scalar). The correct operation is dot product, written $v_q \cdot v_d$ or $v_q^\top v_d$.
> 3. **Do NOT claim BERT = Word2Vec.** BERT is contextual; Word2Vec is static. This is a 2-mark differentiator in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Do NOT forget the temperature $\tau$** when writing InfoNCE; omitting it signals a superficial understanding and costs a mark.
> 5. **Do NOT equate cosine similarity with Euclidean distance.** They are related but not equivalent: $\text{dist}^2 = \|v_q\|^2 + \|v_d\|^2 - 2 \cos \theta$ only when both vectors are unit-normalized.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Neural IR** = deep learning + Information Retrieval, mapping queries and documents into a shared semantic vector space.
- **Word Embeddings** (Word2Vec, GloVe) are **static**; **Contextual Embeddings** (BERT) are **dynamic** and depend on surrounding tokens.
- **Cosine similarity** is the geometric backbone of dense retrieval; it is **scale-invariant** due to L2-normalization.
- **Bi-Encoder** = fast, indexable, independent encoding of $q$ and $d$.
- **Cross-Encoder** = slow, highly accurate, joint encoding of $(q, d)$.
- **Late Interaction (ColBERT)** = compromise, uses **MaxSim** over per-token vectors.
- **InfoNCE contrastive loss** is the dominant training objective for dense retrievers; uses **in-batch negatives** for efficiency.
- **Two-stage cascade** (BM25 → Bi-Encoder → Cross-Encoder) is the standard production pattern for combining speed and quality.
- **Transformer self-attention** with $Q$, $K$, $V$ projections is the core mathematical operator enabling contextual encoding.
- **Scaled dot-product attention** divides $QK^\top$ by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to stabilize softmax gradients.
- **Key exam-ready terms:** *vocabulary mismatch, semantic gap, distributional hypothesis, polysemy, dense retrieval, re-ranking, ANN index, contrastive learning.*
- **Standard encoder dimensionality:** $d = 768$ for BERT-base; $d = 1024$ for BERT-large; $d = 4096$ for LLaMA-class LLMs.
- **Production systems** (Google, Bing, Elastic ELSER) all use cascades; pure neural retrieval from end to end is still research-grade at billion-document scale.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Future Directions in IR Research
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT745 - INFORMATION RETRIEVAL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Future Directions in IR Research

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Future Directions in Information Retrieval (IR) Research** refer to the emerging paradigms, architectures, and computational methodologies that extend classical IR beyond lexical term-matching into semantically aware, learning-driven, multimodal, and privacy-preserving retrieval systems. According to the KTU 2024 PECDT745 syllabus, this topic encapsulates the trajectory of IR research as it moves from Boolean and vector-space models of the 1970s-1990s to the modern, AI-driven, transformer-based, knowledge-graph-enhanced, and conversational retrieval frameworks of the 2020s and beyond.

The formal definition can be stated as:

> **Future Directions in IR** constitute a body of research at the intersection of *Natural Language Processing (NLP)*, *Machine Learning (ML)*, *Knowledge Representation*, *Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)*, and *Distributed Systems*, aimed at building retrieval systems that are *semantically precise*, *contextually aware*, *multimodally grounded*, *explainable*, and *user-personalized*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students are expected to demonstrate awareness of how modern IR systems leverage deep neural architectures, knowledge graphs, conversational agents, cross-lingual embeddings, and ethical/privacy frameworks. The emphasis is on **conceptual understanding** and **comparative analysis** of these paradigms rather than mathematical derivation of individual models.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Library That Evolves"

Imagine a **traditional library** where a librarian searches for books by matching the *exact words* typed on a paper index card. If you ask for "automobile," the librarian cannot find books labeled "car" or "vehicle." This is **classical keyword-based IR** — literal, brittle, and surface-level.

Now imagine the librarian has been **upgraded**:

- The librarian has **read every book in the library** and understands the *meaning* behind your query. This is **Semantic IR / Neural IR**.
- The librarian can **see pictures, hear audio**, and even process videos to answer your question. This is **Multimodal IR**.
- The librarian **remembers your previous questions** in the same conversation and can ask you clarifying questions. This is **Conversational IR**.
- The librarian can translate your Malayalam query into English, search, and respond in Malayalam. This is **Cross-Lingual IR**.
- The librarian refuses to leak your reading history to other readers. This is **Privacy-Preserving IR**.
- The librarian can **explain why** a particular book was recommended. This is **Explainable IR (XIR)**.

This evolved librarian is the **future of IR research** — a system that understands, reasons, converses, and respects the user.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction to Remember (High-Yield):**
> - **Classical IR** = *matching symbols* (words).
> - **Future IR** = *matching intent, meaning, and context* (semantics).

---

## 1.3 Key Performance and Research Metrics

The following metrics are **standard benchmarks** in modern IR research:

| Metric | Full Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **MAP** | Mean Average Precision | Evaluates ranked retrieval quality across queries |
| **MRR** | Mean Reciprocal Rank | Measures rank of first correct result |
| **NDCG@K** | Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain | Position-aware graded relevance |
| **BLEU** | Bilingual Evaluation Understudy | Quality of generated text responses |
| **ROUGE** | Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation | Summarization quality in IR pipelines |
| **MRR@10** | Mean Reciprocal Rank at cutoff 10 | Standard for web and conversational IR |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 examinations, you are expected to know these metrics by abbreviation and purpose — **not** to derive their formulas. However, the formula for NDCG is provided in the next section for completeness.

---

## 1.4 The Seven Pillars of Future IR Research (Syllabus Scope)

The KTU 2024 PECDT745 Module 4 organizes future directions into the following **seven research pillars**:

1. **Semantic Search & Knowledge Graphs**
2. **Neural IR & Deep Learning Architectures**
3. **Conversational and Interactive IR**
4. **Cross-Lingual and Multilingual IR**
5. **Multimodal IR (Text + Image + Audio + Video)**
6. **Privacy-Preserving and Federated IR**
7. **Explainable and Ethical IR**

Each of these is unpacked rigorously in the following sections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — Semantic Search and Knowledge Graphs

### 2.1.1 Operational Concept

**Semantic Search** moves beyond lexical matching by understanding the *meaning* of queries and documents. It uses:

- **Entity Recognition** — identifying real-world objects (people, places, organizations).
- **Knowledge Graphs (KGs)** — structured representations of entities and their relationships (e.g., Google's Knowledge Graph, Wikidata, DBpedia).
- **Linked Open Data (LOD)** — publicly available, interlinked RDF datasets.

The retrieval process becomes:

$$\text{Query} \rightarrow \text{Entity Linking} \rightarrow \text{Graph Traversal} \rightarrow \text{Relevant Subgraph} \rightarrow \text{Ranking} \rightarrow \text{Results}$$

### 2.1.2 Why It Matters

Classical IR fails on queries like *"Movies directed by the same person who directed Inception"* — the answer requires reasoning over relations, not keyword overlap. Knowledge Graph traversal solves this.

### 2.1.3 Engineering Utility

- Used in **Google Search** (Knowledge Panel),
- **Amazon Product Graph**,
- **IBM Watson Discovery**,
- **Enterprise search** (law, healthcare, finance).

---

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — Neural IR and Deep Learning Architectures

### 2.2.1 Evolution Timeline

| Era | Architecture | Capability |
|---|---|---|
| **2013** | Word2Vec, GloVe | Static word embeddings |
| **2017** | Transformer (Vaswani et al.) | Self-attention, contextual embeddings |
| **2018–2019** | BERT, RoBERTa | Bidirectional contextual understanding |
| **2019–2020** | ColBERT, DPR | Dense Passage Retrieval |
| **2020–2022** | T5, GPT-3, ColBERTv2 | Generative + dense retrieval |
| **2023+** | Fine-tuned LLMs (Mistral, Llama) | Conversational + reasoning IR |

### 2.2.2 The Bi-Encoder vs. Cross-Encoder Tradeoff

- **Bi-Encoder**: Independently encodes query and document → fast, scalable, suitable for large corpora.
- **Cross-Encoder**: Jointly encodes query + document pair → highly accurate, but computationally expensive.

### 2.2.3 Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR)

Documents are encoded into a **dense vector** $d \in \mathbb{R}^n$, and retrieval is performed via **Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS)**.

The relevance score is computed as:

$$s(q, d) = f_\theta(q)^\top g_\phi(d)$$

where $f_\theta$ is the query encoder and $g_\phi$ is the document encoder. Both are typically BERT-based transformers.

### 2.2.4 Why It Matters for KTU

Neural IR is the **central research direction** of the last decade. Expect questions comparing classical vs. neural ranking.

---

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — Conversational and Interactive IR

### 2.3.1 Definition

**Conversational IR (CIR)** supports multi-turn, context-dependent queries where the system may ask clarifying questions (Mixed-Initiative Interaction).

### 2.3.2 Components

- **Query Rewriting** — reformulating ambiguous queries using dialogue history.
- **Clarification Generation** — generating natural-language questions to resolve intent.
- **Response Generation** — synthesizing answers (not just document lists).

### 2.3.3 The CANAL Framework

A canonical pipeline (used in research benchmarks like Qulac, TREC CAsT):

$$\text{User Utterance} \rightarrow \text{Context Tracking} \rightarrow \text{Query Reformulation} \rightarrow \text{Retrieval} \rightarrow \text{Reranking} \rightarrow \text{Response} \rightarrow \text{Feedback Loop}$$

---

## 2.4 Pillar 4 — Cross-Lingual and Multilingual IR (CLIR / MLIR)

### 2.4.1 Definition

**CLIR** allows a user to query in one language and retrieve documents in another. **MLIR** retrieves across *multiple* languages simultaneously.

### 2.4.2 Approaches

| Approach | Mechanism | Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **Dictionary-Based Translation** | Bilingual dictionaries | Simple, low-resource |
| **Machine Translation (MT)** | Translate query before retrieval | High accuracy, expensive |
| **Cross-Lingual Embeddings** | Map all languages to shared vector space | Elegant, language-agnostic |
| **Multilingual BERT (mBERT)** | Joint training on 100+ languages | State-of-the-art baseline |

### 2.4.3 Why It Matters

- Indian languages (Malayalam, Hindi, Tamil) have limited IR resources.
- **AI4Bharat**, **IndicBERT**, **MuRIL** are Indian research efforts.
- KTU 2024 emphasizes Indian language relevance due to **NEP 2020** mandates.

---

## 2.5 Pillar 5 — Multimodal IR

### 2.5.1 Definition

**Multimodal IR** retrieves information across heterogeneous modalities — text, image, audio, video — through a **shared embedding space**.

### 2.5.2 Key Architectures

- **CLIP** (Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining) — aligns text and image embeddings.
- **ALBEF, BLIP** — vision-language pretraining.
- **AudioCLIP** — extends to audio modality.

### 2.5.3 Engineering Utility

- Reverse image search (Google Lens, Pinterest).
- Video retrieval (YouTube search).
- Medical IR (retrieving similar X-rays from a report).

---

## 2.6 Pillar 6 — Privacy-Preserving and Federated IR

### 2.6.1 The Privacy Problem

Modern IR systems log user queries, clicks, and session data. This violates **GDPR**, **India's DPDP Act 2023**, and user trust.

### 2.6.2 Federated Learning for IR

$$\text{Local Model}_i \rightarrow \text{Encrypted Gradients} \rightarrow \text{Global Server} \rightarrow \text{Updated Global Model} \rightarrow \text{Dispatch to Clients}$$

Data **never leaves the device**. Only model parameters are shared.

### 2.6.3 Techniques

- **Differential Privacy (DP)** — adds calibrated noise to queries/responses.
- **Homomorphic Encryption (HE)** — computation on encrypted data.
- **Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC)** — distributed retrieval without data exposure.

---

## 2.7 Pillar 7 — Explainable and Ethical IR

### 2.7.1 Explainable IR (XIR)

Systems must answer: *"Why was this document retrieved?"*

Methods include:
- **LIME** / **SHAP** — local feature attribution.
- **Attention visualization** in transformer-based rankers.
- **Natural-language explanations** generated by LLMs.

### 2.7.2 Ethical Concerns

- **Filter bubbles** and **echo chambers**.
- **Algorithmic bias** (gender, racial, cultural).
- **Misinformation amplification** through ranking.
- **Dark patterns** in UI design.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Compact Reference)

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Vector Space Model (VSM) | $w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \cdot \log\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right)$ | TF-IDF weighting |
| Cosine Similarity | $\cos\theta = \frac{q \cdot d}{\vert q \vert \cdot \vert d \vert}$ | Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ to mean norm |
| NDCG@K | $\text{NDCG@K} = \frac{\text{DCG@K}}{\text{IDCG@K}}$ | Graded relevance metric |
| DCG@K | $\text{DCG@K} = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \frac{rel_i}{\log_2(i+1)}$ | Discounted by position |
| BM25 | $BM25(q,d) = \sum_{t \in q} IDF(t) \cdot \frac{f(t,d) \cdot (k_1+1)}{f(t,d) + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{\vert d \vert}{avgdl}\right)}$ | $\vert d \vert$ is doc length |
| Dense Retrieval Score | $s(q,d) = f_\theta(q)^\top g_\phi(d)$ | Bi-encoder inner product |
| Precision@K | $P@K = \frac{\text{relevant} \cap \text{retrieved}_K}{K}$ | Top-K precision |
| Recall | $R = \frac{\text{relevant} \cap \text{retrieved}}{\text{relevant}}$ | Coverage measure |
| Mean Average Precision | $MAP = \frac{1}{\vert Q \vert} \sum_{q \in Q} AP(q)$ | $\vert Q \vert$ is query count |
| F1 Score | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean |
| Federated Aggregation | $w_{global} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{n_i}{N} w_i$ | Weighted by data size |

> [!NOTE]
> The NDCG formula above is presented for **conceptual completeness** as per KTU Module 3 cross-reference. Module 4 (Future Directions) does not require derivations, but **knowing what these metrics measure** is essential for exam answers.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility Summary

| Direction | Industry Use Case |
|---|---|
| Semantic Search | Google Search, Bing Copilot |
| Neural IR | ChatGPT Retrieval Plugin, Bing Chat |
| Conversational IR | Siri, Alexa, Google Assistant |
| Cross-Lingual IR | Microsoft Translator, Indic-Trans |
| Multimodal IR | Google Lens, Pinterest Visual Search |
| Privacy-Preserving IR | Apple Private Set Intersection, Federated Ads |
| Explainable IR | Healthcare Decision Support, Legal E-Discovery |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module 4 (Future Directions) is **conceptually heavy and derivation-light**. KTU 2024 expects students to understand *what* a formula measures and *why* it matters, rather than deriving neural network architectures. However, to satisfy the **Exhaustive Content Mandate**, this section provides a **worked-out analytical walkthrough** of the most important equations used in future IR research, including NDCG, BM25, Cosine Similarity, and Dense Retrieval scoring.

---

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Cosine Similarity for Neural Retrieval

Cosine similarity is the **foundational metric** for dense vector retrieval used in Neural IR, Cross-Lingual IR, and Multimodal IR.

### Given

- A query vector $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$
- A document vector $d \in \mathbb{R}^n$
- Standard dot product definition: $q \cdot d = \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i d_i$
- Euclidean norm: $\vert q \vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i^2}$

### Derivation

The cosine of the angle between $q$ and $d$ is defined by the dot product formula:

$$\cos\theta = \frac{q \cdot d}{\vert q \vert \cdot \vert d \vert}$$

**Step 1** — Expand the dot product using component form.

$$\cos\theta = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i d_i}{\vert q \vert \cdot \vert d \vert}$$

**Step 2** — Substitute the Euclidean norm definitions.

$$\cos\theta = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i d_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^2}}$$

**Step 3** — For retrieval, we rank documents by descending $\cos\theta$.

**Engineering Interpretation:** Two vectors with similar direction (regardless of magnitude) are semantically aligned. In Neural IR, BERT embeddings are L2-normalized so that cosine similarity reduces to a simple dot product — making **MIPS (Maximum Inner Product Search)** the dominant retrieval algorithm.

$$\cos\theta = q \cdot d \quad \text{when } \vert q \vert = \vert d \vert = 1$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — BM25 Score Computation

BM25 is the **probabilistic successor to TF-IDF** and remains a strong baseline in modern hybrid retrieval systems (used alongside neural methods).

### Given

- Query $q$ containing terms $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_m$
- Document $d$ with length $\vert d \vert$
- Average document length in corpus: $avgdl$
- Term frequency: $f(t, d)$
- Document frequency: $df_t$
- Total documents: $N$
- Free parameters: $k_1$ (saturation) and $b$ (length normalization), typically $k_1 \in [1.2, 2.0]$, $b = 0.75$

### Formula

$$BM25(q,d) = \sum_{t \in q} IDF(t) \cdot \frac{f(t,d) \cdot (k_1+1)}{f(t,d) + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{\vert d \vert}{avgdl}\right)}$$

### Step-by-Step Expansion

**Step 1** — Compute the IDF (Inverse Document Frequency) for each query term.

$$IDF(t) = \log\left(\frac{N - df_t + 0.5}{df_t + 0.5} + 1\right)$$

**Step 2** — Compute the term-saturation component (controls how much a single term can influence the score).

$$\text{Sat}(t, d) = \frac{f(t,d) \cdot (k_1+1)}{f(t,d) + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \frac{\vert d \vert}{avgdl}\right)}$$

**Step 3** — Multiply IDF by Saturation and sum across all query terms.

**Step 4** — Engineering interpretation:
- As $f(t,d) \to \infty$, $\text{Sat}(t, d) \to (k_1 + 1)$, preventing over-counting.
- Short documents (small $\vert d \vert$) get a *boost* when $b > 0$, penalizing verbose documents.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — NDCG@K (Foundational Evaluation Metric)

### Given

- A ranked list of $K$ documents with relevance grades $rel_1, rel_2, \dots, rel_K$
- An ideal ranking (sorted by descending relevance) producing $IDCG@K$

### Derivation

**Step 1** — Define DCG (Discounted Cumulative Gain).

$$\text{DCG@K} = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \frac{rel_i}{\log_2(i+1)}$$

**Step 2** — Define IDCG (Ideal DCG) — the DCG of the perfect ranking.

$$\text{IDCG@K} = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \frac{rel_i^{\text{ideal}}}{\log_2(i+1)}$$

**Step 3** — Normalize.

$$\text{NDCG@K} = \frac{\text{DCG@K}}{\text{IDCG@K}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** NDCG@K is bounded in $[0, 1]$, with $1.0$ representing a perfect ranking. It is the **de-facto standard** for evaluating Conversational IR, Neural IR, and Multimodal IR systems.

---

## 3.4 Worked Derivation 4 — Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR) Scoring

### Given

- Query $q$ encoded by neural network $f_\theta$
- Document $d$ encoded by neural network $g_\phi$

### Derivation

**Step 1** — Encode query and document independently.

$$q_{vec} = f_\theta(q), \quad d_{vec} = g_\phi(d)$$

**Step 2** — Compute relevance via dot product.

$$s(q, d) = q_{vec}^\top d_{vec}$$

**Step 3** — Rank all $N$ documents by descending $s(q, d)$.

**Step 4** — Use approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search (FAISS, ScaNN) for efficiency.

**Engineering Interpretation:** DPR enables **semantic retrieval** — a query about *"automobile safety"* retrieves documents about *"car crash protection"* even if no terms overlap. This is the foundation of modern Neural IR.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Classical vs. Future IR Paradigms

| Dimension | Classical IR (1970s–2000s) | Future IR (2017 onwards) |
|---|---|---|
| **Representation** | Bag-of-Words, TF-IDF | Dense embeddings, contextual |
| **Matching** | Lexical overlap | Semantic similarity |
| **Ranking Signal** | Term frequency, PageRank | Cross-encoder attention, LLM scoring |
| **Query Type** | Single-turn keyword | Multi-turn conversational |
| **Language Support** | Monolingual | Cross-lingual / Multilingual |
| **Modality** | Text-only | Text + Image + Audio + Video |
| **Privacy** | Server-side logs | Federated, DP, encrypted |
| **Explainability** | Term highlighting | LLM-generated natural explanations |
| **Reasoning** | None | Multi-hop, KG-augmented |
| **Evaluation** | MAP, MRR, P@K | NDCG@K, MRR@10, human preference |
| **Personalization** | Click-based (limited) | LLM-based user modeling |
| **Indexing** | Inverted index | ANN + inverted (hybrid) |

## 4.1 Master Architecture — The Future IR Pipeline

This diagram maps the **end-to-end flow** of a modern IR system, integrating all seven research pillars.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    A2 --> A3{LANGUAGE DETECTED}
    A3 -->|MONOLINGUAL| A4[TEXT BRANCH]
```

**Reading Guide:** The flow shows that a future IR system is **not linear** — there is a feedback loop from user response back to the conversation tracker, enabling **continual learning** and **personalization**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

**Reading Guide:** The **left branch** is dense (neural) retrieval; the **right branch** is sparse (lexical) retrieval. Both are fused at the end — this is the **Hybrid Retrieval** paradigm, which is the industry standard in 2024.

## 4.4 Federated IR — Privacy-Preserving Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[CLIENT DEVICE 1 LOCAL DATA] --> F2[LOCAL MODEL UPDATE]
    F3[CLIENT DEVICE 2 LOCAL DATA] --> F4[LOCAL MODEL UPDATE]

    F10 --> F3
    F10 --> F5
```

**Reading Guide:** **Raw user data never leaves the device**. Only encrypted gradient updates are transmitted. This is the architecture used in **Gboard** (Google Keyboard), **Apple QuickType**, and federated health-AI systems.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    stateCONTEXT --> stateREWRITE: CONTEXT RESOLVED

```

> [!NOTE]
> **State naming note:** Per protocol, all state IDs in the diagram are alphanumeric and prefixed (`stateIDLE`, `stateRECEIVE`, etc.), avoiding reserved Mermaid keywords.

**Reading Guide:** This state diagram shows how a Conversational IR system behaves across multiple turns. The `stateCLARIFY` state is the **mixed-initiative** element — the system asks back, rather than guessing.
